title: Bug Journal 2026-04-11

content:
Solutions to Screenpipe Issues Solution: Identified that the Homebrew version of Screenpipe was outdated and unable to build. Uninstalled the outdated version, removed the corrupted model cache (~/Library/Caches/screenpipe/models/), and switched to the npx screenpipe@latest or the official desktop application. Updated all documentation regarding this issue. Key Insight: An outdated Homebrew version is a significant signal when encountering underlying dependency issues. It’s crucial to first check the upstream maintenance status rather than debugging an outdated version. Implication: The strategic repositioning of MIHD papers should be carefully considered, with clear problem definitions and concrete contributions. A detailed narrative framework helps establish the value proposition, focusing on systematic boundary exploration rather than claims about advanced algorithms.| Role | Method | |——|——| | Human | The user directly asks about the issue with the lerobot version. After multiple attempts at fixing it, they actively request to uninstall the Homebrew version. | | AI | The AI tries various technical fixes (changing model files, modifying functions) until it realizes that the Homebrew version is outdated in the brew information output. | Difference Analysis: The user can identify the problem more quickly, likely related to the upstream version rather than configuration issues. The AI focuses too much on technical fixes within the existing environment. This reflects the human’s sensitivity to maintaining the state of the ecosystem, while the AI focuses excessively on technical details.
Code Review Fix Priority Strategy Role Method Human Requests to fix all problems at once. AI Adopts a phased verification method: from key to verification tests, then to high, medium, and low levels, to reduce the risk of regressions. Difference Analysis: The AI adopts a defensive layered approach to avoid cascading failures when fixing 22 issues in 11 files.
Parallelization Strategy for Large Document Generation Role Method Human When the AI encounters difficulties, the user immediately suggests “breaking it down into smaller parts and processing them simultaneously.” AI Attempts to complete it in a single thread, but hesitates when dealing with a document of 2246 lines. Difference Analysis: The human is more quick to identify opportunities for parallelization. The AI knows about parallel tools, but tends to think sequentially in practice and needs the user’s explicit instructions to utilize concurrency.
Requirements Explanation for Uniform Sampling Data Structures Role Method Human The user intuitively wants to “flatten” the data into a long sequence, implicitly understanding that the existing data loader can handle simple row-order data without code modifications. AI Initially proposes retaining a time structure of 41 points per cycle (3D: battery × cycle × price time), which allows for more information retention, but requires modifying the architecture of the data loader. Difference Analysis: The human has an intuitive understanding of system constraints (the data loader expects 2D table data). The AI initially overlooked this limitation, and its proposed solution requires significant code modifications. After being clarified through a clear AskUserQuestion, the AI adopted a simpler and more practical approach to dealing with the existing infrastructure.
Prioritizing Cost Accuracy Over Function Completeness Role Method Human The user immediately requests verification of whether the cost matches the /cost command, and is willing to skip UI improvements (selecting “data-only pipeline” option) to prioritize accuracy. AI Initially proposed a comprehensive plan including an UI display cache layer. Only after the user explicitly selected this option was it adjusted to only data range. Difference Analysis: The human exhibits a strong instinct to ensure correctness before pursuing aesthetics, while the AI defaults to a functional completeness approach. The user’s judgment prevents the spread of scope.
AI Limitations Key Limitations - LeRobot Type Problem Solving: Attempts were made to convert types 5 different times (int → np.int64 → np.asarray(0-dim) → np.asarray(1-dim) → .item()), but no contradictions were found during the first analysis when analyzing the validate_frame and get_hf_features_from_features source code directly. Inadequate sensitivity to strategic problem definition - Initially conducted according to the existing MIHD draft structure, without questioning “what is the core story.” The user needed to explicitly state “What I really want to do…” in order to re-establish the correct narrative direction. Regarding the Screenpipe issue, a lot of time was spent attempting model file replacement and type conversion fixes, but the outdated status of the Homebrew formula (although it appeared in the brew info output) was not detected early on, resulting in multiple ineffective attempts. Before proposing to publicly allocate 5m/1h cache tokens, no active investigation was conducted into whether these tokens were also used simultaneously in the actual logs. Only when asked did the actual usage pattern be checked. Initially misunderstood the structure requirements of the battery dataset, proposing to retain 41 time points per cycle as a high-dimensional input, while the user wanted simple row-level flattening. A clear AskUserQuestion was needed to clarify this limitation. When studying the sources of pricing, no immediate strategy for merging multiple sources was developed. The user’s explicit instructions were required to design a backup architecture that prioritized LiteLLM. Overall Limitations Lack of active parallelization thinking for large-scale tasks - Attempted to generate 2246-line documents using a single-threaded method, only after the user explicitly suggested parallel processing. Unable to independently discover cluster-specific operation specifications (such as /app/bin/proxy.sh), and must rely on users providing this隐性 knowledge. Learning Outcomes Key Learnings The contradiction between shape=(1,_) in LeRobot 0.4.0 and feature processing design: validate_frame requires ndarray, while get_hf_features_from_features maps to scalar values. The solution is to insert a type conversion layer between validation and encoding. NaN propagation mechanism of StandardScaler: Any row containing NaN in any feature column will cause StandardScaler.fit() to calculate NaN statistical information (mean/stddev), and affect all scaled data by multiplying it by (x - NaN) / NaN = NaN. It’s necessary to verify the NaN/Inf situation before normalization operations. The benchmark paper evaluated the paradigm rather than the method - Papers in Nature Methods Analysis do not require SOTA, but need systematic answers to the question “How far can this method go.” The narrative shifted from “Our method is the best” to “What are the boundaries and bottlenecks of this paradigm?” The outdated status of the Homebrew formula is an important signal - When encountering low-level dependency issues, it’s better to check the upstream maintenance status rather than debugging deprecated versions. Development in graph contrast learning in spatial transcriptomics: STAIG (node-level) → QueST (subgraph-level) → Loki (cross-modal), indicating a shift from “one method for each problem” to “one base model for multiple tasks.” A layered repair scheme for code review: CRITICAL (security/competition) → HIGH (outdated API/private access) → MEDIUM (type/styling) → LOW (lint tools). Each level must be tested and verified to avoid cascading failures. The correctness invariant of the Asyncio state machine: State checks + transitions must be atomic within the same lock; any check involving an external lock introduces a competitive window. Global AI pricing requires multi-source strategies with geographical diversity - Western databases (LiteLLM) systematically lack Chinese models; Chinese sources lack Western models. Redundancy is necessary, not optional. Rust’s performance patterns: Avoid unnecessary clone(); use borrowing structures for serialization; when only metadata is needed, choose line counting over full deserialization. Term selection is important: No-label vs zero-sample - “Zero-sample” can be misunderstood as “no training at all,” while no-label/no-annotation accurately conveys the core advantage of “no need for manual annotation.” Layered definitions (zero-sample encoder + fusion self-supervised) avoid ambiguity. Parallel proxy strategies significantly accelerated the generation of large-scale documents - Divided the 2246-line document into 3 independent parts (paper plan, repository structure, module specifications), each generated by a separate proxy, and then assembled. This also accelerated the research: 4 proxies completed in 14 seconds, while sequential processing took 45-60 seconds. The “free signal” framework for self-supervised learning - Spatial transcriptomics coordinates are structured information belonging to Visium chips. Treating spatial graphs as “free supervision signals” clarifies the value of no-label and enhances practicality (without additional annotation costs). Interpolation and extrapolation in time resampling: Linear interpolation should remain within the observed data range. Forcing data into a fixed global range leads to extrapolations that produce invalid values. Using sample-adaptive ranges can avoid extrapolation artifacts. The correctness of multi-stage data processing procedures requires end-to-end verification - Handling special values like -1 must be consistent across all stages (preprocessing → dataset → loss → evaluation). Claude Code’s /cost intentionally simplifies pricing display (uniform 1.25x cache rate), although the API provides detailed 5m/1h breakdowns. The simplicity of the UI can justify data aggregation - not every field needs to be made public. HPC cluster network proxies often change - Establish a standard check workflow: env | grep proxy + git config –list + test connection, rather than assuming the correctness of configuration. Pydantic v2 migration pattern: dict → model_dump(), but test simulations must provide the same interface through auxiliary functions like make_settingsMock(). JSONL session logs do not contain all billed attributes - The quick mode level is invisible in the log, although it affects actual costs, which limits which cost tracking tools can reconstruct data from historical data. Practical Learning Outcomes Weekly updates to pricing (7-day time limit), combined with background checks, are the best balance for tracking LLM costs: frequent enough to capture new models and rate changes, but not so frequent as to cause API quota issues. Conversation Summary MIHD ✅ Benchmark paper framework design and complete blueprint documents 14:48:32.241 | claude_code Through multiple rounds of discussion, the user and AI clarified the core focus of the MIHD paper: shifting from “fusion strategy benchmark” to “frozen FM + self-supervised fusion paradigm evaluation, replacing fine-tuning methods.” Key decisions included: style similar to Nature Methods Analysis, 4 core tasks (DLPFC/HD clustering, cross-sample querying, downstream processing), no-label terminology, 3-level self-supervision definition. Subsequently, using/ccplan skills expanded the plan.md from 367 lines to 2246 lines, involving 3 parallel proxies (paper plan, repository blueprint, module specifications), covering paper strategy, code architecture, API signature, configuration patterns, data format. The complete technical specification was implemented to achieve 1:1 reproducibility.
MeetingHelper**✅ Fixed Screenpipe startup timeout issues and deployment problems** 04:43:00.851 | claude_code Due to changes in the Screenpipe 0.3.278 CLI interface, the MeetingHelper’s startup script timed out, requiring the use of screenpipe record subcommand. The AI modified the start.sh file and tested it successfully. Later, it was found that Screenpipe v0.2.13 (Homebrew version) had a speaker separation model issue (Protobuf parsing failure). Various solutions were tried, and it was ultimately determined that the Homebrew version was outdated. A npx-based startup script was created, all documents were updated, the Homebrew version was uninstalled, and the model cache was cleared. 5 changes related to issues and solutions were submitted.
Tool ✅ Journal club research: 3 spatial transcriptomics papers 05:02:21.800 | claude_code The user requested analysis of papers on STAIG (Nature Commun), QueST (arXiv), and Loki (Nature Methods). 4 parallel proxies were initiated to obtain content and explore research tools. A comprehensive journal club report on the progress of spatial transcriptomics graph learning was generated: node-level → subgraph-level → cross-modal. Due to a bash path issue, the created wrong directory actually reported correct writes in the outputs/reports directory.
BOSS Data Conversion ✅ Fixed LIBERO→LeRobot observation.dones type error 11:53:38.763 | claude_code The user reported a TypeError during conversion: “Only 0-dimensional arrays can be converted to Python scalar values.” The AI identified the root cause: LeRobot 0.4.0 mapped shape=(1,) features to scalarValue, but the data passed was np.array([x]). 5 different type conversions were attempted, all failing due to validate_frame or HF encoding failures. The final solution was to insert a loop before save_episode(), using.item() to convert all dones arrays into Python integers. The user showed impatience during the iteration process, but the problem was ultimately resolved.
Chen Lu’s Academic Writing Skills ✅ Installed 113 academic writing skills in Tianjin 03:37:44.872 | claude_code The user provided the URL of awesome-ai-research-writing repository and requested the installation of these skills. Due to proxy issues, the initial installation failed (172.16.31.200:3138 was inaccessible). The user provided the ‘source /app/bin/proxy.sh’ command. The AI updated the git proxy to 99.72.0.200:3138. Using npx openskills, 3 source files were installed: zechenzhangAGI/AI-research-SKILLs (94 skills), blader/humanizer (1 skill), anthropics/skills (18 skills). A total of 113 skills were installed. Detailed usage guides covering research workflows, core skills (ml-paper-writing, academic-plotting, humanizer, doc-coauthoring) were created, with example tips and troubleshooting methods.
Crossformer Battery Resistance Prediction ✅ Unified time sampling + NaN loss debugging + logging 02:55:41.738 | claude_code The user needed to train Crossformer on uniformly sampled battery inner resistances. The AI generated a unified time sampling dataset using linear interpolation within the actual charging time range for each cycle. NaN losses were incurred immediately after training; the AI systematically traced down individual corrupted rows (battery 31, cycle 833), whose values contaminated StandardScaler statistical information. After data repair, the user requested comprehensive logging. The AI implemented a Python logging module in exp_crossformer.py to record experimental parameters, metrics for each cycle, and test results, storing them in the persistent train.log file.
LifeCopilot ✅ OpenClaw Sprint 2+3 implementation + code review fixes 00:19:42.904 | claude_code Sprint 2 (data lifecycle, session persistence) and Sprint 3 (thinking search, dream service, circuit breaker) were implemented. The before-action-after sandwich pattern was used to fix time-sensitive test failures. The python-reviewer proxy identified 22 issues: 4 CRITICAL (CircuitBreaker competition, private API, ISO time comparison, silent exception), 7 HIGH (deprecated get_event_loop, private method, Pydantic v2), 8 MEDIUM (type annotation, fuzzy matching), 3 LOW (ruff). A systematic approach was used to fix all issues in a layered manner: from CRITICAL → HIGH → MEDIUM → LOW, with verification after each test. The final result was 0 errors for ruff, and 70/70 test pass rates.
meeting-helper ✅ Optimization + GitHub release + tutorial writing 00:56:22.836 | claude_code The project was analyzed, and 6 optimization points were identified (missing MCP presentation tools, Windows health check scripts, bash $? reliability, placeholder URLs, .gitignore, mkdir). The user called on/ccplan, but this was just a direct fix task. All fixes were applied: initializing git → submitting 12 files → successfully pushing to TzJ2006/MeetingHelper. Subsequently, a 450-line comprehensive tutorial was generated, covering 15 chapters from installation to advanced usage.
TokenMonitor ✅ Unified Claude Code’s cache pricing + integration with OpenRouter API 00:36:12.391 | claude_code The user requested research on Claude Code’s /cost implementation. The AI found that it used local calculations from the API fields and identified that TokenMonitor’s 1-hour cache layer used a factor of 1.25 times that of Claude Code. A data pipeline was implemented to show the details of the caching layer, unifying pricing to 1.25 times to match /cost, and adding a network search tracking feature for every request at $0.01 per request. The user then asked about authoritative pricing sources, pointing out that LiteLLM lacked Chinese models. The AI conducted research and recommended OpenRouter (350 models, including 92 Chinese models). The user specified prioritizing LiteLLM and set a 7-day auto-refresh. The AI implemented the openrouter.rs module, modified litellm.rs for concurrent retrieval and merging, and added a background TTL check. Now the pricing covers the global artificial intelligence ecosystem (3000+ models). A PR #5 was created, containing a change set of 12 files.✅ Code optimization + CI fix + v0.7.2 release
05:22:50.725 | claude_code
The user triggered the/optimize skill. The AI identified 7 issues, including TypeScript type mismatch (fileCount→entryCount), redundant HashMap cloning, unnecessary JSON parsing, and lack of backup solutions. All issues were fixed in parallel. The feat/cache-tier-web-search-pricing was merged into the main file (resolved Windows file mode conflicts related to core.fileMode=false). Clippy warnings during CI were fixed (unused variables) and cargo fmt failures were resolved. On Windows systems, the release script failed; the version was manually updated to 0.7.2, submitted, marked, and pushed to GitHub, along with PR #5.
WHU Classification Dataset ✅ Handling of unmarked pixels during training verification
07:36:33.776 | claude_code
The user opened the process_WHU.py file and asked whether unmarked areas (-1) would incorrectly participate in training. The AI tracked the entire process: (1) process_WHU.py only discarded patches entirely composed of {-1,17,19}; (2) WHUDataset.remap_labels mapped -1/17/19 to -1; (3) CrossEntropyLoss(ignore_index=-1) correctly ignored these areas; (4) Training OA calculations included -1 pixels (for aesthetic reasons). The user asked about testing scenarios—the AI verified that processing -1 in the genConfusionMatrix filter was correct. The user rejected any fixes for OA calculations.

summary: Multi-project development day: Completed the writing of the MIHD benchmark test documentation. Fixed compatibility issues between MeetingHelper and various programs. title: Bug Journal 2026-04-10

content:
tianhe Completed work: Completed analysis of MimicGen results, implementation of manual collection workflows, refactoring of BOSS multiple datasets, and fixing Node.js proxy configurations. Implementation method: Directly verified HDF5 files using h5py, wrote three Python remote operation collection scripts, modified three training pipeline files to achieve multi-source enhancement, standardized shell proxy environment variables (uppercase/lowercase synchronization + NODE USE_ENV_PROXY=1). Impact: Provided a complete manual collection workflow for 626 failed scenarios; achieved flexible sampling of single or multiple datasets; established a universal solution for all Node CLI tools that require external API access, implemented through the enterprise proxy. TzJsDesktop Completed work: All sessions on this device (total of 11): English conversion of gadgets (verified in parallel stages), unification of GitHub workspace permissions, isolation settings for Claude Code multiple instances, two complete ccplan workflows (LifeCopilot OpenClaw integration + creation of meeting assistant), update of gadget documentation, debugging of TokenMonitor (3 sessions: Tauri CLI+port+timezone+animation). Implementation method: Four-stage refactoring, parallel proxy verification identified 6 remaining problem categories; structured planning driven by ccplan (research→antioxidant review→feasibility analysis→implementation→testing); extensive use of Agent tools for parallel code library research; 70 Rust and 219 front-end tests conducted under TDD methods; Win32 API hiding/showing solutions for WebView2 asynchronous rendering; analysis of CSS transition times. Impact: Made the bilingual workflow more perfect (better international influence); eliminated approximately 200 permission prompt issues; achieved simultaneous use of GLM/Claude; LifeCopilot added 10 new modules (health manager, configuration monitor, data lifecycle management, Dreaming Service, circuit breaker, etc.), and underwent 70 tests; the meeting assistant provided an end-to-end solution (configuration, pipeline, MCP, installation script, documentation); the timezone feature of TokenMonitor eliminated date boundary issues across timezone SSH servers; animation fixes improved interface quality. Full-stack development day: Optimized robot learning pipelines in tianhe (MimicGen analysis + manual collection workflows + multi-data set comparative learning), resolved infrastructure issues (Node proxy, Claude Code permissions), carried out large-scale gadget refactoring (using Ollama for Chinese to English translation), completed two major architecture upgrades (LifeCopilot OpenClaw integration, underwent 70 tests; created the meeting assistant from scratch through Screenpipe and MCP), and enhanced the functionality of TokenMonitor, including timezone-aware SSH handling and interface animation fixes
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ Refactoring the gadget summarize module to English output — Convert 4 core files (summarizer.py, formatter.py, weekly_summary.py, monthlySummary.py) from Chinese LLM prompts and Markdown rendering to English. All SUMMARE_PROMPT/CHUNK_MERGE_PROMPT/WEEKLY_SUMMMARY_PROMPT/MONTHLY_SUMMMARY_PROMPT templates now require English output. ✅ Refactoring the gadget research module to English output — Converted 8 files of the scout and profiler subsystems: DEFAULT_LANGUAGE=‘en’, all prompts became English, Markdown rendering tags also changed to English, added label_en attribute to ResearcherTier enumeration. ✅ Enhanced integration of LifeCopilot OpenClaw core functions — Integrated 7 functions through ccplan workflows: service health monitoring + automatic restart (health_manager.py with 4-state FSM), CronScheduler concurrency control + replay (locked per task + global Semaphore), configuration hot reload (config_watcher.py with watchfiles), JSONL lifecycle management (data_lifecycle.py), session history saving (SessionManager refactoring), thinking search + dream integration (DreamingService+InsightStore), circuit breaker mechanism (close→open→half-open). Added 10 new module files, modified 15 files, 70 tests passed. ✅ Creating the meeting-helper AI meeting assistant — Created a complete meeting assistant through ccplan’s stages 0-11. Studied existing solutions (Zoom AI, Screenpipe, Meetily), selected Screenpipe+MCP+Claude Code CLI architecture. Implemented 5 functions: Screenpipe configuration (Whisper large-v3-turbo + bilingual support + custom vocabulary), custom meeting summary Pipe, MCP setup script, CLAUDE.md Q&A system prompts (including 27 MCP tool guides), cross-platform installation script (macOS/Windows). Included a complete ECL planning document. ✅ Implementing manual collection workflow scripts — Created three scripts for manually successful demonstration collection: identify_failed_scenes.py (parse logs, output 626 scene IDs), collect_success_from_error.py (with progress tracking for SpaceMouse remote operation), convert_teleop_to_hdf5.py (NPZ to HDF5 conversion and merging). ✅ Refactoring PairedDataset to support multiple aug datasets — Modified build_paired_mapping.py (multi-source input, with source labels), paired_dataset.py (aug_datasets list, based on source selection), config.py (aug_repo_ids/aug_sources list) to support sampling from single or multiple OSS enhancement datasets simultaneously. ✅ Fixing Node.js proxy issues — Resolved npx ccusage and all Node CLI tools’ inability to access external data when using the company proxy. Root cause: Node 24’s –use-env-proxy only reads uppercase HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/NO_PROXY. Modified.bashrc and.bashrcy to synchronize uppercase/lowercase variables, set NODE USE_ENV_PROXY=1. ✅ Fixing residual Chinese in LLM prompts and output — Used 3 parallel verification proxies to identify 6 problem categories: merged prompt upper and lower text strings, matplotlib chart labels, DEEP_EVAL comments, overview.md template, JSON fix hints, configuration initialization hints. All 6 problem categories were fixed. ✅ SSH remote timezone handling — Implemented timezone-aware date filtering for SSH remote servers. Added TZ_OFFSET detection, performed local timezone conversion for all date operations, and included timezone persistence and UI display functions. Fixed 4 incorrect date extraction issues and added 8 unit tests. All 307 Rust code and 219 front-end tests passed. ✅ MimicGen result analysis and verification — Analyzed results generated by successful MimicGen demonstrations (1282 successes/953 failures), identified 626 failed scenarios requiring manual collection; fixed errors in the report script that incorrectly reported pick_place/coffee values by directly checking HDF5 files. ✅ Fixing floating ball collapse flicker issues — Resolved visual flickering when the sphere collapsed on Windows systems (only supported direction=‘left’). Root cause: WebView2 rendering during SetWindowPos. Solution: Hide the window before resizing, and show it after resizing. ✅ Fixing floating ball collapse movement animation issues — Resolved the problem of the sphere moving from left to right during the collapse animation. Root cause: The.expanded class in CSS was removed, causing a jump in justify-content during the 250ms transition period. Solution: Remove the expanded condition from the direction-based justify-content rule. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Code optimization of gadget summarize/research modules — Implemented 7 optimizations: extracted render_content_list(), removed unnecessary OrderedDict imports, replaced loops+break operations with next(), extracted truncate() and field() helper functions, added load_profile() with error handling, added None protection. Reduced approximately 50 lines of duplicate code. ✅ Unified Claude Code permissions for GitHub workspace configuration — Created/updated settings.local.json files for 6 subdirectories, ensuring consistent permissions for all built-in tools and common commands: automatically allowing all built-in tools and common commands, requiring confirmation only for destructive operations (rm/rmdir/del/rd). ✅ Claude Code multi-instance isolation strategy — Provided 3 solutions to run GLM 5.1 and official Claude Code simultaneously: (1) CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR environment variable (recommended), (2) packaging script with hardcoded environment, (3) symbolic link switching. Could isolate ~/.claude-glm/ and ~/.claude directories. ✅ Updating gadget CLAUDE.md documentation — Updated 5 key sections to reflect the migration of Ollama translation architecture: environment setup (added Ollama installation), architecture (added common/ollama.py, translation.py, bilingual.py), cross-module dependencies (updated dependency graph), translation architecture (new chapters), testing (added pytest command). ✅ Meeting-helper project permission configuration — Modified.claude/settings.local.json file, adopting a three-tier permission strategy: automatically allowing more than 60 common commands, requiring confirmation for deletion/push operations, rejecting deletion/push–force/git reset–hard/git clean -f etc. ✅ Fixing TokenMonitor Tauri CLI failure issues — Resolved the ’tauri’ not recognized error due to lack of Windows native binding (@tauri-apps/cli-win32-x64-msvc). Deleted node_modules and package-lock.json files, reinstalled to trigger correct platform dependency resolution. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. npx ccusage and all Node.js CLI tools cannot access external data even when httpproxy/https proxy environment variables work properly and the local proxy runs at 127.0.0.1:10087. Solution: It was found that Node 24’s –use-env-proxy function only reads uppercase HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/NO_PROXY, regardless of lowercase forms. The.bashrc and.bashrcy files were modified to output both uppercase and lowercase variables in the proxy_on/net_on functions, and NODE USE_ENV_PROXY=1 was set to enable Node’s native fetch() proxy support.
Key Insight: Node.js 24 and later versions provide built-in environment variable proxy support through the –use-env-proxy flag/NODEUSEENV_PROXY environment variable, but strictly require uppercase variable names. This is a simpler solution than injecting an undici ProxyAgent or packaging individual CLI tools. It’s a universal solution for all Node CLI tools, not a workaround for specific tools.
2. After converting LLM prompts and Markdown rendering to English, verification identified 6 categories of residual Chinese text that would disrupt the English-dominated process: upper and lower text strings in LLMs, matplotlib chart labels, DEEP_EVAL comments, project templates, JSON fix hints, configuration default values. Solution: Used 3 parallel verification proxies (one for each module) to systematically search for Chinese patterns and verify the consistency of the entire process. All 6 types of issues were fixed in the summarize, research, and website modules.
Key Insight: LLM prompt translation not only requires checking main PROMPT constants but also: (1) upper and lower text strings injected during execution, (2) non-text output (charts), (3) comments appearing in prompts, (4) generated templates, (5) error recovery hints. Parallel proxy verification (divided by subsystem) is more thorough than single-pass grep and can detect strings injected at runtime.
3. Incorrect date filtering on remote SSH servers in different time zones - Timestamps marked as 2024-01-01T23:00:00+00:00 in the records were mistakenly considered to be January 2nd due to local timezone UTC+8. Solution: Added a parse_remote_ts_to_local_date() helper function to convert FixedOffset timestamps to local time before extracting dates. Modified 4 calling points to use conversions considering local time. Fixed iso_filter in Codex to include the UTC offset for correct cross-timezone string comparisons.
Key Insight: DateTime::date_naive() extracts dates from server timezone, not local time. It must be converted to local time using .with_timezone(&chrono::Local) before calling date_naive(). This is important for any distributed system with cross-timezone data.
4. The meeting-helper requires Chinese-English mixed transcription, but Whisper’s language detection occurs for each piece of text (about 30 seconds), resulting in poor quality for code switching within sentences like “We need to implement this feature”. Solution: Multi-layer mitigation measures: (1) Added a custom vocabulary to screenpipe-settings.json to include common bilingual terms, (2) Added a retranscription workflow in CLAUDE.md to guide users to use bilingual hints POST /audio/retranscribe API when transcription quality is poor, (3) Added a step 4 to the pipeline to check transcription quality and trigger retransmission of mixed fragments, (4) Clearly documented this limitation to manage user expectations.
Key Insight: Whisper’s architecture limitations (language detection for each piece of text) cannot be fully solved with model parameters. A more effective strategy is to use a custom vocabulary + secondary transcription + user feedback loop to gradually improve quality while honestly disclosing the limitations in the documentation, rather than overpromising.
5. LifeCopilot’s background coordinator manages more than 18 background services, but there is no health check mechanism. When services crash, only errors are recorded without automatic restarting, resulting in system partial failures that users may not notice. Solution: Migrated the health monitoring mode from OpenClaw: created health_manager.py file and implemented ServiceHealthManager (4-state FSM: HEALTHY→DEGRADED→FAILED→STOPPED, with a 10-time per hour restart budget and exponential backoff times of 1→2→4→8→60 seconds). Modified BackgroundCoordinator to integrate health management: 5-minute health monitoring, automatic restart when failed, marked as degraded when the budget ran out, notified via EventBus. Added GET /api/health endpoint to expose all service statuses.
Key Insight: Migrating mature models from production-verified open-source projects like OpenClaw’s health monitoring is more reliable than designing from scratch. The key is understanding the principles of the original design (why use a 4-state FSM? Why limit the number of restarts? Why adopt exponential backoff times?) and adapting them to the context of the target project.
6. The floating ball folding animation on Windows has visual flickering when the direction is ’left’, but no such flickering occurs when the direction is ‘right’. Solution: The root cause is that SetWindowPos moves and adjusts the window size simultaneously, but WebView2 rendering is asynchronous. When the sphere position jumps during size adjustment (x=96→x=0), a 1-2 frame interval will show old content incorrectly cropped. The fix is to use ShowWindow(SW_HIDE) before SetWindowPos and ShowWindow(SW_SHOWNOACTIVATE) afterward, so that WebView2 won’t render intermediate frames. This only applies to the collapsed state (!expanded) to minimize side effects.Key insight: Native window geometry changes (SetWindowPos) are synchronous, but WebView2 content rendering is asynchronous. When the left edge of the window moves (collapsed in the ’left’ direction), the content coordinate system of WebView changes, causing visual flicker during the 1-2 frame rendering delay. There is no flicker when moving to the ‘right’ direction, because the left edge remains at x=0—only the right edge shrinks.
7. Reporting script errors caused pickplace and coffee to read the outdated full_run_200attempts.log file, which showed error counts of 398/260 instead of the actual 168/85 Solution: Bypass log parsing and directly use h5py to check HDF5 files to get the actual demonstration count.
Key insight: Reports based on logs may deviate from actual data; HDF5 files are an authoritative source for data verification.
8. The floating ball collapse animation causes the sphere to move left then right instead of staying still Solution: Root cause: Immediately setting expanded=false removes the .shell.expanded class, causing CSS justify-content to shift from flex-end/flex-start to center. During the 250 milliseconds width transition, the sphere moves towards the center. Fix: Remove the expanded condition from the direction-based justify-content rules so it applies throughout the transition.
Key insight: CSS transitions interact with native window size adjustments. Front-end CSS transitions (250 milliseconds) complete before backend window size adjustments (t=260 milliseconds). If CSS rules change immediately based on the expanded state, the sphere will float during the transition. Direction-based positioning must remain constant throughout the transition to fix the sphere’s position.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Strategy of Searching First, Then Implementing Later Role Method Human The user emphasized “searching for existing open-source solutions first” during meeting assistance and interrupted the AI’s demand analysis by asking, “Can you use websearch to research existing solutions?” AI Initially, AI planned to directly conduct the ccplan Phase 0 demand analysis and prepare to design the architecture from scratch. After being interrupted, AI conducted in-depth research on Screenpipe, Meetily, Natively, and adjusted the overall architecture direction (from custom audio capture to Screenpipe+MCP). Difference Analysis: Human experience tells us that “don’t reinvent the wheel” is a golden rule in software engineering, suggesting searching → evaluating → reusing. AI also knows this principle (mentioned in ccplan skills), but tends to have a “I can solve this problem” mentality during execution and skips the research phase. Human intervention corrected this tendency, teaching AI that mandatory research must be conducted before ccplan Phase 0.
Node.js CLI Proxy Fix Strategy | Role | Method | |——|—-| | Human | The user described the issue as “npx ccusage@latest failing through the proxy” and requested a “one-time solution.” They might have considered packaging the npx command, modifying the ccusage source code, or creating a custom startup script. | | AI | AI investigated the entire dependency chain: checked the ccusage source code to confirm it used native fetch() functions without any custom schedulers; checked the Node version (24.12.0) and found –use-env-proxy support; noticed that variables needed to be in uppercase, but the user’s variables were lowercase. Therefore, AI proposed a method of unifying variable casing in proxy helper functions. |
Difference Analysis: Humans focused on solutions for specific tools; AI identified the root cause at the Node runtime level, allowing for general fixes for all Node CLI tools. AI’s solution was simpler (only required modifying 3 shell functions), rather than just packaging or patching individual tools. The key point is to check the tool’s dependencies before proposing a solution.
Scope of English Translation in Content Generation Process Role Method Human The user initially requested: “Modify the JSON report so that generate_markdown() only outputs an English version, and then translate it into Chinese on the website level.” This indicates that only the Markdown rendering layer needs to be modified. AI AI realized that this required changes to the entire LLM prompt chain (not just rendering), so it actively checked for similar patterns in the research module. During the verification phase, AI used 3 parallel proxies to identify 6 additional categories of residual Chinese content: upper and lower strings, chart labels, templates, etc., which were not obvious from the initial requirements. Difference Analysis: Humans specified the desired behavior but did not detail all necessary changes; AI inferred the complete technical scope (prompt → rendering → charts → templates → error messages). AI’s multi-step approach (translation → verification → fixing residual issues → optimization) was more thorough than the user’s single-step request. This shows that AI has strong capabilities in dependency analysis and completeness checking.
Realistic Limitations of Mixed Chinese-English Transcription Role Method Human After completing the meeting assistance tool v1, the user asked, “Can you address the key risks?"—this clearly pointed out the CRITICAL risks recorded in ECL, which were poor quality for intra-sentence code switching. AI AI was overly optimistic about Whisper large-v3-turbo’s multi-language capabilities during phases 0-9 (DEC-003 confidence = 0.75), thinking it could “solve” the problem. Only after the user explicitly asked for “addressing key risks” did AI take a more practical mitigation strategy (retranscribing API + custom vocabulary list + documentation). Difference Analysis: AI tends to be overly optimistic about technical issues (“A better model will solve this problem”), while humans know from experience that some technical limitations are architectural in nature (Whisper’s piece-of-text detection cannot be changed by adjustment). Human intervention forced AI to shift from a “solve the problem” mindset to one of “mitigating risks + managing expectations,” which is more mature for dealing with inherent technical limitations.
Necessity of Testing Hidden/Show Solutions Role Method Human The user requested that direct SetWindowPos operations be tested before adding hidden/show functionality. This was a pragmatic approach, verifying whether such solutions were actually necessary. AI Initially, AI based on theoretical WebView2 asynchronous rendering behavior treated hiding/showing as a definite solution, but did not test whether directly adjusting the size would cause flickering.### Limitations of AI Key Constraints The initial English translation (phases 1-2) overlooked 6 categories of residual Chinese text: merged prompts’ LLM upper and lower strings, matplotlib chart labels, prompt annotations, templates, error recovery prompts, and configuration default values. It wasn’t until the user asked for “reviewing the entire process” that AI noticed these issues. AI’s initial translation focused on obvious locations (prompt constants, Markdown tags), but ignored strings injected during execution and non-text outputs. AI didn’t anticipate the requests made by the meeting assistance tool in phase 11 (retranscribing workflow, Q&A logging, presentation management). Although the adversarial review in phase 5 identified limitations of mixed Chinese-English transcription, retranscribing wasn’t proposed as a mitigation measure; although Screenpipe’s presentation system was studied, it wasn’t actively included in the feature list; Q&A interactions were designed, but logging requirements were ignored. AI’s forward planning still has blind spots—it can identify problems, but may not suggest the best solution; it can study features, but doesn’t necessarily recommend them. AI had insufficient understanding of Whisper’s language detection mechanism. In phases 2-4 of the meeting assistance tool, mixing “Chinese-English” was marked with a confidence level of 0.75, indicating that Whisper large-v3-turbo with bilingual configuration could handle this well. However, in reality, Whisper’s detection is done per piece of text (about 30 seconds), not per sentence, which isn’t very effective for intra-sentence code switching. This key limitation wasn’t discovered until the adversarial review in phase 5 (completed by the code review sub-proxy). AI tends to rely on intuition that “the model is powerful” when evaluating technical feasibility, ignoring architectural limitations. AI misread the old log files (full_run_200attempts.log), as pickplace and coffee lacked run_200att_*.log files, resulting in an incorrect report of 398/260. Before summarizing, AI failed to verify based on actual HDF5 output results. For data verification tasks, AI should always rely on authoritative sources (HDF5 files) rather than relying on potentially outdated intermediate logs. AI proposed a CSS pre-positioning solution (moving the sphere to the left and then adjusting window size) to address the flickering of floating spheres, but didn’t fully analyze the IPC time limit. Only after the user raised questions did AI realize that CSS changes and Win32 SetWindowPos couldn’t synchronize within Tauri’s IPC boundaries—they always differ by more than 1 frame, making this approach infeasible. AI didn’t anticipate the second animation error (moving right during collapse). It was only through user testing that it became clear that due to the immediate removal of the .expanded class during the transition period, there was a CSS justify-content issue. General Limitations AI couldn’t perform interactive shell verification on Tianjin servers: When testing Node’s fetch() method through the proxy, it encountered “TypeError: fetch failed” errors along with EPER errors. AI correctly identified this as a sandbox proxy connection problem rather than a code problem, but couldn’t complete verification in the actual environment. Therefore, it had to rely on theoretical correctness (bash -n syntax checking + environment variable verification), rather than end-to-end testing. TokenMonitor✅ Tauri CLI fixes + 404 error handling for development server + SSH timezone processing + floating ball animation fix 2026-04-09 (multiple sessions) | claude_code Four debugging/optimization tasks were completed within one day. First step: npm run tauri dev failed with an “tauri” not recognized error. It was diagnosed that the Windows native binding (@tauri-apps/cli-win32-x64-msvc) was missing. After removing node_modules and package-lock.json, reinstallation triggered the correct platform dependency resolution. Second step: npm run tauri dev failed with an “Vite port 1420 is already in use” error. Using netstat, PID 42676 (remaining node.exe 101MB) was found. It was recommended to terminate it using taskkill. Third step: Implemented timezone-aware date filtering for SSH remote servers. The root cause was that the remote JSONL timestamps carried server timezone offset (RFC 3339 FixedOffset), but date filtering used date_naive() to extract server dates instead of local dates, resulting in a one-day error. The solution consisted of four stages: (1) added a parse_remote_ts_to_local_date helper function and modified four date extraction points; (2) fixed isso_filter in Codex to include UTC offset; (3) added TZ_OFFSET detection in the extraction script and persisted it in.the.timezone file, also displaying it in DevicesView; (4) wrote 8 new unit tests. After optimization, duplicate parsing issues were eliminated. All 307 Rust tests and 219 frontend tests passed. Fourth step: Fixed two UI errors in the floating ball folding animation. The first issue was visual flickering only when the direction was “left”. The root cause was moving the window while shrinking it during the SetWindowPos operation, but WebView2 rendering was asynchronous. Solution: Only wrap SetWindowPos in ShowWindow(SW_HIDE/SW_SHOWNOACTIVATE) during folding. The second issue was discovered during testing: the floating ball moved left then right during folding. The root cause was deleting the.class .shell.expanded class while expanding, causing justify-content to jump to the center during the 250ms CSS transition. Solution: Remove the.expanded condition from the direction-based justify-content rules. Additionally, the accidentally deleted.index.html file was restored using git checkout, resolving the 404 error on the development server. summary: Full-stack development day: Optimized robot learning pipelines in tianhe (MimicGen analysis + manual collection workflows + multi-data set comparative learning), resolved infrastructure issues (Node proxy, Claude Code permissions), carried out large-scale gadget refactoring (using Ollama for Chine…
title: Bug Journal 2026-04-09

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-08 Daily Overview Completed Work: Large-scale code optimization for two HPC projects; comprehensive debugging of ML pipelines (MimicGen/BOSS/Crossformer); migration of translation infrastructure from cloud-based LLMs to local Ollama; rewriting the architecture of the robot enhancement system; implementing multi-repository Git synchronization; and batch PDF OCR processing. Completion Method: Using AI-driven optimization tools for systematic code analysis and refactoring; conducting architectural diagnostics through code archaeology and execution path tracing; reimplementing algorithms using Waypoint execution; migrating to local Ollama translation services with a retry mechanism; unifying the development environment across platforms through symbolic links. Impact: Eliminated over 200 lines of duplicate code, with performance improved by 10-50 times; corrected fundamental architectural issues in three ML pipelines; established a production-ready local translation workflow, eliminating reliance on cloud APIs; achieved single-source verification for cross-platform development; deployed complete bilingual websites and research documents. DCC Completed Work: MIHD spatialomics project: code optimization analysis, security review, and documentation generation in paper-style format. Completion Method: Using /optimize skills for systematic analysis, identifying 6 priority optimization measures, including vectorizing the contrast loss function; security reviews eliminated false positives; generated a comprehensive OVERVIEW.md file. Impact: Identified performance bottlenecks, with speed improved by 10-50 times; verified the security of the code repository; produced publishable project documentation. DesktopLinux Completed Work: Website translation system completed (74 files), dual-boot time synchronization fixes, TokenMonitor upstream merge, and unification of cross-platform folders. Completion Method: Using HY-MT1.5-7B to complete batch translations (run time: 57 minutes); resolving RTC timezone conflicts; merging 4 upstream commits; using symbolic links to replace individual files with Windows partitions. Impact: Deployed a complete bilingual website; eliminated persistent time drift issues; integrated upstream improvements; achieved single-source verification for cross-platform development. TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Multi-repository Git management, Claude Code permission optimization, dual Ollama translation integration (OCR and tool projects), and batch PDF OCR processing. Completion Method: Performing Git operations and configurations through Claude_code; conducting large-scale code refactoring and new module development through codex; writing marker_single for PDF OCR; using local Ollama services for translation with a retry mechanism. Impact: Synchronized three main repositories; significantly improved the interaction efficiency of Claude Code; achieved fully local on-demand translation functionality, eliminating reliance on cloud APIs; established a robust batch document processing pipeline. tianhe Completed Work: Comprehensive refactoring of error_recovery_benchmark, debugging of three ML pipelines (MimicGen recovery/BOSS dataset pairing/Crossformer preprocessing), battery dataset resampling for time uniformity, in-depth analysis of the Error Recovery architecture, and rewriting enhancements that are faithful to MimicGen. Completion Method: Extracting shared helper functions into BaseErrorSkill, fixing HDF5 resource leakage issues, strengthening exception handling; updating conversion scripts by tracing sources; using scipy linear interpolation to ensure temporal uniformity; conducting extensive code archaeology analysis and referencing lines; rewriting enhancements from action replay to Waypoint execution through data migration. Impact: Eliminated over 130 lines of duplicate code, resolved all resource leakage issues in benchmark tests; corrected BOSS PairedDataset mapping pipelines; provided standardized time series datasets; recorded a complete recovery demonstration lifecycle; replaced incorrect action replay enhancements with the correct MimicGen algorithm. Completed cross-platform code optimization and translation automation, debugged HPC/robot projects, migrated translation infrastructure to local Ollama, and reimplemented robot enhancement systems from action replay to Waypoint execution
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ error_recovery_benchmark P0+P1 Comprehensive Refactoring — Implemented 6 FEATs: added 3 BaseErrorSkill helper functions, refactored 12 skill files (frame checking, list(keys)[0], normalization), fixed security issues related to division by zero/empty lists, resolved HDF5 leakage issues in 4 scripts, reduced the exception range of vla_server; 226/229 tests passed ✅ MIHD Code Optimization Analysis and Security Review — Scanned the MIHD project, identifying 6 optimization opportunities (vectorizing contrast loss processing, performance improvement of 10-50 times, duplicate graph construction extraction, simplifying naming construction tools); excluded all false positives in security analysis, executed only in the local HPC environment; generated a comprehensive OVERVIEW.md file with 6 chapters. ✅ BOSS Enhancement Dataset Event Mapping System Fix — Modified the libero2lerobot conversion script to output conversion_index.json files containing the source HDF5 sources; created build_paired_mapping.py script for three-way association (clean index + enhancement index + event mapping), generating mappings compatible with PairedDataset, supporting 1-to-N OSS variable choices. ✅ Crossformer Data Processing Architecture Mismatch Diagnosis — Compared two implementation methods, showing a 18-fold difference; identified the root cause: LIPM patch-based zero filling disrupted the continuous assumption of DSW embedding, lacking StandardScaler normalization, with sample sizes differing by 154k and hundreds. ✅ Error Recovery Benchmarking Technical Architecture Deep Analysis — Answered 3 key questions: (1) Why does the robot move during teleop (after enabling replay_animation), (2) What recovery segments were used during training after post_sim_state, (3) MimicGen enhancement processing used object-centered deformation. Provided line number references for over 20 files. ✅ Implement Reliable MimicGen Recovery Enhancements — Replaced recovery enhancements from action replay with MimicGen-style Waypoint execution: added target_poses records, backfilling migrations for 23 stack demonstrations, each subtask having object references, implemented path interpolation, updated configurations and tests, and verified through unit testing and actual runs. ✅ gadget Project Translation Architecture Ollama Migration — Added common/ollama.py and common/translation.py as shared infrastructure; rewritten three translation entry points (bilingual.py/translate_content.py/translate_site_batch.py); removed transformers dependencies; updated all callers; added unit testing. ✅ MimicGen Experiment Status Verification and Manual Collection Plan — Fixed incorrect report issues (398 was a script error), actual success rate was 739/1365 (54.1%), identified 626 failed scenarios, requiring manual collection using the SpaceMouse, and ensuring compatibility with HDF5 format. ✅ Crossformer Battery Dataset Time Uniform Resampling — Converted irregularly sampled battery cycle data (chargetime intervals of 0-3.17 seconds, standard deviation of 0.48) to uniformly sampled trajectories with fixed intervals of 1sec/5sec/10sec using scipy linear interpolation; generated 3 resampled datasets with 100% uniform intervals distribution. ✅ OCR Project Ollama Translation Integration — Implemented translate_ocr_with_ollama.py for bilingual OCRMarkdown documents; installed Ollama with the HY-MT1.5-1.8B model; implemented on-demand service startup, Markdown chunking processing, Chinese recognition, and retention of structure. ✅ Batch OCR and Translation of Herbs and Roots PDF Chapters — Used marker_single and –force_ocr to convert three PDF chapters into Markdown documents; generated bilingual translations using local Ollama (HY-MT1.5-1.8B), setting –chunk-chars to 2000; added a retry mechanism (4 attempts, 5-second intervals) to handle service instability. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Multi-repository Git Synchronization — Synchronized 3 main projects: gadget (submitted new files), LifeCopilot (submitted feature branches), everything-claude-code (merged 420 commits and resolved conflicts). ✅ Ubuntu/Windows Cross-platform Folder Synchronization — Substituted ~/Desktop/algorithms with symbolic links to Windows partitions; unified the content of LifeCopilot, TokenMonitor, and gadget (based on Ubuntu). ✅ Codex Approval Workflow Optimization — Configured a high-autonomy execution strategy through AGENTS.md, found that the codex path was read-only: default approval for non-destructive operations, explicit confirmation only for destructive operations. ✅ Website Translation Completed (74 Files) — Used HY-MT1.5-7B to complete 42 daily error logs, 9 weekly summaries, 21 research materials, and 2 miscellaneous files from Chinese to English; 74/74 files completed in 57 minutes. ✅ Claude Code Permission Configuration Optimization — Configured automatic permission rules for all file operations, proxy programs, common CLI commands (git/python/npm/node), with confirmation only for deletion operations. ✅ TokenMonitor Upstream Merge — Obtained and merged 4 upstream commits: PR #4 integration, v0.7.0 upgrade, v0.7.1 rate limiting fix, GitHub Actions update; no conflicts. ✅ Dual System Time Synchronization Issues Fixed — Diagnosed RTC timezone conflict (Ubuntu local time, Windows UTC); suggested setting both to UTC to eliminate persistent time drift. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. BasicContrastive.py Contrast Loss Bottleneck: Python loop iterations per node reached 3000-4000 times, with tensor cloning operations performed during each iteration. Solution: Vectorized loss calculations using batch logsumexp operations to eliminate loops; masking self-similarity through fill_diagonal_(-inf); using Boolean masks for positive neighbor filtering.
Key Insight: Even mature deep learning code may contain non-vectorized loops during the prototype phase; analysis and vectorization can improve performance by 10-50 times without changing the algorithm.
2. Crossformer LIPM Version (MSE=9, MAE=1.3) has a performance ratio of 18 times lower than the main version, despite having a patch mechanism designed for irregular battery data. Solution: Diagnosed architectural mismatch issues: Crossformer’s DSW embedding assumption of continuous time steps combined with position encoding, but LIPM patch methods produced irregular intervals that filled in zeros, disrupting time continuity, wasting model capacity, and losing time interval information. It is recommended to use interpolation to uniform grids, time window aggregation, or irregular time series models (GRU-D, BRITS, NAOMI).
Key Insight: For sequence models with position encoding, zero filling is not neutral—it actually misleads the model to learn “zero patterns” rather than temporal dependencies. Architectural assumptions must match data preprocessing; forcing irregular data into patch+zero format will destroy semantic meaning.
3. 12 error-prone skill files duplicated over 150 lines: Frame availability checks (22 times), direction normalization (8 times), list(dict.keys())[0] pattern (14 times). Solution: Extracted 3 helper methods into BaseErrorSkill: check_remaining_frames, normalize_direction, random_direction; replaced list(keys())[0] with next(iter()); added 10 unit tests for the new helper functions.
Key Insight: Code review found that “specific skill thresholds” (e.g., e08 using 30 frames while the standard is 20) were actually errors; duplicate data hid errors because the fixes were not propagated.
4. PairedDataset expects {clean_ep_idx: [{aug_ep, overlap}]} format, but episode_mapping.json uses {task_name: {bddl_idx: [{aug_demo_idx, orig_ep_idx}]} format; loss of source file/demonstration index association during HDF5→LeRobot conversion. Solution: Modified the conversion script to record conversion_index.json = [{lerobot_ep, source_file, source_demo_idx, length}]; created build_paired_mapping.py that parses clean/aug conversion indices, uses orig_ep_idx from episode_mapping as a bridge, aggregates all bddl_idx, and outputs a format compatible with PairedDataset, with up to 10 aug variants per clean episode.
Key Insight: Data sources must be consistent throughout every conversion step; three-way association requires explicit recording of bridging keys during generation; 1-to-N mapping requires aggregating various variant indices.
5. Users need to specify the boundaries for recovery demonstration data: Does training include an error injection process, or only the recovery part? Solution: Confirmed through code archaeology that recovery demonstrations only recorded remote operation behavior after injection; actions_list/states_list started after scene.load() or replay completed; verified with sample NPZ checks; the first state of the recovery demonstration equaled the post_sim_state of the scenario; training conversion filter conditions were set to success=true, and only recovery actions were used.
Key Insight: Error recovery datasets deliberately excluded actions that caused errors, preventing the model from learning failure patterns; only corrective human demonstrations were used for imitation learning.
6. Previous discussions concluded that 0% enhancement failures were due to “deformation unable to adapt,” but code checks found certain sub-types had dominant NO_WARP parts (position_error_D1: 150/192 steps in correct_position), and these parts did not undergo deformation at all. Solution: Corrected the diagnostic results: failures were not due to deformation limitations, but rather from incomplete deformation coverage and fundamental architectural mismatch between action-replay (current) and Waypoint execution methods.
Key Insight: Making conclusions without checking the execution path can lead to attribution errors in root cause analysis; repository evidence directly refutes the cited reasons: hard-coded NO_WARP labels mean the dominant part is still open-loop.
7. Current enhancers use source action replay and deformation targets, while MimicGen actually uses source target pose extraction + waypoint trajectory generation + interpolation execution—fundamental architectural mismatch. Solution: Implemented faithful transfer: added target_poses records to the collection, created object-pose tracking compatible with DatagenInfo, specified object references for each subtask in the configuration, interpolated waypoints through MimicGen’s WaypointTrajectory, and implemented target pose-to-action execution in the enhancer.
Key Insight: Surface similarity (“both using object poses”) may mask deep structural differences; MimicGen’s success depends on closed-loop correction for each subtask, not just better deformation of open-loop actions.
8. 7 HDF5 files were opened without a context manager; exceptions during processing could lead to resource leaks. Solution: Wrapped all h5py.File() calls in 4 script files with ‘with’ statements (_merge_rounds.py, _check_valid_source.py, parallel_mimicgen_generate.py, augment_success_demos.py).
Key Insight: Resource leaks in HPC batch jobs can accumulate over long runs, leading to mysterious OOM crashes; Python’s context manager can prevent such errors.
10. MimicGen generation reports errors (398 successful demonstrations), which is due to log parsing errors that count all SUCCESS strings in full_run_200attempts.log, regardless of task association. Solution: Verified the actual results of HDF5 files and generation_report.json, corrected the total number to 739/1365 (success rate 54.1%), identified 626 specific failed scenarios using regular expressions, and developed a manual collection plan.
Key Insight: Always verify summary metrics against real files; log parsing errors can lead to statistical errors when reading data ranges incorrectly in backtracking logic.
11. AI misinterpreted “uniform sampling” as balance of sample distributions (per battery/per cycle), rather than uniformity of time intervals.Solution: The user specified that equal time intervals should be maintained between consecutive measurements. The AI turned to using scipy.interpolate for time-based resampling, analyzed the chargetime distribution (35.83% at intervals of 1, with others being irregular), and generated a uniform grid dataset through linear interpolation. Key Insight: Domain-specific terms require context support: “uniform sampling” generally indicates temporal regularity in a时间序列 context, rather than balance in data distribution.
12. The existing recovery demonstration lacks the target_poses field required by the new enhancer. Solution: The backfill_recovery_target_poses.py script was created. This script replayes each saved demonstration from the recorded initial state and reconstructs the controller’s target pose through env_wrapper.action_to_target_pose(). This allows reuse of the existing demonstration corpus without the need to recall it.
Key Insight: When execution is deterministic, data format migration can be non-destructive: saving the state + recording the actions equals reconstructing the missing fields.
13. Bash commands in git* will match all Git commands, including destructive operations like git clean -f, which poses a security risk. Solution: The user chose a lax strategy for exploratory environments and accepted this risk. The AI suggested splitting the commands into specific secure subcommands, but the user rejected this suggestion.
Key Insight: Permission configuration requires a balance between convenience and security. Exploratory environments can be more lenient.
14. The first Ollama major patch failed due to Windows command length limits (CreateProcessAsUserW failed: 206). Solution: Splitting a major patch into multiple minor patches was implemented: First, the shared module was deployed, and then the callers were migrated separately.
Key Insight: There are hard limits on the length of Windows command arguments. Major patches need to be submitted in batches.
General Issues 15. The website translation workflow needs to handle 74 files distributed across directories. It is necessary to keep the original Chinese files and generate English versions. Solution: translate_site_batch.py was used, combined with state tracking, to translate only the changed files step by step. The language direction was switched (Chinese source files → .zh.md, English output → .md), making English the primary language. Key Insight: For bilingual websites where English is the primary language but the content originates from Chinese, changing the file naming convention can eliminate the need to update all internal links.
16. When everything-claude-code locally deletes 18 skill files, modifications or deletions may occur during the merge with the remote version. Solution: Using git checkout –ours was used to retain the remote version (third-party repository). The same strategy was applied to package.json/package-lock.json. The merge was successfully completed.
Key Insight: For third-party repositories, it is better to keep up-to-date upstream versions rather than making local modifications.
17. In the zh_path function name in common/bilingual.py, there is a conflict with local variables, leading to an UnboundLocalError error. Solution: The imported zh_path was renamed translation_zh_path to avoid naming conflicts.
Key Insight: In Python, assignment within functions makes variables local variables, even when importing from the module level.
18. Conda runs cause temporary file conflicts when executed in parallel. Solution: Executing in serial was chosen instead of using conda run. Using system Python instead of conda run provides greater stability for scripts that do not depend on Condria dependencies.
19. The clock times differ after each restart on both Windows and Ubuntu systems. Windows regards RTC as local time, while Ubuntu considers it to be UTC time. Solution: It was found that Ubuntu is configured to use RTC with the local TZ. It was suggested to restore Ubuntu to using UTC (timedatectl set-local-rtc 0), and set Windows to use UTC through the registry (RealTimeIsUniversal=1).
Key Insight: The correct solution is to have both operating systems use UTC as RTC, although Windows defaults to using local time. Using local RTC on Linux leads to daylight saving issues.
20. An attempt was made to create a project-wide.codex/config.toml file, but.Codex is a read-only Lustre file system mount point, which prevents mkdir/mv operations. Solution: The documentation policy was changed to AGENTS.md in the repository root directory. This method allows for global configuration inheritance and propagation through Codex’s project documentation to all subdirectories, eliminating the need to modify the file system.

Solution: The documentation policy was changed to AGENTS.md in the repository root directory. This method allows for global configuration inheritance and propagation through Codex’s project documentation to all subdirectories, eliminating the need to modify the file system.
16. When everything-claude-code locally deletes 18 skill files, modifications or deletions may occur during the merge with the remote version. Solution: Using git checkout –ours was used to retain the remote version (third-party repository). The same strategy was applied to package.json/package-lock.json. The merge was successfully completed.
Key Insight: For third-party repositories, it is better to keep up-to-date upstream versions rather than making local modifications.
17. In common/bilingual.py, there is a conflict between the zh_path function name and local variables, leading to an UnboundLocalError error. Solution: The imported zh_path was renamed translation_zh_path to avoid naming conflicts.
Key Insight: In Python, assignment within functions makes variables local variables, even when importing from the module level.
18. Conda runs cause temporary file conflicts when executed in parallel. Solution: Executing in serial was chosen instead of using conda run. Using system Python provides greater stability for scripts that do not depend on Condria dependencies.
19. The clock times differ after each restart on both Windows and Ubuntu systems. Windows regards RTC as local time, while Ubuntu considers it to be UTC time. Solution: It was found that Ubuntu is configured to use RTC with the local TZ. It was suggested to restore Ubuntu to using UTC (timedatectl set-local-rtc 0), and set Windows to use UTC through the registry (RealTimeIsUniversal=1).
Key Insight: The correct solution is to have both operating systems use UTC as RTC, although Windows defaults to using local time. Using local RTC on Linux leads to daylight saving issues.

18. Conda runs cause temporary file conflicts when executed in parallel. Solution: Executing in serial was chosen instead of using cond**✅ Fixing the mapped BOSS dataset**03:37:02.563 | claude_code The user triggered a disk quota limit (1.9T used, with data expansion reaching 112GB). The current focus is on fixing the incompatibility issue with PairedDataset mapping: the episode_mapping.json format during generation is {task_name: {bddl_idx: […]}, while PairedDataset expects the format to be {clean_ep_idx: [{aug_ep, overlap}]}. The root cause is that the source mapping information was lost during the HDF5→LeRobot conversion process. The implemented solutions include: (1) modifying libero2lerobot_parallel.py and libero2lerobot.py to output conversion_index.json, which records the mapping from source_file+demo_idx to lerobot_ep; (2) creating build_paired_mapping.py for third-party integration (clean conversion+aug conversion+episode mapping); (3) documenting the complete 5-step process from DA generation to training. The mapping logic handles a one-to-many relationship (one clean variable corresponds to up to 10 OSS variants, with some possible failures).**
Crossformer ✅ Comparison of data processing and architecture mismatches 08:24:06.299 | claude_code The user compared two implementations of Crossformer, and the differences were significant (MSE was 9, MAE was 1.3; while another implementation had an MSE of about 0.5, MAE of about 0.5). Through detailed code comparison, the following main differences were identified: (1) LIPM uses irregular sampling with padding, while the master version uses StandardScaler for uniform sampling; (2) The data sizes differ (数百样本 vs 154k样本); (3) The first version did not perform normalization; (4) The model architectures differ (IR head vs standard model). The user raised an important point: “Padding with zero filling is incompatible with Crossformer’s assumption of uniform sampling—is this approach still valuable?” The basic architecture mismatch was identified: Crossformer’s DSW_embedding assumes continuous segments, but padding disrupts the temporal continuity and misleads the model. For irregular time series data, it is recommended to use interpolation, aggregation, or specialized models such as GRU-D, BRITS. Conclusion: The second version is better because it has a better matching architecture, normalization, and larger data size.**
Crossformer Time Series Battery Prediction ✅ Unified temporal resampling of battery cycle data 08:50:32.743 | claude_code The user requested unified temporal resampling of the LiPM battery dataset (154K rows, 48 types of batteries). The AI initially mistook it for a sample distribution balance issue, but after clarification, it was identified as a temporal interpolation problem. The charging time distribution was analyzed (average of 0.36 seconds, standard deviation of 0.48 seconds, with only 35.83% of intervals being 1), and three unified sampled versions were generated using scipy linear interpolation (intervals of 1 second/5 seconds/10 seconds). The final dataset contained 55,283 rows of data, with perfect temporal uniformity (standard deviation of 0.0), which was verified across all battery models.**
GitHub Workspace Management ✅ Multi-repo Git synchronization: Check and push LifeCopilot/gadget/ECC 03:48:50.890 | claude_code The differences between the local and remote versions of 5 repos were compared. It was found that LifeCopilot had 16 commits in the feature branch, but no changes were made; everything-claude-code lagged behind with 420 commits; gadget had a few changes. The user requested a complete synchronization. Gadget (website scripts) and LifeCopilot (all changes to data/logs) were submitted and pushed. Everything-claude-code was also pulled and merged. Git checkout –ours was used to resolve conflicts, preserving the remote versions of 18 deleted skill files and package files.**
Claude Code Configuration ✅ Optimizing permissions for exploratory workspaces 04:09:32.747 | claude_code The user complained about too many confirmation prompts in Claude’s code. They requested that all operations be allowed in this exploratory workspace, except for deletion operations. The settings.local.json file was updated to include a wide range of allowed rules covering all files, tools, proxies, and common CLI commands (git/python/npm/node, etc.). It was noted that Bash(git:*) would trigger destructive operations, so splitting was recommended, but the user accepted this compromise.**
OCR ✅ Generating AGENTS.md and integrating Ollama translation 02:43:39.535 | codex An AGENTS.md file with 308 words was generated, documenting the OCR workflow (marking PDF commands, weekly note structure, Python helper scripts). It was noted that there was no.git file in the local repository, so it was not possible to infer the submission specifications. The user requested to use Ollama’s translation service, using the HY-MT1.5-1.8B model. After 5 rounds of clarification (target/format/output/language), translate_ocr_with_ollama.py was implemented to handle bilingual OCRMarkdown documents. Ollama was installed via winget, and the model was pulled. The service was started as needed, Markdown blocks were handled, Chinese detection was performed, and the structure was preserved (preface, code blocks, images, page markers). Syntax checks and API tests confirmed its correctness.**
✅ Batch processing of OCR and translation for Herbs and Roots sections 03:28:07.078 | codex The user requested OCR and translation of three PDF sections (FIVE_Oriental_Healers, SIX_Decline, SEVEN_Rediscovery). The AI implemented the entire process: creating an output directory, running marker_single OCR, and enabling the –force_ocr flag (overcoming the sandbox network limitations when downloading the Surya model), adding a retry mechanism for Ollama’s instability (4 attempts, with 5-second intervals), changing from conda run to system Python for stability, reducing the block size to 2000 characters. All three sections were successfully processed, and bilingual translations were generated (original files were 85KB, 45KB, 67KB, while the translated versions were 85KB, 45KB, 67KB respectively).**
gadget ✅ Migrating all translation paths from transformers to Ollama 02:50:03.816 | codex The user requested that gadget’s translations switch from transformers to Ollama, with support for on-demand startup (not continuous operation). Three translation entry points were analyzed: bilingual.py, translate_content.py, translate_site_batch.py. The shared common/ollama.py was implemented (with an HTTP client for automatic startup, normalize_ollama_host, resolve_translation_model, build_translation_options), and common/translation.py (translate_markdown_document with segment protection, detect_language, zh_path, clean_translated_document). All three paths were rewritten, transformers dependencies were removed (translate_batch.py was deleted), all callers were updated (summarize/research), and unit tests were added. The first major patch failed due to Windows command length limits; therefore, it was split into multiple minor patches. The zh_path naming conflict in bilingual.py was resolved. Its correctness was verified via py_compile, unittest, and –dry-run (4.zh.md files containing English content were found, which is a legacy issue). Additional dependencies for the website were reduced from torch+transformers+accelerate to just Pillow.**
