title: Bug Journal 2026-04-08

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-07 Daily Overview What was done: Conducted a comprehensive review of the website/update.sh script, fixed 4 bugs in the scripts, and helped set up a complete Hugo site deployment environment. This allowed the website update process to recover from continuous failures with exit codes 1/127/128 to successful completion in all 7 steps. How it was done: Implemented a hierarchical verification strategy: first read the code, then checked the dependencies, and finally ran the scripts. This helped identify missing components (Hugo, themes, deployment repository) and logical bugs (duplicate PID’s, unsubstituted files, directory protection issues). After manually installing missing components, multiple verifications were performed. Impact: The website CI pipeline was restored to usability. Hugo build and GitHub Pages deployment paths were established, so subsequent content releases no longer required manual intervention. Additionally, silent errors in the video compression branch were identified and fixed, preventing future content loss. Fixed 4 bugs in the website/update.sh script and set up a complete Hugo deployment environment. The website update process recovered from complete failure to successful completion in all 7 steps. A full daily/weekly MCP synchronization process was also initiated.
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Verified availability of the website/update.sh script — The system checked all dependencies and directory structures of update.sh. Critical and minor issues were identified, such as missing Hugo, themes/PaperMod/, public/ repository, and uninstalled pngquant/HandBrakeCLI. ✅ Fixed bugs in the update.sh script — Fixed 4 bugs: inconsistent step numbers (1/6→1/7), duplicate PID’s added to the pids array for video compression, HandBrakeCLI branch compression that did not replace original files, and set -e termination when static/images and static/videos directories were missing. ✅ Set up a complete Hugo deployment environment — Completed by users: installed Hugo extended v0.154.5, cloned the PaperMod theme to themes/PaperMod, cloned tzj2006.github.io to public/, and configured GitHub authentication. Implementation & Fixes 🔄 Initiated full daily/weekly/monthly MCP synchronization and merging — Checked status using the MCP gadget tool. It was found that there were sessions for 6 dates (Apr 1-5, Apr 7) but no reports. Data from Apr 7 was exported, and subsequent data will be merged in batches before generating weekly reports. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Missing Hugo, causing the script to terminate with ‘hugo: command not found’ at Step 5 (exit code 127) Solution: Users installed Hugo extended v0.154.5 using the system package manager.
Key Insight: set -e converts any command failure into a script termination. The absence of Hugo prevented the entire 7-step process from reaching the critical build stage.
2. Missing themes/PaperMod/ and public/ directories, preventing Hugo build and deployment Solution: Users cloned the PaperMod theme and tzj2006.github.io repository.
Key Insight: Hugo site deployment depends on three independent components (hugo binary, theme repository, deployment git repository). Any missing component will cause a silent or termination failure. These conditions should be clearly stated in the documentation.
General Issues 3. Missing static/videos directory, causing an error with find command and set -e termination at Step 4 Solution: Added a directory existence check before the find command ([ -d “$DIR” ]). If the directory does not exist, skip this step.
Key Insight: For optional/empty directories, protection must be added in the set -e script. Otherwise, normal “directory empty” scenarios can cause unexpected script termination.
4. Duplicate PID’s were added to the pids array for HandBrakeCLI branch compression, and original files were not replaced after compression (output was .hb.mp4) Solution: Moved pids+=($!) and echo statements into their respective if/else branches. Added && mv after the HandBrakeCLI command to replace the original file.
Key Insight: The PID tracking logic for parallel background tasks must be precisely aligned with the if/else control flow. Putting common code outside of branches can lead to duplicate execution and silent data loss bugs.
5. Failed git push due to missing GitHub authentication (could not read Username for ‘’) Solution: Users reran the script after configuring SSH keys or gh auth. The script passed successfully.
Key Insight: HTTPS remote needs preconfigured credentials in non-interactive shells. The deployment repository should use SSH remote to avoid environment differences.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Script verification strategy Role Approach Human Users proposed verification tasks without specifying methods, expecting to know if the script worked properly AI AI preferred static analysis: first read the script and CLAUDE.md to understand the architecture, then check external dependencies, directory structures, and logical correctness. Finally, the script was run for verification Difference Analysis: AI’s hierarchical verification identified all critical issues before running, avoiding multiple blind runs. However, AI cannot solve environment problems at the sudo level, ultimately requiring manual intervention by users.
Implementation Level Hugo installation method Role Approach Human Users chose the system package manager (ubuntu apt) to install, resulting in v0.154.5 AI AI suggested using snap or downloading pre-compiled binaries from GitHub releases (v0.147.4), but this was not possible due to lack of sudo permissions Difference Analysis: The user’s choice of system package method is more straightforward, and the version is also newer than what AI recommended. AI’s suggestion focuses on version control and compatibility across distributions, which may be overkill for a local development environment.
AI Limitations General Limitations Unable to execute commands requiring sudo (installing Hugo, pngquant, etc.). Only able to generate scripts that users must manually execute. This prevents the entire environment setup from being completed. Unable to configure GitHub authentication (SSH key or gh auth), which prevented the first full deployment from running. Users needed to intervene manually. Parallel tool calls failed multiple times due to race conditions during this session, forcing a serial call. This significantly reduced inspection efficiency. Learnings Key Learnings Hugo site deployment requires three independent dependencies to be present simultaneously (hugo binary, theme repository, deployment git repository). Any missing component can cause failure at different stages. These conditions should be clearly listed in the project documentation. Practical learnings: In bash set -e scripts, add a directory existence check for optional directories. Otherwise, normal “directory not created” scenarios can cause unexpected script termination. The code for parallel background tasks (& + pids tracking) must be precisely aligned with the if/else control flow. Putting common code outside of branches can lead to duplicate PID tracking and silent file replacement errors. Conversation Summaries Gadget Website ✅ Verified and fixed critical environment issues and logical bugs in the website/update.sh script, fully completing the Hugo deployment process 04:12:15.435 | claude_code Users requested verification of whether the website update script worked properly. AI identified three critical issues through hierarchical analysis: missing Hugo, missing themes/PaperMod/, and missing public/ repository. It also identified 4 logical bugs: inconsistent step numbers, duplicate video PID tracking, HandBrakeCLI branch compression that did not replace original files, and set -e termination when optional directories were missing. After users installed the environment dependencies and configured GitHub authentication, all 7 steps passed successfully. Hugo build and GitHub Pages deployment were also verified.
Gadget Summary 🔄 Initiated full daily/weekly/monthly MCP synchronization and merging process using the MCP gadget tool 04:25:03.485 | claude_code Users requested parallel execution of daily/weekly/monthly synchronization and merging tasks. AI checked the status using the MCP tool. It was found that there were sessions for 6 dates (Apr 1-5, Apr 7) but no reports. Considering the dependency order from day to week to month, AI decided to export data for Apr 7 first and then merge it in batches. The session ended while the export was ongoing, so the subsequent process was not completed.
Token Usage Claude Code Overview Metric Value Total Tokens 33,667,509 Input Tokens 5,417 Output Tokens 139,884 Cache Creation 3,640,724 Cache Read 29,881,484 Cache Hit Rate 89.1% Total Cost (USD) $31.4112 Model Breakdown Model Input Output Cache Creation Cache Read Cost Share claude-opus-4-6 3,620 38,870 951,290 24,719,883 $19.2954 61.4% claude-sonnet-4-6 1,531 96,562 2,495,858 2,613,324 $11.5965 36.9% claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 266 4,452 193,576 2,548,277 $0.5193 1.7% Codex Overview Metric Value Total Tokens 8,314,268 Input Tokens 8,211,120 Output Tokens 103,148 Reasoning Tokens 65,215 Cache Read 7,212,160 Total Cost (USD) $5.8477 Model Breakdown Model Input Output Reasoning Cache Read Cost Share gpt-5.4 8,211,120 103,148 65,215 7,212,160 $5.8477 100.0%
summary: Fixed 4 bugs in the website/update.sh script and set up a complete Hugo deployment environment. The website update process recovered from complete failure to successful completion in all 7 steps. A full daily/weekly MCP synchronization process was also initiated.
title: Bug Journal 2026-04-07

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-06 Key Points of the Day Completed: Edited three annotations in the MIHD paper draft. Planned and executed other tasks: revised the explanation of the motivation in the abstract, reorganized section P1 of the introduction, expanded the R5 cross-slice analysis to 6 paragraphs, and wrote case studies for the R6 P3 immune microenvironment section. Implementation Method: First, conducted a thorough analysis of the codebase and existing experimental results using multiple sub-proxies. Then, developed a detailed execution plan. While editing the text, three new RM-IDEAL cross-slice benchmark experiments were run on the CPU backend, using actual data results to refine the original narrative. Significance: The paper draft shifted from placeholder text to a data-driven narrative. This led to the discovery of a new core argument: STAIG is good at clustering, but may overmerge boundaries; attention fusion is more suitable for retrieving immune microenvironments. Additionally, the issue of invisible query points in HD Niche query visualization was resolved. Completed the planning and execution of three annotations in the MIHD paper draft, including revising section P1 of the introduction, expanding the abstract and R5/R6 to 6 paragraphs, and adding a QueST+TLS reference. A detailed execution plan was developed based on the codebase and experimental results.
✅ Three Annotations in the Paper Draft — Analyzed three handwritten annotations in draft_en.md: the explanation of the motivation in the abstract, section P1 starting from the main figure, and expanding R5 to 6 paragraphs with a QueST reference added. A detailed execution plan was developed based on the codebase and experimental results. ✅ R5 Expanded to 6 Paragraphs + QueST Reference — Added a QueST reference and an explanation of the value of cross-slice retrieval in section P1 of R5. Updated the method description in P2. In R6 P3, replaced the placeholders with actual HD immune analysis data to illustrate the balance between clustering and retrieval. Both English and Chinese versions were updated. ✅ Rewritten Section P1 of the Introduction — reorganized the original background paragraphs into a 6-sentence guide structure that corresponds to the titles of the result sections. Key numbers like ARI 0.26→0.55 were introduced at the beginning of each paragraph, with updates in both languages. ✅ Supplementary Explanation of Motivation in the Abstract — Added an explanation of the frozen FM embedding mechanism at the end of the abstract, which naturally supports cross-slice retrieval. The discovery of the balance between clustering and retrieval was updated from “immune boundary discovery” to a more accurate “balance”. Updates were made in both languages. ✅ HD Immune Microenvironment Niche Query Analysis — Created the analyze_hd_immune_niche.py file, calculating T cell/B cell scores, identifying 23 epithelial-connective tissue boundary T cell enrichment points, and using PCA/attention mechanisms to compare retrieval results. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Fixed Issues with HD Niche Query Visualization — Resolved the issue of invisible query points in visualize_hd_niche_query.py: increased the star size from 20 to 26, added a white outer ring marker, marked all 4 panels, and added arrows and text labels for the H&E panel. Three images with visible query points were generated again. ✅ W14 Weekly Report Writing — Created the WEEKLY_REPORT_2026_W14.md file, covering 8 sections including Leiden experiments, HD experiments, paper writing, code updates, etc., along with complete numerical data tables. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. In HD immune analysis, T cell markers are very sparse (1278/17502 points, average 0.014), while TLS markers are even rarer (242 points). The argument that “multimodal can better capture immune infiltration” does not hold. Solution: Changed the narrative in R6 P3 from “multimodal recognition of immune infiltration boundaries” to “balance between clustering and retrieval”. Clustering results showed STAIG had a concentration of 96% in the epithelial domain, while PCA gene expression showed T cell enrichment was 3.8 times that of STAIG. Attention fusion performed more evenly in the boundary region (60/40 epithelial/conductive tissue). Key Discovery: The best strategies for clustering (spatial graph contrast learning) and retrieval (attention fusion) are different. This is a more significant discovery than expected.
2. benchmark_rm_ideal.py writes results to the same summary.csv file by default, causing one experiment’s results to overwrite another. Solution: Copied backup files immediately after each experiment was completed (e.g., summary_151673_151674.csv). Used heredoc to manually reconstruct experiments 151673↔151669 because they were overwritten by results from 151674. Key Discovery: Batch experiments need to have a directory for the output in the script, or the results should be aggregated after the pipeline is complete.
3. The conda activate command produces no output in the background, and the output files of all three experiment processes are always empty. Solution: Use instead of . This ensures that correct background outputs are written. Key Discovery: The Claude code’s background bash command environment does not inherit the shell’s activation state. Using conda run can ensure environmental isolation.
4. The same experiment was run twice through two different processes (conda activate version + conda run version). Running 6 benchmark tests simultaneously wasted resources. Solution: Used ps aux to find the PID of the conda run process and terminated the duplicate 3 processes. Retained the original conda activate version. Key Discovery: When background processes have empty output, it is necessary to confirm whether the process is actually running (using ps aux), rather than directly resubmitting the task.
5. In HD Niche query visualization, query points (red stars) are difficult to visualize and cannot be used for paper presentation. Solution: Modified the script: increased the star size from markersize=20 to 26, added a white background marker (size=32), marked all 4 panels, and added arrows and text labels for the first panel. Key Discovery: In paper illustrations, query points require multiple visual cues (color, size, outer ring markers, labels). A single red star is completely unrecognizable against a heatmap background.
Human Thinking vs Artificial Intelligence Thinking Strategic Level TLS/T Cell Parameter Correction | Role | Method | |——|—-| | Human | Wrote “TLS T cells” in human-readable form. Expected to find areas with TLS/T cell enrichment through multimodal analysis in HD colon cancer data.| | AI | Conducted actual analysis and found that T cell markers were very sparse (only 7.3% of markers). Additionally, PCA gene expression detected T cell enrichment better than STAIG’s multimodal detection methods. Therefore, a more precise “balance between clustering and retrieval” theory was proposed.**
Difference Analysis: Human intuition is based on the prior knowledge that TLS is common in colon cancer immune structures. However, AI found that the裁剪 area lacked sufficient immune infiltration to support the original argument. AI discovered a more theoretically valuable balance scheme.
Identification of Annotation Content and Priority Determination Role Method Human Clearly knew the content of the three annotations (motivation, introduction structure, R5 expansion), and knew that QueST needed to be referenced, along with examples of TLS/T cell biology. AI First read the file and identified the differences. Then explained the meaning of each annotation. Finally, used multiple sub-proxies to explore the codebase and understand experimental results and feasibility.** Difference Analysis: Humans provided direction and scientific requirements, while AI was responsible for verifying feasibility and implementing details. AI explored the codebase more comprehensively than humans but relied on code exploration to determine which experiments had results/needed new experiments.
Implementation Level Visualization Quality Judgment Role Method Human Directly observed the images and gave explicit user feedback, pointing out issues with unclear query points. AI Confirmed the issues after reading the image files but required manual intervention to discover them. AI identified and fixed specific issues at the code level (too small markers, insufficient contrast). Difference Analysis: Human visual perception and paper usability judgment are better than AI’s code review. AI is more efficient in implementing fixes.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence General Limitations Generated visualization results without actively verifying quality (e.g., visibility of query points). User feedback was needed to identify issues. There was no active quality assurance for image usability. Failed to detect that benchmark_rm_ideal.py would overwrite summary.csv, causing results from 151673↔151669 to be overwritten. Data had to be manually reconstructed based on memory. Initially using as the background command prefix caused all three experiment processes to output nothing. It took time to solve this problem; conda run should be used first. Due to uncertainty about the status of the first batch of processes, repeating submission of the same three experiments led to 6 parallel processes. Duplicates had to be terminated manually. Takeaway of Today Key Takeaways Discovered the difference between optimal clustering and retrieval strategies: STAIG’s spatial graph contrast learning is suitable for global domain clustering (average ARI is 0.546), but its strong spatial homogenization leads to excessive focus on the same domain at boundaries (96% epithelial tissue). Attention fusion (60/40 distribution) performs more evenly in boundary microenvironments (60/40 epithelial/conductive tissue). Results from three new RM-IDEAL experiments show that Spearman ρ between donor slices is as high as 0.86, while it reaches 100% between tags @50. However, there is a negative correlation between layers of different donors (ρ=-0.73), indicating systematic differences in the third layer morphology of different donors. In HD colon cancer data, T cell markers (CD3D/CD3E) were expressed at an extremely low rate of 7.3% on 8μm slices. PCA gene expression detected T cell enrichment better than visual and gene fusion methods (2.2 times that of STAIG), indicating that T cell signals are mainly driven by gene expression rather than tissue morphology. Introduction strategy: Directly presenting key numerical results (ARI from 0.26 to 0.55) is more persuasive than general background settings. Using the corresponding result section titles for each paragraph can improve the consistency of paper structure. Dialogue Summary ✅ Paper Editing and Execution: Revised section P1 of the introduction, updated the abstract, and expanded R5/R6 content (including English and Chinese).** 21:30:00.000 | claude_code AI simultaneously translated the 6-sentence structure of section P1 into English and Chinese. Added an explanation of motivation at the end of the abstract. In R5 P1/P2, added a reference to QueST and updated placeholder content. At the same time, three new cross-slice RM-IDEAL experiments were running in the background, along with HD immune microenvironment analysis. The unexpected results caused by STAIG’s overmerging boundaries led AI to modify the argument in R6 P3, changing “immune infiltration recognition” to “balance between clustering and retrieval”. This was the most significant narrative adjustment during this editing process.
✅ Three Cross-Slice RM-IDEAL Experiments Completed: Compared the consistency between donor-specific experiments.** 22:00:00.000 | claude_code All three experiments took about 2 hours (CPU computation time). Donor-specific groups (151673↔151674, 151508↔151509) showed high Spearman ρ (up to 0.86) and same_label@50 values (up to 100%). Different donor groups (151673↔151669) showed a strong negative correlation in the third layer (ρ=-0.73). This indicates that the tissue microstructure between donor slices is highly consistent, while there are systematic differences between donors, providing key quantitative evidence for R5.
✅ Annotation Analysis of Paper Draft: Identified content and developed execution plans for the three annotations.** 21:02:12.402 | claude_code Users provided two paper drafts. AI identified three handwritten annotations through file comparison. Using multiple sub-proxies, AI thoroughly explored the codebase, existing experimental results, and RM-IDEAL implementations. It completed a comprehensive evaluation of annotation feasibility and generated detailed execution plans involving computational experiments and text editing. The plan was approved by users. The strategy was to run new experiments in parallel while editing the text, without relying on experimental results, thereby improving parallelism. ✅ Fixed HD niche query visualization issues: Resolved the problem of invisible query points. 23:00:00.000 | claude_code Users reported that query points in HD niche images were invisible. AI read the images and corrected the code in visualize_hd_niche_query.py: increased the star size from 20 to 26, added a white background marker (size=32) to improve contrast, marked all 4 panels, and added arrows and text labels for the H&E panel. After generating three images again, the query points became visible.
✅ Generated W14 Weekly Report: Summarized this week’s work. 22:28:27.357 | claude_code Users requested a summary of this week’s work. AI used git logs, file modification times, and experiment logs to reconstruct the content of WEEKLY_REPORT_2026_W14.md. The report included 11 sections, covering complete data tables for Leiden experiments, rankings of 12 methods in HD experiments, lists of visualization scripts, and plans for next week’s work.

title: Bug Journal 2026-04-06

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W14 (2026-03-30 ~ 2026-04-05) This week, three main tasks were carried out simultaneously: preparation for the MIHD multi-modal spatial transcriptomics paper (5 days, DCC); completion of the TEDDY full-embedding benchmark, Leiden comparison experiments, HD fusion evaluation, and writing of a bilingual paper framework. The research focus was established as “zero-shot FM embedding fusion” with differentiated studies. Additionally, systematic fixes were made to TokenMonitor Linux FloatBall over 5 days. After multiple iterations, the issue evolved from symptom-based fixes to a fixed-window + GDK Input Shape architecture重构. All 21 code review issues in PR #4 were also resolved. The Life-Copilot Discord Bot was completely reimplemented over 2 days, resulting in a net reduction of 2823 lines of code. A multi-CLI agent architecture was introduced, and new features such as screen time tracking, Lark input channels, and MCP tool integration were implemented. Furthermore, research on 63 papers from the NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track and setup of a new Linux workstation environment were completed.
Weekly Overview Indicator Value Date Range 2026-03-30 ~ 2026-04-05 Active Days 7 / 7 Total Conversations 29 Projects Involved 12 Tasks Completed 44 Ongoing Tasks 9 Total Tokens 328,660,991 Total Cost $218.53 Claude Code Tokens 273,046,863 Claude Code Costs $189.80 Codex Tokens 55,614,128 Codex Costs $28.73 Average Daily Cost $31.22 Project Progress MIHD Multi-modal Spatial Transcriptomics Paper (5 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Completed extraction of TEDDY-G 400M full-embedding data for 11 slices and multi-strategy evaluations. It was confirmed that the STAIG framework is highly robust for gene encoders (Teddy ARI=0.495 vs PCA ARI=0.497). Integrated Leiden clustering (binary search matching n_clusters) and STHD pathology GT, completed full-Leiden comparison on DLPFC (9/9 groups). The key conclusion was that high-quality embeddings are superior to clustering methods (STAIG+mclust=0.546 vs STAIG+Leiden=0.513). Completed evaluation of 13 HD fusion strategies. Key negative findings included: simple fusion (concat ARI=0.191) being inferior to single-modal PCA (ARI=0.351), and adaln crashing on large-scale HD data (ARI=0.003). Established the core narrative framework for the paper (Setting=No system study on zero-shot FM embedding fusion/Finding=Fusion Strategies > Simple Fusion is a Trap, Spatial Graph Learning is Key/Application=Cross-slice Retrieval). A complete bilingual paper framework was created by referring to Yuan et al. 2024 Nature Methods. Created 4 GPU Slurm job scripts and 3 paper analysis scripts (cross-slice query aggregation, figure visualization, HD Marker Gene visualization). The docs/ folder was reorganized, and 12 expired scripts were archived. Blocked Issues:
⚠️ Basic contrastive HD fusion experiment is still running (48h limit). The HD learning-based fusion result set is not complete. ⚠️ The root cause of the 151676 slice STAIG model collapse has not been thoroughly analyzed. It is currently handled by skipping it. TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux Adaptation (5 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Fixed Wayland positioning issues in the Linux main window (forcing GDK_BACKEND=x11 to fallback to XWayland). Completed all 21 code review fixes in PR #4: assert!() → Result propagation, SSH lock across asynchronous boundaries, path traversal whitelist protection, I/O error differentiation, Svelte 5 double fetch, FloatBall listener race conditions. There were zero Rust warnings and 199 front-end tests passed. Identified the root cause of GTK size negotiation issues (WebKitGTK natural size causing set_size() to be ineffective). This was resolved by using with_webview() to call widget.set_size_request(). Also, deferred repositioning, multi-monitor misjudgment, pointerdown race conditions, and scroll bars were fixed. Completed the fixed-window + GDK Input Shape architecture重构: The window permanently maintained an expanded size. Cairo::Region was used to control the click-through area. Approximately 150 lines of probe/corrective patch code were removed. 284 Rust+205 front-end tests passed. Organized git history (git reset –soft compressed 24 commits into 2 semantic commits) and pushed it to GitHub. A new Linux workstation version v0.6.1 was built (deb/rpm/AppImage). Blocked Issues:
⚠️ The fixed-window + GDK Input Shape solution needs to be verified on actual devices. ⚠️ There are architectural constraints regarding Linux WM clamping. The correct solution is to fix it with a fixed large size and internal CSS. This needs to be fully verified. Life-Copilot Discord Bot (2 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Completely reimplemented 3170 lines of complex intent routing into a four-layer architecture consisting of CLIAdapter+SessionManager+MCP Server (19 tools) + Skills (4). A net reduction of 2823 lines was achieved, with 419 tests passed. Implemented Screen Time Tracker (GNOME Introspect D-Bus → xprop → cmdline three-level downgrade, AI application classifier, independent asyncio.Task sampling loop, 26/26 tests passed). Integrated Lark Bot for quick idea capture (daemon thread asyncio bridge solved lark-oapi blocking issues, 10 categories of automatic classification). Added Codex/Anthropic backend. The fallback chain was extended to 11 levels. Fixed two overlapping problems in MCP tool calls: Claude -p requires –mcp-config and –permission-mode bypassPermissions. Added structlog structured logging + complete dialogue JSONL audit. Blocked Issues:
⚠️ Accessing OpenReview reviewers’ comments is blocked due to a general 403 error in the API. The OPENREVIEW_USERNAME/OPENREVIEW_PASSWORD environment variables are required. New Linux Workstation Environment Setup (2 active days) — ✅ Completed Completed:
Completed the full system initialization (NVIDIA driver confirmation, Miniconda, SSH key migration of 7 files, TinyProxy proxy, GitHub SSH verification). Verified that RTX 5090 has BF16=220 TFLOPS/s. Configured gadget rclone for two-way synchronization (gdrive remote, covering four types of data: summarize/research/website/test). Installed Claude Code skills and 12 plugins. Research on NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track (1 active day) — ✅ Completed Completed:
Analyzed 63 papers under the gadget research scout framework. Deeply analyzed 5 top papers, generating a three-dimensional report on writing structure/ publication elements/knowledge extraction, and a comprehensive writing guide. Identified 3 actionable research directions: multi-dimensional evaluation benchmark for VLM credibility, TTA and fine-tuning synergistic effect benchmarks, online continuous learning timing benchmarks. Key Tasks ✅ TokenMonitor PR #4 All 21 Code Review Fixes (April 02) — System fixes were carried out according to CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM priorities: changing assert!() to Result propagation, SSH lock across asynchronous boundaries (Clone released in advance), path traversal whitelist protection, I/O error differentiation, Svelte 5 double fetch, FloatBall listener race conditions, NaiveDate overflow. There were zero Rust compilation warnings and 199 front-end tests passed. ✅ Life-Copilot Discord Bot Complete Reimplementation (April 04) — Replaced the complex intent routing with a four-layer architecture consisting of CLIAdapter+SessionManager+MCP Server + Skills. Supports Claude/Codex/Gemini CLI fallback chains. The Skills JSON protocol triggers Discord UI decoupling. A net reduction of 2823 lines was achieved, with 419 tests passed. ✅ TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux GTK Size Negotiation Fix (April 04) — Identified the root cause of WebKitGTK natural size causing set_size() to be ineffective. Resolved this issue by using with_webview() to call widget.set_size_request(). Also, deferred repositioning, multi-monitor expansion direction misjudgment, pointerdown race conditions, GTK ScrolledWindow scroll bars, and two-stage collapse were fixed. All tests passed. 🔄 TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux Fixed-Window + GDK Input Shape Architecture Reimplementation (April 05) — Reorganized the float ball from a dynamic resize architecture to a fixed expanded-size window + GDK input_shape_combine_region control for click-through. Eliminated the systematic WM +305px offset. Approximately 150 lines of probe/corrective patch code were removed. 284 Rust+205 front-end tests passed. Waiting for final runtime verification. ✅ MIHD Paper Narrative Line Organization and Bilingual Framework Creation (April 03) — Established the research focus as “zero-shot FM embedding fusion” with a three-layer narrative structure: Simple Fusion is a Trap → Spatial Graph Learning is the Bridge → Correct Fusion Transcends Single Modal. A complete bilingual paper framework was created by referring to Yuan et al. 2024 Nature Methods (including Abstract/Introduction/Results R1-R6/Discussion/Methods). ✅ Leiden vs KMeans Full-Embedding Comparison Experiments (April 01) — Completed comparison of Leiden clustering on 9/9 combinations. The key conclusion was that STAIG+mclust(0.546) > STAIG+Leiden(0.513). Leiden significantly improved weak embeddings (+29%), but had little additional benefit for strong embeddings (+6%). This established the core argument that high-quality embeddings are superior to clustering methods. ✅ MIHD Fig 1-6 Framework Planning (March 31) — Referred to MISO, STAIG, QueST, and Yuan’s papers to plan Fig1=framework, Fig2=DLPFC benchmark, Fig3=Ablation, Fig4=HD application, Fig5=Cross-slice Query, and Fig6=Query bio-discovery. ECL YAML and plans.md were written. ✅ Life-Copilot Screen Time Tracker Full Implementation (April 04) — Planned a 9-stage ccplan + Critic adversarial review (pre-detected Wayland compatibility risks). Implemented GNOME Introspect D-Bus → xprop → cmdline three-level downgrade window tracking. AI application classifier (100+ pre-settings + persistent cache). Independent asyncio.Task sampling loop. Passed 26/26 tests. ✅ Life-Copilot Discord Bot MCP Tool Call Fixes (April 05) — Fixed two overlapping issues: Claude -p requires explicit –mcp-config to load project-level MCP configuration; non-interactive mode requires –permission-mode bypassPermissions. The –allowedTools wildcard was unreliable due to competition with MCP server initialization. By running the Claude -p command directly, the root cause of permission_denials field issues was identified. ✅ NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track Systematic Paper Research (April 04) — Searched for 63 papers and deeply analyzed 5 top papers (TTA-VLM, DATE-LM, CAB, KG-RAG Pitfalls, DCAD-2000). Generated a three-dimensional analysis report on writing structure/ publication elements/knowledge extraction, and a comprehensive writing guide. Identified 3 actionable research directions. ✅ Teddy + STAIG Fusion Evaluation (Full 11 Slices) (March 30) — Completed evaluation of 10/11 slices (skipped model collapse on 151676 models). Avg ARI=0.495 compared to PCA+STAIG’s 0.497. On NMI, Teddy won 6 out of 10 slices. This revealed the high robustness of the STAIG framework for gene encoders, providing experimental evidence for future encoder replacement strategies. ✅ Life-Copilot Lark Bot + Codex/Anthropic Backend + Full-Linkage Token Verification (April 04) — Implemented ThoughtCaptureService shared service layer + AutoCategorizer (10 categories: Chinese & English) + daemon thread asyncio bridge. Added CodexProvider and AnthropicProvider. The fallback chain was extended to 11 levels. Created key_validator.py to verify all services with real APIs. Fixed Google Calendar scope mismatch issues. ✅ FEAT-003: STHD Ground Truth Integration (March 31) — Added a load_sthd_annotations() function to automatically load 545913 annotations. Combined CE and Tumor (Neoplasm+Non-neoplastic Epithelium→Epithelium). 17502/17502 spots had 100% match rate. 3 valid clusters were identified, providing a GT basis for HD ARI/NMI evaluation. ✅ TokenMonitor Linux Main Window Wayland Positioning Fix (April 02) — Identified the Wayland protocol-level prohibition of client-side absolute position settings. After detecting WAYLAND_DISPLAY in the run() entry, forced GDK_BACKEND=x11 to make the application run via XWayland. 283 Rust tests passed. ✅ MIHD GPU Slurm Scripts and Paper Analysis Scripts Batch Creation (April 02) — Created 4 independent Slurm GPU job scripts (STAIG/contrastive/LLaVA/AdaLN) and 3 Python analysis scripts (cross-slice query aggregation, paper figure visualization, HD Marker Gene visualization). All scripts passed syntax verification. ✅ Life-Copilot Structured Logging and Operation Audit System (April 05) — Replaced stdlib logging with structlog. Implemented dev-color output + prod JSON format + 10MB rotation. Created audit_log.py to write complete dialogue JSONL audits in Discord on_message, FastAPI middleware, and CLISelector.send. Fixed ExtraAdder omission issues. 420 tests passed. ✅ TEDDY-G 400M Full-Embedding Extraction (11 Slices) (March 30) — Extracted 1024d gene embeddings for DLPFC’s 11 slices in the conda teddy environment and cached them in embeddings_cache/gene/teddy/. Total size was 185MB. Also extracted UNI2 staig_strict visual features (256×256 patch, no ImageNet normalization, frequency-domain filtering). 🔄 HD Learning-Based Fusion GPU Experiments (April 01) — staig(ARI=0.258)/llava(ARI=0.201)/adaln(ARI=0.003) were completed. The basic_contrastive experiment is still running (48h limit). Key negative findings included: adaln crashing on large-scale HD data (17502 spots), revealing that data size is a key variable in fusion methods. ✅ docs/ Folder Reorganization and Code Gap Analysis (April 03) — Archived 5 expired documents. Created a three-level structure in docs/paper/figures/. Systemically analyzed the capabilities required by draft_en.md vs existing code. Archived 12 expired scripts. Planned Leiden result recovery scripts, figure generation scripts, spatial continuity metrics, and support for 4 code tasks with multiple seeds. Issues and Solutions 1. GTK bottom-up size negotiation caused FloatBall windows to always be 400x400. Tauri’s set_size() was completely ineffective [TokenMonitor] (April 04)### Next Week’s PlansNext week’s key focus areas are as follows: (1) MIHD paper – Collect the complete HD evaluation table for basic_contrastive HD experiments and advance the code gap tasks (Leiden result restoration script, figure generation script, spatial continuity metrics, multi-seed support). Start generating papers and writing drafts to reach a submission-ready level.
(2) TokenMonitor – Finalize the fixed-window + GDK Input Shape solution on real Linux devices. Confirm that the WM clamp issue has been fully resolved. If verification is successful, submit a PR for merging. Close this long-term task related to Linux FloatBall fixes after nearly two weeks.
(3) Life-Copilot – Obtain OpenReview account credentials to unlock access to NeurIPS review comments. Verify the stability of Screen Time Tracker in production environments. Continuously monitor the performance of the 11-level fallback chain in real-world usage.
(4) Research exploration – Translate the research conclusions from NeurIPS D&B Track into specific experimental designs. Evaluate the feasibility of a multi-dimensional benchmark for assessing VLM credibility. Also follow up on the progress of the AionUI remote SSH access feature request.

title: Weekly Summary 2026-W14

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-05 Key Points of the Day Completed Work: Reorganized Linux FloatBall from a dynamic sizing architecture to a fixed-size window layout. Implemented GDK Input Shape solution for Life-Copilot Discord bot. Additionally, added a structured logging system called structlog to address issues with missing MCP tool configurations and permission denied errors in Claude CLI -p mode. Implementation Method: For FloatBall, ccplan generated six alternative solutions, which were then implemented using Cairo::Region and GTK geometry_hints for fixed window layouts. During dragging operations, the input shape temporarily expands to cover the entire window. For Life-Copilot, the system analyzed nested process call chains to resolve permission denied errors in non-interactive mode. Achievements: Eliminated systematic position offset caused by Linux WM +305px/+113px layout issues and removed approximately 150 lines of debugging/compensation code. The complete conversation history of the Discord bot was stored locally, and the Google Calendar MCP tool now operates perfectly in production environments. By restructuring Linux FloatBall with fixed windows and GDK Input Shape, we effectively resolved platform-level geometric problems. Additionally, we addressed two main issues: lack of structured logging for Life-Copilot Discord bot and permission denied errors when using MCP tools in non-interactive mode.
Tasks for Today Architecture and Strategy 🔄 TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux Architecture Reorganization (Fixed Window + GDK Input Shape) — After generating six alternative solutions using ccplan, a fixed window layout was implemented. The window size always remains 152×56. GDK input_shape_combineRegion is used to control the click area: only the ball area is displayed when folded, while the entire window is shown when unfolded or during dragging. Three issues were resolved: ball deformation, inability to expand, and dragging failures. The snap function was removed to ensure stable movement. Currently, there are 284 lines of Rust code and 205 frontend tests passed, with final verification in progress. ✅ Complete Fixes to Life-Copilot Discord Bot’s MCP Tool Calls — Two-stage fixes were implemented: In the first stage, it was discovered that Claude -p would not automatically load project-level.claude/mcp_servers.json files. The –mcp-config parameter was added to the claude_adapter.py command to load 19 MCP tools. In the second stage, running Claude -p directly revealed the root cause of permission denied errors in non-interactive mode. Due to timing issues, the –allowedTools wildcard was unreliable, so bypassPermissions was used to solve this problem. Verification showed that calendar queries returned correct results. ✅ Life-Copilot Structured Logging System (structlog + File Rotation + Operation Audit) — Replaced standard library logging with structlog, achieving development-level color output, production-level JSON format, and file rotation of 10MB. audit_log.py was added to three locations: Discord’s on_message, FastAPI middleware, and CLISelector.send, capturing complete conversation records (user input + bot response). The issue of extra fields being lost due to ExtraAdder processor problems was resolved. Four print statements were removed. Currently, there are 420 tests passed. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Backout of FloatBall Session Changes — More issues arose due to CSS min() modifications and folding processes in some AI sessions. Users requested a backout. The AI mistakenly used git stash to restore all uncommitted changes. After using git stash pop, the specific changes made during this session were manually corrected. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Linux WM fails to handle transparent borderless window sizing atomically, leading to systematic position offset +305px/+113px, flickering detection/compensation mechanisms, and inconsistent sizes between WebView and native windows. Solution: Set the window size to expanded state (152×56), without any adjustments. Use GDK input_shape_combineRegion to control the click area: only the ball area is displayed when folded (56×56), while the entire window is shown when unfolded or during dragging. Key Insight: This is a platform architecture issue, not a computational error. Linux WM interprets the same rect request differently. Solutions need to be found at the sizing level; local fixes can only temporarily solve the problem.
2. Life-Copilot Discord bot continues to report “OAuth authentication required” for calendar queries. There are two overlapping issues at play: (1) Claude -p does not load project-level MCP configurations; (2) permission denied errors occur when using MCP tools in non-interactive mode. Solution: Add –mcp-config and –permission-mode bypassPermissions parameters to the claude_adapter.py command. Running Claude -p directly revealed the root cause of permission denied errors. Since there is a conflict with MCP server initialization, the –allowedTools wildcard is unreliable in non-interactive mode. Using bypassPermissions is the correct method for backend service calls.
3. After implementing the fixed window layout, three platform-specific issues arose: ball display deformation (window builder still uses 56×56 size), inability to expand, and dragging failures (input shape gets blocked after leaving the ball area). Solution: (1) The window builder should use the expanded size and lock min=max based on GTK geometry_hints. (2) CSS justify-content should always take effect based on data-direction. (3) A new set_float_ball_dragging command was added to temporarily expand the input shape during dragging, with it being restored after the process is complete.
Key Insight: WebKitGTK has default minimum size limits. The X11 layer intercepts input shape events before setPointerCapture, so synchronizing the area expansion in Rust is necessary.
General Issues 4. When using structlog to wrap standard library logging, extra {} fields disappear silently from the JSON output. Solution: Add the ExtraAdder() processor to the shared_processors entry, ensuring that additional fields are merged into the structured output.
Key Insight: When using structlog to wrap standard library logging, additional parameters do not get passed automatically. It is necessary to explicitly add these parameters so that the field disappears silently without reporting any errors.
5. When users request a backout of “changes made during this session,” the AI mistakenly uses git stash, which may lead to loss of historical work. Solution: After using git stash pop, manually identify and correct the specific changes made during this session.
Key Insight: Git stash affects all uncommitted changes, so it is not suitable for situations where only “changes made during this session” need to be corrected. It is better to use git diff to clearly define the scope of changes before making individual corrections.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Diagnosing the root cause of Linux FloatBall and selecting solutions | Role | Thought Process | |——|——-| | Human | Completed a comprehensive diagnosis before starting the session: 4 layers of geometric inconsistency, 11 attempts and failures, log evidence (systematic pattern: +305px = expanded width, +113px = contracted width) | | AI | After confirming the diagnosis through the Explore agent, it was organized into six alternative solutions. These solutions were then reviewed and feasibility verified. The fixed window solution was recommended and fully implemented. |
Difference Analysis: Humans far surpass AI in terms of diagnostic depth and evidence quality. The value of AI lies in its ability to build and execute solutions, not just identify problems.
MCP Problem Diagnosis Strategy Role Thought Process Human Continuously forced the AI to conduct deeper investigations instead of accepting superficial explanations. AI Investigated from tokens to MCP server startup to CLI call chains, ultimately using direct tests with Claude -p commands to determine the root cause of permission denied errors. Difference Analysis: Humans ensure that problems are completely resolved, not just hurriedly fixed. AI provides a systematic multi-level investigation method.
Discovery of Unrecorded AI Bot Responses Role Thought Process Human Intuitively asked “How does the Discord bot respond to my messages?” and found that logging only recorded user input while ignoring AI responses. AI Only focused on audit points related to user input during implementation, without considering the integrity of the conversation. Difference Analysis: Humans identified key omissions from the user’s perspective (conversation integrity), while AI only considered system-level audit points.
Requirements Definition for Logging System Role Thought Process Human Simply stated “Write code to be logged,” and after being asked, clearly selected file persistence + structured logging + comprehensive operation audit. AI Scanned existing code and found that 73/114 files already had logging capabilities. Actively identified vague requirements and proposed three directions for the user to choose from. Difference Analysis: Humans posed vague requirements, while AI translated them into specific options through code analysis. The final direction was chosen by humans and executed by AI.
Implementation Level Judgment of Git Backout Scope Role Thought Process Human Intended to back out only the changes made during this AI session, preserving previous work. AI Used git stash operations, which affected a scope beyond what users expected (backed out all uncommitted changes). Difference Analysis: AI failed to accurately understand the difference between “changes made during this session” and “all uncommitted changes,” choosing an overly broad scope of backout.
Priority Selection for FloatBall Functions (Removing Snap Function) Role Thought Process Human After testing and finding that movement was still unstable, explicitly requested the removal of the Snap function, prioritizing core movement issues. AI During the design phase, Snap was listed as a requirement to be retained and implemented. It did not actively propose a phased priority. Difference Analysis: AI tends to implement all functions at once, while users made more pragmatic priorities during testing.
AI Limitations Major Limitations Unit tests passed, but it’s impossible to predict behavior on specific platforms: All 284 Rust tests and 205 frontend tests passed FloatBall functionality, but issues such as ball deformation, inability to expand, and dragging failures were not caught. Time issues in Claude CLI -p mode—allowing tools to be used in non-interactive mode also failed to pass the test. When first implementing, details specific to platforms were often overlooked. User testing or log feedback was needed to identify problems: Window builders had to use expanded sizes, WebKitGTK required GTK geometry_hints constraints, and ExtraAdder should explicitly add when using structlog. General Limitations Misjudgment of Git backout scope: When users requested a backout of “changes made during this session,” git stash operations were used, which could lead to loss of historical work. Achievements Today Core Achievements Linux X11 WM does not guarantee atomicity for transparent borderless windows when sizing. Different WMs interpret the same coordinates differently, leading to systematic offset issues. The only reliable solution is to eliminate sizing functionality (fixed window + GDK input shape). Local patches can only temporarily solve the problem. Claude CLI -p mode requires two steps to complete MCP configuration: (1) Explicitly pass –mcp-config to load project-level tools; (2) Add –permission-mode bypassPermissions to avoid permission confirmation in non-interactive mode. In this case, the –allowedTools wildcard is unreliable because it conflicts with MCP server initialization. BypassPermissions is the correct method for backend service calls. GDK Input Shape intercepts events on the X11 layer. setPointerCapture cannot override it. In dragging scenarios, a set_float_ball_dragging command in Rust should be used to temporarily expand the input shape to the entire window. With cairo-rs 0.18 and gdk 0.18, input_shape_combineRegion can be used directly in Tauri with_webview callbacks, eliminating the need for additional complex dependencies. When debugging nested process CLI tools (main process → Claude CLI → MCP server), it’s more efficient to first record the actual command parameters and then isolate and verify the effects of each parameter. The ccplan method effectively transforms complex platform architecture problems into executable plans by exploring alternative solutions, going through a review process, and testing feasibility. The ECL documentation provides cross-session context persistence, reducing the cost of repeated analysis. Practical Achievements When using structlog to wrap standard library logging, it’s necessary to explicitly add extra={} parameters as ExtraAdder() processors so that they appear in the output fields. Otherwise, the field will disappear silently without reporting any errors. Session Summary TokenMonitor 🔄 Linux FloatBall Architecture Reorganization: ccplan Planning and Implementation of Fixed Window Layouts and Multiple Debugments 02:52:43.137 | claude_code
Users provided a comprehensive analysis of the root causes (4 layers of geometric mismatch, 11 failed attempts and reasons, log evidence) to initiate the ccplan process. AI generated six alternative solutions, recommending the fixed window layout with GDK Input Shape. After approval, the implementation was completed. Three issues arose during operation: ball deformation, inability to expand, and dragging failures. These issues were resolved through GTK geometry_hints, CSS direction positioning, and the set_float_ball_dragging command. To ensure stable movement, the snap function was removed. After 284 lines of Rust code and 205 frontend tests, final verification is in progress. During this process, a git stash operation issue was discovered and corrected through stash pop. Then, the specific modifications made during this session were also manually adjusted.
Life-Copilot ✅ Construction of Discord Bot’s Structured Logging System + Complete Fixes to MCP Tool Calls
01:35:00.000 | claude_code
Through ccplan planning, a structured logging system was implemented (development color output/production JSON format, rotated every 10MB). JSONL audit logs were generated at three entry points (Discord/API/CLI) and complete conversation records were saved. After 420 tests, two-stage issues related to MCP tool calls were resolved: First, the –mcp-config parameter was added to load 19 project-specific MCP tools. Then, by running Claude -p directly, the root cause of permission denied errors was found. Using –permission-mode bypassPermissions completely solved this problem in non-interactive mode. Verification showed that in Discord, Google calendar queries returned correct results.

content:
Monthly Summary — 2026-03 This month’s work was highly focused on the MIHD spatial transcriptomics multimodal clustering research project: after completing environment initialization on the DCC HPC cluster, we systematically integrated the TEDDY-G 400M foundation model, Leiden clustering algorithm, and STHD pathology annotation Ground Truth. Multi-encoder benchmarks revealed STAIG framework’s high robustness to gene encoders and the systematic transfer loss of Foundation Models on clustering tasks. The paper’s Fig 1–6 framework was finalized, marking the project’s transition from the experimental phase to the paper-writing phase.
Monthly Overview Metric Value Active Days 4 / 31 Total Conversations 14 Projects Involved 5 Tasks Completed 19 Tasks In Progress 3 Total Tokens 63,798,066 Total Cost $39.58 Daily Average Cost $9.89 Project Progress MIHD (Spatial Transcriptomics Multimodal Clustering) (4 active days) — 🔄 active The month began with issue triage and documentation, mid-month saw new encoder integration and bug fixes, and the end of the month brought systematic expansion of evaluation dimensions (Leiden, HD dataset, Ground Truth), culminating in a finalized paper structure with experimental work largely complete. Best ARI ~0.546 (DLPFC), STAIG framework robustness verified; slice 151676 remains an unresolved systematic challenge.
Key Milestones:
Identified root cause of cross-slice retrieval failure (PCA/STAIG coordinate spaces are incomparable per slice; scGPT used as cross-slice encoder) Integrated TEDDY-G 400M and completed full 11-slice embedding extraction (185MB) Multi-encoder STAIG fusion benchmark confirmed framework robustness (Teddy vs PCA ARI difference: 0.002) Integrated Leiden clustering (binary search to match n_clusters), STHD Ground Truth (100% match), and HD ARI/NMI evaluation Paper Fig 1–6 framework finalized, referencing structures from four papers: MISO/STAIG/QueST/Yuan DCC HPC Environment Setup (1 active day) — ✅ completed Completed from-scratch environment initialization in a single day, establishing the foundation for running MIHD experiments on DCC. The main obstacle was that config files do not migrate automatically and had to be reconstructed from existing configs.
Key Milestones:
rclone + sync.py completed Google Drive sync for four data categories Investigated GPU partition resources (scavenger-h200 H200×8 available) Installed cchelper skill Key Achievements This Month Identified root cause of MIHD cross-slice retrieval failure and found a solution (2026-03-23, MIHD) — Confirmed root cause is that PCA/STAIG processes each slice independently, producing incomparable embedding spaces. scGPT, due to its shared coordinate system, is the optimal encoder for cross-slice retrieval (average SL@50=0.416, 100% hit rate), revealing the architectural principle that single-slice clustering and cross-slice retrieval require different encoders. Discovered and fixed 4 critical bugs in extract_teddy() (2026-03-29, MIHD) — Systematic source code review revealed: incorrect Tokenizer class name, wrong model forward parameter name (input_ids→gene_ids), wrong output key (last_hidden_state→outputs[‘cell_emb’]), and missing gene symbol to Ensembl ID mapping. After fixes, the first slice (151508, 4384 spots) was successfully validated. Verified STAIG framework’s high robustness to gene encoders (2026-03-30, MIHD) — Teddy 1024d (ARI=0.495) and PCA 50d (ARI=0.497) performed nearly identically under the STAIG framework, with a gap of only 0.002, demonstrating that STAIG’s spatial graph contrastive learning mechanism dominates clustering performance and the impact of gene embedding quality is greatly diluted. Paper Fig 1–6 framework planning completed (2026-03-31, MIHD) — Referencing four competing papers — MISO (Nat Methods), STAIG (Nat Comms), QueST, and Yuan (Nat Methods) — established a complete paper structure: Fig1=framework diagram, Fig2=DLPFC benchmark, Fig3=ablation, Fig4=HD application, Fig5=cross-slice Query, Fig6=Query biological discovery. Written into ECL YAML and plans.md, marking the project’s official transition to the writing phase. TEDDY-G 400M full 11-slice embedding extraction completed (2026-03-30, MIHD) — Completed 1024d gene embedding extraction for all 11 DLPFC slices (151508–151676) in a dedicated conda teddy environment, totaling 185MB, providing a complete data foundation for multi-encoder comparison benchmarks. STHD Ground Truth integration (100% match rate) (2026-03-31, MIHD) — Added _load_sthd_annotations() function, achieving 17502/17502 spots 100% match via r_big/c_big coordinates; CE+Tumor biological merging (Neoplasm + Non-neoplastic Epithelium → Epithelium) yielded 3 meaningful clusters, providing quantitative Ground Truth for HD evaluation. HD full fusion strategy evaluation revealed naive fusion information dilution (2026-03-31, MIHD) — Completed 10/13 HD fusion strategy experiments; found that simple fusion strategies (concat/mean/element_wise_sum) produced ARI (0.19–0.20) lower than unimodal PCA (0.351), while attention fusion approached PCA (ARI=0.342). UNI2 vision-only achieved the highest NMI (0.387) but lower ARI than PCA, revealing that NMI and ARI can be inconsistent across different datasets. Summarized systematic transfer loss pattern of Foundation Models on clustering (2026-03-30, MIHD) — Teddy gene-only ARI (0.186) underperformed PCA (0.255). Systematic analysis confirmed the root cause: Foundation Models are optimized for disease discriminability (not variance maximization), high-dimensional spaces (1024d) degrade KMeans performance, and training data (dissociated scRNA-seq) differs in distribution from spatial transcriptomics. Leiden clustering binary search integration (+15% ARI) (2026-03-31, MIHD) — Implemented binary search in clustering.py to match target n_clusters (range [0.01, 10], up to 20 iterations), enabling fair comparison between Leiden and KMeans. Leiden improved ARI by ~15% over KMeans on DLPFC, but improvement over STAIG was limited. First structured documentation of MIHD project historical issues (2026-03-23, MIHD) — By consolidating plans.md, RESULTS.md, and diagnostic reports, generated docs/PROBLEM_SUMMARY.md — the first centralized record of best ARI 0.546, root cause of cross-slice retrieval failures, fixed bug inventory, and a 4-phase ablation experiment plan, providing a structured entry point for subsequent research. Recurring Issues 1. Systematic failure on a specific slice (151676) (occurred 3 times) Dates: 2026-03-23, 2026-03-30, 2026-03-31 Root Cause: Slice 151676 has data-specific issues: zero-variance embeddings cause mclust to fail and fall back to KMeans (dragging down the global mean), and model collapse under the STAIG framework cannot be recovered. The root cause remains unidentified, possibly related to anomalous gene expression distribution or spatial structure in this slice. Status: 🔄 Ongoing
2. Pipeline hardcoded configuration inconsistent with slice-specific parameters (occurred 2 times) Dates: 2026-03-30, 2026-03-31 Root Cause: Pipeline global configuration (e.g., top_genes=3000, dataset defaults, image path structure) has implicit divergences from slice-specific config (151676 uses 5000 genes) or dataset-specific structure (HD vs DLPFC image paths), causing cache check false positives (sys.exit) and visual extraction failures. Status: 🔧 Workaround available
3. HPC environment configuration obstacles (conda activation, missing config files) (occurred 2 times) Dates: 2026-03-23, 2026-03-31 Root Cause: Config files do not automatically migrate to new machines (sync.json must be manually reconstructed), and conda initialization on SLURM compute nodes differs from login nodes (requires eval $(conda shell.bash hook) rather than source activate). Both issues stem from a lack of experience with environment migration. Status: ✅ Resolved
4. Cross-session context loss requiring repeated background provision (occurred 2 times) Dates: 2026-03-23, 2026-03-29 Root Cause: Claude’s session isolation mechanism means key research progress (e.g., Teddy test results) is not persisted to the memory system, requiring users to re-provide arXiv links or background information in subsequent sessions. Status: 🔄 Ongoing
Human-AI Collaboration Trends Human-led key insights: 7 AI limitation pattern: Cross-session memory loss: context at critical research milestones is not automatically persisted, requiring users to repeatedly provide background AI limitation pattern: Hardcoded assumptions delay bug discovery: defaults to global config rather than dynamically resolving slice-specific parameters, tracing root causes only after actual errors occur AI limitation pattern: Excessive reliance on cluster resources: unable to proactively judge whether small datasets (17502 spots) require GPU/SLURM, defaulting to job submission AI limitation pattern: Convention assumptions about non-standard APIs: assumed HuggingFace standard interfaces without reading source code, leading to 4 TEDDY API usage errors AI limitation pattern: Inefficient monitoring strategy: background tasks used multi-round sleep+ls polling instead of event-driven mechanisms Improvement direction: Proactively trigger memory persistence at critical research milestones to avoid cross-session context loss Improvement direction: Proactively identify path/parameter dataset-specific risks when dealing with multi-dataset pipelines Improvement direction: Do not assume API conventions for non-standard libraries before reading source code; declare upfront that verification is needed Improvement direction: Provide proactive experiment prioritization suggestions rather than just listing options and waiting for user decisions Improvement direction: Estimate compute requirements based on data scale, distinguishing locally runnable tasks from those requiring the cluster Selected Insights This Month Architecture STAIG framework is highly robust to gene encoders: Teddy 1024d vs PCA 50d under STAIG shows only 0.002 ARI difference, demonstrating that spatial graph contrastive learning dominates clustering performance and the impact of gene embedding quality is greatly diluted. Therefore, the bottleneck for optimizing MIHD clustering performance does not lie in the choice of gene encoder. (Source: 2026-03-30) The optimal encoder for single-slice clustering (STAIG/PCA) differs from the optimal encoder for cross-slice retrieval (scGPT): the comparability of embedding spaces (shared coordinate system) is more critical than local quality. Foundation Models naturally suit cross-slice retrieval due to their fixed representation space output, while PCA processes slices independently, producing incomparable coordinate systems. (Source: 2026-03-23) Foundation Models exhibit systematic transfer loss on cross-domain downstream tasks: TEDDY achieves SOTA on disease classification (ACC=0.72) but underperforms PCA on spatial transcriptomics clustering (ARI 0.186 vs 0.255). Root cause is optimization objective mismatch (discriminative representation vs variance maximization), and high-dimensional space (1024d) is unfavorable for KMeans. (Source: 2026-03-30) Domain Knowledge HVG1500 (direct concatenation of highly variable genes without dimensionality reduction) achieves ARI=0.330 on single slices, outperforming all Foundation Models; Leiden clustering improves ARI by ~15% over KMeans on DLPFC, but improvement over STAIG is limited — STAIG’s advantage comes from spatial graph structure and is relatively independent of the clustering algorithm. (Source: 2026-03-30, 2026-03-31) NMI and ARI show inconsistency on HD data: UNI2 vision-only achieves the highest NMI (0.387) but lower ARI than PCA (0.344 vs 0.351); naive fusion (concat/mean) on HD yields ARI (0.19–0.20) lower than unimodal PCA (0.351), indicating information dilution from simple fusion. Both metrics should be reported together. (Source: 2026-03-31) Tools Single-cell foundation model forward interfaces in the HuggingFace ecosystem vary significantly and standard interfaces cannot be assumed: TEDDY uses gene_ids (not input_ids), GeneTokenizer (not Tokenizer), outputs[‘cell_emb’] (not last_hidden_state), and its vocab uses Ensembl IDs (43808 tokens) rather than gene symbols. Source code must be read to confirm. (Source: 2026-03-29) DCC HPC cluster practical notes: SLURM compute nodes require eval $(conda shell.bash hook) && conda activate to initialize conda (source activate does not work); scavenger-h200 provides H200×8 preemptible nodes; account TRES limits are cpu=100/mem=500G; Glob tends to time out on large HPC filesystems — use exact path search instead. (Source: 2026-03-23, 2026-03-31) Debugging Implicit divergence between slice-specific configuration (e.g., staig num_gene) and pipeline global defaults is a high-risk bug type: cache check logic should dynamically resolve actual configuration rather than hardcoding defaults; multi-dataset pipeline image paths should be explicitly differentiated rather than using a unified data_root/section_id logic. title: Bug Journal 2026-03-31

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-30 Key Points of the Day Completed Work: Integrated TEDDY-G 400M into the MIHD process, completed gene embedding extraction for all 11 DLPFC slices, evaluated 5 non-STAIG fusion strategies, and conducted STAIG fusion experiments. Implementation Method: Extracted embeddings in a dedicated conda Teddy environment and saved them in standard cache format. Fixed errors related to staig_gene_feat caching in evaluation_planner.py. Executed the first-phase extraction and second-phase evaluation in stages. Significance: Confirmed that the ARI of Teddy+UNI2+STAIG is approximately 0.495, which is comparable to PCA+UNI2+STAIG (ARI ≈ 0.497). Demonstrated the robustness of the STAIG framework for gene encoders, providing experimental evidence for future encoder replacements. Completed the benchmark tests for TEDDY-G 400M embedding extraction and multi-strategy fusion for all 11 DLPFC slices. W13 weekly report was written, summarizing last week’s research findings.
Tasks for Today Architecture and Strategies ✅ Teddy + STAIG Fusion Evaluation — Completed evaluation for 10/11 slices (failed due to model crash); average ARI is 0.495, compared to PCA+STAIG’s 0.497. Teddy performed better in 6 of the 10 slices. ✅ Completed Embedding Extraction for TEDDY (11 Slices) — Used conda Teddy environment to extract 1024d gene embeddings from 11 DLPFC slices (151508–151676) and cached them in embeddings_cache/gene/teddy/. Total size: 185MB. ✅ STAIG Fusion Evaluation for TEDDY (5 Strategies × 11 Slices) — Evaluated configurations using only Teddy, teddy_uni2_concat, teddy_uni2_mean, teddy_uni2_attention, and teddy_uni2_llava_mlp. The best ARI for gene-only configuration was 0.186; the best ARI for llava_mlp was 0.305. ✅ UNI2 staig_strict Visual Feature Extraction — Extracted UNI2 staig_strict variants from all 11 slices (256×256 patches, no ImageNet normalization, frequency domain filtering). Implementation and Fixes ✅ W13 Weekly Report Written — Summarized docs/plans.md, git history, and file timestamps to create docs/WEEKLY_REPORT_2026_W13.md. Summed up two main experiments and key conclusions from last week. ✅ Fixed staig_gene_feat caching errors in evaluation_planner.py — Previously used 5000 genes in staig_gene_feat_5000.npz, but the planner only checked 3000 genes. This caused sys.exit(1). Now it dynamically checks the actual file size. ✅ Research on TEDDY base model background — Summarized technical details of TEDDY paper through web search (arXiv:2503.03485). The model has a bidirectional Transformer architecture, supervised training for disease/ tissue/cell types, and trained on 116M cells across 5 scales (10M–400M). ✅ Feasibility Assessment of TokenMonitor HPC Build — Checked RHEL 9.7 HPC environment: Node.js/npm available, but Rust and Tauri require system libraries like webkit2gtk4.1-devel, which were missing. No sudo privileges, so TokenMonitor couldn’t be built on HPC. Problems and Solutions Major Issues 1. TEDDY’s gene-based ARI (0.186) is lower than PCA’s (0.255). The base model failed to outperform the simple baseline in clustering tasks. Solution: Analyzed root causes: TedDY was optimized for disease classification, not maximizing variance. The high-dimensional space of 1024d reduced KMeans performance. Training data was scRNA-seq data different from spatial transcriptomics distribution.
Key Insight: There is a fundamental contradiction between the “universal representation” of the base model and its “clustering adaptability.” PCA directly maximizes data variance, making it naturally suitable for KMeans. The base model learned representations specific to diseases, leading to systematic losses in downstream clustering tasks.
General Issues 2. evaluation_planner.py miscached staig_gene_feat in slice 151676, causing the second phase to terminate with sys.exit(1). Solution: Modified the has_staig_gene_feat check logic to dynamically determine the actual number of top_genes in the slice configuration (default value changed from 3000 to 5000 for 151676) to ensure correct caching.
3. During STAIG fusion evaluation, model crash occurred in slice 151676, invalidating clustering results. Solution: Skipped this slice and reported results based on average ARI of 0.495 for the 10/11 slices.
4. Due to lack of Rust, webkit2gtk4.1-devel dependencies, and no sudo privileges, TokenMonitor couldn’t be built on HPC. Solution: Recommended three methods: build binary locally with permissions, request system dependencies from HPC administrators, or build Linux packages through GitHub Actions CI.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Role Thoughts Human When I saw that llava_mlp’s ARI was 0.305 and other fusion strategies were worse than PCA, I actively requested STAIG fusion experiments. AI When presenting non-STAIG results, the AI only provided a list of options without suggesting a priority order. Difference Analysis: Humans have prior knowledge about which fusion strategy is most likely to match SOTA. Therefore, we actively promoted STAIG fusion. The AI tends to leave decision-making to users rather than predicting problems proactively.
Implementation Level Discovery and Fixing of staig_gene_feat Miscache Errors Role Thoughts Human Not involved; fully discovered and fixed by AI. AI The AI traced the trigger path of sys.exit, checked the actual file (5000.npz vs 3000.npz), identified hidden configuration splits, and made fixes. Reviewing Last Week’s Work Role Thoughts Human Asked about “last week’s plans and completed work,” expecting a comprehensive summary. AI The AI comprehensively analyzed docs/plans.md, git log timeline, file timestamps, and experimental results, reconstructing last week’s work from multiple perspectives. Difference Analysis: Humans pose high-level questions, while the AI systematically breaks down tasks into “planning vs execution” levels and provides quantitative results.
Limitations of AI Main Constraints Failed to deeply analyze the root cause of model crashes in slice 151676. Only labeled it as a “known difficult slice,” without attempting to adjust hyperparameters like tau or num_epochs. General Constraints Failed to identify the hard-coding problem with staig_gene_feat in evaluation_planner.py before triggering sys.exit. Inefficient progress monitoring of background tasks: Using multiple rounds of sleep + ls polling, rather than inotifywait or structured progress files, leading to significant waiting time. Syntax errors occurred when constructing multi-line Python f-string in bash commands (unterminated string literals). Commands need to be modified accordingly. Today’s Summary Key Summary - The STAIG fusion framework demonstrates high robustness in handling gene encoders: Teddy 1024d (ARI=0.495) and PCA 50d (ARI=0.497) perform similarly under the STAIG framework, indicating that the STAIG’s spatial graph comparison learning mechanism has advantages in clustering performance. The impact of gene embedding quality is significantly reduced. The base model shows systematic transfer losses in cross-domain tasks: Teddy performs well in disease classification (ACC=0.72), but worse than PCA in spatial transcriptomics clustering (0.186 vs 0.255). The root cause lies in the mismatch between optimization goals (discrimination vs variance maximization). HVG1500 (directly connected high-variance genes, no dimensionality reduction) has an ARI of 0.330 on slice 151673, outperforming all base models. This indicates that high-dimensional original features may be beneficial for certain slices by retaining more local variation information. Practical Conclusions The hidden configuration split between slice-specific configurations and pipeline global defaults is a high-risk error. These should be dynamically resolved in cache checking logic, rather than being hardcoded. Meeting Summary MIHD Process ✅ TEDDY-G 400M Full DLPFC Benchmarking: Embedding Extraction → Multi-Fusion Evaluation → STAIG Fusion 00:01:05.759 | claude_code In the DCC, we used a conda teddy environment to extract Teddy 1024d embeddings for each slice. Then, we evaluated 5 non-STAIG fusion strategies. The best ARI using only genes was 0.186, while the best ARI for llava_mlp was 0.305. Users requested more STAIG fusion experiments. AI extracted UNI2 staig_strict visual features and fixed errors related to staig_gene_feat caching in evaluation_planner.py. Finally, we used Teddy+STAIG to evaluate 10/11 slices, with an ARI comparable to PCA+STAIG (ARI=0.497).
✅ Review of MIHD Experiments Last Week and Weekly Report Generation 21:13:39.566 | claude_code Users asked about last week’s plans and actual work. AI synthesized the plan document, git log information (latest commit was on 2026-02-22, no new commits), file timestamps, and experimental results, summarizing two main tasks last week: 8 encoders benchmarks (151673 single-slice) and Teddy full fusion experiments. These were used to create the weekly report in docs/WEEKLY_REPORT_2026_W13.md, including experimental objectives, methods, results, and key conclusions.
✅ Research and Introduction to TEDDY Base Model 22:58:07.566 | claude_code Users requested detailed information about the TEDDY base model. AI gathered technical details from papers (arXiv:2503.03485, Merck/BCG X) through web search. The model has a bidirectional Transformer BERT architecture with 5 parameter sizes (10M–400M), two variants: TEDDY-G/TEDDY-X, for supervised training of disease/ tissue/cell types/gender, and trained on 116M cells. It was also compared to scGPT/Geneformer. Discussions regarding integration methods and limitations were held in relation to the MIHD project.
TokenMonitor 🔍 Feasibility Assessment of Building TokenMonitor on HPC (RHEL 9.7) 21:22:13.566 | claude_code Users asked about system requirements and whether TokenMonitor could be built. Node.js/npm was found to be available, but Rust/Cargo had not been installed yet. The webkit2gtk4.1-devel system library required by Tauri was missing, and such modules were not present in the module system. There was no pre-built release package on GitHub. It was recommended to install Rust via rustup, contact HPC administrators for system dependencies, or build Linux packages through GitHub Actions CI. However, it should be noted that this GUI application requires X11/Wayland support when running on HPC login nodes.
