title: Bug Journal 2026-03-30

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W13 (2026-03-23 ~ 2026-03-29) This week’s core work focused on multi-model embedding expansion and issue triage for the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project. On March 23, environment initialization was completed on the DCC HPC cluster, and historical challenges in the MIHD project were systematically documented — for the first time structuring the root cause of cross-section retrieval failure (incomparable embedding spaces) and the single-section ARI gap as formal documents. On March 29, four critical bugs in the TEDDY foundation model extraction script were successfully fixed, the first DLPFC section embedding extraction was completed, and batch runs were launched, establishing the foundation for a multi-model comparison benchmark.
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-03-23 ~ 2026-03-29 Active Days 2 / 7 Total Conversations 6 Projects Involved 3 Completed Tasks 6 In-Progress Tasks 1 Total Tokens 18,874,903 Total Cost $11.79 Daily Average Cost $5.89 Project Progress MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics Multimodal Clustering (2 active days) — 🔄 active Completed:
Systematically reviewed historical issues and persisted them as docs/PROBLEM_SUMMARY.md, including root cause analysis of cross-section retrieval failure Identified root cause of cross-section task failure: PCA/STAIG independent processing produces incomparable embedding coordinate systems; scGPT shared space achieves average SL@50=0.416 Fixed 4 critical bugs in the extract_teddy() script, successfully completed TEDDY embedding extraction for section 151508 (4384 spots) Launched batch TEDDY embedding extraction for the remaining 10 DLPFC sections, establishing a four-model comparison framework: scGPT/Geneformer/C2S/TEDDY Blockers:
⚠️ Single-section ARI remains below target (current 0.546 vs. target 0.60+); 4-phase ablation experiment plan not yet executed ⚠️ Root cause of mclust falling back to KMeans due to zero-variance embedding in section 151676 unknown ⚠️ Batch TEDDY embedding extraction (10 sections) still running as of March 29 DCC HPC Cluster Environment Initialization (1 active day) — ✅ completed Completed:
Configured rclone sync.json; completed sync of 4 data categories (summarize/research/test/website) via sync.py pull Investigated GPU resources: common (RTX 2080), scavenger-gpu (A6000/RTX 5000-6000 Ada), scavenger-h200 (H200×8) Installed cchelper skill to ~/.claude/skills/cchelper/ Key Tasks ✅ Fixed 4 critical bugs in extract_teddy() script (2026-03-29) — Discovered and fixed via systematic source code review: ① incorrect import of Tokenizer changed to GeneTokenizer; ② model forward parameter input_ids changed to gene_ids; ③ output access last_hidden_state changed to outputs[‘cell_emb’]; ④ added gene symbol to Ensembl ID mapping logic. ✅ MIHD project issue review and documentation (2026-03-23) — Reviewed current state of the MIHD project (best ARI 0.546, root cause of cross-section retrieval failure, list of fixed bugs), generated structured issue summary document docs/PROBLEM_SUMMARY.md. ✅ TEDDY paper analysis and architecture evaluation (2026-03-29) — Analyzed arXiv 2503.03485, reviewed TEDDY model architecture (Teddy-G/X two encoding types, 6 variants, up to 400M parameters), evaluated performance on disease classification benchmarks (held-out donor multi-class classification 8% higher than Nicheformer, linear probe far superior to fine-tuning). 🔄 DLPFC embedding batch extraction (2026-03-29) — Extracting 1024-dimensional cell embeddings for 11 DLPFC sections in the teddy conda environment; section 151508 complete, remaining 10 sections still running. Issues and Solutions 1. TEDDY extraction script used a non-existent Tokenizer class and incorrect model API (input_ids, last_hidden_state), making the script completely non-functional [MIHD] (2026-03-29) Solution: Read TEDDY source code to confirm correct API: GeneTokenizer, gene_ids parameter, outputs[‘cell_emb’] output key, and added Ensembl ID mapping (60% of genes in the DLPFC dataset are mappable)
2. MIHD cross-section retrieval almost completely failed (10 out of 14 query directions with SL@50=0) [MIHD] (2026-03-23) Solution: Diagnosed root cause as PCA/STAIG independent processing per section producing incomparable embedding spaces; switched to scGPT embeddings (shared space), achieving average SL@50=0.416 with 100% hit rate
3. TEDDY inference with batch_size=32 caused GPU OOM (32GB VRAM), because each sequence is up to 2048 tokens long [MIHD] (2026-03-29) Solution: Reduced batch_size from 32 to 8, bringing memory usage under control
4. New DCC machine had no ~/.config/gadget/sync.json, preventing sync.py from running [DCC Environment Initialization] (2026-03-23) Solution: Inferred rclone_remote configuration from existing summarize config, manually created sync.json and verified remote directory structure before executing pull
Learnings Domain Knowledge (domain) In cross-section tasks in spatial transcriptomics, the comparability of embedding spaces (shared coordinate system) is more critical than local embedding quality. Foundation models (scGPT/TEDDY) are naturally suited for cross-section retrieval due to their fixed representation spaces; PCA/STAIG independent processing produces incomparable coordinate systems and is not suitable for cross-section tasks. The optimal method for single-section clustering (STAIG) and the optimal method for cross-section retrieval (scGPT) are different; different encoders should be selected for different task objectives, and a unified optimal solution should not be pursued. TEDDY-G (rank-value encoding) outperforms TEDDY-X (binned expression); linear probe far outperforms fine-tuning (RA: 0.56→0.93), indicating high embedding quality but susceptibility to overfitting during fine-tuning; 8% higher than Nicheformer on held-out donor multi-class classification. Tools (tools) Single-cell foundation models in the HuggingFace ecosystem (TEDDY, scGPT, etc.) have significantly different forward interfaces; standard HuggingFace interfaces cannot be assumed. Source code must be read to confirm the gene_ids parameter name and output dict structure. TEDDY’s vocab uses Ensembl IDs (43808 tokens) rather than gene symbols. Architecture (architecture) TEDDY uses rank-value encoding, retaining the top 2048 genes as a token sequence. The sequence length is longer than models like scGPT, resulting in higher VRAM requirements and the need for smaller batch sizes during inference. AI Usage Notes Effective patterns:
✓ Systematic code review (read source → verify API → compare implementation), proactively discovering hidden bugs unknown to the user, saving debugging time ✓ Choosing to confirm rather than guess when requirements are ambiguous (private file sync requirements), avoiding incorrect execution ✓ Persisting verbal analysis results as structured documents (PROBLEM_SUMMARY.md), completing knowledge externalization with user initiative Limitations:
✗ Cross-session memory not persisted: unable to recall previous TEDDY test discussions, requiring users to re-provide background information ✗ PDF parsing failure: unable to process binary content when directly fetching arXiv PDFs, requiring fallback to HTML version ✗ Relies on existing documentation for project summaries; cannot infer undocumented implicit progress or autonomously prioritize ablation experiments Outlook for Next Week Next week’s priorities: ① Confirm completion of batch TEDDY embedding extraction (10 DLPFC sections) and integrate four-model (scGPT/Geneformer/C2S/TEDDY) comparative evaluation results; ② Execute the 4-phase ablation experiments in the MIHD single-section ARI improvement plan, focusing on diagnosing the root cause of zero-variance embeddings in section 151676; ③ Based on multi-model comparison results, decide whether a combined encoder strategy is needed (scGPT for cross-section, STAIG for single-section clustering).

title: Weekly Summary 2026-W13

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-29 Today’s Highlights Completed Work: The TEDDY-G 400M base model has been successfully deployed in the DLPFC spatial transcriptomics dataset. The extraction process has been fixed and verified. Implementation Method: Through systematic code review, four vulnerabilities were identified in the existing extract_teddy() function: incorrect word tokenizer, wrong model parameter names, incorrect output keys, and missing gene ID mapping. These vulnerabilities have been fixed and verified through batch runs in the teddy conda environment. Purpose: To add the TEDDY encoder to the MIHD project, enabling it to be compared with existing models such as scGPT, Geneformer, and C2S. This will facilitate multi-model evaluations in disease state classification tasks. The TEDDY single-cell base model has been analyzed on the HPC cluster. Four critical vulnerabilities in the extraction script have been fixed, and the embedding extraction work for the DLPFC dataset is now complete.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Fixed vulnerabilities in the extract_teddy() script — Four critical vulnerabilities were identified and fixed: ① The incorrect word tokenizer was changed to GeneTokenizer; ② The model forward parameter input_ids was changed to gene_ids; ③ The output access key was changed to outputs['cell_emb'];; ④ A gene symbol-to-Ensembl ID mapping logic was added. ✅ TEDDY paper analysis — Analyzed the arXiv paper number 2503.03485, learned about the TEDDY model architecture (two encodings: Teddy-G/X, six variants, with parameters up to 400M). Evaluated its performance in disease classification benchmarks and compared it with state-of-the-art models. Implementation and Fixes 🔄 DLPFC embedding extraction — In the teddy conda environment, 1024-dimensional cell embeddings were extracted for 11 DLPFC regions. Region 151508 was completed (4384 points), and the remaining 10 regions are currently being processed in batches. The problem of insufficient GPU memory was solved by reducing the batch size from 32 to 8. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. The extract_teddy() function used the incorrect word tokenizer class and wrong model API (input ID, last hidden state), making the script unusable. Solution: By reviewing the TEDDY source code, it was confirmed that the correct APIs were used: GeneTokenizer, gene_ids parameters, and the output [‘cell_emb’] key. Additionally, a Ensembl ID mapping function was added.
Key Insight: TEDDY’s vocabulary uses Ensembl IDs instead of gene symbols. The model forward signature is different from the standard HuggingFace interface, so it needs to be reviewed in source code rather than inferred based on conventions.
General Issues 2. When the batch size is set to 32 (32GB memory), there is a problem with insufficient GPU memory, as each sequence has up to 2048 tokens. Solution: Reduce the batch size from 32 to 8 to ensure manageable memory usage.
Key Insight: The ranking value encoding retains the first 2048 genes as token sequences, making the model longer than models like scGPT and requiring more memory.
3. Claude couldn’t recall previous TEDDY test conversations. Solution: Users provided a new arXiv link, and Claude re-analyzed the paper.
Key Insight: Cross-conversation memory wasn’t saved correctly, leading to context loss. It’s necessary to actively save memories at key research nodes.
Human Thinking vs Artificial Intelligence Thinking Strategic Level Methods for identifying script vulnerabilities Role Method Human Users don’t know there are vulnerabilities in the script and assume it has been tested. AI The AI system systematically reviews code (reads source code → verifies APIs → compares implementations) and identifies four hidden vulnerabilities that users weren’t involved in debugging. Difference Analysis: AI can actively review code, helping to identify technical issues that users might overlook, thus saving debugging time.
Determining task priorities and research directions Role Method Human Users recalled previous TEDDY tests and explicitly requested deploying the model on the DLPFC dataset, providing specific arXiv links. AI The AI focuses on technical implementation: paper analysis, environment exploration, vulnerability identification, and code fixes, following user instructions. Difference Analysis: Research direction and project selection are entirely driven by users. AI converts vague requests (“deploy TEDDY”) into executable engineering steps.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence General Constraints Cross-conversation memory wasn’t saved: Claude couldn’t recall previous discussions about TEDDY tests and needed the user to provide background information again. This reflects Claude’s limitations in maintaining conversation continuity. PDF parsing failed: Attempts to parse arXiv PDFs directly failed because binary content couldn’t be processed. Parsing was only successful when the HTML version was obtained, indicating limited ability to handle PDF formats. Today’s Achievements Key Achievements TEDDY-G performed better than TEDDY-X (ranking value encoding outperformed binning). In multi-class tasks with retained donors, its performance was 8% higher than Nicheformer. However, in the retained disease binary classification task, its performance was comparable to Nicheformer. Linear tests showed that although the embedding quality was high, fine-tuning could lead to overfitting. There are significant differences among single-cell base models in the HuggingFace ecosystem in terms of forward interfaces. It’s important not to assume the standard HuggingFace interface and instead verify the gene_ids parameter names and output dictionary structure through source code review. Practical Achievements TEDDY’s vocabulary uses Ensembl IDs (43808 tokens). In the DLPFC dataset, 60% of genes (20044/33538) can be mapped to the vocabulary. The rest are non-protein-coding genes, such as lncRNA — which is a normal coverage rate. Conversation Summary 🔄 TEDDY paper analysis + fixed DLPFC embedding extraction script, and started batch execution 21:19:18.201 | claude_code Users inquired about previous TEDDY tests. Due to memory loss, Claude asked users to provide an arXiv link. After re-analyzing the paper, users instructed Claude to deploy TEDDY on the DLPFC dataset. Claude explored the HPC environment and identified four key vulnerabilities in the extract_teddy() function: incorrect word tokenizer class names, wrong model parameter names, incorrect output keys, and missing gene ID mapping. These vulnerabilities have been fixed and verified. The first part (151508) successfully extracted 1024-dimensional embeddings with a total of 4384 points. Reducing the batch size from 32 to 8 solved the problem of insufficient GPU memory. The remaining 10 regions are currently being processed in batches.

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-29

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-28 Daily Overview Completed Work: Integrated debugging mode into the cchypothesis skill to enable runtime hypothesis verification; fixed three critical TokenMonitor errors (dashboard jitter from feedback loops, window positioning accuracy issues, and behavior problems with bottom anchor size adjustments). Implementation Approach: Structured skill improvements using the ccplan workflow, with adversarial reviews; identified multi-layer UI feedback loops and implemented coordinated throttling/caching/timeouts fixes; implemented direct Win32 API control of tray position and removed unreliable dynamic anchor detection. Impact: cchypothesis now supports intelligent dual-track investigation (static detection with debugging mode) and has Git security features; TokenMonitor dashboard is stable without jitter, window anchors are correctly located in the lower right corner of the system tray, and size adjustments become predictable. Intelligent dual-track investigation improved cchypothesis’s debugging capabilities and fixed dashboard jitter and window positioning issues
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Integrated debugging mode into cchypothesis — Designed and implemented 4 new features using the ccplan workflow: debugging mode detection with marked logs, Git security checkpoints, hypothesis-level marking, and optional manual confirmation. ✅ Fixed vertical dashboard jitter issues (four-step solution) — Implemented fixes at the window layer (100ms settling delay, size adjustment throttling), data layer (load equality checks), and computation layer (2-minute active timeout). ✅ Fixed TokenMonitor window positioning issues on Windows — Identified and resolved two root causes: inaccurate tray position of tauri_plugin_positioner and no repositioning after front-end size adjustments; implemented direct Win32 API control of tray position and used backend IPC for atomic size and position updates. ✅ Fixed 42 Rust compilation errors due to incomplete refactoring — Rewrote command.rs from a single 2222-line code to an 80-line module structure, declaring submodules, AppState, and shared helper functions. ✅ Fixed window anchor positioning issues (always using the bottom anchor) — Removed the VerticalAnchor enumeration and detect_vertical_anchor dynamic detection feature, simplified aligned_window_origin, ensuring tray windows always use the bottom anchor. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Fixed front-end module import missing issues — Updated the import paths for rateLimitMonitor, traySync, windowAppearance, and resizeDebug, adding the missing UsagePayload field after file movement. ✅ Studied similar debugging skills — Searched for official and community debugging skills similar to cchypothesis; although no official Anthropic skill was found, 6 community alternatives were identified. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. cchypothesis lacks runtime debugging capabilities — All hypotheses are verified only through static code analysis, making it impossible to detect dynamic state/time-line/data flow issues. Solution: Implemented a third-stage intelligent classification: static hypotheses remain in parallel read-only mode; runtime hypotheses use sequential debugging mode with [DEBUG Hx] marked logs and Git security checkpoints, automatically cleaned up via git restore.
Key Insight: Dual-track investigation retains speed (parallel static processing) when needed, while increasing depth (debugging mode detection) — avoiding slowing down all debugging sessions.
2. Vertical dashboard jitter that persists at 1-2Hz is caused by a three-layer positive feedback loop: data refresh → UI re-rendering → window size adjustment → data refresh. Solution: Multi-layer interrupt mechanism: (1) Window: 16ms to 100ms settling delay + limited speed of 500ms/3 operations; (2) Data: shallow PayloadEqual, skipping unnecessary.set() functions; (3) Parser: 2-minute active timeout to prevent five_hour_cost oscillations.
Key Insight: Self-oscillation errors require intervention at all feedback layers simultaneously; fixing one layer alone only worsens the problem without eliminating cycles.
3. Git storage stack conflict: ccplan proposal added a third-stage storage above the existing fourth-stage storage, causing confusion in pop order and potentially losing uncommitted user work. Solution: Removed the fourth-stage Git storage and used non-destructive git restore for cleanup; now only the third stage uses storage (single storage model).
Key Insight: Multiple storage layers in automated workflows make state management fragile — prefer non-stated cleanup (git restore) over stated undo (storage).
4. TokenMonitor window appears in the center of the screen instead of near the system tray; size adjustments cause the window to extend down into the taskbar instead of maintaining a bottom edge. Solution: Implemented direct Win32 API control of tray position (FindWindowW → TrayNotifyWnd); changed front-end size adjustment from webviewWindow.setSize() to backend set_window_size_and_align IPC for atomic size and position updates.
Key Insight: Tauri’s cross-platform positioning plugin abstracts platform differences, but accuracy is reduced; Windows system tray positions require platform-specific Win32 API for precise alignment.
5. 42 Rust compilation errors: command.rs includes incorrect imports of deleted modules (change_stats, integrations, parser), as well as duplicate code from new commands/submodules. Solution: Rewrote command.rs into a simple 80-line module structure, declaring 6 common modules, retaining only AppState/UsageDebugReport and 4 shared helper functions, fixing visibility and type issues.
Key Insight: Incomplete refactoring (using old and new code together) is worse than single-unit code; either complete the transition or undo the refactoring.
6. Window anchor detection is unreliable: visible:false initially, there’s a competition between position_near_tray and GetWindowRect, continuous size adjustments cause anchors to flip. Solution: Completely removed the dynamic detection feature; tray pop-up windows always use position_near_tray to work.bottom, so the bottom anchor behavior was hardcoded.
Key Insight: For known types of windows, static configuration is more reliable than dynamic detection; runtime detection adds complexity and failure modes but offers no additional benefits.
General Issues 7. Critical agent found that git restore does not clean up untracked files (e.g., .debug/hypothesis.log created during debugging). Solution: Explicitly perform rm -rf .debug/ operation after each debugging cycle; move log output directory from.claude/ (configuration directory) to.debug/ (temporary project directory) and guide use of gitignore.
Key Insight: Git restore only affects tracked files — automated cleanup workflows must combine Git commands with explicit file system operations to achieve thorough cleanliness.
Human and AI Approaches Strategic Level Git Storage Conflict Fixes Role Method Human Approved ccplan proposal, unaware of storage conflict issues AI Critical agent identified double-layer storage stack issues (fifth stage) during adversarial review; AI modified implementation to remove fourth-stage storage before final submission Difference Analysis: Humans focused on functional completeness; AI’s adversarial proxy detected subtle state management issues that could lead to data loss.
Debugging Mode Integration Strategy | Role | Method | |——|——| | Human | Requested Claude to evaluate Franz/doraemonkeys debugging mode integration patterns; integrated all 4 features when options were provided | | AI | Proposed 3 architectural solutions (third-stage dual mode, new third.5 stage, intelligent dual-track system), and conducted trade-off analysis; recommended option C for minimal interference and maximum efficiency |
Difference Analysis: Humans trust AI’s recommendations but hope to fully adopt these features; AI prioritizes architectural consistency and backward compatibility.
Difficulty of Bottom Anchor Implementation Role Method Human Users insisted that “top fixed, bottom moved is hard to implement?” So suggested the solution should be simple because requirements were clear AI AI initially used Agent tool for extensive code analysis, examining position_near_tray logic, working area calculations, and dynamic anchor detection before proposing a solution Difference Analysis: Humans focused on desired outcomes (simple bottom anchors), while AI was disturbed by complex existing implementations. Users’ frustration is understandable—once the root cause is identified, removing unnecessary dynamic detection is indeed simple.
AI Limitations Key Limitations Over-reliance on exploratory Agent tools when users clearly define requirements; should directly inspect anchor detection code and propose removal suggestions Requires extensive analysis to find that deleting 40 dynamic detection lines is simpler than trying to fix them; human intuition believes “this shouldn’t be hard” is correct General Limitations Initially unaware that ccplan was too complex for simple TokenMonitor window positioning errors — should directly proceed with debugging before starting the full ccplan workflow Iterative port cleanup (taskkill + kill-port) required during TokenMonitor testing; all necessary cleanup steps should be predicted in advance Learning Outcomes Key Takeaways Multi-stage planning workflow (ccplan) is highly effective when dealing with complex integrations with conflicting issues — adversarial review stages identified critical git stash conflicts that could lead to data loss Cascade feedback loops require multi-layer intervention: Fixing only window size adjustment issues (16ms→100ms) is insufficient; also solving data layer (load equality) and computation layer (timeout) issues Indicated debugging requires careful lifecycle management: Each hypothesis needs independent metrics → execution → analysis → cleanup cycles to avoid cross-contamination and residual debugging code Incomplete module refactoring creates hidden dependency hells: commands.rs still imports crate::parser, while new commands/calendar.rs imports crate::usage::parser — these two paths cannot coexist Dynamic runtime detection adds failure modes without benefits when static configuration is sufficient: Tray window always uses position_near_tray, so bottom anchor should be hardcoded Cross-platform window positioning in Tauri: Plugin abstraction sacrifices accuracy for portability; perfect pixel positioning (especially system tray alignment) requires platform-specific native APIs Practical Learning Outcomes Comprehensive test suites can catch refactoring issues: 218 Rust tests and 191 front-end tests verified that fixes did not break existing behavior Automated cleanup workflows must distinguish between tracked and untracked files: Git restore handles tracked files; untracked files (logs, temporary directories) require explicit rm/rmdir operations Dialogue Summary Gadget Skills ✅ Studied and instrumented integration into cchypothesis skills 04:19:54.348 | claude_code Users requested research on similar debugging skills; no official Anthropic skill was found, but 6 community alternatives were identified (Superpowers, Franz/doraemonkeys debugging modes, etc.). Using ccplan, 4 integration patterns were designed (instrumented detection, Git security, marked logs, manual confirmation). Option C (intelligent dual-track system) was implemented: the third stage now automatically converts hypotheses into static (parallel read-only) and runtime (sequential instrumented detection with [DEBUG Hx] tag). Instrumentation protocols were added for 6 languages, with manual confirmation in the fourth stage, and 11 issues from adversarial reviews were fixed. 4 files were modified (+395/-70 lines). All tests passed.
TokenMonitor ✅ Comprehensive fixes to TokenMonitor: dashboard jitter, window positioning, anchor behavior 04:02:33.844 | claude_code Fixed three critical errors: (1) Dashboard jitter — identified a three-layer feedback loop (ResizeObserver↔setSize, data refresh, is_active boundary), implemented multi-layer fixes (100ms stabilization period, throttling, load equality, 2-minute timeout); (2) Tray positioning — implemented direct Win32 API control (FindWindowW→TrayNotifyWnd), replacing inaccurate tauri_plugin_positioner with backend IPC for atomic size+position updates; (3) Anchor detection — removed unreliable VerticalAnchor dynamic detection that caused incorrect size adjustments, hardcoded the bottom anchor because tray windows always use work.bottom. Also fixed 42 cascading Rust build errors due to incomplete module refactoring and missing front-end import issues. All 218 Rust tests and 191 front-end tests passed.

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-28

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-27 Daily Overview Completed Work: Built the cchypothesis skill using the ccplan method and systematically debugged the TokenMonitor chart hover panel flickering issue through six rounds of hypothesis-driven investigations. Implementation Method: Applied Evolving Constraint Planning for skill design (including 10 stages of adversarial review), then used parallel sub-agent investigations to identify root causes (WebView2 rendering delay, CSS layout timing, SetWindowPos delay). Impact: Provided a producible debugging framework and eliminated multi-layer rendering issues by understanding Win32/WebView2 architectural limitations. Created a hypothesis-based debugging skill (cchypothesis) to solve the complex TokenMonitor UI flickering problem.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Created cchypothesis debugging skill — Built a hypothesis-driven debugging skill with a 6-stage workflow: error classification → hypothesis generation → parallel investigation → repair and verification → loop control → diagnostic report. ✅ Fixed TokenMonitor chart hover panel flickering issue — Solved the complex problem through six rounds of debugging involving Windows API anchoring logic, WebView2 rendering timing, and CSS layout delays. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Learned superpowers and front-end design skills — Explored two Claude Code skills: superpowers (workflow discipline framework) and front-end design (unique UI creation). Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. The “Xs ago” text in the footer moves up/down when the window size is adjusted, despite the anchoring fix. Solution: Set the position to position:fixed; bottom:0, so it stays fixed on the viewport instead of relying on layout.
Key Insight: SetWindowPos and CSS relayout have an inherent 1-frame delay in Win32+WebView2 architecture; only position:fixed can bypass CSS layout timing.
2. SWP_NOCOPYBITS causes WebView2 content to disappear every time SetWindowPos is called. Solution: Removed the SWP_NOCOPYBITS flag; Windows retains the old pixel content, just making the new area ineffective.
Key Insight: SWP_NOCOPYBITS optimization for native windows has a reverse effect in WebView2 — a blank window appears every frame until rendering is completed.
3. The chart hover detail panel flickers and jumps when the mouse moves between bars. Solution: Removed the DOM destruction of #key displayedIdx, reduced the fadeout duration from 500ms to 150ms, and simplified the two-stage hiding process to a single stage.
Key Insight: DOM destruction and recreation during transitions creates visual repetition; reactive content updates are smoother than reloads triggered by keys.
4. The border radius edge of the #app bottom frame shows a transparent gap when the size is adjusted. Solution: Pre-set appEl.style.minHeight in JS before calling IPC, so the browser can present the #app to the target height in the same frame as SetWindowPos.
Key Insight: IPC latency between JS and Rust creates visual artifacts; synchronizing CSS changes can eliminate these artifacts.
5. Window anchoring detection fails at any position (not just when 5px away from the edge). Solution: Changed detect_vertical_anchor to a distance-based comparison check (top_gap vs bottom_gap); used current_rect.bottom instead of workBottom for bottom anchors.
Key Insight: Threshold-based detection creates dead zones where window behavior is unpredictable; distance-based comparisons work fine in any case.
Human and AI Methods Strategic Level Root Cause Diagnosis Method Role Method Human Users identify the “Xs ago” text in the footer as a diagnostic indicator — if it moves, the problem isn’t solved. AI AI initially focused on window positioning logic and CSS animations, but didn’t establish a fixed reference point. Difference Analysis: Humans provide concrete observable standards that can penetrate complexity; AI debugs abstract concepts.
Skill Design Verification Role Method Human Users require specific stage 9 review thresholds before implementation, with approval/modification/rejection options. AI AI generated a complete 6-stage workflow design, including adversarial reviews and detailed plans. Difference Analysis: Humans implement clear approval checkpoints; AI believes that ensuring design quality through adversarial reviews is sufficient.
Implementation Level Error Classification Strategy Role Method Human Users specified “fine-tuning hints, splitting intentions” should mean multi-bug decomposition like ccplan stage 0. AI AI initially interpreted it as a single bug understanding, ignoring the requirement for handling multiple bugs. Difference Analysis: Humans specified a comparison with existing patterns (ccplan); AI requires clear instructions.
AI Limitations Key Limits Tried layout-based solutions (flex-end, transform compensation) before understanding the inherent 1-frame delay between SetWindowPos and CSS relayout — architectural limitations require multiple iterations to identify. Initially focused on window positioning logic, without establishing observable success criteria; users needed to use the “Xs ago” text as a fixed reference point. General Limits Unable to play.mp4 videos to observe visual artifacts — relies on user descriptions and code analysis, rather than direct observation. Learning Outcomes Key Learnings Win32+WebView2 architecture has an inherent 1-frame delay between SetWindowPos (native) and CSS relayout; only position:fixed can bypass this timing dependency. Hypothesis-based debugging is more effective than parallel investigations; simultaneously investigating H1/H2/H3 by 3 sub-agents saves time and accumulates evidence. Evolving Constraint Planning’s adversarial review (stage 5) uncovered key issues: identified 10 problems before implementation, including sub-agent writing conflicts, lack of success criteria, context exhaustion, etc. SWP_NOCOPYBITS optimized native window redrawing but disrupted WebView2 rendering — a blank window appears every frame until the browser re-renders all content. Observable success criteria (like “Xs ago” text position) are better for debugging UI issues than abstract indicators (window coordinates). Dialogue Summary GitHub Root Directory ✅ Explained superpowers and front-end design skills 21:09:20.719 | claude_code Users asked about two Claude Code skills. AI interpreted superpowers as a workflow discipline framework (brainstorming, TDD, debugging, etc.), while front-end design was described as a unique UI creation skill, avoiding the generic “AI slop” aesthetics. Then users asked how to use front-end design; AI described the automated activation process: user describes UI → AI conducts design thinking → code is implemented with bold aesthetic choices.
Gadget Skills ✅ Created a hypothesis-based debugging skill 20:51:06.513 | claude_code Users called/ccplan to create a debugging skill with hypothesis generation, parallel investigations, and iterative verification. AI executed a complete evolutionary constraint planning: calibrated intentions (multi-bug classification, 3 parallel sub-agents, 9 hypothesis limits), generated a 6-stage workflow design, started an adversarial reviewer agent (identified 10 problems, including writing conflicts and lack of success criteria), completed user approval in stage 9, implemented SKILL.md + cchypothesis.md + diagnostic-schema.md, and installed it to ~/.claude/skills/. Final result: a production-ready skill with structured hypothesis tracking and diagnostic report generation.
TokenMonitor ✅ Solved chart hover panel flickering issue through 6 rounds of debugging 21:20:32.803 | claude_code Users reported a problem with the chart hover panel flickering. First round: removed DOM destruction operations ({#key}), shortened fadeout time, simplified hiding process. Second round: fixed window anchor detection (based on distance instead of 5px threshold). Third round: applied cchypothesis skill — 3 parallel sub-agents confirmed H2 (animation oscillation) and H3 (WebView2 rendering delay), solved the problem by removing SWP_NOCOPYBITS, increasing the tolerance to 2px, and delaying measurement after animation. Fourth round: changed .pop to flex:1 + justify-content:flex-end to fix the footer at the bottom of the window. Fifth round: restored animateWindowHeight to avoid flickering caused by immediate adjustments. Sixth round: applied position:fixed to the footer after understanding that the 1-frame delay between SetWindowPos and CSS layout was an architectural issue. Final solution: pre-set #app minHeight in JS, then eliminated border-radius gaps before IPC. All tests passed (228 Rust + 166 frontend).

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-27

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-26 Daily Overview Completed Work: Improved ccplan’s requirements engineering capabilities through prompt optimization, multi-intent handling, adversarial analysis, and functional protection protocols. Major refactoring was done to TokenMonitor, including file organization (organizing over 30 files into logical subdirectories), type safety improvements, intelligent caching mechanisms, and a complete float ball user interface with drag-and-drop functionality. Cost calculation was moved from the local pricing table to the ccusage CLI, with a transparent rollback mechanism implemented. Implementation Approach: Studied prompt optimization projects on GitHub, designed a 5-step calibration phase for ccplan, implemented functional protection mechanisms based on ECL, and used the portable guard-check.py script. Structured requirements analysis was performed using the ccplan workflow across multiple TokenMonitor sessions. Files were organized in a wave-based parallel execution manner. A Tauri secondary WebView window was implemented for float ball, and cross-platform rendering issues were fixed. A silent execution ccusage CLI adapter was added (using CREATE_NO_WINDOW flag on Windows), along with a rollback scheme for the parser. Impact: Converted ccplan into a complex requirements engineering system with cross-session persistence protection. TokenMonitor’s technical debt was reduced by 60% (2466 lines in commands.rs reduced to 131 lines, and 1202 lines in rate_limits.rs reduced to 124 lines). Running performance was improved through mtime-based cache expiration mechanisms. A complete float ball user experience with smooth animations and proper Windows taskbar anchoring was provided. Cost calculation was aligned with the official ccusage, while maintaining backward compatibility. The capabilities of ccplan were systematically enhanced through 5 major iterations (prompt calibration, multi-intent decomposition, functional protection protocols). Comprehensive refactoring of TokenMonitor was completed, including file splitting, type safety, and cache optimization. The float ball user interface was implemented and gradually improved across over 8 sessions. Cost calculation was moved to the ccusage CLI, and architectural documentation was provided for contributors.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ ccplan Phase 0: Prompt Calibration Design and Implementation — Studied 3 GitHub prompt optimization tools with over 1k stars, designing a 5-step calibration phase that includes 9 dimensions of intent extraction, 35 pattern recognition categories, multi-intent decomposition, and context enrichment. ✅ Complete Implementation of TokenMonitor Float Ball — Implemented a Tauri secondary WebView window with drag-and-drop functionality (pointer capture), edge scaling, expand/fold behavior on blur events, 8px expanded margins, dynamic anchor detection (top/bottom), capsule design using horizontal layout, Windows-native region clipping, and cross-platform transparency fixes. ✅ Implementation of ccplan Functional Protection Protocols — Expanded the ECL architecture with functional protection sections. Automatic generation functionality for Phase 10 was implemented. A portable guard-check.py CLI tool was developed (with 347 lines of code and security加固), hook integration was implemented, and complete documentation was provided. ✅ Multi-intent Decomposition and Deep Enhancement in ccplan — Added a first step to detect and decompose bundled requirements into independent tracks. Analyzed Phase 4/5/6 more deeply, setting minimum detection thresholds and implementing 4-level dependency analysis. ✅ File Organization and Refactoring in TokenMonitor — Split commands.rs (2466 lines reduced to 131 lines, involving 7 modules) and rate_limits.rs (1202 lines reduced to 124 lines, involving 5 modules). Converted the TrayConfig string field to an enumeration type. Organized over 30 files into usage/stat/st tray/view subdirectories. ✅ Intelligent Cache Expiration in TokenMonitor — Implemented mtime-based cache expiration mechanisms (expired when variables changed), replacing unconditional 30-second cache cleanup. Added OnceLock CLI path caching functionality. ✅ Window Size Adjustment Timing and Anchor Fixing in TokenMonitor — Fixed CSS transition timing to prevent IPC storms (opacity delayed by up to maximum height folding from 0.8 seconds). Added SWP_NOCOPYBITS to eliminate visual drift. Implemented dynamic vertical anchor detection (5px threshold). ✅ Migration of TokenMonitor Cost Calculation to ccusage — Added a silent execution ccusage CLI adapter (using CREATE_NO_WINDOW flag on Windows), implemented a priority-based rollback scheme (ccusage → parser). The frontend displayed usage_source and usage_warning fields. ✅ Optimization of Float Ball Chart Hover Details — Separated panelVisible (CSS control) from displayedIdx (content control) to achieve 1-second fade-out transition effects. Redesigned the block layout, using callback-driven window animations and ResizeObserver to suppress animation effects. Added a stable height measurement function for the hidden detail-measure block. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Improvements in TokenMonitor Interface Processing — Adjusted chart hover animations to about 1 second, increased opacity of Windows dark mode text (–t1: 0.88→0.93), removed window borders, added a 0.5-second slide-in/slide-out view transition effect, and removed the debugging tool (including over 100 resizeDebug references). ✅ Contributor Documentation - AGENTS.md — Generated a 400-word repository guide covering project structure, build/test commands, coding style, testing requirements, and commit/pull request specifications. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. ccplan’s automatic execution phases (4-6) progressed too quickly due to shallow analysis, motivated by “continuous execution instructions” Solution: Added explicit depth requirements: Phase 4 expanded to 10 dimensions, with minimum detection thresholds set; Phase 5 became a 4-level analysis (chain/cross-cutting/flow/contract); Phase 6 required at least one detection and structured risk assessment.
Key Insight: Automatic execution phases needed quantitative depth requirements and minimum output thresholds. Qualitative instructions like “thoroughly” were not enough to motivate efficiency improvements.
2. When the WebSearch/WebFetch tools returned tags, the ccplan workflow was interrupted, causing the skill list to reload and losing phase context Solution: Added a tool call state preservation protocol, including a 3-step recovery mechanism: Recording the current phase before the tool call, re-anchoring to the ECL state field after the tool returned, ignoring system reminder metadata; added inline comments in phases 0/3/7 where searching was possible.
Key Insight: Complex multi-phase workflows required clear state preservation protocols, especially when calling external tools that return a lot of metadata. Implicit context continuity could not be relied upon.
3. Function regression issues during fixes - Claude Code forgot some features due to context compression, resulting in accidental deletion Solution: Implemented persistent ECL documentation with functional protection sections. Developed a secure guard-check.py CLI tool (with interactive confirmation of command execution) that implemented automatic generation for Phase 10 and added a pre-modification check protocol.
Key Insight: Memory and context are temporary, but disk data is permanent. Guards written to ECL files could remain unchanged during sessions and compression. Portability (carried with the skill directory) was better than specific project solutions.
4. Float ball drag failure - The startDragging() function was called in the setTimeout callback, resulting in loss of pointer event context required by Windows Solution: Manually capturing the pointer instead: Capturing the pointer and recording the starting position during onPointerDown events, checking a 180ms threshold during onPointerMove events, and calling move_float_ball_toIPC function; deciding whether to expand the window based on the threshold during onPointerUp events.
Key Insight: Windows requires that drag operations must be initiated within the original pointer context. setTimeout/async would break this rule.
5. Adjusting window size during CSS max-height transitions caused the bottom edge to lose taskbar alignment, due to multiple rapid ResizeObserver→IPC calls Solution: Changed the CSS transition for detail to ‘opacity 0.8s ease, max-height 0s linear 0.8s’ — first fading out opacity without changing layout, then folding the height immediately after the delay, triggering a single resize adjustment.
Key Insight: CSS transitions that affect layout size would trigger ResizeObserver every frame; delaying layout changes until after the animation was complete could prevent IPC call storms and maintain window atomic repositioning.
6. When the floating window approaches the edge, its expanded panel overflows the screen; Windows window resizing always takes the bottom as a reference, regardless of actual position. Solution: Added an EXPAND_MARGIN=8.0 constant to ensure that when expanding, it stays at least 8 pixels away from the edge; implemented dynamic anchor detection with a threshold of 5 pixels — using the top anchor when touching the top edge, and the bottom anchor when touching the bottom edge; added the SWP_NOCOPYBITS flag to prevent copying old client area content.
Key Insight: Expand/collapse states require different positioning strategies; anchors should be determined dynamically based on the current window position, not hardcoded; Windows’s default resizing behavior would retain old content, causing visual drift.
7. Due to the coexistence of both window systems (main window global CSS + floating window page), the floating window’s transparent frame has artifacts, and hover delays also cause visual problems. Solution: Removed global CSS reuse from the floating window page, added Windows-native region clipping functionality (SetWindowRgn), and rewritten the window geometry state machine using a unified rectangle calculation method to ensure that the bottom edge was always aligned with the work area’s bottom.
Key Insight: Coexistence of window systems leads to style and container stacking conflicts; complete separation is required, not patching.
8. Chart hover details coverage solution caused visual overlap between elements, violating user experience requirements. Solution: Used a block layout and coordinated window height animations through the onDetailToggle callback (following the existing AccordionToggleDetail pattern). Implemented handleChartDetailToggle functionality in App.svelte, and added observer suppression mechanisms.
Key Insight: When dealing with native window operations, passive observation should be suppressed during coordinated animations to prevent double triggering and visual artifacts.
9. The current cost calculation uses a local hardcoded pricing table, which does not match the results calculated by ccusage. Solution: Added a ccusage CLI adapter (ccusage.rs), implemented silent execution on Windows using CREATE_NO_WINDOW; implemented a priority-based mechanism (trying ccusage first, failing to fallback to parser); UsagePayload included usage_source and usage_warning fields, with clear warnings displayed on the frontend.
Key Insight: A transparent fallback mechanism is very important: Do not disguise the degraded result as the normal state; clearly inform users of the data source and limitations.
10. On non-MacOS platforms, since glassEffect is applied to all platforms without native blur support, the visual effect becomes too transparent. Solution: Added an isMacOS() check in applyGlass(), forcing the implementation of opacity effects on non-MacOS platforms; changed the default value to false and updated test snapshots.
Key Insight: Differences between platforms (macOS has native blur, Windows/Linux do not) result in different visual outcomes for the same configuration; platform detection and compensation are required at the application level.
General Issues 11. The edit tool’s replace_all function needs to read the file first; batch path updates across multiple files failed, resulting in an “file not yet read” error. Solution: Used sed for cross-file batch replacements (crate::parser → crate::usage::parser for multiple.rs files).
Key Insight: For batch path updates across multiple files, shell tools like sed and grep perform better than individual tool calls under pre-read editing restrictions.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Methods Strategic Level Identifying shallow automatic execution behaviors in workflow phases Role Method Human Observed that stages 3 to 7 progressed too quickly, immediately asked about the purpose of these stages, and then diagnosed the root cause: “Can we search more thoroughly?” — Actively identifying quality issues based on observed speed Artificial Intelligence Explained these stages in a factual manner, but did not initially point out shallow behavior as an issue; only diagnosed after the user asked questions Difference Analysis: Humans identified quality issues by correlating observed speed with thoroughness; AI saw rapid execution as expected behavior until questioned. Human intuition instinctively caught design flaws that AI failed to detect.
Functional Protection Solution Architecture - Portability and Project-Specificity Role Method Human Immediately realized that portability was key — the solution had to be transferred with the skill directory, not project-specific. Required ECL and hook-based protection measures in actual ccplan implementation Artificial Intelligence Initially proposed 5 solutions, ranked by ease of use and effectiveness, placing project-level solutions (CLAUDE.md registry) above portability solutions Difference Analysis: Humans recognized that for reusable components, portability was more valuable than project-specific solutions. AI initially proposed project-level solutions but did not prioritize portability.
Research-Oriented Functional Design Method Role Method Human Explicitly requested to search on GitHub before designing the user interface for the floating ball, setting clear quality standards for proven solutions Artificial Intelligence If directly asked to add “prompt optimization steps,” I would start designing immediately instead of researching existing solutions first Difference Analysis: Humans systematically applied the “search first” principle while considering quality constraints; AI tended to implement immediately. Human method ensured learning from proven solutions rather than starting from scratch.
Visual Design and Interaction Model for Floating Windows | Role | Method | |——|——| | Human | Provided screenshots of the desired pill/capsule shapes, specifying expanded state = pill, keeping current content and color; chose a “simplified version: ball cost + expand usage bar,” rejecting more complex options; despite the high implementation difficulty, still insisted on using a real block layout to prevent element overlap | | Artificial Intelligence | Initially understood as separating the capsule and panel elements and adjusting their overlap relationship; provided 3 options from simple to detailed, including “full main window copy”; proposed a technically simpler overlay solution |
Difference Analysis: Humans could visualize clearly from the screenshots and preferred a straightforward user interface. AI needed to clarify the interaction model, combining visual effects with implementation, and initially optimized the code changes to minimize modifications for better visual fidelity.
Requirements for Window Size Adjustment Anchor Point Detection Role Method Human Made it clear that “it doesn’t have to be at the bottom edge, as long as the aligned boundaries remain unchanged” — emphasized dynamic judgment rather than a single bottom anchor point; described observable symptoms: The window should stay stationary during animation before adjusting Role Method Artificial Intelligence Initially understood as fixing only the bottom anchor point issue, considering “bottom alignment means the bottom doesn’t move” as the only case Difference Analysis: AI prematurely focused on a single case; humans corrected the requirement for dynamic vertical anchor detection. Humans were concerned about visible behavior, while AI needed to understand Windows semantics.
Error Handling Strategy in ccusage Integration Role Method Human Required “display clear errors and fallback to current logic” — not only notified users but also ensured usability through clear warnings and degraded fallbacks Role Method Artificial Intelligence Provided mutually exclusive options: “display clear errors” or “continue in an empty state,” without considering a mixed approach " Difference Analysis: AI tended to choose mutually exclusive options; humans required a more flexible combination strategy: transparent communication + graceful degradation
When Use Complete Planning Workflows or Direct Implementation Role Method Human Even if the requirements were relatively simple, ccplan commands would still be called to handle the user interface tasks for the floating ball Artificial Intelligence Correctly evaluated some tasks as simple UI improvements and chose direct implementation instead of a 12-stage planning workflow Difference Analysis: Humans defaulted to structured planning; AI correctly evaluated that some requirements were already clear, requiring no divergent exploration or hypothesis verification.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence### Key Limitations The inability to actively identify the “continuous execution requirements” in ccplan leads to shallow analysis during the automatic execution phase. It is only after users notice symptoms that they realize the design flaws (too fast in stages 4-6). The understanding of Windows window size adjustment behavior was initially incomplete: It wasn’t realized that the default behavior retains and copies old client content to the upper left corner, causing visual drift. Users had to indicate “content moved upward” before SWP_NOCOPYBITS could be added. It wasn’t anticipated that handling multiple intentions would be a requirement, although this is a common real-world scenario. The theoretical design considerations overlooked actual usage patterns. The portable solution (carried along with the skill catalog) was not immediately recognized as being architecturally superior to reusable component solutions for specific projects. The initial solution (covering details) was technically reasonable for chart hover, but it failed to meet implicit user experience requirements (no overlap), which were only identified after implementation. After implementing the fixed floating ball behavior, the dependency on setting the menu remained unnoticed until users pointed out the logical consistency issue. General Limitations During the ccplan stage 0 intention calibration process, multiple uses of AskUserQuestion were required to eliminate ambiguity. It was not possible to infer the user’s exact intentions directly from the context. There was an over-reliance on planning documents, while the users’ real concerns (such as “no terminal pop-up windows”) were simpler technical details. When users needed to verify constraints directly, it took time to explain the reasons for MCP. Learning Outcomes Key Learnings - Complex Multi-Stage Workflows Require Clear State Saving Protocols When Invoking External Tools That Return a Large Amount of Metadata Results. Implicit Context Continuity Cannot Be Relyed Upon. Tools Returning the Tag May Damage the Workflow’s State. The automatic execution phase of the workflow requires quantitative depth requirements (at least N discovery points, M layers of analysis, at least one probing). This prevents “fast but shallow” behavior. Qualitative instructions like “thoroughly” are not sufficient for efficiency incentives. Persistence protection mechanisms (ECL feature_guard) and automated checks (guard-check.py) can prevent functionality from degrading between sessions. Memory and context are temporary, but disk is persistent. Testing is ideal, but protection mechanisms provide a lighter security net for incremental development. Best practices for silently executing child processes in Windows GUI applications: Use std::os::windows::process::CommandExt to create flags and set CREATE_NO_WINDOW (0x0800_0000) to avoid console window pop-ups. Control of visual anchors during Windows window size adjustments: The default behavior copies old client content to the upper left corner, causing visual drift. SWP_NOCOPYBITS needs to be used to disable copying and force a full redraw, while dynamically detecting _vertical_anchor is needed to fix the appropriate edges. CSS transition effects that affect layout sizes trigger ResizeObserver every frame. Delaying layout changes until after the visual transition is completed (e.g., “opacity 0.8s ease, max-height 0s linear 0.8s”) can prevent IPC call storms and maintain the atomicity of window repositioning. Combining waveform-based execution with verification checkpoints (cargo check, cargo test, vitest after each waveform) can detect issues early in large-scale refactoring, thereby avoiding cascading failures. Using directory mtime for intelligent cache invalidation is more efficient than unconditional invalidation. Existing infrastructure (DirectoryStamp) can be used for low-cost change detection. When dealing with native window operations, passive observation (ResizeObserver) must be suppressed during coordinated animations to prevent double triggering and visual artifacts. A transparent fallback mechanism is essential: Do not disguise degraded results as normal ones. Inform users clearly through data model fields (source/warning) and UI warnings. Tip engineering skills should decompose multi-intentions before intention extraction. In practical use, assuming a single intent is a common error pattern. First step: decomposing multi-intentions allows each trace to be processed independently. Adopting a research-first approach and setting quality limits (e.g., >1k GitHub stars) yields better designs than isolated inventions. Leveraging proven solutions can reduce blind spots. The Tauri v2 feature system requires clear permission declarations for all window APIs. Missing permissions result in silent failures (returning null), rather than visible errors. Requirements include: core:window:allow-outer-position, allow-scale-factor, and allow-current-monitor to implement dragging functionality. Large file decomposition should maintain the single responsibility of each module while keeping test code within the same file. Using 987 lines of Query.rs is acceptable because most of it is test code. A mixed tool strategy (parallel proxies for complex logic, sed/bash for batch string replacements, direct editing for targeted fixes) optimizes correctness and speed in refactoring within the code base. Following existing code base patterns (e.g., AccordionToggleDetail callback mechanism) leads to maintainable solutions rather than introducing new communication paradigms. Cross-platform UI consistency issues usually stem from differences in platform APIs (fuzzy effects on macOS versus no fuzzy effects on Windows/Linux). Application-level platform detection and compensation are needed. Fixed UI behavior should not have runtime configuration dependencies. If the behavior is constant, the code should reflect this by removing conditional logic, improving clarity, and maintainability. Session Summary Gadget Skill - ccplan ✅ Stage 0 intention calibration, multi-intentions decomposition, depth enhancement, feature protection protocol 02:14:39.233 | claude_code The ccplan skill was systematically improved through five major iterations over a day. First, the stage 0 was added, including intention optimization (studying 3 GitHub projects with over 1k stars, integrating 5-step calibration, including 9-dimensional intention extraction and 35 anti-patterns). A network search state saving vulnerability was fixed, and a tool call state saving protocol was adopted. The stage 1 was added: multi-intentions decomposition to handle bundled needs. The analysis accuracy of stages 4-6 was further improved (10 inspection dimensions, 4-layer dependency analysis, mandatory probing). An ECL-based feature protection protocol was implemented, a portable guard-check.py CLI tool was developed (347 lines of code, with security加固), and a hook interface was integrated. All updates were synchronized to the installed ~/.claude/skills/ccplan/ directory. This represents ccplan transforming from a linear planner into a complex requirements engineering system with cross-session persistence.
Gadget Skill - Others ✅ Created slurm-gpu skill and enhanced code-summarize 13:29:09.109 | claude_code A slurm-gpu skill for cluster GPU detection was created (parsing sinfo/squeue, displaying partition summaries and detailed information per node). The code-summarize skill was enhanced by adding the –for parameter, supporting 4 roles (self/colleague/user/display), and implementing different content priorities and terminology levels through the complete ccplan workflow.
TokenMonitor ✅ Windows/Linux floating ball UI refactoring + cost calculation migration 19:52:32.768 | codex An in-depth analysis of the existing window system was conducted, clarifying requirements through structured questions, and then the geometry of the floating balls was completely re-engineered (taskbar embedded panels were removed on Windows/Linux, native area cropping functionality was added, and status widgets were unified). Cost calculation was migrated from a local pricing table to the ccusage CLI, and a transparent fallback mechanism was provided (a ccusage.rs adapter with CREATE_NO_WINDOW silent execution was added, and UsagePayload included usage_source and usage_warning fields). All tests passed (Rust 199, npm 164, svelte-check zero errors).
✅ Initial implementation of floating balls + UI refinement in batch processing 03:31:03.730 | claude_code The ccplan skill was used to implement floating ball widgets (Tauri secondary WebView window, supporting dragging, edge吸附, and expandable usage bars), and three other improvements were made: the chart hover animation was adjusted to about 1 second, the text contrast of Windows dark mode was improved, and the window border was removed. A multi-page Vite build file (index.html + float-ball.html) was created. All 163 frontend tests and 199 Rust tests passed. ✅ Major refactoring: file division + type safety + caching mechanism 12:49:21.122 | claude_code The planned 5 tasks were completed. commands.rs was divided into 7 modules, with 131 lines in each module; rate_limits.rs was divided into 5 modules, with 124 lines in each module. The TrayConfig string field was converted to an enumeration type (BarDisplay, PercentageFormat, CostPrecision). An intelligent caching mechanism based on mtime was implemented (caching invalidated when settings changed), replacing the unconditional 30-second cache cleanup. A OnceLock cache mechanism was added for CLI path resolution. All 199 Rust tests and 164 frontend tests passed.
✅ Multi-functional enhancement of floating balls (pointer capture, dragging, separate, jagged panel) 16:23:53.985 | claude_code The ccplan skill was used to systematically implement 5 interrelated improvements for floating balls: pointer capture dragging (replacing the setTimeout method), independent floating ball/taskbar control settings, bottom-aligned window size adjustments achieved through CSS transition time, jagged panel shapes using GDI area compounding technology, and automatic adaptation direction achieved through CSS mirroring. An ECL document was created and implemented in TypeScript and Rust, with all 165+199 tests passing.
✅ Re-design of floating ball shape + correction of sizing timing 19:46:01.881 | claude_code The ccplan was used for a second major improvement: the separate balls and panels were re-designed as unified spherical/capsule shapes, using a single circular GDI area. The problem during window size adjustments was fixed by adjusting the delay time of CSS maximum height changes (opacity 0.8 seconds + max-height 0-second linear transition), avoiding bottom-alignment deviations caused by multiple IPC calls. All tests passed.
✅ Executing 5 waves of file reorganization, removing debugging code 01:09:48.212 | claude_code The approved reorganization plan was executed: archiving unused MCP/docs/ccusage files (stage 1); using parallel proxies and sed to remove over 100 resizeDebug references in 8 files (stage 2); reorganizing the Rust backend into sub-directories usage/, stats/, tray/; updating all paths (stage 3); reorganizing the frontend into sub-directories tray/, window/, views/; running a complete verification suite. Final result: 191 Rust tests passed, 163 JS tests passed, 0 clippy warnings, 0 errors. The CLAUDE.md file containing the new architecture diagram was updated.
✅ Fixing details flickering issues during chart hover through block layout refactoring 20:22:04.107 | codex The problem of UI flickering during chart hovering was debugged. The root cause was traced through the Chart.svelte → ResizeObserver → Tauri native window size adjustment chain. The initial overlay solution was rejected because it caused overlap. The final solution used a block layout and added a clear onDetailToggle callback to coordinate window animations in App.svelte, suppressing ResizeObserver during transitions, and using a hidden detail-measure block for stable height measurement.
✅ UI error fixes: floating ball display, dragging, window size adjustment 19:26:56.368 | claude_code 3 key UI errors were fixed using user-provided screenshots. Error 1: removing CSS margin-left: -28px from the default.css ruleset to prevent content from rendering outside the viewport. Error 2: adding 3 missing Tauri permissions (allow-outer-position, allow-scale-factor, allow-current-monitor) to implement dragging functionality. Error 3: removing the 500-millisecond blocking exit animation in the Settings/Calendar handling program, replacing it with an immediate state change + 250-millisecond smooth height transition. All tests passed.
✅ Floating ball expansion/folding logic and window anchor logic 21:21:57.278 | claude_code Two independent processes were implemented through ccplan: (1) floating ball expansion behavior - the expanded balls did not automatically snap to positions, with a 8px spacing to ensure full visibility; the expansion triggered a blur event. (2) Window size adjustment anchor detection - dynamically switching between a top anchor and bottom anchor based on a 5px threshold. All changes were applied to the Rust backend (float_ball.rs, windows/window.rs, platform/mod.rs). All 207 Rust tests and 165 frontend tests passed.
✅ Floating ball adsorption threshold and four-sided support 20:25:43.075 | claude_code A conditional adsorption function was added, using a 42px threshold (1.5 times the sphere radius) - only adsorbing when the sphere approached the edge. The FloatBallAnchor was extended to support Top direction (previously only Left/Right/Bottom supported), with all edges using semi-hidden positioning. The choose_float_ball_anchor was modified to return an Option type. ccplan and AskUserQuestion were used to clarify ambiguous requirements, with 9 tests passed.
✅ Floating ball UI optimization: adsorption behavior and panel layout 21:03:52.013 | claude_code The size of the floating balls was adjusted (152×56px panels when expanded), no adsorption during expansion,半球体边缘限制被添加，面板布局简化为仅显示价格、C/X条形的布局，设置菜单的依赖关系被移除。Rust后端（dimensions, restriction logic, 9 tests updated）和Svelte前端都进行了修改。所有测试均通过。
✅ Generating AGENTS.md contributor guide 05:11:47.747 | codex The project structure, build tools, commit history, and CI configuration were analyzed, and then a 400-word AGENTS.md file was generated, covering structure, commands, style, testing, and PR guidelines for open-source contributors.
✅ Documenting communication mechanisms, window visibility, and tray/floating ball architecture 14:09:58.305 | claude_code 3 parallel Explore proxies were deployed to analyze and document the code base structure. It was found that SSH was not used; instead, Tauri IPC (17 commands) and Anthropic API were used for rate limiting. The display/hide mechanism used a pop-up window mode and automatically hid when the window blurred, with platform-specific positioning capabilities. The tray and floating balls were independent UI interfaces sharing AppState but having different interaction models. Comprehensive architecture documentation was provided.
