However, the environment creation process was interrupted, preventing further verification and testing steps. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Post-fusion Harmony improved batch hybrid metrics but reduced the quality of cross-sample niche retrieval Solution: After analyzing the results, it was concluded that Harmony’s linear correction couldn’t handle non-linear dimensional meanings, leading to inconsistencies in the independent training of each part. It is recommended to use Joint STAIG multi-part training in the next iteration.
Key Insight: Batch correction methods like Harmony assume a linear offset, but fail when the independently trained embeddings have inconsistent dimensional semantics—batch mixing and semantic alignment are different attributes. Linear correction of independently trained embeddings can’t maintain semantic correspondence between samples.
2. CalendarPro can only handle a single intent in each message, missing subsequent actions such as “scheduling a meeting at 3 PM and changing dinner time to 7 PM” Solution: Replace the single-intent double-verification process with Claude CLI MultiIntentAnalyzer, which returns a series of intents. Updated _process_message_queue to iterate through intents and combine responses. Removed the 7-tier keyword coverage system.
Key Insight: Single-intent classification is a fundamental architectural limitation, not a parsing issue. Keyword techniques (always generic, context-dependent, 7-tier coverage) are symptoms of an abstract failure. Switching to LLM-native multi-intent processing eliminates the need for all heuristics.
3. MimicGen enhancement failed: calculated pos_delta but never applied to warped actions, resulting in a success rate below 5% Solution: Fixed _replay_with_warping so that it applies pos delta to warped actions[:, :3] before adding noise. Expected success rate: For D0 translation errors, the success rate should be above 50%.
Key Insight: MimicGen enhancement requires actual coordinate transformation, not just noise injection. The path from 10 to 1000 depends on: 10 seeds × 200 targets × 50% success rate = 1000 demonstrations. Without pos delta fix, the success rate will drop significantly.
4. Yuke Zhu’s initial depth-width analysis used a single 5MB prompt (containing 100 papers), which far exceeded the LLM’s processing capacity, resulting in failure Solution: Implemented a three-tier solution: ① Added _MAX_PROMPT_CHARS=150K threshold to automatically trigger segmentation; ② Used a greedy algorithm with year-based grouping and separate splitting for annual papers; ③ Used an LLM to merge 47 segmented analyses into a complete report.
Key Insight: The key to super-large text analysis lies in “divide and conquer + semantic merging”: Technically, it controls the single LLM load through dynamic segmentation, while cognitively guides the LLM to extract consistency across segments, rather than simply combining prompts for simple connection. 150K characters is roughly equivalent to about 15-20 medium-length papers, which is the ideal point in experience.
5. Despite grad_norm=0.7-1.0, task completion model training still stalled with a loss of about 0.035, while the positive sample ratio was 4% and the negative sample ratio was 96% Solution: Applied positive sample weighting (pos_weight=10) to the BCE loss to balance gradient contributions, effectively increasing the positive sample ratio from 4% to about 30%.
Key Insight: In datasets with low imbalance, low loss doesn’t guarantee good positive sample recall. It’s misleading just based on the magnitude of gradients, as negative samples are often learned well. Clear gradient balancing is needed for class imbalance.
6. CalendarPro returned “No events today” immediately after creating an event for queries like “this week” and “tomorrow” Solution: Adopted a three-tier repair approach: (1) Added intent classification to BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT in (2) _handle_query(), to cover LLM failures; (3) Fixed get_today_events to include_past=True to show events that have already started; (4) Added _DPending_warnings for Discord error feedback.
Key Insight: INTENT_PROMPT_FRAGMENTS and BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT serve different code paths—the main LLM classification uses BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT. Keyword fallback is a reliable LLM safety net. Past events must remain visible to prevent re-creating them.
7. The code library contained 8 fully implemented modules (about 2400 lines of code), which never were imported during runtime, causing maintenance burdens Solution: Systematic 4-stage refactoring: (1) Mapping all import statements and identifying isolated modules; (2) Archiving unused modules with a README file attached; (3) Merging duplicate parse_datetime_with_timezone functions into the specification location; (4) Deleting truly useless code. Verified through 373 successful tests.
Key Insight: For full implementations, archiving > deletion approach was used. Isolated but still running code was saved in archives and attached with context to be re-integrated when cleaning up active tree structures. Audit found changes in configuration and import statements errors.
8. Due to always failing checks, 19 breakfast events, 19 lunch events, and 18 dinner events were repeatedly created Solution: Adjusted the priority of the date parsing function from display string (‘08:00’) to the ISO date field. Changed the meal event visibility so that ongoing meals remained visible.
Key Insight: The order of field parsing is crucial—ISO format fields should always be processed first to avoid silent parsing failures. To prevent repeated creation, past events must remain visible.
9. Deadlock occurred in the summarization mechanism: historical log files with finalized=False remained permanently, preventing LLM merge from completing Solution: The root cause was identified: the two-tier finalization mechanism (logs + reports) requires all sources to be finalized before marking the report. The reason for keeping historical logs in a False state was due to: (a) only one export was performed, thus maintaining the False state; (b) changes in the number of dialogues after the first export led to writing False. The finalized flag is embedded in the JSON content and synchronized using rclone. Solution: Re-export the affected devices on these dates—since the date has passed and the number of dialogues is stable, it will be automatically marked as True. Another method is to manually edit the finalized field in the JSON to True, then upload it using rclone.
Key Insight: The two-tier finalization mechanism requires all sources to be finalized before marking the report. The flag in the JSON content synchronized by rclone ensures consistency across devices. However, historical False entries can lead to permanent deadlocks. The key value is to save LLM API resources (merge phase), rather than rclone download time. Re-exporting through the “data repair” method is more in line with the existing architecture than the “logical change” method—since removing all checks like all_sources_finalized would break consistency.**
When using a block size of 60K for the first split, 100 papers were divided into 102 blocks (almost one paper per block), resulting in excessive LLM calls. Single papers lacked context.** Solution: Increase the block size from 60K to 150K, adjust the splitting logic to prioritize year integrity, and ultimately reduce the number of blocks from 102 to 47. This significantly reduces the number of LLM calls while maintaining time context.**
Key Insight: The granularity of splitting needs to be balanced between “LLM capacity limits” and “semantic coherence”: too fine will lead to loss of temporal relationships, while too coarse will cause timeouts. A block size of 150K corresponds roughly to 15-20 medium-length papers, which is the optimal point empirically.**
Confusion over researchers with the same name in academic databases: Semantic Scholar merges multiple researchers with the same name into one entry, leading to misleading trajectory analysis (e.g., Yuke Zhu covers medical imaging, computer vision, and astrophysics—clearly different individuals).** Solution: AI actively identifies confusion signals in analyses: excessive domain spans, fragmented author networks, discrepancies between citation counts and paper numbers. Warnings are marked at the beginning of and it is recommended to split manually or perform analysis at the paper level. For Yuke Zhu, he is distinguished based on institutional affiliation and consistency in research fields: he is a robotics researcher at UT Austin/NVIDIA, a researcher at Mybank Technology, a mathematics researcher at Zhejiang University, and a researcher at Ant Group.**
Key Insight: Automated researcher analysis systems must include multi-dimensional signal detection modules for duplicate researchers: author clustering, topic clustering, temporal continuity, consistency in institutional affiliation. We cannot rely on the identity recognition capabilities provided by the database.**
Since has a default value of True, old-style dialogue logs were incorrectly skipped during export.** Solution: Change line 1726 in daily_summary.py from to and add the –force flag to explicitly control re-export.**
Key Insight: The default values in the data pipeline logic must be consistent with semantics: the export phase should treat missing as incomplete (False), while the merge phase can consider it stable (True).**
Due to LLM timeouts, multiple analyses failed (the initial 300-second timeout was insufficient for a merge of 187K papers; 420 seconds was still not enough).** Solution: A dynamic timeout formula was designed: is used as the base, with an additional 120 seconds added every 50K characters. The final merge step was successfully completed within a timeout of approximately 660 seconds.**
Key Insight: Timeouts should be related linearly to the data size, rather than being fixed values. Large models are relatively fast (about 200-300 characters per second), and it is a reliable strategy to estimate time based on the prompt length plus 50% buffer time.**
LLM-generated researcher information JSON has systematic issues with unescaped citations, resulting in JSON parsing failures (more than 12 errors).** Solution: Identify and manually escape special symbols in fields like trajectory_summary and breakthrough[].description (e.g., “Let the robot…” → “Let the robots…”, single quotes in technical terms); for truncated JSON objects, add missing right parentheses and array end symbols based on context.**
Key Insight: LLM’s handling of long JSON field generation is unstable when it comes to escaping embedded citations. This causes format errors especially in academic texts containing frequently cited terms (e.g., “Image-based Understanding” becomes “Moving While Looking”). Solutions include: (1) explicitly requesting escape special characters in prompts; (2) setting length limits for overly long text fields, or using multi-round splitting generation; (3) adding JSON schema validation steps to automatically fix common format errors after generation.**
LLM-generated long JSON outputs have systematic formatting errors: truncation (especially in final fields), unclosed citations, unescaped Chinese quotes (""), missing closing parentheses in nested structures, especially when containing technical descriptions, Chinese text, and deep nesting (>3 levels).** Solution: A four-stage repair process was implemented: (1) attempting json.loads directly; (2) extracting json code blocks; (3) filling in unclosed structures based on depth of parentheses matching; (4) using the LLM repair mechanism for complex errors, dealing with them in a haiku→sonnet→opus manner. Special JSON repair prompts instruct AI to extract and fix JSON, returning only pure JSON without explanations. All failures are recorded in outputs/logs/json_repair_failed/ for schema analysis and future optimization.**
For high-productivity researchers (130+ papers), using the full corpus for one-time analysis can lead to prompt length errors or insufficient depth of analysis. Analysis of Yuke Zhu’s 206 papers failed due to “prompt过长” errors. Solution: An iterative analysis strategy was adopted, dividing papers by publication year. Each round analyzed 1-3 representative papers, focusing on their breakthroughs (description, why not doing it, impact, technical insights). This kept the depth of analysis while controlling the complexity of a single call. Paper selection: based on year grouping + citation count ranking + conference award recognition (best paper/ highlight/focus/LLM-identified oral report), with no more than 10 papers per year.**
Key Insight: The year-based segmentation strategy is better than analyzing the full corpus at once (prompt过长/analysis insufficient) or sampling randomly (missing temporal evolution). It balances paper quality while maintaining temporal integrity. Compared to a “many but shallow” one-time approach, a “few but deep” iterative model is more suitable for academic analysis.**
Repeating the VLM prefix forward pass is required for head reasoning (once in sample_actions, once in predict_task_completion). Solution: The sample_actions_with_completion() function was created to capture the forward pass from the first VLM and reuse that value for completing the head reasoning, returning a tuple of actions and completion_prob.**
Key Insight: Stream matching has high noise cancellation costs, but unnecessary VLM forward passing increases measurable overhead. The unified method eliminates redundancy with minimal restructuring.**
The SpaceMouse device cannot be opened in physical connection mode because the driver is locked. Solution: The 3Dconnexion driver process was terminated, and the robosuite macros_private.py file was modified to set SPACEMOUSE_PRODUCT_ID to 0xc63a to access the physical device.**
Key Insight: macOS system drivers can exclusively lock HID devices. Using macros_private.py overrides the mode rather than modifying the main configuration file.**
Daily report merge task: How to avoid copying all tasks individually and instead synthesize a comprehensive view. Solution: Specific actions include: (1) merging similar tasks into one entry, keeping the latest status; (2) integrating human and AI analysis of the same topic; (3) combining multiple sessions for the same project into one dialogue summary; (4) retaining tasks with higher importance values during deduplication. This requires the LLM to understand the causal relationships between tasks (e.g., “implementing functionality” → “fixing errors” → “verifying fixes” should be recognized as different stages of the same work thread) and topic similarity.**
Key Insight: Direct connection leads to information redundancy. Synthesis requires identifying causal relationships between tasks (sequential stages versus truly parallel cases) and topic similarity (repeated debugging of the same module should be treated as one task, with different stages), not just string matching.**
Human-AI Approaches Strategic Level Minor default value errors discovered in the data pipeline Role Method Human The user analyzed the _final logic during export/merge phases and identified semantic inconsistencies: the default values for exports should be False (incomplete), while those for merge should be True (stable) AI The AI initially focused on adding the –force flag without questioning the existing defaults. It wasn’t until the user pointed out line 1726 that the AI realized this mistake Difference Analysis: Humans conducted domain reasoning about pipeline semantics, while the AI focused on implementing required functions without reviewing the existing logic.**
Trade-offs in segmentation strategy Role Method Human The user provided high-level requirements (“add splitting and merging”), but did not specify details of the segmentation algorithm or specific block sizes AI The AI chose the “greedy year-based grouping + individual paper splitting” algorithm. The initial block size was 60K (too small), later adjusted to 150K (empirical value); the AI independently decided to use LLM instead of simple string concatenation for merging Difference Analysis: The user provided high-level requirements, and the AI took care of choosing algorithms and parameter adjustments. In the initial implementation of the AI, the block size was too conservative, resulting in 102 blocks. After user intervention (indirectly indicated by timeout failures), the AI adjusted it to 150K.**
Dimension mismatch identified in post-merging batch correction Role Method Human The user accepted Claude’s analysis and believed that Harmony improved batch mixing but reduced semantic consistency, agreeing with the recommendation for Joint STAIG training AI The AI systematically compared batch metrics and cross-sample query metrics, identifying contradictory improvements/degradation, and provided a mechanistic explanation (for non-linear dimension mismatches, linear correction is ineffective) Difference Analysis: In this case, the AI provided deeper analysis than humans explicitly requested—humans trusted and agreed with the explanations for next steps. The AI’s systematic metric decomposition revealed hidden trade-offs.**
Architectural decision-making for multi-intentional analyzer Role Method Human Humans immediately recognized the limitations of a single intent as the root cause and proposed a Claude CLI solution, bypassing the incremental improvement to the existing double-validation system AI The AI first explored the existing architecture before proposing solutions. Human intuition was correct—the complexity of double-validation did not solve the fundamental multi-intentional problem Code library audit delegation vs. direct execution Role Method Human Humans refused to let the AI attempt to delegate the audit task to 3 parallel exploration agents and insisted on using read/grep/glob tools for direct execution AI The AI initially tried to parallelize the audit by creating 3 agents (src/audit, tests/audit, duplication finder). Humans stopped calling all three agents Difference Analysis: Humans realized that over- delegation would lead to indirectness and loss of the “overview” needed to make architectural judgments. Using tools directly allowed keeping findings within the main context, enabling coherent reports. This is a lesson on when not to use agents.**
Training and testing error scenario generation strategy | Role | Method | |——|——| | Human | The user recognized the need to separate training and testing scenarios into different directories and required a uniform distribution of difficulty levels (not random) to control the progression of difficulty | | AI | The AI initially focused on using random scenarios from the same directory to replicate the existing process. Later, it had to switch to stratified sampling and control the random number generation |
Difference Analysis: Humans understood the rigor of experiments required splitting training/test data and controlling variables. The AI initially overlooked issues related to data contamination and experimental design.**
Academic trajectory analysis framework: “Why now is feasible” as core insight Role Method Human The user designed a comprehensive analysis framework, including trajectory summaries, breakthroughs (which must include the field of “why now is feasible”), research topics, methodological evolution, and contributions to the field. A requirement for “why now is feasible” analysis was explicitly requested, rather than just technical descriptions AI The AI initially tended to describe technical solutions without delving into historical conditions. It required user-defined pattern constraints to enforce structured “why not now is feasible” analysis Difference Analysis: Humans understood the core value of academic analysis in making timely judgments about “why now is feasible,” rather than just providing pure technical summaries. The AI defaults to using technical overviews and lacks historical insights, requiring structured prompts for guidance.**
Determining error repair strategy: Data repair or logical change Role Method Human The user identified the problem as a permanent historical false flag event and advocated repairing it by re-running the export on affected devices, rather than modifying the finalization logic of merge. This prevented the AI from attempting to alter the logic AI The AI initially proposed modifying the finalization logic in the merge phase (removing all_sources_finalized checks) and tried to find the root cause Difference Analysis: Human method: “Repairing data” (re-exporting with the correct flag that matches the existing architecture). AI method: “Changing logic” (modifying the code so that merging no longer depends on the finalization source log state). Humans prevented the AI from modifying and explicitly stated that one export run was sufficient to fix the problem.**
Researchers’ analysis iteration strategy: Multi-angle sampling to verify robustness Role Method Human The user chose to analyze multiple times the same researcher (Yuke Zhu), using subsets of papers from different years (2022/2023/2025/2026) to implicitly test whether the tool could infer overall research directions from partial information. AI The AI independently generated trajectory summaries and breakthroughs for each analysis, lacking consistency checks across analyses. It failed to explicitly express inheritance relationships between papers from different periods.** Difference Analysis: Humans tested the robustness of the tool by sampling multiple times; while the AI treated each analysis as an independent task, without actively establishing causal chains over time.**- The resource-intensive Explore agent was launched into an empty directory without checking whether exploration was needed, resulting in a waste of time until the user interrupted it.
When the –force flag was added, the logic between the export and merge stages was not audited properly; users were required to notice the semantic inconsistencies. Although the task requires overall architecture judgment, the code library audit was overly entrusted to 3 parallel agents. Users correctly recognized that this would undermine understanding ability, so they stopped entrusting it. The risk of data contamination was not identified: Initially, it was recommended to generate scenarios without training/test splits until users explicitly requested separate directories. AI attempted 5 times during the analysis of Yuuke Zhu’s data, adjusting parameters (block size, timeout formula) each time, but failed to predict the best parameters through a single analysis or reasoning, which significantly prolonged the total time (about 2-3 hours). Due to the limit on prompt length, it was unable to handle researcher profiles with complete publication history (more than 200 papers). The analysis of 206 papers failed due to the “prompt too long” error. There was a lack of built-in sampling or hierarchical analysis strategies for large corpora. Initially, researchers with the same name could not be independently detected solely based on paper metadata. Users were required to explicitly mark them to distinguish different researchers with the same name. After the first correction, it was improved to actively flag inconsistencies between institutions/fields. In the daily report merging task, initially, it was preferred to list all details rather than actively synthesize them. It took users to repeatedly emphasize “focus on synthesis and refinement, not word-for-word copying of entries” in order to trigger the integration logic, indicating a lack of an inherent awareness of “readability” and “information density” balance. When analyzing multiple papers by the same researcher, it was unable to identify the temporal evolution relationship between papers from different years. Each analysis was conducted independently, without a dimension of time-causal inference. When analyzing academic papers, it tended to describe “what was done” and “impact,” but users needed explicit prompts to delve into the historical dimension of “why it was not feasible before.” By default, only technical summaries were provided, without historical insights. Initially, it was impossible to detect name conflicts in researcher analysis without an explicit prompt. Only after users marked field inconsistencies was a unified trajectory of papers from different researchers generated. The initial renaming plan only covered the display name/code reference, lacking the infrastructure layer (conda environment, folder, repository, systemd). Manual corrections were needed to expand the scope. Performance efficiency was rationalized: Duplicate VLMs were accepted as an “acceptable overhead” until users questioned redundancy. Query intent classification was added to INTENT_PROMPT_FRAGMENTS (used only by multi_intent.py), rather than BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT (used by the main classification process), but the code path differences were not understood. When dealing with overly long text fields, there was a risk of truncation, resulting in incomplete JSON objects (field_impact and other fields being truncated in the middle). There was a lack of active output length monitoring and warning mechanisms. AI initially chose a block size of 60K (too conservative, leading to 102 blocks), and failed to recognize the implicit “reasonable number of blocks” constraint during a single attempt. It needed to be adjusted to 150K after timeout failure feedback. There was a lack of prior reasoning about “users expecting a reasonable number of blocks (10-50).” In the search module usage pattern, initially, relative imports (from ..browser) were not detected, and browser/ was mistakenly thought to be dead code. The error was only discovered by directly inspecting executor.py. It was impossible to autonomously capture the user’s implicit intent. Multiple analyses of the same researcher’s subsets over different years actually tested the tool’s paper selection ability, but AI did not realize this verification purpose. AI’s batch processing of gadgets/summarize tools caused 4-5 dates to fail due to API 32000 token output limits, but AI did not actively detect output length limits or propose a batch processing solution. Instead, it passively accepted the failure results. It was impossible to actively identify errors in the self-generated JSON format. Manual external verification (such as a JSON parser) was needed to detect problems and provide feedback. There was a lack of a global progress tracking function for batch processing—it was impossible to actively inform users of “more N days of historical data processing” or the current completion percentage. Users had to manually track the task progress. The originally designed daily report tool was a single-layer end-to-end generation, lacking the need for hierarchical aggregation (conversation → device summary → daily report). This was corrected through a recursive architecture by users. General Limitations There was a misunderstanding of domain terminology: “by title” and “by summary” were assumed to be selection logic based on LLM, while the user’s actual intention was “only summarize titles,” not “summarize summaries.” Clear clarification was needed. When dealing with large amounts of historical daily report data, it was impossible to effectively track the list of processed data and pending tasks. Users needed to provide a clear task checklist and a mechanism for verification. Initially, only the ERROR logs from calendar API failures were added, rather than user-readable Discord message outputs. Users needed explicit instructions to understand the product requirements for error feedback in chat. Lessons Learned Key Lessons - Batch correction methods like Harmony can achieve global distribution mixing, but they cannot maintain semantic consistency when independent training produces embeddings with inconsistent dimensionality meanings. This requires shared training (Joint STAIG) or a base model. Batch mixing and semantic consistency are independent attributes. The enhancement in MimicGen style is essentially about coordinate transformation, not noise injection. It is necessary to apply pos_delta to the action trajectory (the offset between the source object and the target object). Without this, the enhancement from 10 to 1000 fails because the success rate is less than 5%. The engineering paradigm for ultra-large text analysis: ① Automatic segmentation (150K characters, about the optimal granularity for 15-20 papers); ② Semantic merging (merging driven by LLM instead of splicing); ③ Dynamic timeout mechanism (linear scaling with prompt length, baseline is 300 seconds, extended to 120 seconds at 50K characters); ④ Caching mechanism (to avoid retry costs). These four are key for the availability of deep-wide patterns. The multi-angle verification method for academic trajectory analysis: By iterating analyzing subsets of different papers by the same researcher (from 1 paper to 100 papers), a three-dimensional cognition can be constructed from the micro level (in-depth analysis of a single representative work) to the macro level (descriptions of complete publication list trajectories). Zhu Yujie’s case shows that there is a clear evolution trajectory from visual understanding → embodied intelligence → robot learning ecosystem construction → LLM-driven embodied intelligence → geometric intermediate representation and VLM alignment. This can only be fully presented when having a complete paper list. Zhu Yujie’s research trajectory shows a clear process of “perception → manipulation → infrastructure → open world”: From visual reinforcement learning at the beginning of 2012 to the DreamDojo world model in 2026, it always revolves around the core proposition of “enabling robots to efficiently learn from data and generalize to the real world.” This represents a development process from “data infrastructure builder” to “base model pioneer” to “world model architect.” A single intent classification is an architectural constraint that cannot be solved through keyword techniques. CalendarPro’s 7-layer keyword coverage system is just dealing with symptoms of abstract destruction. Multi-intent LLM analysis eliminates the need for all heuristics. In binary classification, explicit gradient balancing is required when there is category imbalance. When 96% of the samples belong to one category, low overall loss may mask the learning deficiency of minority categories. pos_weight can effectively rebalance the gradient contributions. Key dimensions in analyzing patterns: ① Data scale (paper/year); ② Analysis depth (summary vs full text); ③ Computing cost (number of LLM calls). The five-mode system combines two boolean dimensions and one numerical dimension, covering a complete range from “quick overview” to “in-depth analysis.” The basic elements of structured data generation prompt engineering: For fixed pattern JSON outputs, it is necessary to explicitly state all required fields in the prompt, their types (string/array/object), length limits, default values (placeholder text when there is no data), and special character escape rules. The daily_overview layered format of the gadget summary tool proves that relying solely on example reasoning cannot guarantee output consistency. Enumeration specifications must be used to clarify the generation logic for each field. The path of research infrastructure construction in the field of robot learning: Zhu Yujie’s academic trajectory reveals an important pattern - breakthroughs in the field often come with work on infrastructure classes (AI2-THOR simulation environment, robosuite standard benchmark tests, RoboTurk crowdsourcing platform, MineDojo Internet-scale datasets). Although the number of citations for these works does not exceed those of algorithm innovation papers, their actual impact on the development of the entire field may be greater. For any emerging research direction, establishing replicable evaluation benchmarks and data collection toolsets is more strategically valuable than publishing a single algorithm paper. The two-level finalization mechanism (log + report) requires all source logs to be finalized before marking the report completion; the _finalized flag in the JSON content is synchronized through rclone to ensure consistency across devices. However, historical error entries can lead to permanent deadlocks. The core value is saving LLM API costs (during the merging stage), not the rclone download time. Fixing solution: Re-executing the operation that does not require modification to automatically correct historical metadata, respecting architectural design. Batch iterative workflows (processing daily reports day by day and analyzing academic data year by year) are more reliable than one-time batch processing. This avoids problems such as JSON truncation and insufficient analysis depth for gadgets tools. For gadget tools, adding an automatic retry mechanism and coordinating with JSON verification is very important. The problem of ambiguity of the same name in academic databases is a high-frequency and significant data quality issue in automated researcher profiling. It is necessary to establish a module for ambiguity detection based on multi-dimensional signals: co-author clustering, topic clustering, time continuity, consistency of institutional affiliation. In the machine learning pipeline, the separation of training/ evaluation data is non-negotiable. The evaluation set must never be leaked into the training process. Different data purposes require different generation quotas and strategies. Even if it has been deprecated, archive files should be deleted. The archive/ pattern with a README note retains optionality (for future re-integration), while also cleaning up active tree structures. In the data processing pipeline, default values must match the semantics of each stage: export (missing = incomplete → False) and merge (missing = stable → True). Minor inconsistencies can lead to silent errors. The order of parsing date-time fields is very important - always prioritize ISO format fields (start_iso) rather than display format strings (start_time ‘08:00’), to avoid implicit parsing errors that could break existence checks. The daily report generation tool for gadget projects demonstrates strong information extraction and structuring capabilities in complex multi-device, multi-project, cross-time scenarios. It can accurately distinguish between parallel work across devices, identify task causality, synthesize dialogue summaries, and verify production readiness. The research analyzer is always able to recognize Zhu Yujie’s core research directions and the methodological evolution in subsets of papers from different years, proving the tool’s academic trajectory extraction ability. Voyager (2023) demonstrated the paradigm of LLM as an autonomous agent: In Minecraft, the number of unique items increased by 3.3 times, and the speed of unlocking technical trees increased by 15.3 times. The stored skills exist in the form of executable JS code, rather than neural weights, avoiding catastrophic forgetting. Open X-embodyment (2023): 22 robots, 21 institutions, and 160K tasks demonstrated a positive transfer across embodyments, establishing an ImageNet-moment for robot data integration. The information density levels of researcher personal profiles: First level = h-index/citation count (quantitative indicators); second level = list of representative works (enumerateable facts); third level = narrative of research trajectories (requiring LLM to extract implicit evolution logic). These three levels correspond to output complexities from fast/normal/deep modes. The fault tolerance of LLM long-text JSON outputs requires a multi-level mechanism: direct parsing → code block extraction → bracket matching → LLM repair (model upgrade). A single strategy cannot cover all failure modes. All failures should be recorded for manual review and subsequent optimization. The framework for analyzing characteristics of breakthrough papers (why_not_before + impact + technical_insight) can effectively reveal the historical inevitability of technological innovation, domain influence, and core insights, which is more valuable than simple abstract summaries. Researchers’ paper selection strategies should balance temporal completeness with quality. A three-tier mechanism based on year grouping + citation count ranking + conference awards recognized by LLM can balance basic early work and latest frontier breakthroughs at a controllable computing cost. Recognition of errors in the JSON system generated by LLMs: Through 12 or more JSON repair tasks, it was found that errors not escaping quotes mainly concentrated in: (1) Citations of academic terms (e.g., “visual understanding”); (2) Mixed use of Chinese and English quotation marks; (3) Special symbols in technical terms (e.g., + in C++, mathematical symbols like σ_w²). The solution requires multi-layer protection: prompts explicitly requiring escape rules + output pattern verification + regular expressions to automatically repair common patterns. A strategy of analyzing by year is better than processing all corpus at once (too long prompt/ shallow analysis) or随机抽样 (missing temporal evolution). While maintaining the integrity of time dimensions, it takes into account paper quality. The “few but deep” iterative model is more suitable for academic feature analysis than the “many but shallow” one-time model. When implementing a scheduler for repetitive events, it must include the events that have already been started in the existence check (include_past=True). Otherwise, events from the past will become invisible and be recreated in each cycle. In a multi-component system, understand the actual usage location of the prompt. INTENT_PROMPT_FRAGMENTS and BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT serve different code paths - the main LLM classification uses BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT, while multi_intent.py uses INTENT_PROMPT_FRAGMENTS. The work of filling in historical daily reports is large but valuable: More than 30 days of history records cover multiple projects on 4 devices, involving dozens of technical decisions and hundreds of task entries. Establishing a complete work record system is crucial for subsequent monthly reports and project reviews. Code refactoring backward compatibility strategy: The default value changes from fast to normal, but the dict still reverts to fast (protecting old profile data); list selection expansion does not destroy old configuration files; re-exporting interfaces allows relevant parties to migrate without attracting attention. This reflects that “gradual evolution” is better than “destructive rewriting.” Experience in developing long-term tasks optimizes: ① Caching mechanisms make retry costs controllable (47 parts fully cached, just re-running the merge); ② Progress observation (tqdm+PYTHONUNBUFFERED); ③ Timeout formula adaptation (avoiding manual parameter adjustment); ④ Parallel Agents handle independent sub-tasks (document updates). The gadget/ research toolkit consists of three independent tools (paper discovery, researcher feature analysis, citation graph analysis), which are run under the research_scout.py CLI. Researcher feature analysis uses citation count + award weight to select papers, rather than LLM scoring. When refactoring the pattern system, retain the existing sorting logic and add new dimensions (quantity, processing depth) as independent parameters. This can minimize risks and maintain predictable behavior. Systematic import analysis (grep for patterns + verifying the export content of init.py) is essential before declaring a module “dead.” Relative imports (..module) require separate search patterns, while absolute imports (src.module) require different search patterns. The keyword fallback mechanism is a reliable LLM safety net - even if the LLM cannot return structured data, it can correctly route through detecting keywords in the original input. Project renaming requires a two-tier approach: (1) Code/Documentation (automated by search replacement), (2) Infrastructure (manual: conda, git, hosting services). Checklists should be included on both levels to avoid partial migration. The “concentrated” daily report synthesis requires the LLM to understand the causal relationship and inclusion relationships of tasks. Identifying “implementing functions” → “fixing errors” → “verifying fixes” as different stages of the same work thread, rather than three separate tasks. This understanding of semantic relationships is key to report readability. pip install -e . (editable mode) is the correct way to import shared utility packages in multi-tool projects, rather than operating through PYTHONPATH. For Python with a state system, hot reloading is almost impossible (with Discord bots with event loops, singleton caches, background schedulers). Process-level restarts via watchfile are a feasible solution. Practical Learning robosuite provides official utility tools (input2action, inputUtils) that can correctly handle the conversion from devices to actions. Manual parsing of action_dict will lose the coordinate transformation and controller scaling. Testing infrastructure hardening (pytest-timeout, @pytest.mark.external, skipping logic) can prevent a type of test-blocking error from blocking the entire suite. This model can be reused in multiple projects. Using max_per_year=0 as a “infinite papers” sentinel value is clearer than using a boolean flag or None, because zero is never an effective paper limit case. The Git rebase workflow (stash → pull –rebase → stash pop) can cleanly integrate分歧分支和独立变化，避免合并提交混乱 Dialogue Summary MIHD Space Transcriptomics ✅ Post-fusion Harmony experiments, strategic planning for ARI improvement and batch effect reduction, Claude code demonstration preparation 02:04:13.480 | claude_code The benchmark_rm_ideal.py was modified to add the –harmony flag for batch correction between embedding loading and cross-sample evaluation during post-fusion. An experiment comparing sections 151508-151673 for Harmony effects was run. The main finding is that Harmony improved the batch mixing metrics (ASW-batch +36%, graph_connectivity +32%), but reduced the semantic alignment effect (Spearman r 0.189→0.023). It is concluded that Harmony’s linear correction cannot handle non-linear dimensionality mismatches caused by independent training of each part. It is recommended to perform Joint STAIG multi-part training next. Multi-agent exploration was initiated to diagnose the ARI gap (0.475 vs SOTA 0.66%) and cross-sample batch effects, and a two-track approach was decided. A comprehensive 30-minute Claude code demonstration was prepared for the lab meeting on Friday, covering the planning pattern, permissions, CLAUDE.md rules, and AI overconfidence examples using DLPFC space data. Complete demonstration materials and presentation scripts were delivered.
Error Recovery (Algorithms and Benchmarks) ✅ Environment setup, task completion model training optimization, comprehensive recovery collection reconstruction, training scenario generation, MimicGen deformation error fixing04:16:46.552 | claude_code The mujoco/robosuite stack was used to set up a recovery_collect conda environment. The error in EnvWrapper.env was fixed. The locking issue of the SpaceMouse device was resolved by disabling the 3Dconnexion driver and configuring product_id=0xc63a. The device was successfully connected, but only 11 out of 29 types of errors could generate scenarios. A training stagnation phenomenon was identified (loss of approximately 0.035, accounting for 4% of positive samples). A weighted BCE loss with pos_weight=10 was implemented to balance gradients. The predict_task_completion inference method was added, and it was optimized by creating sample_actions_with_completion to reuse the VLM prefix features and avoid redundant forward passes. Major refactoring: The SpaceMouse initialization issue was fixed (removed env parameter, used input2action), rendering support was added, and a dynamic PROJECT_ROOT resolution setting was implemented. A portable deployment package was created (compressed from 171MB to 252MB), including setup/shell scripts and tutorials. All 139 unit tests passed. The generate_training_scenes.py function was implemented, capable of generating exactly 10 scenarios per task×subtype, with values evenly distributed between 0.0 and 1.0. The ControlledRng class was created to make the uniform() function deterministic (based on fractional interpolation), while retaining the randn() function to provide direction diversity. In-depth technical discussions clarified the data collection architecture: collecting 5-10 examples/subtypes, without any failed examples, starting with 1-2 tasks. It was found that RecoverySegmenter is suitable for Phoenix MCM/MPM (not MimicGen). Key errors were identified: pos_delta was calculated in _replay_with_warping but not applied. The deformation issue was fixed, making 1000 enhanced examples effective with 10 seeds×200 targets×50% success rate. It was clarified that 3 data pools were needed: training data (10), enhancement data (200), evaluation data (100+).
CalendarPro/Life Copilot ✅ Complete system refactoring: multi-intent classifier, calendar query fixes, code library audit and second-phase cleanup, duplicate meal error fix, project renaming, development mode implementation, test suite optimization 00:53:36.473 | claude_code The complete refactoring of the intent classification system was completed. The Claude CLI MultiIntentAnalyzer replaced the dual-validation+7-layer keyword approach, supporting multi-intent decomposition. The MultiIntentAnalyzer class was created, with a 4-stage JSON parsing function. The MultiIntentResult/AnalysisContext model was added, and the always_general_keywords and context_dependent_keywords collections were removed. The process_input was simplified from 7 steps to 4 steps. CLI errors were fixed. Weekly query support was added. 30 new tests were written, and all 424 tests passed. Users pointed out fundamental limitations (dual-validation only returns one intent, complex messages may lose subsequent actions). The existing architecture was studied, and a 7-layer keyword approach was identified, followed by a two-tier solution design. A comprehensive implementation plan was developed. dev.py was implemented, using watchfiles to monitor src/*.py files, and multiprocessing.spawn for clean process isolation. The –dev flag was added. Debounce, grace period, and ignore filters were configured. pytest tests hung indefinitely. Thepytest-timeout dependency was added, with a global timeout of 30 seconds. External service tests were marked as @pytest.mark.external and skipif was enabled for automatic detection. The Typer CLI functions were renamed to avoid collection issues. Now all 399 tests pass within 40 seconds. Using grep/glob/read tools, a systematic mapping of 29K LOC in over 90 files was performed (executed directly after the user rejected proxy calls). 8 isolated modules were identified (approximately 2400 LOC), a duplicate parse_datetime_with_timezone function, an expired broker_models.py file, 3 isolated configuration settings, and corrupted browser service imports. A detailed stage-by-stage refactoring plan was generated. All 4 stages were implemented: 8 source modules+9 test files were archived, check_skills_status.py was created with a README description; duplicate parse_datetime_with_timezone was merged; broker_models.py and 3 isolated configuration settings were removed; corrupted browser import issues were fixed. Passed verification with 373 tests. CLAUDE.md was updated. Users reported 19 duplicate breakfast events, 19 lunch events, and 18 dinner events. The root cause was identified: _check_meal_exists failed due to isoformat when using display-format time strings, while schedule_essentials excluded past activities. Two errors were fixed, a cleanup script was created, 53 duplicates were removed, and the test verification passed. Users requested a renaming plan, which was then interrupted to expand the scope to include conda environments, folders, repositories, and Discord applications. The code library was re-studied, with approximately 62 changes made to 15 files (code, documentation, scripts, configuration), providing a manual checklist of 6 items. The user executed/init triggered CLAUDE.md improvements. 4 new architecture modules were added, and many details were corrected. Subsequently, users reported calendar query errors (queries for this week/week next week always returned “no activity”). A three-tier solution was implemented to fix the issue: query classification guidance was added in BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT, a keyword fallback mechanism was added in _handle_query(), the include_past parameter was added in get_today_events(), and a Discord error feedback mechanism was added. All tests passed, and both documentation and functionality were completed.
gadget (Summary + Research Tool) ✅ Comprehensive knowledge management infrastructure expansion: The research tool system for 5 modes was upgraded, block-based long-text analysis capabilities were implemented; a profiling of Yuke Zhu’s complete academic trajectory was performed (more than 10 iterations, over 100 publications); historical daily reports were batch-integrated covering more than 30 dates; a mechanism failure diagnosis plan was finalized, and the JSON repair process was improved. 04:10:40.556 | claude_code
Users encountered a ‘ModuleNotFoundError: common’ error when running the daily_summary.py export command. After diagnosis, it was found that a certain package was missing, so the package was installed in editable mode using the ‘pip install -e .’ command. It was successfully possible to export the date information for 23 conversation logs. Users requested forced re-export of all date information. It was discovered that the finalized=True parameter was used during the export stage (line 1726), causing old logs to be incorrectly skipped. The default setting for exports was changed to False, and the –force flag was added for explicit control. For 17 dates, forced exports were performed, and all data was successfully uploaded to rclone. The gadget repository was synchronized using git stash, rebase, and push workflows. The daily_summary.py tool from gadget/summarize was used to process a large batch of historical conversation logs from February to mid-March 2026. It covered work records for multiple devices (DCC/MacBook/TzJsDesktop/tianhe) and multiple projects (MIHD, error-recovery-benchmark, CalendarPro, Desktop Video, gadget). Each generated report included a structured daily_overview (global layer and device layer), tasks, problems and solutions, comparison of humans and AI, limitations of AI, learning outcomes, and conversation summaries. 12 JSON format errors were identified, requiring specialized fixes. A critical deadlock issue was diagnosed in the finalize mechanism: it was found that 3 device-date combinations caused permanent finalized=False situations during report finalization, leading to an infinite loop in the LLM API usage cost. The solution was to re-run the export operation or manually edit the JSON file. The (finalized) suffix was added to the export output to improve diagnostic efficiency. Users provided a detailed implementation plan, which required expanding the mode from 2 modes (fast/verbose) to 5 modes (fast/normal-depth/extensive-depth/extensive-extensive), and adding automatic segmentation and merging functions for extensive and extensive-extensive modes. AI modified 7 source files (analysis.py added MODE_CONFIG and block logic, prompt.py added TRAJECTORY_MERGE_PROMPT, cli.py/research_scout.py/config.py/models.py updated options) + 3 documentation files, implemented a dynamic timeout formula (300 seconds + 120 seconds/50K characters), and a segmentation algorithm with 150K characters/block. Yuke Zhu’s in-depth extensive analysis was successfully completed: 100 full-text papers were retrieved from ArXiv, automatically segmented into 47 time blocks, LLM analysis was performed on each time block, and finally, a complete academic trajectory report was generated (including 7 breakthroughs, research topic evolution, guiding relationships, etc.). 5 iterations of optimization were performed, increasing the block size from 60K to 150K, optimizing the timeout formula, and ultimately generating a complete academic profile report. A detailed academic analysis of Yuke Zhu was conducted, with over 10 iterations of year-by-year analysis, analyzing 18 to 100 representative papers, covering Visual Genome (2016) → robomimic/MAPLE (2021) → CDL/MineDojo/VIMA (2022) → Voyager/S3O/LIBERO (2023) → DexMimicGen/Sirius-Fleet/PRESTO/LongVILA (2024) → DEAS/Cosmos-Predict2.5/QLIP/ASAP/GraspMolmo/DreamDojo (2025-2026). Each analysis generated a detailed trajectory summary, including reasons why not earlier than before, impacts, technical insights, etc., as well as research topics, methodology evolution, and domain impact. The evolution process from “data infrastructure builder” (robosuite/ROBOMIMIC) to “basic model pioneer” (MineDojo/VIMA/Voyager), and then to “world model architect” (Cosmos/DreamDojo) was identified. Researchers with the same name were actively detected and marked. 9 JSON repair cycles were processed, resolving issues with truncated or formatted incorrect outputs. Attempts to analyze all 206 papers failed due to an error related to “too long prompt”, which confirmed the necessity of using a year-by-year sampling strategy. The user ran/init command to verify the accuracy of research/CLAUDE.md. The assistant identified and fixed two minor errors. Subsequently, the user proposed expanding the mode for researcher analysis from 2 modes to 5 modes, setting different paper limits and processing depths. After clarifying that paper selection should maintain citation and reward-based methods, the assistant developed an implementation plan including analysis.py, cli.py, and prompt.py modifications. Approximately 16 historical dates were processed, generating structured daily reports from February 8, 2026, to March 17, 2026. Issues with truncated, unclosed quotation marks, unescaped Chinese quotation marks, and missing closing parentheses in the format-corrupted JSON files generated by the LLM were fixed. A four-stage repair process was applied. research/CLAUDE.md was updated (6 modifications), research/TUTORIAL.md was updated (4 modifications), and the parent CLAUDE.md with parallel proxy processing was updated, ensuring that descriptions of all modes, example commands, and parameter tables matched the code.

summary: We have conducted extensive research and development across various devices. Space transcriptomics alignment experiments revealed the trade-offs involved in semantic batch mixing. The robot error recovery process, from training optimization to portable deployment, has been validated through 139 tests. CalendarPro/Life Copilot achieves complete architectural restructuring, eliminating bottlenecks associated with single-intent classifiers while supporting the classification of multiple intentions. Additionally, we have expanded our comprehensive knowledge management infrastructure, including improved tools for analyzing long-text data. Yuke Zhu completed a comprehensive analysis of his academic career from 2012 to 2026, publishing over 100 papers and undergoing more than ten iterative analyses. Finally, we performed batch processing on the historical daily reports for the 30 days between February and March 2026.

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-18

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-17 Daily Overview Completed Work: Completed large-scale MIHD refactoring (9 optimizations, including improvements from O(N²) to O(Nlogk), achieved comprehensive understanding of QCVLA, and proposed 11 improvement measures through cross-project analysis (FLARE + 9 VLA papers). Fixed critical VLA inference errors, separated loss monitoring functions, generated 2920 error benchmark scenarios, optimized data conversion processes, configured remote development environments, and enhanced GPU monitoring tools. Implementation Methods: Conducted parallel proxy-based code reviews of MIHD, iterative Q&A and literature research on QCVLA, systematic debugging and architecture refactoring of VLA systems, executed multi-task error injection processes, implemented non-blocking I/O in interactive monitoring, and automated server settings through SSH. Impact: MIHD: Eliminated code duplication and potential errors, prevented OOM issues with HD data. QCVLA: Identified P0-level fast improvement measures, proposed perturbation and bridging enhancement methods to break Dagger saturation. VLA: Stabilized the inference system, implemented independent action_loss/task_loss tracking. 90% of error benchmark testing was completed (D0 samples needed to be supplemented). GPU monitoring functions were significantly improved. The remote server is ready for distributed development. DCC Completed Work: Conducted comprehensive code reviews and refactoring of MIHD multimodal integration code libraries, involving 21 modified Python files, focusing on eliminating redundancy and performance bottlenecks. Implementation Methods: Simplified the workflow through three parallel proxy reviews (code reuse, quality, efficiency), implemented 9 targeted fixes, including centralizing NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS constants, improving device resolution, replacing O(N²) cdist algorithm with cKDTree, removing legacy packaging layers, and reusing DataPreparer instances. Impact: Eliminated 8 duplicate device resolution checks, fixed the issue of missing adaln_attention in spatial coordinate fusion lists (potential error), prevented OOM issues with 17K+ points of high-definition data through algorithm optimization, and improved maintainability by centralizing constants. MacBook Completed Work: Conducted in-depth research on QCVLA’s potential bridging architecture, proposed cross-project improvements through 9 papers, FLARE integration analysis, and remote server environment configuration. Implementation Methods: Created CLAUDE.md documentation, understood the 7-step process and issues through iterative Q&A, searched for literature on VLA efficiency/robustness (such as VLA-Cache, AC²-VLA, Spec-VLA, KerV, etc.), extracted transferable techniques from FLARE papers (perturbation enhancement, hard fault mining, phase-aware scheduling, visual-based bridging, modular LoRA), and automated athena.egr.duke.edu setup through SSH + Node.js + Claude/Codex installation. Impact: Fully understood the bridging mechanism (calculation savings of 57.6%, performance decrease of 4%). Identified P0-level fast improvement measures (making existing Action-Aware Channel Importance Loss possible) and valuable P1-level improvements (for adaptive scheduling Bridge Confidence Head). Proposed perturbation and bridging methods inspired by FLARE to solve Dagger saturation problems (+1pp platform effect). The server is ready for distributed development. tianhe Completed Work: Fixed critical errors in VLA inference, implemented separation of training losses, completed generation of error benchmark data (2920 scenarios), optimized CALVIN dataset conversion, integrated LIBERO custom test suite, resolved GPU monitoring display issues, diagnosed RoboCasa MimicGen download failures, and fixed git proxy and wandb configuration problems. Implementation Methods: Code reviews identified and fixed 4 inference errors (no check order, prompt reset, log threshold, state update); modified compute_loss to return a tuple with has_aux=True (loss_array, aux_dict); executed 6 task error injection processes (taking 3.5 hours); corrected the key mapping from RLDS to LeRobot, added progress bars; created LIBERO_OBJECT_COM classes and BDDL files; implemented ENV+FD verification and parent chain deduplication in gpumon.py; added non-blocking keyboard navigation functions (arrow keys, command scrolling); tracked RoboCasa errors to box_links_ds.json through the registry; tested proxies and updated local git configuration; provided a wandb API key environment variable solution. Impact: VLA inference system stabilized (eliminated crashes and infinite loops), training process now monitors action_loss/task_loss separately in wandb, 90% of error benchmark datasets was completed (2920 scenarios generated, some error types required D0 samples to be supplemented), dataset conversion processes were robust and user-friendly, LIBERO test framework was extended, GPU monitoring tools reduced the number of displayed processes from 35 to 8 unique ones, with interactive functions similar to nvitop, RoboCasa analysis identified upstream data publishing issues (no MimicGen links in box_links_ds.json), git and wandb configuration problems were resolved. Cross-device collaboration on MIHD spatio-metrics refactoring, in-depth research on QCVLA’s potential bridging architecture, improvement measures inspired by FLARE, fixes to VLA training/inference infrastructure, generation of error benchmark data, and enhancement of system tools
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ QCVLA project documentation creation and improvement plan — Created initial CLAUDE.md files through.init, covering the 7-step process, SingleStepDiT architecture, and key issues. Generated improve_analysis.md file, which includes 11 suggestions from FLARE and content from 9 papers. Documented perturbation and bridging data augmentation strategies. Analyzed FLARE papers and extracted transferable techniques (perturbation enhancement, hard fault mining, phase-aware scheduling, visual condition bridging, modular LoRA). ✅ VLA inference system error fixing — Identified and fixed 4 critical errors in vla_infer.py: None check order errors could lead to crashes, prompt not resetting after task completion caused infinite loops, inconsistent debug log thresholds (no exceptions in blocks 3/4), task completion status not updated to idle. ✅ MIHD code library refactoring and optimization — Performed /simplify operations on 21 modified Python files, applied 9 key fixes: centralized management of NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS (fixed missing adaln_attention), improved device resolution (removed 8 duplicates), replaced O(N²) cdist algorithm with cKDTree to prevent HD OOM issues, removed unused import statements and outdated packaging functions, reused DataPreparer instances. Updated CLAUDE.md file to reflect architectural changes. ✅ Training loss monitoring separation — Modified BaseModel compute_loss function to return tuple [loss_array, aux_dict], implemented independent action_loss and task_loss return values in pi0/pi0_fast models, updated training scripts to use has_aux=True for unpacking and logging to wandb. ✅ Error recovery benchmark data generation — Completed 6 task error injection processes (pick_place, coffee, stack, stack_three, threading, three_piece_assembly), generating 2920 error scenarios. Some D0 samples had insufficient quantities (<10), requiring reruns; the threading task only had colision_empty injections because all phase_labels were pre_reach. 🔄 Error benchmark D0 sample supplementation — Some D0-level error type samples had fewer than 10 (e.g., collision_self: 2, drop_at_wrong_place: 8), requiring rerunning processes for supplementation; the threading task needed analysis of why only colision_empty could be injected. ✅ CALVIN dataset conversion script optimization — Corrected dataset name (calvin_abc vs calvin_abc_d) and key mapping in RLDS to LeRobot conversion script, removed non-existent fields, added progress bars and overwrite confirmation logic. ✅ LIBERO custom test suite integration — Added libero_object_com suite to LIBERO benchmark framework, modified task_map, registered Benchmark class, created BDDL files, updated client main.py. Implementation and Fixes ✅ GPU monitoring tool duplicate process fix and interactive navigation — Implemented ENV+FD verification and parent chain deduplication functions, resolving gpumon.py’s 35 duplicate processes (reduced to 8). Added interactions similar to nvitop: arrow key navigation, command scrolling, visual highlighting of selected lines. ✅ RoboCasa MimicGen dataset download failure diagnosis — Traced “No datasets for this task” errors through download_datasets.py → dataset_registry_utils.py → box_links_ds.json, confirming no MimicGen entries (upstream data publishing issues). ✅ Remote server environment configuration (athena.egr.duke.edu) — Configured SSH key authentication (users manually ran ssh-copy-id after initial failures), automatically installed fnm and Node.js 22, Claude Code 2.1.77 and Codex CLI 0.115.0, transferred qcvla_bridge_pipeline project via rsync (51 files, 3.1MB). Server is ready for distributed development. ✅ Git proxy configuration fix — Diagnosed git fetch network errors, tested multiple proxies, found localhost:9999 effective, updated local git configuration to override unavailable global proxies. ✅ Wandb account configuration — Provided solutions for 4 directory-level wandb accounts, recommended using WANDB_API_KEY environment variable or direnv method. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. QCVLA: Only improved by 1 percentage point (60%→61% SR) in the second round of Dagger, indicating saturation. Bridgers can only see their own natural error distributions, which are limited in diversity. Solution: Proposed perturbation and bridging data augmentation methods: During Dagger propagation, randomly inject short action sequences followed by reverse recovery actions. This allows Bridgers to capture larger deviations that would never occur naturally, thereby expanding the training distribution. At the same time, train the Bridger (for feature prediction of perturbation states) and action heads (recovery action sequences).
Key Insight: When the model only sees its own error patterns, self-supervised training can become saturated. Injecting synthetic but real deviations (inspired by FLARE’s perturbation strategy) can expand the distribution range. Compared to Gaussian noise per frame, coherent action perturbations are more effective in robot control.
2. VLA Inference: Infinite loops occurred because current_prompt was not reset after task completion, keeping chunk_count non-zero and the model continuously outputting high scores. Solution: Set current_prompt = "" after task completion and call publish_state(“idle”) to prevent immediate triggering in the next round; move None checks before task_completed.item() to avoid crashes in pi05 models.
Key Insight: The state machine needs to explicitly reset all related variables at each termination node, otherwise unpredictable loops can occur. Multi-device compatibility (pi05 and non-pi05) requires handling boundary conditions in advance.
3. MIHD: NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS tuple is duplicated in pipeline/runner.py and pipeline/evaluation_planner.py, and both lack adaln_attention (potential error), making it difficult to maintain consistency when adding new fusion strategies. Solution: Centralize NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS as a constant in models/Fusion.py, with both files importing this constant. Add the missing adaln_attention to the tuple, solving the problem of fusion strategies not being able to handle spatial coordinates.
Key Insight: Duplication of constants between modules leads to inconsistencies, requiring a single source to ensure no potential errors and simplify maintenance work.
4. MIHD: refine_labels_spatial_majority in utils/clustering.py uses scipy.spatial.distance.cdist, which has an O(N²) memory complexity, causing OOM issues on Visium HD data with 17K+ points. Solution: Replace the cdist algorithm with scipy.spatial.cKDTree.query, which has an O(N*k) complexity suitable for k-nearest neighbor searches. Switch from calculating complete distance matrices to querying radius neighbors as needed.
Key Insight: For large point clouds, using tree-based structures (such as KDTree, BallTree) can avoid memory explosions. For HD spatial data (10K+ points), the algorithm choice is more important than implementation details.
5. Combining combined_action_loss and task_loss into one value and logging it to wandb prevents monitoring each value separately. Solution: Modify compute_loss return value to (tuple)(combined_loss, {“action_loss”: …, “task_loss”: …}), and use nxn.value_and_grad(…, has_aux=True) in the training loop to parse auxiliary metrics and add them to the info dictionary. Key Insight: JAX/Flax’s has_AUX=True mechanism allows for returning additional monitoring metrics without affecting gradient calculation; all compute_loss implementations (pi0 and pi0_fast) need to be synchronized to maintain consistency of interfaces.
6. In error benchmark testing, thread tasks can only inject collision_empty type errors, other types cannot be generated. Solution: Analysis revealed that all clean trajectory phase_labels for threads were ‘pre_reach’, missing grasp/lift/transport stages, resulting in errors dependent on these stages (such as drop_in_transit) not being injected; the get_task_phase logic or thread task definition needs to be checked.
Key Insight: Error injection depends on the division of task phases; incomplete phase identification can lead to certain types of errors being overlooked; it’s necessary to verify all tasks cover expected phases when generating opportunity graphs.
7. gpumon.py counts bash/ffmpeg/claude processes, which have inherited CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES as GPU processes, and displays DDP working processes separately from parent processes. Solution: Modified get_proc_gpus() function to require both CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES environment variables and open/dev/nvidia* file descriptors; added parent chain traversal functionality in get_gpu_procs() to merge child processes with the same GPU set with their ancestors.
Key Insight: Detecting process use of GPUs requires checking environmental variables for inheritance and actual device file descriptors to avoid false positives.
8. All 317 RoboCasa tasks encountered errors downloading MimicGen data, unclear whether it’s a code issue or data availability problem. Solution: System analysis showed that 257 tasks had no mg_path registered (yellow warning), while 60 tasks had mg_path but no MimicGen download links in box_links_ds.json (red error). This confirmed an upstream data publishing issue rather than a code defect.
Key Insight: When system-wide download failures occur, it’s necessary to check the availability of data before attempting to fix the code. Using grep on box_links_ds.json to search for ‘/mg/’ yielded no matches, clearly indicating a lack of download links.
9. CALVIN RLDS dataset conversion failed with KeyError: ‘image’, the actual field names should be ‘rgb_static’ and ‘rgb_gripper’. Solution: Read features.json to confirm the actual schema, modified the keys for frame extraction, and removed missing environment_state and relative_action field definitions from the data set.
Key Insight: Before converting dataset formats, it’s necessary to verify the schema of the source data; RLDS features.json is an authoritative source for determining the actual field names.
10. How to implement interactive keyboard navigation in monitoring tools that require periodic updates while avoiding blocking input. Solution: Use select.select() for non-blocking keyboard input detection with a timeout of 0; use termios/tty for raw mode terminal control, storing selected_idx and scroll_offset states for multiple rendering processes, and apply reverse video (\033[7m) for highlighting.### Server Environment athena.egr.duke.edu✅ Setting up a Remote Development Environment for QCVLA 20:32:16.991 | claude_code Configuring athena.egr.duke.edu as the remote development server. Initial SSH connection failed due to public key rejection; the user manually executed ssh-copy-id to upload the public key. Next steps are automated: installing the fnm package manager, Node.js 22, Claude Code 2.1.77, and Codex CLI 0.115.0. The qcvla_bridge_pipeline project (51 files, 3.1MB) is transferred via rsync. The server now has a password-free SSH connection, with an AI coding toolchain ready for distributed development.
GPU Monitoring Tool (Tianhe Server) ✅ Fixing the issue of duplicate processes being displayed and adding interactive navigation features 03:01:28.167 | claude_code Users reported that gpumon.py displayed approximately 35 duplicate GPU processes, when in reality there were only 8 unique processes. AI identified two reasons: false positives for only ENV processes, and duplicates from DDP workers. A fix was implemented that considers both CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES and /dev/nvidia* file descriptors. Additionally, the parent chain was deduplicated. Interactive features similar to nvitop were added: arrow keys for navigation, command scrolling, and visual highlighting. The number of processes was reduced from 35 to 8 unique entries.
RoboCasa Dataset Management ✅ Diagnosing issues with failed MimicGen dataset downloads in all tasks 03:00:36.287 | claude_code Users encountered an error message “No datasets available for this task!” when downloading the MimicGen pre-trained data using download_datasets.py. AI analyzed the download script, registry, and box_links_ds.json to identify two issues: 257/317 tasks lacked mg_path registrations (as expected), but more importantly, despite having 350 manual data links, there were no MimicGen download links in box_links_ds.json. It was confirmed that this was an issue with the upstream data release, not a code defect. It is recommended to contact the maintainers for information about the data release schedule.

summary: Cross-device collaboration on MIHD spatio-metrics refactoring, in-depth research on QCVLA’s potential bridging architecture, improvement measures inspired by FLARE, fixes to VLA training/inference infrastructure, generation of error benchmark data, and enhancement of system tools
