content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-15 Daily Overview Completed Work: Implemented full-stack optimization for CalendarPro (semantic routing, hybrid classification, system prompt splitting, provider retry mechanism, scheduling scoring, learning loop, caching). Reorganized the main toolkit by extracting common modules/packages, integrating output directories, and unifying research tools. Conducted a comprehensive academic landscape mapping in the fields of robotics technology/embodied AI. Implementation Method: Deployed 5 parallel proxies to implement CalendarPro optimization, created unified/package files that can be installed via pip, adopted a modular architecture, re-exported compatible code, implemented scholar discovery functionality based on homepage scraping, used LLMs to extract information, built a three-stage evaluation process, analyzed citation impacts, and utilized ArXiv/Semantic Scholar API for batch researcher profiling. Results: The accuracy of CalendarPro’s intent understanding and task execution reliability have significantly improved. Code duplication between the four tools has been eliminated. A production-ready unified research workflow has been created to quickly understand the field. The advisor relationships and research trajectories of 13 leading robotics technology researchers were successfully mapped, including Sergey Levine, Chelsea Finn, Pieter Abbeel, Yuke Zhu, Yunzhu Li, Mingyu Ding, Ruoshi Liu, Xiaolong Wang, and Shuran Song. Implemented CalendarPro optimization in 7 stages (230 tests passed). Unified the research toolkit into a single CLI, integrated paper discovery/Profiler/citation functions, extracted shared tools into common/packages, eliminated over 400 lines of duplicate code, organized all output directories into structured output/hierarchical structures, profiled 13 leading robotics technology/AI researchers, and established systematic academic research tracking.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Full optimization implementation of CalendarPro (7 stages) — Implemented semantic routing thresholds, hybrid keywords + embedding routing, system prompt splitting (530 lines → base code + 11 segments). Used exponential backoff-based provider retry logic, scheduling scoring system, automatic learning from mismatches, and caching using ThoughtStore. All 230 tests passed with 5 parallel proxies used. ✅ Unified Research Scout + Profiler + Citation Graph Tools — Integrated three independent research tools into a single CLI: research_scout.py now includes paper discovery functionality, researcher profiling via ‘profile’ subcommands, citation graph analysis via ‘citations’ subcommands. The three-stage evaluation process includes citation impact analysis and supports multiple backend LLMs (claude_cli/anthropic/openai). Deleted duplicate ‘New feature/’ directories. ✅ Extracting shared tools into common/packages — Created unified/package files that can be installed via pip, containing 6 modules (io, cache, json_utils, llm, hugo, paths). Eliminated over 400 lines of duplicate code. Converted summarize/llm_backends.py and research/cache.py to re-export compatible code. Eliminated hack in sys.path, updated pyproject.toml and all documentation. ✅ Organizing output directories into unified output/hierarchical structures — Moved all tool-specific output paths to a unified output/hierarchical structure organized by type (reports/, logs/, cache/, data/, site/). Created centralized path constants in common/paths.py, updated all imports and documentation. ✅ Robotics technology research tracking and comprehensive researcher profiling — Created robotics technology leader projects, profiled 13 researchers (Sergey Levine h-index 166, Pieter Abbeel h-index 164, Chelsea Finn, Yuke Zhu, Yunzhu Li, Mingyu Ding, Ruoshi Liu, Xiaolong Wang, Shuran Song). Identified doctoral advisors, generated detailed trajectory reports with breakthrough papers and research topics. Discovered Open X-Embodiment, MineDojo (Best Paper at NeurIPS 2022), Value Iteration Networks (Best Paper at NeurIPS 2016). ✅ Implementing homepage-based scholar discovery functionality — Added source/status fields to the StudentCandidate model. Created research/homepage_discovery.py based on HTMLParser for text extraction and LLM student name extraction. Redesigned the discover_students() function, adopting a homepage-first + collaborator supplement strategy, and setting a polite rate limit (1 request/2 seconds). ✅ Updating comprehensive documentation (CLAUDE.md, TUTORIAL.md, README.md) — Rewrote the root CLAUDE.md with MCP server documentation and subproject references. Updated summarize/CLAUDE.md to fit the new import structure. Redrewed research/CLAUDE.md covering Profiler architecture and unified CLI. Expanded the Chinese TUTORIAL.md from 10 chapters to 13 chapters, adding multi-source search/Profiler/citation sections and 4 new workflow examples. Updated the README.md for missing tools. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Deploying researcher profiling into a separate Hugo section — Created a website/content/research/section for researcher profiling (separated from bugJournal temporal reports). Added deploy_to_hugo() function in research/output.py. Updated config.yml file with research menu items, adding –deploy and –hugo-site CLI flags. ✅ Analyzing CLAUDE.md and similar projects — Analyzed CalendarPro’s CLAUDE.md (strongly comprehensive, no need for modification). Studied similar open-source projects (FluidCalendar, CoPaw, Khoj, OpenDAN, Cal.com, Leon, Rasa, Home Assistant). Concluded that CalendarPro’s energy-aware scheduling + three-layer architecture + double verification combination is unique. ✅ Fixing mcp_server.py import errors — Corrected issues with function name mismatches: replaced global_load_known_arxiv_ids with load_known_paper_ids in 4 places. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Collaborator analysis showed zero students for all testing professors (Sergey Levine, Pieter Abbeel, Chelsea Finn), despite the implementation being correct. Solution: Professor homepages clearly listed student information; implemented homepage-first discovery functionality using LLMs to extract student names from professor pages. Use collaborator data as supplementary enrichment while retaining source tracking for transparency.
Key Insight: Professor homepages are authoritative sources of student information, making them more reliable than inferring relationships based on collaborators. When heuristic algorithms fail due to sparse data, direct information sources (professor webpages) should be used instead of debugging the scoring algorithm. Practical verification is better than reasoning.
2. Semantic Scholar matched over 6 researchers with ambiguous names (Ming Yu → Explosive Chemist, Xiaolong Wang → Veterinary Scientist/Geologist/NLP Researcher). The first 6 queries failed 5 times. Solution: Humans provided full name clarification information (Mingyu Ding); relied on ArXiv paper matching for more accurate results. AI can identify data quality issues by returning clear warnings when incompatible research fields are identified, instead of generating incorrect trajectories. It is recommended to verify common Chinese names with ORCID + institution verification.
Key Insight: Common Chinese names lead to serious ambiguity in academic databases. Therefore, mandatory institution/ORCID matching is required. Aggregating data for the same name is an important data quality issue—field-based clustering or institution filtering is needed before analysis. By clearly tracking sources, integrating multi-source data (homepages, ArXiv, S2) can improve fault tolerance. It’s better to return “data unavailable” than spread incorrect information.
3. Output files were scattered in tool-specific subdirectories (e.g., summarize/logs/, research/cache/, test/benchmark_results.csv). Hardcoding the Path(file).parent pattern led to tight coupling and difficulty managing disk space. Solution: Designed a unified output structure, categorized by type (reports, logs, cache, data) and tool. Introduced standardized path constants in common/paths.py, replaced all hard-coded paths, and updated environment variable backends.
Key Insight: When multiple tools have similar output patterns, organizing by type (reports, logs, cache, etc.) is easier to maintain than by tool. Centralized path management allows for atomic refactoring—changing one constant will take effect throughout the system without causing code errors. Phasing out and setting up verification checkpoints can prevent cascading failures.
4. Two overlapping research tools in the same directory (monolithic research_scout.py and modular analysis package) led to code duplication, unclear single entry points, and no documented functionality. The initial/init attempt was interrupted due to users suggesting AI focus on improving documentation rather than architecture. Solution: Users provided a detailed 9-step integration plan, including precise function signatures, line counts, and architectural decisions. AI executed the plan: kept research_scout.py as a stable CLI entry point, refactored modular packages into importable libraries, added analysis subcommands, implemented three-stage evaluation, and added support for multiple backend LLMs.
Key Insight: For complex architectural changes, a detailed written plan can prevent scope creep and ensure that AI follows the plan. Hybrid integration (retaining a stable CLI while refactoring internal code) is easier to migrate than complete rewriting. Human architectural vision combined with AI tactical execution is more effective than letting AI adjust on its own. Phasing out is safer than making one-off changes.
5. A new modular tool (researcher analyzer, containing about 15 Python modules) was completely unrecorded in CLAUDE.md, leading to misunderstandings of code capabilities. AI initially ignored the “new feature” directory entirely. Solution: Used proxy exploration to thoroughly map package structures and then re-wrote CLAUDE.md to record the architectures, usage, and configuration information of these two tools separately.
Key Insight: When adding new features, if the guide document is not updated, the documentation will quickly become messy. Therefore, active maintenance or automatic detection of unrecorded code paths is required. Update CLAUDE.md immediately after structural changes to prevent information bias.
6. Despite using correct author names, Yuke Zhu’s trajectory analysis still couldn’t parse JSON after three LLM fixes, resulting in incomplete reports. Solution: Accepted partial results (excluding the paper list of trajectory analysis) and recorded failures; although the analysis was completed, the output quality declined.
Key Insight: LLM fixes have limitations. When all three model levels fail, the problem may lie in prompts/context rather than model capabilities. Continuous failures indicate that the input structure needs to be re-examined or the problem needs to be decomposed.
7. Despite using correct author names, when searching on arXiv, zero results were found for robotics leaders due to sorting/time issues with the search API. Solution: Switched from arXiv search API to Researcher Profiler, which uses arXiv and Semantic Scholar data sources for more powerful multi-source access.
Key Insight: API search behavior is not always intuitive; multi-source data acquisition is more reliable than single API queries. When better data sources exist, don’t debug the quirks of the API.
General Issues 8. The initial research profiles were mixed into the bugJournal section, lacking semantic separation of content. Solution: Humans pointed out that materials should be placed in a separate research folder; created a new website/content/research section and added dedicated menu items and index.md files; updated deploy_to_hugo() function to point to the new section.
Key Insight: Different types of content require separate Hugo sections for better organization and navigation. Research materials are reference material, while bugJournal is temporal reports. Semantic classification is important for discoverability of content.
9. When running research_scout.py directly, the research analyzer encountered an error saying “no module named research”. Solution: Changed the execution method from “python research/research_scout.py profile” to “python -m research analyze”, respecting the package structure correctly.
Key Insight: Complex Python packages require the correct entry point. Using the -m flag ensures that packages with internal imports are properly resolved as modules.
10. When reinstalling the package, the pip install -e . command failed on Windows systems due to file locking, causing the gadget-mcp.exe program to fail to run properly. Solution: Confirmed that common/package imports can be executed directly from sys.path because the editable installation already provides the package root directory; skipped the reinstall step and continued with the operation.
Human and AI Methodology Strategic Level Student Discovery Method Role Method Humans Recognized that professor homepages clearly listed student information. Therefore, after collaborator analysis failed, homepage scraping was used as the main method; utilized domain knowledge of professors maintaining authoritative student lists. AI Initially only relied on Semantic Scholar data for collaborative analysis and adopted a four-dimensional scoring criterion (first author 40%, time concentration 25%, collaboration frequency 20%, timeliness 15%); if possible, AI would continue to debug the scoring algorithm instead of changing the method Difference Analysis: Humans directly determined the most reliable information sources based on their actual understanding of academic practices. AI relied on quantitative signals in publication metadata. Humans knew where the real situation was; AI relied on available data APIs and statistical inference. This indicates that humans have an advantage in identifying authoritative sources, while AI has an advantage in processing structured data patterns.
Cause Analysis of CalendarPro | Role | Method | |——|—-| | Humans | Users described severe symptoms (“general experience was poor”, “intent understanding was inaccurate”, “task completion was unreliable”) and used Claude Local CLI for description, hoping to achieve “comprehensive improvement”. | | AI | Immediately carried out a systematic cause analysis, breaking down system-level problems into 7 specific technical issues: problems with the intent classification layer (no confidence threshold, LLM timeout, no learning function), and problems with the task execution layer (no provider retry, rough token estimation, incomplete proxy implementation). |
Difference Analysis: Users provided symptoms and context; AI systematically debugged complex systems to identify operable technical problems. AI has an advantage in systematic debugging, while humans can only see overall dysfunction and realize that the problem lies not in the model but in process design.
Architecture Integration Strategy Role Method Humans Users requested “keep research_scout.py as a unified entry point. Import from modular packages as library code.” While leveraging modular code, retained a single entry point. Provided a detailed 9-step plan with specific file paths and line counts. AI Initially attempted to improve the existing CLAUDE.md documentation but did not propose architectural changes. Only carried out unified operations after receiving a clear integration plan. AI will focus more on incremental documentation improvements. Difference Analysis: Humans saw the strategic value of a unified interface, which improved the user experience and provided specific guidelines. AI focused on incremental improvement and required architectural guidance for cross-domain changes. Humans provided “what,” while AI provided “how” to implement it.
Citation Scoring Method for Paper Evaluation Role Method Humans Clearly stated that citation counts should only be used for sorting/drawing ties, not affecting overall relevance scores; recognized that popularity and project relevance are orthogonal. AI Initially proposed incorporating citation counts into the comprehensive evaluation score, mixing “important papers” with “relevant to the project”. Difference Analysis: Humans realized that highly cited papers might not be relevant to a project’s focus. AI defaults to using citation counts as a quality signal rather than a discovery indicator. This indicates that AI tends to optimize overall importance rather than specific context relevance.
Depth of Researcher Trajectory Analysis Role Method Humans Provided a list of original papers with basic metadata (title, year, citation count) and requested advanced trajectory analysis. AI Generated comprehensive multi-paragraph descriptions covering stage transitions, reasoning about why there was no breakthrough at certain times, explaining historical background, research topics, and the development of methodologies. Difference Analysis: Humans requested high-level analysis; AI generated multidimensional summaries, including causal reasoning about why breakthroughs occurred at certain times. AI went beyond pattern recognition to provide comprehensive analysis of historical backgrounds.| Role | Method | |——|—-| | Human | Immediately identified the name “Ming Yu” as too generic when reviewing incorrect results. Provided a full name “Mingyu Ding”; aware that common Chinese names may have disambiguation issues. | | AI | Accepted the first semantic Scholar match result without questioning its correctness; only marked data quality issues after seeing明显错误的论文（如爆炸化学、兽医科学等）。 |
Difference Analysis: Humans knew in advance that common names needed disambiguation; AI detected problems only after reviewing the results. Humans utilized prior knowledge of system issues; while AI relied on empirical detection.
When Reference Analysis is Triggered in the Reporting Process Role Method Human Always automatically analyzes the top five papers; prioritizes convenience over explicit control based on actual usage patterns (in most cases). AI Proposed making reference analysis an optional option with the –citations flag; for potentially costly operations, defaulting to explicit user selection is used. Difference Analysis: Humans considered friction in real-world workflows, while AI focused on theoretical flexibility and cost optimization. Humans prioritized reducing friction in common workflows rather than pursuing architectural purity.
Methods for Implementing the Refinement Plan Role Method Human Provided a detailed multi-stage plan with specific file paths, number of lines, expected changes, and clear checkpoints—specifying exact steps. AI Read files in parallel to understand the current state, then verified the import chain after each stage. Added verification steps and parallel I/O optimization, which were not included in the original plan. Difference Analysis: Humans used a specification with clear checkpoints; while AI added verification steps and parallel processing. Both emphasized systematic phased execution rather than atomic, disruptive changes. Human detailed plans eliminated ambiguity and ensured consistent implementation.
Hugo Content Organization Role Method Human Clearly indicated that researcher information should be placed in new research/sections, not in bugJournal/. Understood the semantic differences between reference materials and time reports. AI May deploy code to bugJournal/ according to existing patterns, as the report of research_scout.py is located there. Both are considered “research content”. Difference Analysis: Humans distinguished between temporal content (daily reports) and reference content (researcher information), requiring separate placement in different sections; while AI categorized all this content into one category and merged them together. Humans understood content classification to facilitate navigation.
Implementation Level Handling Missing Conference Award Data Role Method Human It is assumed that AI may include award information in its training data; requires AI to identify papers with conference awards. AI Recognized knowledge limitations—could not confidently confirm specific awards, returned an empty list with explanations instead of guessing, maintaining a conservative “unknown” response. Difference Analysis: Humans assumed that AI’s training data might contain award information; while AI understood the boundaries of uncertainty and chose not to propagate potentially incorrect information. This indicates that AI can understand when to say “I don’t know”.
Document Language Selection Role Method Human Explicitly requested to update tutorial.md in Chinese, recognizing that target audiences (Chinese researchers) needed tutorials written in their native language. AI Has been using English for code/comments, but switched to comprehensive Chinese documentation immediately when required. For technical documents, English is used by default. Difference Analysis: Humans identified the needs of end-users based on the target audience; while AI adopted a neutral default setting. This reflects humans’ understanding of demographic data, while AI adopts an approach independent of language.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Limitations Failed to proactively detect semantic ambiguity issues in Semantic Scholar names during preliminary researcher analysis—acceptingly received incorrect match results (such as explosion chemistry, veterinary science, geology, etc.), and required human-observe symptoms or clear instructions to trigger error detection. Despite domain mismatches, no doubts were raised about the matching results. Required a clear 9-step integration plan to execute complex architectural changes; initial attempts were interrupted as users believed AI was more suitable for document improvement rather than structural restructuring. For cross-cutting changes, architectural guidance was needed. The original design only considered student collaborative research, without considering that teacher homepage is an authoritative and explicit source of student lists; human advice was needed to switch methods. Ignored the entire “new feature” directory during the first analysis of the code base; this issue needed to be pointed out through clear user prompts before discovering an unrecorded analysis tool containing approximately 15 Python modules. Even after fixing the three-layer LLM, it was still unable to parse Yuke Zhu’s trajectory analysis JSON files, resulting in incomplete reports with only a list of papers without analytical insights. Required explicit user corrections to understand that researcher information should be stored separately from daily research reconnaissance reports, lacking semantic understanding of content classification (reference content vs. chronological order). Used citation counts as part of the comprehensive relevance score, but did not realize that popularity and project-specific relevance are independent dimensions; confused “important papers” with “project-related”. General Limitations Unable to automatically test end-to-end functionality after large-scale restructuring—verified import operations, but did not run actual tool commands using the new paths to confirm correctness. Unable to reliably verify conference awards (best papers, spotlight, oral reports) solely based on training data—required real-time access to official conference websites or paper metadata APIs. Returned an empty array with a disclaimer instead of guessing results. Did not propose cost optimization solutions for reference analysis (e.g., citation count thresholds, selective flags); blindly implemented the rule of “always analyzing the top 5”, without considering the cost impact of the API. Defaulting to English documentation, even though the code base belongs to Chinese user projects; required explicit instructions to switch tutorial.md to Chinese. Unable to automatically summarize the final state of multiple parallel agents; after five agents completed their work, manual verification was needed to ensure all 230 CalendarPro tests had passed. When pip installation failed due to Windows file locking, no alternative methods were suggested, such as stopping the MCP server process or using the –user flag. These measures were not taken until after the first failure. By default, reference analysis is optional (with the –citations flag), rather than considering usage patterns, allowing for convenient automatic execution. Lessons Learned Key Lessons ### Practical Experience Summary Hugo content organization benefited from semantic separation: Reference materials (researcher profiles) and dynamic content (daily reports) should be placed in different sections to facilitate navigation and improve user satisfaction. Python package entry points are important: For complex module structures with internal imports, ‘python -m package’ is more reliable than executing scripts directly. Using the –m flag ensures proper module parsing. Polite web crawlers (requesting once every 2 seconds) combined with rate limits are crucial when scraping academic homepages on a large scale to avoid being blocked or causing server overload. Dialogue Summary CalendarPro ✅ Comprehensive optimization and research in similar projects 21:29:45.386 | claude_code User feedback indicated that CalendarPro had a poor experience, with issues related to intent understanding errors and task completion failures. AI analyzed the code base and identified 7 root causes (no confidence threshold, lack of retry mechanism, rough token estimation, LLM timeout, absence of learning loop, incomplete agent implementation), and designed a 7-stage optimization plan to address issues at the intention classification and task execution levels. The plan uses 5 parallel agents to execute: semantic routing threshold, mixed keyword + embedding routing, system prompt splitting (530 lines → base section + 11 segments), provider retry mechanism, and automatic learning from misfits. All 230 tests were passed. Additionally, existing CLAUDE.md files were analyzed (well-documented without need for modification), and similar open-source projects (FluidCalendar, CoPaw, Khoj, OpenDAN, Cal.com, Leon, Rasa, Home Assistant) were studied. It was concluded that CalendarPro’s energy-aware scheduling, three-layer architecture, double verification, and unified lifecycle management are unique combinations in the open-source community.
Gadget Research Toolkit ✅ Unified integration of research toolsets, documentation writing, and comprehensive researcher profiling functions 02:16:04.409 | claude_code A completely undocumented modular researcher profiling tool was identified, along with existing research_scout.py paper discovery script. Users requested integrating these two tools and adding citation graph analysis functionality. AI used parallel agent tasks to explore these two code bases, then executed a detailed 9-step integration plan: removing duplicate ‘New feature/’ directories, adding Semantic Scholar’s citation API (get_paper_by_id/citations/references), creating personal profiles and citation subcommands, implementing three-stage evaluation, including citation impact analysis (always for top 5 papers), adding multi-backend LLM support (claude_cli/anthropic/openai), changing Chinese TUTORIAL.md from 10 chapters to 13 chapters. A robotic project was created to profile 13 researchers: Sergey Levine (h-index 166, 160K citations), Pieter Abbeel (h-index 164, 177K citations, identifying three stages: manipulation/LfD → deep reinforcement learning revolution, including TRPO/GAE/MAML/SAC → generative model/LLM integration), Chelsea Finn (initially mis-matched S2, but recognized as a professor at Stanford University), Yuke Zhu (Best Paper at MineDojo NeurIPS 2022), Yunzhu Li (Best Paper at Open X-Embodiment NeurIPS 2023), Mingyu Ding (breakthroughs in VRDP, DaViT, CAE, Open X-Embodiment), Ruoshi Liu (Zero-1-to-3, Objaverse-XL, evolution from physics to 3D vision to embodied intelligence), Xiaolong Wang (name disambiguation error, field incompatibility). Successfully found doctoral mentors for six researchers: Mingyu Ding → Jitendra Malik, Ruoshi Liu → Carl Vondrick, Xiaolong Wang → Abhinav Gupta, Shuran Song → Thomas Funkhouser, Yunzhu Li → Antonio Torralba, Yuke Zhu → Li Fei-Fei. Encountered multiple Semantic Scholar name disambiguation issues and some JSON parsing failures in analysis. Updated all CLAUDE.md documents, including the root directory, summary, and research catalogues. Result: A production-ready unified academic research toolset with a complete paper→researcher→citation workflow.
Gadget Toolkit Infrastructure ✅ Extracting common/打包并整合输出目录 21:37:49.486 | claude_code Achieved comprehensive 4-stage restructuring, integrating approximately 400 lines of repeated LLM calls, JSON parsing, atomic writing, and configuration loading from summarize/, research/, test/ into a new pip-installable common/. Created 6 modules (io, cache, json_utils, llm, hugo, paths), and re-exported to maintain backward compatibility (summarize/llm_backends.py, research/cache.py), eliminating the workaround of sys.path. Due to Windows file locking issues, pip installation failed, but verified that functions imported directly from editable installations worked properly. Then executed a 7-stage output directory integration: created common/path.py, containing standard constants (GADGET_ROOT, OUTPUTS_DIR, REPORTS_DIR, LOGS_DIR, DATA_DIR, SITE_OUTPUTS_DIR), migrating all tool-specific paths to a unified output hierarchy organized by type, updating the.gitignore file, and updating all documents. All CLI commands and imports were verified to work properly. Also updated main gadget CLAUDE.md: added a reference to sub-project CLAUDE.md at the top, reduced the Running Scripts section from 68 lines to 33 lines, updated the architecture section, introduced a modular structure for research profiling, reduced the document length from 175 lines to 146 lines while improving clarity.
Gadget Research Profiling ✅ Based on homepage student discovery and Hugo deployment 20:04:52.986 | claude_code After failed analysis of co-authors without finding students of famous professors, implemented an 8-step plan as the main method for discovering students on homepages. Created research/homepage_discovery.py, using text extraction based on HTMLParser, two new prompt templates (EXTRACT_STUDENTS_PROMPT, PARSE_STUDENTS_LIST_PROMPT) for LLM student extraction, and multi-strategy URL discovery. Re-structured discover_students() to use homepage information first, combined with co-author supplementary information, merging results with source tracking (source=‘homepage’/‘coauthorship’, status field). Added polite rate limits (1 request per 2 seconds). According to user requirements, created separate websites/content/research/Hugo sections instead of mixing them with bugJournal reports. content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-14 Daily Overview Completed Work: Enhanced GPU monitoring script for robobrain_pi project, created HDF5 camera data visualization tool for RoboTwin, designed 4 sets of experiments for predicting control progress, suitable for pi05 policy model Implementation Methods: Used ANSI escape codes to implement alternative screen buffers and adaptive layouts, decoded JPEG images using OpenCV and concatenated multiple camera views, added MLP decoder and special marking mechanism in JAX/Flax framework Impact: Monitoring tool achieved nvitop-level user experience, able to fully visualize 50 experiments with 4 cameras, established a complete progress prediction experiment framework Implemented adaptive UI for GPU monitoring script, HDF5 data visualization tool, and 4 sets of progress prediction experiments for pi05 model
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Design of pi05 model progress prediction framework — Designed 4 comparative experiments: last_token vs special_token × time vs distance ✅ Implementation of pi05 model modifications (6 files) — Modified pi0_config.py/model.py/tokenizer.py/robotwin_policy.py/config.py/pi0.py, added MLP decoder, special marking, conditional injection, and auxiliary loss function ❌ Data conversion script update — Need to modify convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py to add progress-related fields for training Implementation and Fixes ✅ HDF5 camera data visualization script — Created visualize_hdf5_cameras.py, concatenating 50 experiments from 4 cameras into a 2x2 grid MP4 video ✅ UI enhancement for GPU monitoring script gpumon.py — Added alternative screen buffer, adaptive column/row size adjustment, deduplication and noise filtering Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Training data not converted to lerobot format, missing progress-related fields causing experiments not to start Solution: Need to modify convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py to add keys such as manip_progress_time/distance_left/distance_right/target_endpose/target_joint
Key Insight: Model experiments depend on data pipeline integrity, data format conversion is a blocking preprocessing step
General Issues 2. os.get_terminal_size() cannot read COLUMNS/LINES environment variables in child processes, resulting in non-adaptive table width Solution: Try reading COLUMNS/LINES environment variables and converting them to integers, using os.get_terminal_size() if failed
Key Insight: Environment variables are more reliable than system calls, suitable for cross-process terminal communication
Human and Artificial Intelligence Approaches Strategic Level Gradient strategy selection in pi05 experiment design Role Method Human Directly choose option A (all stop_gradient), considered the safest approach as it does not affect action prediction. Adjust if results are poor — reflects conservative startup strategy in iterative experiments AI AI proposed 3 options (A completely blocking/B completely returning/C hybrid method) and analyzed trade-offs, tending to choose option A but emphasizing specificity of special marking Difference Analysis: Humans make quick decisions based on engineering experience, while AI tends to list options and trade-offs, but ultimately human’s concise decision-making strategy (first conservative handling, then iteration) is reasonable
Experiment configuration naming and organization Role Method Human Require “configuration switch, easy to start experiments” reflects emphasis on experiment efficiency AI AI designed a unified ProgressConfig class and 4 independent named configurations (exp1~4), allowing direct selection through tyro CLI Difference Analysis: Humans focus on practicality and convenience, while AI translates it into configuration classes and CLI interfaces. Both agree on the goal of “easy to switch experiments”
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Constraints AI did not actively detect data pipeline issues in the end — should immediately check if data loading after model modification supports new fields, rather than waiting for user errors to be discovered AI did not run actual training smoke tests when modifying pi05, only tested import of configuration classes, resulting in missing dependencies in data conversion script Lessons Learned Key Lessons For complex model experiments, integrity checks should cover model code, configuration, data pipeline, and training scripts, not just verification of model code Using stop_gradient to implement auxiliary tasks is a common multi-task learning pattern that can utilize features while avoiding interfering with the main task Special marking mechanism requires 3 steps: tokenizer registration of marker IDs, model embedding layer extension, and concatenation during data loading prompts Dialogue Summary Robobrain-PI (GPU Monitoring) ✅ UI enhancement for GPU monitoring script, with adaptive layout 09:24:23.170 | claude_code User requested alternative screen buffer similar to nvitop and adaptive column/row size adjustment. Implemented ANSI escape code for switching screen buffers, dynamically calculating width/height based on COLUMNS/LINES environment variables, scaled column size adjustment, and deduplication functionality. The script now provides professional terminal UI, allowing clean exit without contaminating terminal history.
RoboTwin VLA (HDF5 Visualization) ✅ Visualizing HDF5 data from 4 cameras as MP4 videos 13:16:50.918 | claude_code User needed to visualize observations from 50 HDF5 experiments (front/head/left/right cameras). Created visualize_hdf5_cameras.py, which decodes JPEG-encoded frames, divides them into 2x2 grids, adds labels, and writes them as MP4 files at 30fps. All 50 experiments were successfully processed (about 2.3MB each), stored in all_videos/ directories.

After discussing the architecture (MLP 2048→256→output, λ=0.1, stop_gradient strategy), modified 6 files to add ProgressConfig, 3 special markers, MLP decoder, conditional injection logic, and auxiliary MSE loss function. Created 4 training configurations (pi05_robotwin_prog_exp1~4). Verified all imported items, but found blocking issues in the data pipeline at the end.

content:
Key Learnings### Practical Learning Outcomes ArXiv HTML Rendering is now more suitable for full-text extraction than PDF: faster, with no OCR errors, and maintaining structure. For papers not processed in HTML format, PDF can be used as an alternative. This indicates that the evolution of infrastructure requires regular review of “best practices”. Ambiguous numeric logs without semantic tags: “1736651” could represent count/ID/size. It’s always better to use clear labels: “Author 1736651” or “1,736,651 papers”. The extra cost is just two words, but it eliminates errors in interpreting the entire category. For academic analysis tools, narrative parts prefer Chinese output, while technical terms prefer English. A dual-language approach: describe structures in English (breakthroughs, why not before, impacts), and explain them in Chinese. When there’s a selection logic upstream (year-based stratification → ≤10/year), downstream stages should not use different criteria for re-filtering. This can lead to inconsistent patterns (papers seen by fast modes differ from those by detailed modes). Multi-agent exploration pattern: Use the Explore agent to understand the code library, the Plan agent to design, and implement them in a sequential manner through user verification. This clearly separates concerns and reviews plans before execution. Dialogue Summary Gadget Research Tools ✅ Research Tool Design and Complete Implementation 20:08:05 | claude_code A complete academic researcher analysis tool was designed and implemented for the robotics field. Initial discussions covered requirements (analyzing why leaders become influential, breakthroughs, recursive student discovery, fast/detail modes). A 15-module Python package was created, including ArXiv/Semantic Scholar API, LLM abstraction (claude CLI), Chinese prompts, BFS recursion, scoring algorithms, and CLI for analysis/display/listing/configuring. Directory structure, requirements.txt, and documentation were also created. The tool supports caching, year-based paper selection with priority given to awards, and generates JSON configuration files and Markdown reports.
✅ Critical Error Fixes and Feature Enhancements 20:45:09 | claude_code Several critical issues were fixed: (1) Data source priorities reversed - S2 as the main source (comprehensive historical coverage), with ArXiv providing supplementary metadata. (2) Year-based selection was implemented: grouped by year, sorted by award weight and citation count, ≤10/year, displayed in chronological order. (3) An LLM-assisted award recognition phase using training data was added. (4) Log ambiguity issues were resolved from “[S2] Obtaining papers: 1736651” to “[S2] Obtaining author 1736651’s papers…”. (5) JSON parsing was refactored with a progression mechanism from haiku to tanka to opus, saving failed responses to disk. (6) Detailed mode issues were fixed by downloading all filtered paper texts instead of the top ten globally.
✅ Researcher Trajectory Analysis 20:28:15 | claude_code Detailed academic trajectory analysis was generated for Sergey Levine and Pieter Abbeel. Levine: career stages from 2016-2025, breakthroughs (Horizon Reduction offline RL, Q-learning Adjoint Matching, π0.5 VLA, natural language Actor-Critic, Multistep Quasimetric Learning), with time dependencies explained (data size, GPU computing, base models). Abbeel: three phases (robotics 2012-2014, deep reinforcement learning 2015-2018, extended period since 2019), five breakthroughs (TRPO, MAML, SAC, DDPM, decision Transformer), h-index of 164, and 177K citations. ✅ Documentation Creation 20:58:09 | claude_code Comprehensive documentation was created: (1) Chinese tutorial.md covering quick start, installation, core commands for all flags, analysis modes (fast vs detailed), student discovery algorithms (4 signals + weights), scoring system (5 dimensions, 4 levels), output formats, caching mechanisms (5 namespaces and TTLs), and common examples. (2) CLAUDE.md for future AI scenarios, including core commands, 9-step process, design choices (CLI-based LLM, JSON parsing strategy, caching/TTL, rate limiting, atomic writes), and output structure.
RoboTwin VLA ✅ Implemented and debugged new variable collection system 15:34:27.123 | claude_code Users requested implementation of the previously designed scheme. Claude added pickle import statements, modified setup_demo() to track states, removed coverage for get_critical_region_label(), added coverage for take_picture() and move(), so as to capture EE positions and process pkl files with new variables. After testing, users reported two issues: critical_region still stored in HDF5 (base classes write unconditionally), and progress_distance had negative values (curve trajectory). Claude resolved these issues by adding pkl_data.pop() and np.clip().
🔄 Post-processing flow for Place Dual Shoes variables 03:39:55.636 | claude_code Users requested removal of critical_region and addition of 5 new variables (manip_progress_time, manip_progress_distance_left/right, target_endpose, target_joint) to the Place Dual Shoes task. Claude used the Explore agent to understand the code structure (place_dual_shoes.py, _base_task.py, pkl2hdf5.py), then used the Plan agent to design post-processing schemes, where coverage for move() could track EE positions at each frame and patch the pickle file after completion. The Plan scheme was recorded, but users did not proceed further.

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-13

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-12 Daily Overview Completed Work: Tried to resolve technical issues in three independent projects across platforms: upgraded the sandbox permission architecture of macOS Wallpaper Application, optimized the output of GPU monitoring tools, and fixed dependency chain problems in the deep learning evaluation environment. Implementation Methods: Identified problems with macOS TCC permission mechanism through system-level log analysis. Used a dependency version matrix to track ABI compatibility in the PyTorch ecosystem. Configured the CUDA toolchain to solve C++ extension local compilation issues. Impact: macOS applications now have production-grade file access persistence features. Two research projects on Tianhe servers have resumed operation (although the checkpoint path issue has not been fully resolved). MacBook Completed Work: Completed the sandbox migration of desktop video applications and refactored the permission system. Fixed a critical bug where historical records couldn’t open old files. Implementation Methods: Enabled App Sandbox to make security-level bookmarks effective. Fixed bookmark parsing rollback logic and added an NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism. Impact: User history functionality is now complete. The application follows macOS best practices, paving the way for App Store release. Tianhe Completed Work: Resolved conflicts in PyTorch dependencies in RoboTwin project. Fixed curobo compilation issues and optimized the readability of GPU monitoring tools. Implementation Methods: Matched torch/torchvision with pip version matrix. Configured CUDA toolchain paths (CUDA_HOME and CPATH) in the conda environment. Rewrote process aggregation logic to limit output width. Impact: Basic functionality in robot strategy evaluation environment is restored. GPU resource monitoring is more efficient, but checkpoint path mapping issues remain unresolved. Fixed problems with the sandbox permission architecture of macOS Wallpaper Application and resolved PyTorch dependency chain and CUDA compilation issues in the deep learning evaluation environment
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Resolved issue where historical records couldn’t open old videos — Enabled App Sandbox, fixed bookmark parsing logic (added non-restrictive rollback), added permission error retry mechanism (NSOpenPanel reauthorization), updated localization and ChangeLog. ✅ Resolved PyTorch version conflicts in RoboTwin evaluation environment — Upgraded torchvision from 0.21.0 to 0.22.1+cu126 to match torch 2.7.1, resolved failure when registering torchvision::nms operator. ✅ Recompiled curobo to match current PyTorch ABI — Deleted old.so file, installed ninja compiler, configured CUDA_HOME and CPATH, and recompiled using pip install -e from source code. ❌ Resolved issue where RoboTwin checkpoint path didn’t exist — eval.sh parameters specified checkpoint_id=5000, but the directory contained only 15000/25000/29999. Session interruption occurred when analyzing parameter mapping logic. Implementation and Fixes 🔄 Optimized output format of GPU monitoring tools — Limited width to 80 columns, removed duplicate process displays, and filtered out noise from multi-process work processes. Users reported session interruptions due to incorrect understanding. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. macOS non-sandbox applications cannot persist file access permissions, causing “Operation Not Allowed” errors for old videos in historical records. Solution: Enabled App Sandbox permissions (com.apple.security.app-sandbox and files.user-selected.read-write). Fixed ensureFileAccess to add non-restrictive bookmark rollback parsing. Added NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism for showVideo/showImage.
Key Insight: macOS TCC requires App Sandbox to create security-level bookmarks. Non-restrictive bookmarks can only parse URLs but cannot grant access, resulting in inability to access old files. Combining sandbox and downgrading paths is needed to solve this problem.
2. ABI incompatibility between torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0 caused failure when registering torchvision::nms operator. Solution: Upgraded torchvision to 0.22.1+cu126 to match torch 2.7.1 version.
Key Insight: When compiling custom operators in torchvision, it links to torch’s C++ API. Incompatible versions can cause operator registration failures. torchvision 0.21.0 corresponds to torch 2.6.x, which cannot be used with torch 2.7.x.
3. curobo’s pre-compiled.so file is incompatible with current PyTorch ABI, and JIT compilation couldn’t find cuda_runtime.h. Solution: Deleted old.so file, installed ninja compiler, set CUDA_HOME to /path/to/conda.env, and configured CPATH to /path/to/targets/x86_64-linux/include. Recompiled using pip install -e.
Key Insight: After upgrading PyTorch version, C++ extensions need to be recompiled. The Nvidia package installed in the conda environment places the CUDA header file in a non-standard path (targets/x86_64-linux/include/), and environment variables must be correctly configured for JIT compilation to find them.
General Problems 4. Output of GPU monitoring tools is too wide and process displays are repetitive, making it difficult to read. Solution: Limited output to 80 columns, aggregated GPUs per process, and filtered out noise from multi-process work processes.
Key Insight: The original implementation grouped GPUs by device, resulting in repeated display of multiple GPUs on multiple GPUs. It’s necessary to aggregate processes and display all card numbers used in the GPU column.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Methods Strategic Level Deep Explanation of macOS File Permission Issues Role Method Human Asking intuitive questions like “Why are there no permissions?” and letting AI explain the basics of TCC and sandbox mechanisms. Artificial Intelligence Providing technical solutions without actively explaining the basics. Humans need to ask questions to get a complete context. Difference Analysis: A human-centered approach guides artificial intelligence to provide a more complete background explanation, rather than directly providing solutions. Humans focus on “why,” while AI focuses on “how to do it.”
Strategy for Choosing Fixes Role Method Human Clearly chose a defensive solution that combines “sandbox” and “NSOpenPanel reauthorization.” Artificial Intelligence Provided two independent solutions (sandbox or reauthorization), but didn’t actively suggest combining them. Difference Analysis: Humans tend to adopt defensive programming (multiple layers of protection). AI tends to choose the best solution. Humans’ combination strategy addresses the root cause and provides a downgrading path, making it more robust.
Implementation Level Understanding of GPU Monitoring Tool Requirements | Role | Handling Method | |——|——| | Human | Revised “Each process should only be displayed once on one GPU” to “Each process should only be displayed once.” Emphasized the many-to-many relationship between processes and GPUs. | | AI | Misunderstood the requirement, combining processes with multiple GPUs into one line, and displaying card numbers in comma-separated format in the GPU column. |
Difference Analysis: Humans are more aware of the many-to-many relationship between processes and GPUs. AI’s over-optimization led to information loss (process resource usage on GPU0 and GPU1 should be displayed in two lines).
AI Limitations General Limitations Failed to actively explain technical background (macOS TCC/sandbox mechanisms). Humans need to ask questions to get a complete context. AI should actively explain “why” when proposing solutions. Misunderstood the requirements for GPU monitoring, simplifying the many-to-one relationship between processes and GPUs, resulting in information loss. Inaccurate understanding of visualization needs for complex data relationships. Analyzed checkpoint path issues too deeply by focusing on code details (consecutive read operations) without first confirming the scope of user questions. Higher-level understanding of parameter mapping logic should have been conducted first. Lessons Learned Key Lessons macOS TCC permission mechanism: Non-sandbox applications cannot create security-range bookmarks; can only create non-security-range bookmarks (only parsing URLs that don’t require access rights), resulting in historical file access failure after application restart. Application sandbox must be enabled to maintain file access permissions. PyTorch C++ extension ABI compatibility: Custom operators in torchvision are linked to Torch’s C++ API during compilation. Versions must match exactly (torchvision 0.21→torch 2.6, torchvision 0.22→torch 2.7). Incompatibility can cause operator registration failures. Condna environment CUDA toolchain configuration: The Nvidia package installed via pip places the CUDA header file in targets/x86_64-linux/include/, not the standard include/ directory. For JIT compilation of C++ extensions, it’s necessary to set CUDA_HOME to the conda environment and configure CPATH to point to the target path. Defensive programming principles: Combining sandbox and reauthorization is more robust than using a single solution. Sandbox addresses the root cause (persistence permission issues), while NSOpenPanel provides a downgrading approach (temporary authorization). Combined use ensures users can always access files. Dialogue Summary Desktop Video ✅ Fixed issue where historical records couldn’t open old videos 07:52:05.359 | claude_code Users reported that they couldn’t open old videos in the historical record (Operation Not Allowed, code=257). Log analysis showed that the application didn’t enable sandbox, resulting in failure to create security-range bookmarks. ensureFileAccess only tried with.withSecurityScope parsing, causing non-security-range bookmarks to fail forever and ultimately being denied by macOS TCC. Three-tier fixes were implemented: enabling app sandbox permissions, fixing non-security-range bookmark parsing, and adding an NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism for showVideo/showImage. Code modifications, localization updates, and ChangeLog were completed, and build verification passed.
robobrain GPU Monitoring 🔄 Optimized the output format of GPU monitoring tools 15:48:28.705 | claude_code Users requested limiting the output width of gpumon.py to 80 columns and displaying duplicate processes. AI rewrote the process table logic, combining each process into one line, displaying card numbers in comma-separated format in the GPU column, and filtering out noise from sub-processes such as multi-processing work threads. Users corrected their misunderstanding, stating that it should be “Each process should only be displayed once on one GPU,” rather than aggregated display, which caused session interruption.
RoboTwin Policy Evaluation 🔄 Diagnosed dependency conflicts that caused eval.sh execution failure 02:23:18.758 | claude_code Users reported an error when running bash eval.sh. AI analyzed three-tier dependency issues: 1) Torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0 versions didn’t match, causing failure in registering torchvision::nms operator; 2) curobo’s pre-compiled.so was incompatible with PyTorch ABI, JIT compilation couldn’t find ninja; 3) Checkpoint path was missing (specified parameter was 5000, but actually only had 15000/25000/29999). AI provided solutions for upgrading versions, and users chose to upgrade torchvision.
🔄 Implemented dependency fixes and resolved subsequent compilation issues 02:34:02.614 | claude_code Implementation plan: Successfully upgraded torchvision to 0.22.1+cu126 and verified its compatibility with Torch 2.7.1; installed ninja compiler; deleted the old.so file, and recompiled curobo by setting CUDA_HOME to the conda environment and configuring CPATH to targets/x86_64-linux/include/; confirmed curobo was imported correctly. When running eval.sh again, issues with missing checkpoint paths were encountered, and AI started analyzing parameter mapping logic in deploy_policy.yml and evalPolicy.py, causing session interruption.

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-11 Daily Overview Completed Work: Reorganized the MIHD project file system, conducted RM-IDEAL benchmark tests; developed a containerized GPU monitoring tool; fixed dependencies issues in VLA training, eliminating 33% of performance gaps through systematic dependency alignment. Implementation Method: Migrated systems files via code updates, checked file systems to detect GPUs, determined versions of torch/torchvision, used parallel exploration agents for comparative configurations, and utilized pyproject.toml editor and uv toolchain for dependency alignment. Impact: MIHD code library structure improved, benchmark metrics enhanced (Spearman r=0.1804); deployed K8s GPU monitoring solution; VLA training environment stabilized; identified and resolved performance bottlenecks in JAX versions (upgraded from 0.5.0 to 0.5.3), achieving approximately 25% expected performance improvement. DCC Completed Work: Reorganized MIHD project files (executed 8-step plan), fixed errors in patch queries (barcode alignment issues), and conducted RM-IDEAL benchmark tests on section 151673-151508. Implementation Method: Used Bash for file migration, updated 14 file path references with Edit tools, and performed CPU-based benchmark tests using conda environments. Impact: Code library structure became clearer, with outputs/{DLPFC,HD,rm_ideal} hierarchy established, reverted cross-section query functionality, and obtained RM-IDEAL benchmark metrics (average Spearman r=0.1804). tianhe Completed Work: Developed nvitop-style GPU monitoring tools for K8s containers; fixed version conflicts between pi05 and torch/torchvision; systematically analyzed and resolved differences in training speed between openpi and pi05 through dependency alignment. Implementation Method: Used a rich TUI interface, mapped GPU processes via /proc/fd and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, fixed versions in pyproject.toml, used parallel exploration agents for deep code comparison, and systematically aligned 6 shared dependencies (JAX 0.5.0→0.5.3, torch 2.5→2.7.1, etc.), using uv lock/sync for alignment. Impact: Deployed GPU monitoring solution for PID namespace isolation; resolved blocking issues in pi05 training; identified JAX versions as the bottleneck causing 20-30% of performance issues; eliminated training time differences by aligning pi05 dependencies, achieving expected performance improvement of approximately 25%. Multi-project productivity: MIHD file structure reorganization + benchmark tests, K8s GPU monitoring tools, VLA training optimization (fixed torch/torchvision issues and achieved 33% performance improvement through JAX/dependency alignment)
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ MIHD File Structure Reorganization — Executed a 8-step plan to migrate outputs/ directories: benchmark_results→DLPFC, hd_results→HD, rm_ideal divided into cross_section/single_section. Updated 14 code files and multiple documents. Cleared old directories. ✅ GPU Monitoring Tool Development (gpumon.py) — Built nvitop-style GPU monitoring tools for K8s containers. Features include: /proc/fd + CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES GPU detection, dual-boundary tables, processes grouped by GPU, color progress bars, automatic filtering of monitoring processes. Supports real-time refresh and one-shot mode. ✅ Analysis and Resolution of Openpi vs Pi05 Training Speed Differences — Investigated differences in training ETA between 15 hours and 20 hours (33% gap). Used parallel exploration agents to identify root causes: JAX version (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3 - main factor), num_workers (8 vs 16), lerobot git revs,IMAGE_KEYS differences, conda base environment. Systematically aligned 6 dependencies in pi05 to match openpi, added override dependencies to resolve Lerobot torch constraints, completed uv lock/sync. ✅ MIHD Patch Query Error Fixes — Fixed 3 errors causing “patch mismatch queries”: inconsistent barcode sorting between cache and data_adata, inconsistent data paths, coordinate system alignment issues. Updated 4 files and added explicit barcode-to-index mappings. ✅ MIHD RM-IDEAL Benchmarking (151673-151508) — Conducted CPU-based RM-IDEAL cross-section benchmarks using existing embedded data and caches. Results: average Spearman r=0.1804, Layer_4/Layer_1 performed well, Layer_3/Layer_6 showed negative correlation. Data saved to summary.csv file. ✅ Pi05 torch/torchvision Version Fixes — Resolved training startup error “operator torchvision::nms does not exist.” Identified incompatibility between torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0. Added torchvision==0.22.1 to pyproject.toml, ran uv sync to confirm upgrade success. Training environment restored. Implementation and Fixes ❌ Error Recovery v5 Visualization Plan — User requested re-rendering of the v5 720p video (original 480p video). Multiple plan attempts were rejected, resulting in task abandonment. Perhaps an environmental issue or user change of intent. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. MIHD patch query returned incorrect physical locations: “Patch mismatch with current patch” Solution: The root cause was inconsistent barcode sorting between the cache (sc.read_10x_h5 order) and visualization (sc.read_visium order). This issue was resolved by using an explicit barcode-to-cache index mapping: {str(b): i for i, b in enumerate(cache_barcodes)}. Also fixed data path and coordinate consistency issues.
Key Insight: Index-based searches fail when the data source order is different. Always use explicit keys for alignment (in this case, barcodes) when bridging cache data with runtime reloading.
2. K8s containers’ PID namespace isolation prevented nvidia-smi from displaying process information Solution: Built a custom GPU monitoring tool that scanned /dev/nvidia* device files using /proc//fd/ and the CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES environment variable. Filtered out monitoring tools that ran all GPUs (nvitop, gpustat, claude). Implemented a rich-toolkit nvitop-style TUI interface.
Key Insight: Containerized GPU monitoring requires access to the proc file system. /proc/fd symbolic links can display open devices; CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES is actual. Monitoring tools that run all GPUs are false positives - filter these tools by checking CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES.
3. Despite identical hardware and configuration, training times for the same commands in both projects differed by 33% (openpi 15 hours vs pi05 20 hours) Solution: Parallel exploration agents determined that JAX version differences (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3) were the main cause. Systematically aligned 6 dependency versions: JAX 0.5.0→0.5.3, torch>=2.5.1→2.7.1, orbax-checkpoint 0.11.1→0.11.13, numpy, beartype, transformers. Added override dependencies to resolve Lerobot torch<2.7 constraints.
Key Insight: A slight JAX version upgrade (0.5.0→0.5.3) can bring about a 20-30% performance improvement because the XLA compiler offers good optimization benefits; multi-project dependency drift is a hidden performance issue that requires regular alignment audits. uv override dependencies provide an non-invasive way to resolve conflicts.
4. MIHD file structure became chaotic due to inconsistent naming (benchmark_results, hd_results, rm_ideal were distributed in different directories) Solution: Executed a systematic 8-step migration plan: created new hierarchies (DLPFC, HD, rm_ideal/{cross_section,single_section}), migrated files, updated 14 code files and documents, cleaned up old directories. Used bash mv and Edit tools for batch path updates. Key Insight: Large-scale refactoring requires upfront planning (create directories first), then file migration, followed by code and document updates. For historical archive documents, sed batch processing can be used.
5. Pi05 training failed at startup: ‘RuntimeError: operator torchvision::nms does not exist’ Solution: Detected incompatibility between torch 2.7.1 + torchvision 0.21.0 (0.21.0 was compiled for torch 2.6.x). Explicitly specified torchvision==0.22.1 in pyproject.toml, then ran uv sync to upgrade. After verification: torch 2.7.1+cu126, torchvision 0.22.1+cu126.
Key Insight: There are strict version pairing requirements between PyTorch and torchvision (2.7.x requires 0.22.x). Indirect dependencies resolved by uv/pip may choose incompatible versions - always explicitly specify critical binary coupling dependencies.
Common Issues 6. Pi05 wandb logs showed num_workers=16, while the code defaults to 8 Solution: Discovered that CLI parameter –num-workers 16 overwrote the default value; recommended not to pass this parameter, instead modify the code.
Key Insight: Configuration parameters may come from multiple sources (code defaults, CLI parameters, configuration files); thorough tracing is needed before making code changes.
7. Initial gpumon.py layout had issues with line width limitations, column truncation, and overly chaotic process lists Solution: Iterative improvements: 1) Fixed column width calculation for GPU information, 2) Adopted table-based layout, 3) Used nvitop’s dual-boundary style, 4) Filtered out monitoring tools processes, 5) Grouped processes under corresponding GPUs.
Key Insight: Terminal UI design requires multiple iterations. Refer to existing tools (like nvitop) to meet user expectations. Automatically filtering noise (monitoring processes) can improve signal-to-noise ratio.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Approaches Strategic Level MIHD File Reorganization Execution Method Role Method Human Humans provided a detailed 8-step plan: create directories first, migrate files by category, update code references, update documents, verify the final structure. There was clear division between stages. AI AI may perform exploratory operations first, followed by step-by-step modification suggestions. No extensive upfront planning is required; focus is on discovery-based development. Difference Analysis: Human system planning avoided issues during execution (such as updating code before file migration). AI tends to be more exploratory and iterative in its approach. For large-scale refactoring involving many interdependent relationships, a human method is more appropriate.
Error Recovery v5 Visualization Plan Rejection Loop Role Method Human After multiple plan attempts were rejected, humans chose the “direct execution” option (wanted immediate execution rather than continuing planning). However, they still continuously rejected attempts to exit the planning mode. AI AI interprets “direct execution” as “exiting the planning mode and executing”. Continuously tries the ExitPlanMode tool. Did not realize that the user might have environmental barriers or changed their intention. Difference Analysis: Communication issues: repeated rejections indicated an unspoken problem (GPU node unavailable? Task no longer needed?). AI should ask “What is preventing us?” after two or three rejections, rather than repeating the same approach.
Dependency Alignment Strategy Role Method Human Directly instructed: “Align all pi05 dependencies with openpi and set num_workers to 8” AI Detailed analysis: compared two pyproject.toml files, categorized dependencies into shared (6 that need updating), pi05-specific (keep RoboTwin package), openpi-specific (not needed in pi05), and fixed git revs (keep Lerobot) Difference Analysis: AI broke down the request into surgical editing (6 specific version changes + 1 new configuration section), rather than replacing everything. This preserved project-specific dependencies, avoiding compatibility issues caused by changing Lerobot git revs.
GPU Monitoring UI Design Expectations Role Method Human Humans explicitly requested “reference nvitop layout”, “process grouping by GPU”, and “do not display processes without using GPUs”. They provided clear reference points and priorities. AI AI’s initial design was a generic TUI with process lists - did not refer to industry-standard tools (like nvitop). Only adjusted after multiple user modifications. Difference Analysis: Humans knew the established pattern (nvitop) and expected it. AI did not actively check existing tool UX patterns. Lesson: When building CLI tools, study category leaders first.
Implementation Level num_workers Configuration Fix Role Method Human Explicitly requested code modifications to set num_workers to 8 AI Investigated actual execution and found that the default value was already 8. Traced back to CLI parameter –num-workers 16, suggested a non-invasive solution (omitting the CLI flag) Difference Analysis: After discovering the real cause, AI chose behavioral correction rather than code modification, avoiding unnecessary code changes.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Limitations Planning mode death spiral: In the v5 visualization task, AI tried ExitPlanMode more than 4 times and was rejected. It only asked the user “What is preventing us?” after finally rejecting the attempt. A cycle of rejection should have been detected earlier, and the process should have switched to clarifying mode. General Limitations Initially editing pyproject.toml, no incompatibility issues with Lerobot’s torch<2.7 constraint were anticipated; this issue was only discovered when uv lock failed, and then a override dependency for torch was added passively. A transitive dependency graph could have been analyzed in advance. Using two parallel exploration agents to handle almost the same code library (openpi and pi05 shared the same OpenPI framework) led to repeated discoveries. Using a single agent for sequential exploration would be more efficient. The initial GPU monitoring design was generic, without considering domain characteristics. No active check on the UX pattern of nvitop (category leader). Multiple user modifications were needed to achieve the desired layout. When building CLI tools, existing tools should have been studied. Only after the user provided error tracking information did the compatibility issue between torch/torchvision versions be predicted. The compatibility of dependencies could have been actively verified when reading pyproject.toml (not explicitly specifying torchvision’s torch 2.7.1 was a warning sign). In the training speed analysis, two parallel Explore agents were started, but initially, the uv.lock files (true basis of installed versions) were not compared. Checking lock files for train.py, config.py, etc. should have been the primary task in handling dependency issues. Lessons Learned Key Lessons - K8s’s PID namespace isolation prevented nvidia-smi from detecting process information. Solution: Use /proc//fd/ to check/dev/nvidia* device files and the CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES environment variable. Monitoring tools that use CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES will run all GPUs without specifying a GPU, which can lead to false positives.### Version Upgrade of JAX/XLA (0.5.0 → 0.5.3) Upgrading to version 0.5.3 can improve training efficiency by 20-30% through compiler optimizations. Even minor framework updates should be subject to performance audits in computationally intensive machine learning projects. Changes in multi-project dependencies are a hidden form of performance issue that needs regular adjustments.
Strict Version Pairing in PyTorch/torchvision For example, torch 2.7.x requires torchvision 0.22.x, and torch 2.6.x requires 0.21.x. If the versions do not match, C++ operator registration will fail. When using torchvision, it is essential to explicitly specify these versions in the dependencies, as indirect solutions may fail.
Barcode Sorting Issues in Spatial Transcriptomics Caches created by sc.read_10x_h5 may have a different barcode order compared to runtime sc.read_visium. Index-based searches will unconsciously map to incorrect locations. Always use a clear mapping from barcodes to indices: {str(b): i for i, b in enumerate(cache_barcodes)}.
User-Unintended Barriers During Plan Rejection When the planning mode is repeatedly rejected, there may be unspoken barriers (environmental availability, priority changes, technical limitations). After two or three rejections, the approach should shift from “Let me fix the plan” to asking “What is preventing us from moving forward?”
Center Calculation in RM-IDEAL For a given query niche, take the embedding vectors of all points in that niche and calculate the average vector to obtain the centroid. Compare the centroid with all reference points using cosine similarity. This can compress N pairs of M comparisons into 1 pair of M, thereby improving efficiency and reliability.
uv’s Coverage Dependency as an Exact Tool uv is a precise tool for resolving transitive dependency conflicts without splitting or patching upstream packages. This tool is particularly useful when you need to fix git versions with incompatible constraints.
Practical Learning Before assuming there is a solution, it is necessary to trace the configuration parameters from all sources: code defaults, CLI parameters, configuration files, environment variables. The values at runtime may differ from the results of code checks. Refactoring large-scale codebases should be done in stages: 1) Create new structures; 2) Migrate files; 3) Update code references; 4) Update documentation (for archived documents, batch editing is sufficient); 5) Verify the final structure; 6) Clean up old directories. Performing code updates before file migration can lead to data corruption. Conversation Summary MIHD ✅ File structure reorganization, patch query fixes, and RM-IDEAL benchmark testing 00:20:01.434 | claude_code MIHD has been thoroughly maintained, including three steps: 1) File reorganization—a detailed plan of 8 steps was created to migrate output directories to DLPFC, hd_results, rm_ideal, etc., updating 14 code files and documentation, and batch updating archived documents. 2) Patch query fixes—problems caused by mismatched barcode orders between caches (sc.read_10x_h5) and runtime (sc.read_visium) were resolved, as well as issues with inconsistent data paths and coordinate conventions. A clear mapping from barcodes to cache indices was added in 4 files. 3) RM-IDEAL benchmark testing—cross-sectional benchmarks were performed on 151673-151508 pairs using CPUs, yielding baseline metrics (average Spearman r=0.1804, strong positive correlation between Layer_4/Layer_1, negative correlation between Layer_3/Layer_6). Users asked about the centroid calculation method and explained the average embedding vector method used for N pairs of M compression.
robobrain_pi (GPU Monitoring) ✅ Develop GPU monitoring tool similar to nvitop for K8s containers 03:05:47.900 | claude_code Users requested an improvement in the interface of the GPU monitoring tool (the initial version had packaging/truncation issues). The design concept of nvitop was referenced, and a GPU-centric process grouping method was proposed (not caring about non-GPU processes). This is achieved by checking /proc//fd/ for /dev/nvidia* device files and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES environment variables. A double-border table layout similar to nvitop was implemented, using rich libraries, color gradient progress bars, and automatically filtering monitoring tools (nvitop, gpustat, Claude). The final gpumon.py supports real-time refresh (default 2 seconds) and one-time mode, grouping processes under corresponding GPU cards.
error-recovery-benchmark (v5 visualization) ❌ Attempted to visualize v5 error scenarios (task abandonment) 00:07:14.853 | claude_code Users requested the visualization of error scenarios. After checking the code repository, it was found that all v5 task videos were in 480p MP4 format. Users wanted to render them at 720p and chose “all v5 tasks” with 3-5 samples included. Existing videos were identified, and it was suggested to run the 1_v5_execute_injections.py script again, specifying –render –resolution 720. After entering the planning mode, although users repeatedly said “Run now,” they ultimately abandoned the task. Due to environmental limitations or priority changes, the task was abandoned.
pi05 (VLA Training) ✅ Resolved torch/torchvision version incompatibility issue 06:47:41.428 | claude_code Users encountered a training startup error: “RuntimeError: operator torchvision::nms does not exist.” It was diagnosed that the versions of torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0 did not match (0.21.0 was compiled for torch 2.6.x, incompatible with the operator registration in 2.7.1). Users confirmed they needed help. The pyproject.toml file was read, and a clear “torchvision==0.22.1” dependency was added. A uv synchronization operation was performed (upgrading from 0.21.0 to 0.22.1), and verified: torch 2.7.1+cu126, torchvision 0.22.1+cu126. The training environment was restored.
VLA Training Performance (openpi vs pi05) ✅ Resolved 33% training time gap through dependency alignment 07:07:16.613 | claude_code Users reported the same training command (pi05_robotwin2, batch_size=32, 30K steps), but with different ETAs: openpi took 15 hours, while pi05 took 20 hours (a difference of 33%). A parallel exploration agent was launched for in-depth comparison. In the first stage (initial analysis): train.py, configuration files, and model architecture were identical; differences in lerobot’s git rev, IMAGE_KEYS (2 pairs of 3 cameras affecting JIT), conda base environment, and dependency versions were identified. In the second stage (root cause and fixes): it was determined that the JAX version (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3) was the main reason for the 20-30% performance difference. The system aligned 6 shared dependencies in pi05’s pyproject.toml to openpi (JAX, torch, numpy, orbax-checkpoint, beartype, transformers). [tool.uv] coverage dependency was added to resolve lerobot’s torch<2.7 constraint conflict. The uv lock/synchronization operation was successfully completed. Expected result: pi05’s current training time is equivalent to openpi’s 15 hours (due to JAX 0.5.3 XLA optimization, speed increased by about 25%).
