title: Bug Journal 2026-03-08

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-07 Daily Overview Completed Work: Parallel progress on MIHD, ErrorRecovery, DesktopVideo, gadget, and CalendarPro projects. Completed the writing of technical documents, started fixing training processes, developed applications, and built research infrastructure. Completion Methods: Used parallel proxy code libraries to generate documentation, start multi-GPU training tasks, fix errors related to evaluation metrics, implement browsing modes for WKWebView web wallpapers, batch generate historical reports, develop a context-aware intent classification system, and build a research proposal management system with LLM-driven enhancements from scratch. Impact: Established complete MIHD technical specifications and research positioning. Demonstrated that zero-shot scGPT performs significantly worse than PCA. Implemented reliable FLARE/BC-RNN training and evaluation processes. Provided a complete Desktop Video solution for any web wallpaper.filled the historical work record for more than 20 days in February, making it easier to track. Improved the intent classification accuracy of CalendarPro significantly. Established an automated paper discovery and evaluation infrastructure with efficient search functions and bilingual support. DCC Completed Work: Updated MIHD CLAUDE.md accurately, created a research positioning document for overview.md, studied the performance of scGPT/scGPT-spatial, and conducted cross-sample RM-IDEAL benchmarks. Completion Methods: Used parallel proxy code libraries to extract APIs and statistics for incremental documentation fixes. Started 4 parallel proxies to retrieve arXiv papers and benchmark data. Executed Python benchmark scripts to analyze Spearman correlations. Impact: Project documentation was upgraded from descriptive overview to precise technical specifications. Established a patch query narrative based on clinical perspectives for zero-shot and STAIG differentiation. Demonstrated that zero-shot scGPT performs significantly worse than PCA in clustering performance (PCA ARI=0.261 vs scGPT=0.170, a difference of 54%). MacBook Completed Work: Implemented the WKWebView web wallpaper function for Desktop Video (for YouTube/Bilibili, etc.). Fixed issues with browsing mode interactions, scroll bar transparency, and volume synchronization. Implemented a gadget summary tool that can be automatically deployed on Windows/UNIX platforms. Batch generated partial historical reports. Completion Methods: Expanded theContentType enumeration to include.web types. Controlled playback through WKWebView, set up sandbox network permissions, and implemented JS injection techniques. Fixed issues with the window_level=.normal interaction layer by removing drawsBackground. Created a update.ps1 PowerShell script and run_hugo_update() cross-platform detection function. Impact: Desktop Video achieved a complete solution for playing any web video as a desktop wallpaper (solved problems such as sandbox network blocking, no interaction in the window layer, and scroll bar transparency). The gadget tool can be automatically deployed on Windows and UNIX platforms. TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Batched processing of gadget reports from early February to early March for more than 20 devices. Implemented a context-based intent classification system for CalendarPro (verified through 360 tests). Developed a research proposal management system with enhancements from scratch (about 750 lines of core script + two-stage evaluation process), including language configuration, reverse initialization, deduplication, and error fixing. Completion Methods: Repeatedly executed the daily_summary.py export and merge commands to handle data for different dates. Implemented context-based keyword classification and session-aware conversation history management functions. Reused summarize/LLM backend mode for arXiv search and implemented a two-stage LLM evaluation process. Installed Hugo Extended through winget. Enhanced the functionality of research_scout.py, including LLM-driven overview.md parsing, three-layer prompt system language configuration, deduplication based on arxiv_id, and early termination logic. Fixed issues with project ID and directory name mismatches. Impact: Completed the comprehensive work archive from February to early March. Established historical traceability. CalendarPro completely solved the problem of inaccurate intent classification caused by keyword pre-checking (e.g., “What time is it?”). Established an automated research paper discovery and evaluation infrastructure with bilingual output, efficient search functions, and reverse project initialization capabilities for 3 to 5 active projects. tianhe Completed Work: Started FLARE D0×4 training (running smoothly, each training took 6.3 seconds). Fixed issues with 4 BC-RNN evaluation metrics (demo_len/trigger_step/transport/RP). Conducted tests on 191 multi-task error scenarios. Started LLaVA MPM/MCM parallel training. Optimized Phoenix data loading (from 5% to over 80% GPU utilization). Resolved the bottleneck problem in downloading LLaVA base models through offline format conversion. Completion Methods: Used PYTHONPATH isolation to solve openpi configuration conflicts. Fixed BC-RNN metric distortion issues through code review. Implemented get_target_pos() function for dynamic target position retrieval during runtime. Achieved 6-task shared generation scripts through task_registry dynamic routing. Obtained LLaVA base models through offline safetensors→pytorch_model.bin format conversion. Resolved the training bottleneck problem caused by ZeRO-3→ZeRO-2+HDF5 preloading. Impact: FLARE 4 D0 tasks ran smoothly on an an53 GPU (each task took about 63 hours). BC-RNN evaluation metrics were reliable (all 127 unit tests passed, with GPU verification RP reaching up to 0.800). The 191 error scenarios laid the foundation for subsequent training. LLaVA MPM/MCM training time was shortened from the expected 13.5 days to about 2 days of parallel startup. However, there is an issue with estimating the dataset size, which requires replanning. The Phoenix GPU optimization solution is ready. Parallel processing on four devices: Completed MIHD documentation system and scGPT research. Started FLARE/Phoenix training and fixed 4 BC-RNN evaluation errors. Implemented Desktop Video web wallpaper function with WKWebView. Batch generated historical reports for more than 20 days in February. Completed CalendarPro context-based intent classification. Developed a complete research proposal management system with language configuration, reverse initialization, and deduplication functions from scratch.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ 4 Fixes to BC-RNN Evaluation Process — Corrected the name error of demo_length→demo_len in collector.py. Fixed the mistake of reading trigger_step from scene.dataset instead of scene.detected_by. Fixed the detection logic in the transport stage (added __get_target_pos() function to dynamically obtain target positions). Added a if success: rp=1.0 condition judgment in collector.py. All 127 unit tests passed. GPU verification showed demo_len=605, trigger_step=55, with RP reaching up to 0.800. ✅ Complete Implementation of Desktop Video WKWebView Web Wallpaper — Created WebNavigationHandler.swift file, expanded theContentType enumeration to include.web types, implemented showWeb/cleanupWebView/saveWebBookmark functions, updated the playback state management in AppDelegate, updated the UI of SingleScreenView (URL input field), updated history, thumbnails, localization, and ChangeLog. Added sandbox network permissions, set customUserAgent to modern Chrome UA. ✅ Develop Research Scout System from Scratch — Developed research/research_scout.py script from scratch (about 750 lines of core code), including project CRUD functions, arXiv search (arxiv package), support for three LLM backends (anthropic/openai/claude_cli), paper evaluation scoring, direction suggestion generation, weekly report output (JSON+Markdown), Hugo deployment, and caching mechanism. Designed a two-stage paper evaluation process: first stage quickly screens all papers (title+abstract, judging relevance levels) → second stage conducts in-depth analysis of highly relevant papers (≤20 papers, 3 highlights: insights/ motivation/ experiments/limitations). Completed the writing of requirements.txt, CLAUDE.md, and TUTORIAL.md Chinese tutorials. ✅ Accuracy Update of MIHD CLAUDE.md — Added implementation details for section 3.2 (detector/injector verifier/classifier coordinator, 5 sections) corrected VLA model type (Pi0-FAST→Phoenix/Flare), updated code statistics (script 18→22, test cases 109→122), updated M14 actual evaluation data (726→6474 cases), added information about database stage/severity distribution, explained the process configuration structure, and detailed the cache directory structure. ✅ Create MIHD overview.md Research Positioning Document — Created a 676-line overview.md file, including a one-sentence positioning description (system evaluation of zero-shot embedding of base models in space transcriptomics multi-modal fusion clustering), 4 core questions, 3-stage technical roadmap, ranking of 8 fusion strategies, 8 open-ended questions, search keywords, literature annotation template, quick reference to project structure, and quick start command. ✅ Systematic Research on scGPT/scGPT-Space and Multi-View Performance — Obtained papers on scGPT-space, zero-shot limit papers, BioLLM framework papers. Analyzed internal results of MIHD through 4 parallel proxies. The summary analysis showed that zero-shot scGPT clustering was essentially inferior to multi-view methods (PCA ARI=0.261 vs scGPT=0.170, a difference of 54%). 🔄 Implementation and Fixing of Desktop Video Browsing Mode and Window Layer — Implemented the browsing mode framework (WallpaperWindow dynamic canBecomeKey, ESC listener program, exit via control panel buttons). The window layer was adjusted multiple times. It was confirmed that the.normal layer could not receive any mouse/keyboard events, so it was used but not fully implemented yet. ✅ Implementation of CalendarPro Context-Aware Intent Classification System — Implemented a context-aware intent classification system (divided general_keywords into always_general and context_dependent to avoid incorrect classification due to vague queries like “What time is it?”). Implemented session-aware conversation history management (automatic compression, session reset, token budget control). Completed the A/B/C/D four-phase plan, added 41 special tests, and all 360 tests passed. ✅ Fixing Errors in Recovering Project Configuration — Fixed 3 key errors: mismatch between ID and directory name in project.json, chapter titles in overview.md not conforming to process regular expression patterns, and lack of automatic tagging. 🔄 Starting FLARE D0×4 Task Training — Ran coffee_D0, stack_D0, stack_three_D0, threading_D0 Pi0.5 LoRA warm-up training on an an53 GPU. Each task had about 36K steps (about 63 hours). The first attempt failed due to PYTHONPATH issues but was successfully restarted after fixing them. Now it runs smoothly, with each step taking 6.3 seconds. ❌ Starting LLaVA MPM/MCM Parallel Training — Confirmed that the paper did not publish LaVA LoRA checkpoints. The cluster lacked a complete llava-v1.5-7b base model. MPM training data llava_motion.json (460MB, 14M lines) was ready. Obtained the base model through offline safetensors→pytorch_model.bin format conversion. Modified lora.sh to use ZeRO-3 to ZeRO-2 to avoid gradient division issues. Started MP- ✅ Generate and improve the CLAUDE.md file for research — Analyzed 1871 lines of the research_scout.py code library, and created a complete CLAUDE.md file containing code organization sections (0-10 chapters), parameter tables, key implementation details, and pipeline function names. ✅ Fixing video transparency issues on desktop and volume synchronization problems — Removed webViewsetValue(false, forKey: “drawsBackground”) to fix the transparency issue with WKWebView scroll bars. Added .background(Color(nsColor:.windowBackgroundColor)) in AppMainWindow.swift to fix the transparency issue with the control panel slider. removed the isWebContent guard in setVolume() so that volume/mute controls are visible for all types of content. Fixed the applyWebSettings function to check for global restrictions when switching content for volume synchronization. ✅ Hugo installation and configuration on Windows — There was an error where ‘hugo’ couldn’t be recognized during the merge step in daily_summary.py. Installed Hugo.Hugo.Extended version 0.157.0 using winget. It was necessary to restart PowerShell to make the PATH environment variable take effect, and finally successfully executed Hugo build and deployment. ✅ Change all ‘weekly reports’ to ‘daily reports’ — There were systematic text replacements in more than 12 places: function names (generate_weekly_report→generate_daily_report), documentation strings, user interface messages, Markdown headings. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. BC-RNN RP(0.003) being significantly lower than the random RP(0.020), making the evaluation results completely unreasonable Solution: Code review identified 4 errors that caused metric distortion: demo_len=0 corrupted SPL, trigger_step=0 further corrupted SPL, transport always limited RP to a maximum of 0.5 False, and successful episode RP not being 1.0 led to baseline bias. Fixed field name demo_length→demo_len in collector.py:487, changed collector.py:488 to read trigger_step from scene datasets, env_wrapper.py added get_target_pos() to dynamically obtain target positions, and collector.py added if success: rp=1.0.
Key Insight: The distortion of evaluation metrics is usually not a single error, but a chain reaction of multiple errors. The limit of RP being 0.5 revealed failures in the transmission phase, demo_len=0/trigger_step=0 revealed field propagation errors.
2. WKWebView cannot receive mouse/k keyboard events during browsing mode, even with ignoresMouseEvents=false; the.docdesktopWindow layer causes non-interactive window operations Solution: Set window.level=.normal when entering BrowseMode (not the top level but interactive), and restore CGWindowLevelForKey(.desktopWindow) when exiting BrowseMode.
Key Insight: In macOS, the.docdesktopWindow layer is lower than the Finder desktop icon layer. Windows at this layer do not receive any input events at system level. IgnoringMouseEvents=false has no effect on this layer. .normal(level=0) is the lowest level that is both interactive and does not affect other applications.
3. LLaVA base model download was hindered due to proxy bandwidth bottlenecks (about 50KB/s, downloading 14GB of models took several weeks); GPU nodes could not be directly connected; xet CDN overproxy timed out Solution: Found nyw format llava-1.5-7b-hf (safetensors) in the cluster, and wrote convert_hf_to_llava.py to convert HF format to the original LLaVA pytorch_model.bin format (including vocabulary truncation from 32064→32000). The entire process was done locally without needing a network.
Key Insight: Don’t assume that downloading is necessary. First search for existing model copies on the cluster, and even different formats can be converted. Formatting HF models involves more than just renaming keys; it also involves semantic differences (such as vocabulary size).
4. GPU utilization for Phoenix diffusion strategy training was only 5-6%: DataLoader num_workers=0 caused GPUs to wait for CPU to read data from NFS HDF5 files randomly Solution: Preload all HDF5 data into memory in one go during init method (uint8 type saves 4 times more storage space), close file handles, and then perform all getitem operations on the numpy array. Set DataLoader’s num_workers to 8 with pin_memory=True.
Key Insight: h5py file handling cannot be shared across subprocesses (this is a limitation of PyTorch DataLoader). The only safe solution is to complete loading in the main process and then fork the subprocess. NFS random reading is much slower than sequential reading, and one-time loading is 10 times faster than按需加载.
5. After starting Phoenix MPM training, serious estimation errors were found (actual time was 13.5 days, expected few hours), dataset size estimation error (160K→1M) Solution: The user paused the training and needed to re-plan and optimize it (possibly reducing training steps, increasing GPU count, optimizing data loading, etc.)
Key Insight: Before starting large-scale training, it is necessary to verify the actual dataset size, time per step, and total number of steps to avoid wasting resources. Errors in dataset size estimation can lead to weeks of wasted computing resources.
6. Fuzziness in CalendarPro intent classification was caused by pre-checking general_keywords: ‘What time are you scheduled?’, matching general_keywords with ‘what time’ led to forcing GENERAL, resulting in an error Solution: Divide general_keywords into always_general (always force GENERAL, such as ‘weather’) and context_dependent (force GENERAL only when there is no active session, such as ‘what time’); add confirmation_words(‘ok’, ‘good’, ‘yes’, etc.) to handle quick paths through_is_confirmation_input(); rewritten_process_input() to have a 6-step process
Key Insight: Context-based intent classification requires combining session state (session_id, last_activity_at) and conversation history. Simple keyword matching leads to fuzziness; when there is an active proposal, confirmation phrases (‘ok’) should be routed directly to that proposal.
7. The current research scout paper evaluation process handles all 50 papers at once, wasting resources and lacking in-depth analysis Solution: Designed a two-stage process: the first stage quickly screens all papers (only titles + first 1000 characters of abstracts, classifying high/low relevance), and the second stage deeply analyzes highly relevant papers (up to 20, extracting 3 highlights + detailed scoring + direction suggestions)
Key Insight: Mimicking the paper reading workflow of human researchers (quick screening → deep reading of high-value papers) can significantly improve the efficiency and quality of LLM evaluation, while also generating literature review records for low-relevance papers for future reference
8. Due to the ID (‘robot-manipulation’) in project.json not matching the directory name (’error-recovery-benchmark’), the process could not read overview.md file Solution: Updated the id field in project.json to match the directory name; the process uses project[‘id’] to build the overview.md path
Key Insight: The directory name and ID in project.json must be consistent; _extract_current_methods() uses project[‘id’] to locate overview.md file
9. In the second-stage evaluation, the title of the overview.md section ‘## 3. Current technical approach’ did not match the process’s regular expression pattern ‘##\s*Current method and progress’, resulting in the current method getting a result of ‘(no record found)’ Solution: Changed the title of the overview.md chapter to match the OVERVIEW_TEMPLATE format; removed numbering prefixes and chapter separators
Key Insight: The pipeline uses strict regular expressions for chapter extraction; it must strictly follow the template format
10. FLARE specification statistics calculation failed: After importing openpi in the script, it was not possible to find the installation version of zhaoganlong, nor could the new configuration name of tangzijia be found Solution: Set PYTHONPATH=/HOME/…/tangzijia/shared_deps/openpi/src:$PYTHONPATH in the script to优先加载 tangzijia’s openpi source code, bypassing older versions in the conda environment
Key Insight: When multiple users share the same conda environment, their modifications can lead to import priority conflicts. PYTHONPATH is a minimal intervention solution that does not affect other users
11. WKWebView scroll bars were tracking transparent states (CSS injection to set background color was ineffective) + Sandbox applications could not load any network resources Solution: Removed webView.setValue(false, forKey: “drawsBackground”) to restore the default opaque background, making scroll bar tracking visible; added com.apple.security.network.client=true in the.configurations file
Key Insight: DrainsBackground=false makes the entire WKWebView rendering layer transparent, while CSS’s translucent style on a transparent layer remains transparent. The fix must occur at the rendering layer rather than the CSS layer. By default, the App Sandbox prevents any external network access
12. LLM output contained too much English content; users expect Chinese; unable to configure output language Solution: Implemented a three-tier language system: _DEFAULT_LANGUAGE constant, _language_instruction() generator, and {language_instruction} in all prompts;
Configuration resolution chain is CLI > project.json > config.json > default
Key Insight: Language instructions must be injected at the prompt level, not later in the processing stage; different chapters require different language rules (JSON keys vs. content)
13. Unable to create a project from existing overview.md, unless keywords/categories are manually extracted Solution: Added create_project_from_overview() function, which uses LLM to parse overview.md and extract titles, search_keywords, arxiv_categories, open_questions; then writes to project.json and copies the original overview.md file
Key Insight: LLMs can reliably extract structured metadata from unstructured text; reuse existing call_llm() and parse_json_response() tools
14. When generating reports in bulk, the JSON output generated by LLM often had incomplete or syntax errors, leading to parsing failures Solution: After the user provided the original text, AI manually extracted and repaired JSON fragments (removing non-JSON prefixes/suffixes, fixing quote escapes, adding missing closing parentheses, etc.)
Key Insight: LLMs generate structured output, especially JSON with many nested objects and long text content, which can have formatting errors; automatic repair and verification steps need to be added to the pipeline
General Issues 15. Unable to find Hugo commands on Windows environment, daily_summary.py merge step called website/update.ps1 failed because ‘hugo’ was not recognized Solution: Installed Hugo version 0.157.0 using winget. After installation, it was indicated that a restart of the shell was required. After重启PowerShell, the hugo command worked properly.
Key Insight: On Windows environment, command-line tools installed through winget need to be updated in the PATH before they can be used normally. They cannot be used immediately in the same session
Comparison of Human and Artificial Intelligence Methods Strategic Level BC-RNN RP abnormality diagnosis strategy Role Method Human The user noticed that during M14, BC-RNN SR was close to 0%, which did not match the training progress. The user directly pointed out possible errors in the evaluation metrics and asked for a systematic check of the metric calculation logic | Artificial Intelligence | Initially, artificial intelligence tended to explain that ’error recovery is inherently difficult’, trying to interpret low SR as an expected result rather than prioritizing suspicion of errors in the evaluation code |
Difference Analysis: Humans infer evaluation problems based on normal behavior observed during training, while AI tends to rationalize abnormal data. Human intuition is more accurate, and ultimately identified 4 chain errors that caused metric distortion
Risk assessment before training starts Role Method Human After Phoenix training started, the user immediately noticed serious estimation errors in training time (13.5 days) and decided to stop it to avoid wasting resources | Artificial Intelligence | Before training began, artificial intelligence did not fully verify the actual dataset size (mistakenly treating 160K as the actual 1M), nor did it evaluate the time per step (7.2 seconds/step) and total number of steps (161K), resulting in serious estimation errors |
Difference Analysis: Humans are better at risk control and timely stopping losses, while AI tends to ignore preliminary verification of resource consumption when performing tasks
Paper reading workflow design Role Method Human The user proposed a two-stage reading strategy - a quick screening stage (30 seconds to decide whether to read in depth) and an in-depth understanding stage (finding 3 key points: motivation/ method insights/ experimental design/limitations) | Artificial Intelligence | Initially, artificial intelligence designed a one-time complete evaluation, sending the full abstracts of all 50 papers to LLM for scoring at once |
Difference Analysis: Humans are designed based on the cognitive load of actual researchers. They recognize that quick screening and in-depth analysis require different levels of information. While AI tends to handle things in one go, ignoring the trade-off between tokens cost and evaluation depth
Debugging project configuration errors Role Method Human After the user provided the overview.md path, it asked the AI to ‘fix the format’, but did not diagnose the root cause | Artificial Intelligence | Through code tracing, artificial intelligence discovered 3 interconnected vulnerabilities: incorrect ID, incorrect title formatting, lack of markers |
Difference Analysis: The systematic code analysis by artificial intelligence reveals chain problems that users cannot see; users correctly identified symptoms (“unable to read overview”), but failed to find the root cause
Iterative decision-making process for Window layer solutions Role Method Human Directly rejected the.floating solution from the user’s perspective, and after AI proposed a non-interactive.documentsWindow, decisively pointed out that the.normal layer should be used | Artificial Intelligence | Artificial intelligence tried various technically feasible solutions: first using.floating to ensure interactivity, then rejecting it and instead keeping.documentsWindow, discovering non-interactivity and waiting for user instructions |
Difference Analysis: Humans quickly rejected solutions that were not reasonable from the perspective of user experience and expected behavior. While AI correctly identified fuzziness and proposed problems rather than guessing
Identifying missing language configuration functions Role Method Human The user immediately realized the existence of an UX problem (“too much English in the summary section”) and asked if language could be configured | Artificial Intelligence | Artificial intelligence did not consider language configuration before asking for a solution from the user; only after that implemented a comprehensive solution |
Implementation Level Adjusting the number of FLARE training tasks Role Method Human After AI started 9 training tasks, the user requested reducing the number of tasks to 4 D0-level tasks, clearly indicating that only 4 D0-level difficulty tasks were needed | Artificial Intelligence | Artificial intelligence initially planned to conduct a complete 9-train task (3×D0+2×D1+1×D2), trying to complete all tasks at once |Token Usage Analysis
Metric Value Total Tokens 124,857,846 Input Tokens 137,406 Output Tokens 393,471 Cache Creation 13,954,478 Cache Reads 110,372,491 Cache Hit Rate 88.8% Total Cost (USD) $106.2547 —— —— claude-opus-4-6 52,817 claude-sonnet-4-6 544 claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 84,045 Usage per Device Device Total Tokens Input Output Cost MacBook 22,837,773 8,116 78,975 $19.2400 tianhe 73,970,131 115,503 221,037 $46.2873 TzJsDesktop 28,049,942 13,787 93,459 $40.7274
summary: Parallel operations were carried out on four devices: development of the MIHD documentation system and related research work for scGPT was completed. The FLARE/Phoenix training tools were also launched. Four evaluation issues related to BC-RNN were resolved. The wallpaper function for desktop video pages was implemented using WKWebView. Historical report data for February, spanning over 20 days, was generated in bulk. Development of the context-based intent classification feature in CalendarPro was also completed. Additionally, a complete scientific paper management system was built from scratch, featuring language configuration, reverse initialization, and duplicate removal capabilities.
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-07

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-06 Daily Overview Completed Work: Involved in six major projects, including spatial transcriptomics analysis, debugging and documentation of the life management chatbot, refactoring the robot control benchmark code library, designing training processes, and containerized simulation rendering. Implementation Approach: Used a parallel multi-agent exploration method to quickly understand the code library, systematically tracked the data flow and context, reorganized projects through symbolic links, matched driver versions with library versions for Docker rendering, and designed comprehensive training infrastructure. Impact: Resolved key data format issues that affected research, diagnosed and designed solutions to the user-side context loss problem, wrote a complete tutorial of 1674 lines, refactored the research project, reduced disk usage by 99% (from 78GB to 37MB), removed Docker GPU rendering from robot simulation evaluations, optimized 67 lines of documentation, and built an end-to-end training process covering 9 tasks, while adopting resource optimization strategies. DCC Completed Work: Analyzed the standardization and preprocessing processes in the ContraVAE and STHD spatial transcriptomics projects, clarifying data formats and processing steps. Implementation Approach: Reviewed h5ad files, tracked preprocessing scripts, verified data features to track the entire process from raw counts to standardized expression matrices. Impact: Eliminated user confusion about data formats, confirmed the meanings and handling methods of X matrices, layers[‘counts’], HVG selections, and spatial coordinates under different preprocessing methods. TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Fixed the CalendarPro Discord robot context loss issue and wrote a 1674-line Chinese tutorial covering end-users and developers. Implementation Approach: Used parallel agents to explore dialogue history management, intent classification, and AI provider call chains, designed a context-based repair solution; systematically extracted all configurations, commands, and architectures to generate structured tutorials. Impact: Identified the three root causes of robot forgetting issues (keyword pre-checking, timing selection, lack of historical parameters), and developed feasible solutions; created complete documentation resources covering user guides (60%) and developer guides (40%). tianhe Completed Work: Optimized the error_recovery_benchmark CLAUDE.md document, reorganized the motion-based self-reflection framework into independent Phoenix/FLARE projects, shared dependencies, designed an end-to-end training process covering 9 MimicGen tasks, created CALVIN documentation, resolved Docker SAPIEN rendering errors, and documented the RoboTwin simulation architecture. Implementation Approach: Simplified document redundancy, managed directory separation through symbolic links to reduce disk usage by 99%, explored the training framework architecture, wrote 7 scripts (data generation, conversion, training, evaluation, orchestration), matched driver versions with library versions and configured the environment to adapt to Vulkan/OpenGL stacks, tracked multi-layer simulation code. Impact: Reduced the number of lines in the documentation from 225 to 167, improved readability, established maintainable project structures (Phoenix 37MB, FLARE 368KB, shared dependencies 9.3GB), built an end-to-end training process including configuration completion and checkpoint reuse strategies, resolved key Docker rendering obstacles that prevented model evaluation, and documented the complex code library used for introductory purposes. The work across projects covered spatial transcriptomics data analysis, CalendarPro error fixing and comprehensive documentation writing, refactoring the robot learning code library, solving Docker rendering stack issues, and designing the Phoenix/FLARE training process.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Analyze Spatial Transcriptomics Data Preprocessing Process — Traced the standardization steps in ContraVAE and STHD projects, clarified the relationship between X matrices and layers[‘counts’], HVG selections, and spatial coordinate systems. ✅ Diagnose CalendarPro Context Loss Problem — Identified three levels of issues that caused robots to lose subsequent message context, and developed solutions. ✅ Design Phoenix/FLARE9 Task Training Plan — Designed complete Phoenix diffusion strategies and FLARE retry frameworks for all MimicGen tasks. ✅ Resolve Docker Container SAPIEN Rendering Error — Diagnosed and fixed the “rendering error” that prevented robot simulation evaluations. The root cause was the lack of NVIDIA Vulkan/OpenGL libraries in Docker. Downloaded the appropriate driver version 535.104.12, extracted the library files, and configured VK_ICD_FILENAMES and LD_LIBRARY_PATH environment variables. ✅ Write Comprehensive CalendarPro Chinese Tutorial — Created a 1674-line docs/TUTORIAL.md document covering installation, features, CLI/Discord commands, architecture, and developer guides. ✅ Separate Phoenix and FLARE Projects and Manage Shared Dependencies — Reorganized the motion-based self-reflection framework into two independent projects (Phoenix for self-reflection, FLARE for post-failure reset), using symbolic links to manage dependency projects. By symbolically linking large data/checkpoint directories back to files, disk usage for each project was reduced from 78GB to 37MB. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Create Phoenix Training Script — Built SLURM job scripts for Phoenix multi-task diffusion strategy training. ✅ Create FLARE Data Generation and Training Script — Built warm-up data generation, LeRobot conversion, training, and evaluation scripts for the FLARE process. ✅ Document RoboTwin Data Collection and Simulation Architecture — Provided a detailed explanation of the two-stage data collection process (seed finding + replay with deterministic seeds), using the place_dual_shoes task as an example to trace the complete simulation execution process. ✅ Optimize error_recovery_benchmark CLAUDE.md Document — Reduced the number of lines from 225 to 167 by removing command sections, adding PYTHONPATH details, and simplifying reference relationships. ✅ Complete OpenPI Training Configuration — Added missing fine-tuning and inference configurations for coffee_task_D1, stack_three_task_D1, and three_piece_assembly_task_D1. ✅ Fix Frozen dataclass mutation errors during strategy loading — Resolved the FrozenInstanceError issue when loading pi05 strategy. Fixed this issue by replacing direct attribute assignments with dataclasses.replace(), and also fixed path duplication errors in norm_stats loading. ✅ Create CLAUDE.md Document for CALVIN Project — Studied the CALVIN code library (combining actions from language and visual benchmarks) and wrote a complete project document covering architecture, build/test commands, Hydra configuration system, and MCIL model processes. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. CalendarPro robots cannot understand subsequent messages, such as “What time is your schedule?” Solution: Identified three levels of problems: (1) general_keywords pre-checking executed before the AI saw the dialogue history; (2) pre-checking used overly broad keywords like ‘what time’; (3) the AI provider’s call did not receive historical data parameters. The designed solution is to eliminate pre-check bypass behavior and pass the complete dialogue history to the AI. Key Insight: Context loss is usually a multi-level problem: data not collected → data not passed → data not used. It must be tracked from storage to the provider interface to prompt construction.
2. SAPIEN encounters ‘rendering error’ in headless Docker containers - RuntimeError: Rendering device not found Solution: Discovered that NVIDIA Vulkan/OpenGL rendering libraries (libGLX_nvidia.so, etc.) were missing. The container had the CUDA computing library but no rendering stack. Downloaded the matching NVIDIA driver version 535.104.12 to match the kernel driver, extracted the library files, and configured VK_ICD_FILENAMES and LD_LIBRARY_PATH environment variables in eval.sh.
Key Insight: Docker containers with NVIDIA GPUs require CUDA runtime and Vulkan/OpenGL libraries for graphical simulations. The driver version must strictly match the kernel module and user space library; otherwise, the Vulkan ICD will not load.
3. Phoenix training checkpoints exist in zhaoganlong’s directory, unclear whether to reuse or retrain Solution: Users indicated that existing checkpoints should be checked and used via symbolic links only when available, and only training the missing parts on an53 idle GPUs.
Key Insight: Before designing a training plan, it is necessary to inventory existing resources (checkpoints, datasets, configurations) to avoid wasting computing resources. Establish a checkpoint reuse strategy first.
4. Users believe standardized data comes from the STHD process, but data characteristics do not match the process code Solution: By tracing the code, it was found that STHD does not perform standardization—it directly uses raw counts. The standardized data users saw came from external scanpy preprocessing (normalize_total + log1p).
Key Insight: It is necessary to distinguish between processing within the process and external preprocessing. Code inspection is more reliable than assumptions based on data appearance.
General Problems 5. Missing warm-up training configurations for 3 out of 9 MimicGen tasks in OpenPI configuration Solution: Used add_finetune_config and add_inference_config helper functions to add fine-tuning and protection model configurations for coffee_task_D1, stack_three_task_D1, and three_piece_assembly_task_D1.
Key Insight: Use configuration generation functions to maintain consistency across task configurations instead of manually repeating the same steps.
6. FrozenInstanceError: Unable to assign field ‘asset_id’ to field in policy_config.py Solution: DataConfig was defined as @dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True), making it unchangeable. Use dataclasses.replace(data_config, asset_id=value) to replace direct assignment (data_config.asset_id = value), allowing for the creation of a new instance.
Key Insight: Frozen data classes enforce immutability at the language level—modifications require using dataclasses.replace() to create a new instance, rather than modifying it in place.
7. FileNotFoundError, path duplication: assets/norm_stats.json/norm_stats.json Solution: When robotwin_repo_id was provided, asset_id was incorrectly set to ’norm_stats.json’ (file name instead of directory). The modified code directly used normalize.load() from the assets/ directory without adding an asset_id sub-directory.
Key Insight: Path construction errors are usually caused by mixing directory identifiers with file names. Explicit handling for special cases (local files vs remote repositories) can prevent path duplication.
8. MulticoreTSNE build failed due to CMake version incompatibility (system 3.26.4 not compatible with <=3.18) Solution: It was found that MulticoreTSNE is only used for optional TSNE visualization, not the core training/evaluation. Recommended to comment out dependencies to proceed with installation (user refused this approach).
Key Insight: It is necessary to distinguish between critical dependencies and optional visualization tools. In research code, non-essential dependencies that cause build problems can usually be skipped.
Comparison of Human and AI Methods Strategic Level Detecting robot context loss issues from the user experience Role Method Human Users noticed from the end-user perspective that robots failed when answering obvious questions like “What time is your schedule?” AI AI diagnosed the root cause by tracing three levels of code: keyword pre-checking mechanism, passing historical data in provider calls, and double-verification intent process Difference Analysis: Humans detect symptoms based on usage experience; AI diagnoses root causes through systematic code analysis. However, AI does not actively monitor the system to prevent such problems—it relies on user error reports.
Reusing checkpoints before training Role Method Human Users indicated that existing checkpoints should be reused via symbolic links, and only the missing parts should be trained for resource efficiency AI AI designed a complete training plan from scratch without first checking existing checkpoints Difference Analysis: Humans have cost awareness and practical limitations; AI tends to adopt simpler implementations. Before planning new work, it is necessary to inventory existing resources.
Implementation Level Installing NVIDIA libraries in a limited Docker environment Role Method Human Due to container security settings, users immediately knew they needed to use custom paths/app/ubuntu/bin/apt-get to access apt-get AI AI initially tried using standard apt-get commands, which led to failures or required sudo privileges Difference Analysis: Humans have domain knowledge of specific container package manager configurations, which cannot be learned from the code library. AI learned this through user corrections.
Tutorial target audience decision-making Role Method Human Users clearly knew that the tutorial should serve both end-users and developers AI AI initially asked about users/developers, not inferred from existing documentation—README for users, CLAUDE.md for developers—a comprehensive tutorial was needed to bridge the gap Difference Analysis: Users have a clearer understanding of project requirements; AI should infer missing documentation gaps from existing distribution versions instead of raising obvious questions.
Handling MulticoreTSNE dependency conflicts Role Method Human Users refused to edit files to comment out MulticoreTSNE, possibly preferring to install the tool in a different way or accepting this limitation AI AI immediately suggested editing requirements.txt to remove problematic dependencies as a quick solution Difference Analysis: Users may want to retain the original configuration or have other solutions in mind. AI mainly focused on removing obstacles that prevented progress, but did not first confirm user preferences.
AI Limitations Key Limitations Did not check existing checkpoints when designing the training plan - Need to remind users to consider reuse strategies. Before implementing, resources should always be inventoried. Initially tried to install NVIDIA graphics libraries (libnvidia-gl-535) without first checking kernel driver version compatibility, resulting in version mismatch (535.288.01 vs 535.104.12). Multiple iterations were required to find the correct download match for the driver and extract the library. General Limitations Although the exploration of dialogue history records was very comprehensive, it required 3 parallel agents to identify the core problem where LLM calls did not receive historical parameters - If the initial exploration had been more targeted, the problem could have been identified faster. Directly creating a tutorial file of 1674 lines without first previewing the structure to confirm user opinions - This method should be verified on larger works. Modified test_render.py by replacing the bare ’except:’ with ’except Exception as e:’, but this pattern (swallowing exceptions without logging) exists in the original code library and was not introduced by AI. AI should have identified this anti-pattern earlier. Multiple runs/init commands conducted similar explorations and analyses, but did not remember previous evaluation results - Lack of session memory functionality. Spent a lot of time conducting systematic explorations (finding commands, vulkaninfo checks), which could have been shortened if AI had a more direct understanding of common Docker+NVIDIA Vulkan setup patterns. Ultimately completed successfully, but the steps were more than what experts would require. Learning Outcomes Key Learnings### Practical Learning Outcomes Python frozen data classes provide guaranteed compile-time immutability, but explicit modifications using dataclasses.replace() are required. Direct assignment will trigger FrozenInstanceError. This pattern forces configuration objects to adopt a functional programming style. Conversation Summary ContraVAE ✅ Analysis of Space Transcriptomics Data Standardization Process 16:37:59.200 | claude_code The user inquired about the standardization of the expression matrix in the data.h5ad file. The AI reviewed the file and found discrepancies between the user’s description and the actual data. It was determined that the user was viewing all_region/adata_8um.h5ad (version with 8μm binning). The complete 4×4 binning + normalize_total + log1p process was confirmed, and it was verified that layers[‘counts’] contained the original UMI counts filtered by Space Ranger.
STHD ✅ Verification of STHD Project Expression Matrix Preprocessing 16:40:51.890 | claude_code Similar data standardization issues regarding the STHD project were addressed. The AI analyzed the STHD code and found that the process did not perform any standardization - directly using raw counts as input for the Poisson model. The user’s standardized data (range 0.2-6) came from external scanpy preprocessing outside of the STHD workflow. It was confirmed that HVG used the scanpy seurat version, with spatial coordinates being full-resolution pixels.
CalendarPro 🔄 Debugging Discord Bot Context Loss Issues in Subsequent Messages 21:03:53.486 | claude_code The user reported that the bot’s response to the obvious subsequent message “When is your scheduled time?” was “I don’t understand”. The AI initiated 3 parallel agents to explore conversation history storage, message queue processing, and the calling patterns of the AI provider. Three issues were identified: the general_keywords pre-check bypassed the AI before seeing historical data, the pre-check used overly broad keywords, and the provider calls did not receive historical data. A fix was designed to remove keyword bypassing and pass on complete conversation history.
✅ Implementing a Comprehensive Chinese Tutorial for Life Copilot 00:51:21.027 | claude_code The user requested the implementation of a pre-designed tutorial plan. The AI used 4 parallel agents to read key source files (configuration, routing, scheduler, services, CLI, API). All configuration items, commands, and architectural details were extracted. A docs/TUTORIAL.md document was created (1674 lines), covering 19 main sections, including user guides (60%) and developer guides (40%). Consistency with the actual code was confirmed.
🔍 Designing a Comprehensive Usage Tutorial 00:32:39.653 | claude_code The user requested a detailed CalendarPro tutorial. The AI used 3 agents to explore project settings, Discord interface, and scheduling system. The user asked about their saving location (docs/TUTORIAL.md selected) and target audience (including users and developers). A two-tier documentation structure was designed. The user canceled ExitPlanMode, possibly to modify the plan.
error_recovery_benchmark**🔄 Designing Phoenix and FLARE Training Processes for 9 MimicGen Tasks** 20:25:19.703 | claude_code The user requested Phoenix and FLARE training plans for all MimicGen tasks. The AI delved into the architectures of Phoenix (diffusion strategy) and FLARE (Pi0.5 LoRA retry framework), data requirements, and existing scripts. 8 tasks were created and implemented: Phoenix’s SLURM training script, FLARE warm-up data generation, LeRobot conversion, OpenPI configuration completion (3 missing tasks: coffee_task_D1, stack_three_task_D1, three_piece_assembly_task_D1), FLARE training script, evaluation script, main process orchestration script, project summary update. The user suggested checking existing checkpoints for reuse; a session interruption occurred during the checkpoint list process.**
✅ Implementing the CLAUDE.md Optimization Plan 20:23:17.875 | claude_code The AI carried out 4 scheduled CLAUD.md improvements: reducing commands from 80 lines to 40 lines (moving specific targets to ‘make help’ reference), adding the PYTHONPATH actual path (mimicgen_workspace/robosuite, mimicgen_workspace/mimicgen), adding pytest single-function examples, and reducing relevant projects from 4 lines to 1 line through external documentation references. The number of files was reduced from 225 lines to 167 lines while retaining all key information.
🔍 /init Command - Analyzing Code Library and Suggesting Improvements to CLADE.md 17:37:19.757 | claude_code The user ran the/init command. The AI studied the code library structure (error frameworks, scripts, configuration, testing) and read the existing CLAUD.md (225 lines) and AGENTS.md. It was found that the existing documentation was already comprehensive. 4 targeted improvements were proposed: streamlining the command section, adding PYTHONPATH paths, increasing single-function test examples, compressing relevant project sections. The user canceled the operation after creating the plan.**
✅ Explanation of Proxy Server Mechanism 20:31:05.355 | claude_code The user inquired why using ‘source setproxy.sh 172.16.31.200 3138’ could enable network access. The AI read the script and explained the proxy mechanism: setting the http_proxy environment variable to route traffic through an internal proxy server with external internet connection. The difference between the ‘source’ and ‘bash’ persistence of environment variables was clarified, as well as the non-proxy handling of internal traffic. The user asked about bandwidth and multi-user impacts; the AI explained the bandwidth limitations of shared proxies, concurrent overhead, and recommendations for downloading during off-peak hours.**
Movement-Based Self-Reflection Framework ✅ Reorganization of Project Separation and Shared Dependencies 16:41:37.220 | claude_code The process was completed to separate Phoenix (movement-based self-reflection) and FLARE (failure-driven reset) into independent projects under the tangzijia/ directory. A shared_deps/ folder containing 9 packages (mimicgen, openpi, robosuite, etc.) was created. The 78GB of data/data checks were replaced with symbolic links. Final structure: Phoenix/ 37MB, FLARE/ 368KB, shared_deps/ 9.3GB. Comprehensive CLAUD.md files containing architecture details and command references were written for both projects.**
CALVIN Benchmarking 🔄 Initializing Project Documentation for Robot Learning Benchmarks 02:09:21.716 | claude_code The user requested the creation of a CALVIN repository using the/init command (combining actions from language and visual aspects - simulating long-range robot control benchmarks). The code library structure was studied, and key components were identified (calvin_env with SAPIEN physical model, calvin_models with MCIL architecture, Hydra configuration system). Comprehensive documentation covering installation, training/evaluation commands, architecture overview, and data formats was created. The user subsequently encountered an installation error for MulticoreTSNE due to CMake version conflicts, but rejected the proposed fix.**
RoboTwin VLA Strategy (pi05) ✅ Resolving SAPIEN Rendering Failures in Docker Containers 06:39:44.361 | claude_code The user reported a “rendering error” when running bash eval.sh. It was traced back to test_render.py capturing the exception, with complete traceback information displayed. After modification, the full traceback showed “RuntimeError: Rendering device not found”. The root cause was the lack of NVIDIA Vulkan/OpenGL libraries in Docker containers. Attempts to install using apt-get install libnvidia-gl-535 were unsuccessful due to driver version mismatches (535.288.01 vs 535.104.12). The matching NVIDIA driver was downloaded, and 22 libraries were decompressed into the user’s directory, with VK_ICD_FILENAMES and LD_LIBRARY_PATH configured in eval.sh. The rendering issue was successfully resolved (“rendering well” printed out).**
✅ Fixing Frozen dataclass Errors in Strategy Loading Process 08:29:17.561 | claude_code After resolving the rendering issue, the user encountered a FrozenInstanceError when loading the pi05 strategy. DataConfig was defined as a frozen dataclass, but the code attempted to assign properties directly. This issue was resolved by replacing ‘data_config.asset_id = value’ with ‘dataclasses.replace(data_config, asset_id=value)’. Then, a FileNotFoundError occurred due to duplicate paths (assets/norm_stats.json/norm_stats.json). This issue was resolved by loading from the assets/ directory directly when providing robotwin_repo_id. Both fixes allowed eval.sh to continue executing the model.**
RoboTwin Simulation Framework ✅ Recording Data Collection and Simulation Architecture 10:10:05.645 | claude_code The user requested an explanation of the implementation of collect_data.sh. A detailed two-stage process was provided: (1) collecting successful seeds by running simulations until a specified number of successes were achieved, saving trajectory plans; (2) replaying deterministic seeds using saved plans to collect training data (RGB, depth, joint states), and saving as HDF5 format. The user then requested a detailed explanation of the task simulation using place_dual_shoes.py. Setup_demo() (SAPIEN engine, robot loading, camera settings), load_actors() (scene randomization), play_once() (two-arm grasping/placement sequence), and underlying motion planning based on Curobo/MPlib were explained. PID control, constrained motion, and data collection time were covered.**

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-06

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-05 Daily Overview Completed Work: Parallel execution of multiple projects on 4 devices: MIHD RM-IDEAL evaluation infrastructure, device documentation system, machine learning training process debugging, CalendarPro autonomous intelligent upgrade Implementation Methods: DCC runs long-running background computing tasks (RM-IDEAL cross-sample benchmark tests), using visual scripts; MacBook executes parallel research agents to generate documentation; Tianhe is responsible for environment setup and deep learning code analysis to fix machine learning-related errors; TzJsDesktop implements a comprehensive personal assistant system with an event-driven architecture, including multi-agent coordination and code quality audits Impact: RM-IDEAL evaluations revealed differences in layer-specific embedding quality (Layer_1/5 showed excellent performance with ρ values of 0.42-0.66; Layer_3/6 showed negative correlations with ρ values between -0.21 and -0.36), providing guidance for subsequent optimizations; Devices received monthly reviews and a 676-line guide; VLA training issues were resolved, Openpi-moe normalization errors were fixed, avoiding scale mismatch problems; CalendarPro transformed from a passive scheduler to an autonomous intelligent housekeeper with 319 tests passed, but audits found critical technical debts that need immediate fixes DCC Completed Work: MIHD project RM-IDEAL evaluation infrastructure: Cross-sample benchmark tests 151673↔151508, written visual scripts, optimized project descriptions to emphasize zero-sample/patch query functions, implemented GPU acceleration planning Implementation Methods: Performed long-running WWL+Wasserstein calculations (3-4 hours per direction), wrote three-panel visual scripts, iteratively optimized project descriptions to highlight the need for zero samples, distinguished from STAIG end-to-end training, initiated Plan agent to design GPU Sinkhorn implementation solutions Impact: Completed bidirectional 7-label evaluations of 151673↔151508, found the highest Spearman ρ value of 0.657 (unidirectional Layer_4), identified key issues where middle-layer embeddings showed negative correlations with topology rankings, established a narrative framework from a clinical perspective to achieve patch query cross-patient knowledge transfer MacBook Completed Work: Device toolkit documentation: Analysis and merging of February monthly summaries, written Claude Code comprehensive guide Implementation Methods: Used LLM to analyze AI conversation daily report JSON data to generate structured summaries, initiated 3 parallel research agents to collect Claude Code information, wrote a 10-chapter 676-line guide after reading study results from the planning mode Impact: Established a systematic review mechanism for historical dialogue data, Claude Code guide covers installation/functionality/integration/best practices/pricing, providing a standard reference document for the team TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Comprehensive upgrade of CalendarPro personal assistant system (16 new services + 21 file modifications + 68 tests), followed by a code quality audit of the system (identified 3 incomplete implementations + 16 dangerous silent exception handlers + missing BackgroundCoordinator startup) Implementation Methods: Studied reference codebases (openclaw multi-agent ACP/gateway/plugin SDK, get-shit-done wave execution/context engineering/200K window), designed a 5-phase 31-point implementation plan, used parallel background agents to process independent modules, directly modified related files, then initiated dedicated exploration agents (TODO/FIXME search, git analysis, exception audit) to detect hidden issues Impact: Turned CalendarPro from a passive tool into an autonomous intelligent housekeeper with task discovery, cross-domain recommendations, and autonomous decision-making capabilities. 319 tests validated core functions; however, audits found critical technical debts (incomplete executor logic, over 50 silent errors, services never started), requiring immediate fixes to ensure system reliability Tianhe Completed Work: Resolved curobo installation issues in VLA training, debugged Openpi-moe training normalization errors causing scale mismatch problems in trajectory VAE. Planned to separate Phoenix/FLARE projects (not executed) Implementation Methods: Analyzed CUDA header paths in the conda environment, set CPL_INCLUDE_PATH/C_INCLUDE_PATH for compilation; tracked data normalization process through multiple source files, found apply_tree(strict=False) silently skipping missing keys; designed a shared dependency architecture for two intertwined projects Impact: Made VLA training environment setup possible; identified and fixed critical errors in trajectory VAE training, where due to the absence of norm_stats.json key and loose apply_tree() default behavior, unnormalized prevActions were merged with normalized actions Parallel development across devices included MIHD spatial transcriptomics (RM-IDEAL cross-sample benchmarks revealed layer-specific performance patterns), device toolkit improvements (monthly summaries + Claude Code comprehensive guides), VLA/Openpi-moe training process debugging (curobo installation + normalization error fixes), and comprehensive upgrade of CalendarPro personal assistant system (31 sub-goals implemented, 16 new services added, followed by a code audit to identify critical errors and dangerous exception handlers)
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ Comprehensive Upgrade of CalendarPro Personal Assistant System – Implemented 31 sub-goals in 5 phases: Autonomous Intelligent Engine (situation_monitor, gap_analyzer, autonomous_executor, reminder_evaluator, context_detector), Deep Thinking Processing (perspective_analyzer, goal_tracker, conversation_summarizer), Comprehensive Life Management (sleep_service, diet nutrition), Learning Adaptation (preference_learner, time-slot efficiency), Multi-Agent Coordination (agent_registry, agent_protocol, wave_executor, monitoring_agent). Modified 21 core files (9 event hooks, over 15 settings, 3 IntentTypes, 3-layer context_assembler), and created 16 new service files. Validated effectiveness through 319 tests ✅ RM-IDEAL Cross-Sample Benchmarks: Bidirectional Evaluation of 151673↔151508 – Conducted PCA+UNI2+STAIG_fusion cross-sample RM-IDEAL evaluations, evaluating 7 sub-labels in both directions 151673→151508 and 151508→151673. Each direction required about 3-4 hours of background computing time. Layer_1/5 showed excellent performance (Spearman ρ 0.42-0.66, SL@50 reached 0.94-1.00), while Layer_3/6 showed negative correlations (ρ -0.21 to -0.36). P@K average was 0, indicating no overlapping exact locations, but overall rankings were consistent ✅ Resolving Normalization Issues in Openpi-moe Training – Found that missing prev_actions keys in norm_stats.json did not cause errors during training. Identified that apply_tree(strict=False) behavior silently skipped normalization of missing keys, leading to scale mismatch between normalized and unnormalized prevActions in trajectory VAE. Fixed this issue by modifying compute_norm_stats.py to dynamically detect and calculate prevActions statistics ✅ MIHD Project Five-Sentence Descriptions with Multiple Optimization Cycles – Human-led narrative structure adjusted while AI provided iterative language optimization. Final version was based on a clinical perspective (cross-patient knowledge transfer), emphasized the need for zero samples (fundamental difference from STAIG end-to-end training), highlighted filling gaps (first systematic evaluation), key findings (performance close to retraining methods), and value enhancement (moving towards clinical paradigms). Each sentence was condensed into two short phrases for clear logic 🔄 GPU Acceleration of RM-IDEAL Optimal Transfer Planning – Current RM-IDEAL uses POT library CPU EMD calculations as a performance bottleneck (about 4000 WWL+Wasserstein calculations per location). Introduced Plan agent to design GPU Sinkhorn approximation algorithm, exploring GeomLoss or geomloss library for GPU parallel batch OT calculations, aiming to reduce single-sample evaluation time from hours to minutes ✅ CalendarPro Code Quality Audit and Key Error Identification – Conducted a systematic audit of the code base using dedicated exploration agents. Identified several key issues: incomplete executor.execute_step() loops (lines 158-161 only showing ‘pass’), stub get_progress() returning None, unimplemented _learn_meal_preferences() placeholders, 16 dangerous silent exception handlers (all passed directly except Exception) appearing in situation_monitor(5), context_detector(5), agent_registry(5), autonomous_executor(3), life_context(2), scheduler(1), energy_projection(1), and missing BackgroundCoordinator.start_all() calls, causing all background services to fail ✅ Fixing Key Errors and Dangerous Exception Handlers in CalendarPro – Fixed 3 unimplemented methods (removed useless executor loops, implemented get_progress() returning execution status, implemented learn_from_history() method for learning meal times based on more than 3 data points). Replaced 16 silent exception handlers with logger.error/warning, added missing log import statements in scheduler.py. All 319 tests passed after fixing these issues ✅ Writing RM-IDEAL Visual Scripts – Written viz_rm_ideal_151673_151508.py visual script for Layer_3 sub-label queries in the 151673→151508 direction, generating 3-panel comparisons: Ground Truth annotations for the reference part, RM-IDEAL score heatmap (structurally similar facts), embedding cosine similarity heatmap, allowing intuitive evaluation of spatial topology capture capabilities of fused embeddings ✅ Writing Claude Code Comprehensive Guide – Initiated 3 parallel research agents to collect official documentation/best practices/AI agent workflow information for Claude code. Read study results from the planning mode and generated a complete 676-line guide claude_code-guide.md, covering 10 chapters (definition/installation/functionality/integration/usage/abilities/limitations/best practices/pricing) and a quick-reference card, suitable as an onboarding standard document for the team 🔄 Separating Plan Phoenix/FLARE Projects – Created detailed plans to separate two intertwined projects (Phoenix MPM/MCM motion framework and FLARE retry reset framework) from the mixed 1TB repository into independent directories with shared dependencies. Plans included file classification, symbolic link strategy for large data (about 789G mimicgen, 245G openpi), and updates to CLAUDE.md Implementation and Fixes ✅ Generating and Merging February Monthly Summaries – Used LLM to analyze AI conversation daily report JSON data, generating monthly summaries with structures like project_overview/key_achievements/recurring_problems/ai_collaboration_trends/learnings_digest. Then merged independent totals from multiple periods into a complete monthly report, supporting long-term work mode review and reflection ✅ Installing curobo Library in RefineVLA’s conda Environment – Set CPL_INCLUDE_PATH and C_INCLUDE_PATH to point to conda environment’s CUDA toolpath targets/x86_64-linux/include, resolving CUDA header path issues and enabling compilation of curobo Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Openpi-moe Training Does Not Error When norm_stats.json Lacks prevActions Key, Leading to Scale Mismatch in Trajectory VAE (Unnormalized hist Actions Merged with Normalized Actions) Solution: The root cause is transforms.py’s apply_tree() defaulting to strict=False, silently skipping normalization of missing keys. Modified compute_norm_stats.py to dynamically detect prevActions in batches and calculate their statistics, solving this issue
Key Insight: Silent failures during data preprocessing are dangerous. The loose behavior of apply_tree(strict=False) allows scale mismatch to propagate during training. Always verify that all expected keys have normalized statistical information, especially for merged features
2. Eager Import in services/init.py Results in Cyclic Dependencies (services ↔ core.scheduler), Making It Impossible to Import Any Service Modules Solution: Changed all imports to lazy loading, keeping only the all list and comments, requiring users to import directly (e.g., from srcServices.calendar_service import CalendarService), avoiding cyclic dependencies during package initialization
Key Insight: Python package init.py doesn’t need to provide import statements immediately; for packages with cyclical dependencies, lazy loading combined with documentation comments is a better choice; exposing interfaces at the package level doesn’t equal mandatory eager loading
3. 16 Dangerous Exception Errors: Ignoring All Errors Results in Incomplete States, But System Still Seems Working, With No Ability to Locate Source of Errors During Debugging Solution: Replaced code with except Exception as e: logger.error/warning(f"Description: {e}"), keeping error messages for diagnosis, distinguishing between errors (critical functions) and warnings (minor functions)
Key Insight: Never use empty passes to handle exceptions; if errors must be ignored, at least record the error message; silent failures in state tracking layers (situation_monitor/life_context) lead to “Schrödinger states” - unable to determine whether it’s a true null processing or loading failure
4. How To Efficiently Implement a Large-Scale 31-Goal Upgrade Plan Without Creating Sequential Bottlenecks? Solution: Used parallel background agent execution mode: Started 4 independent agents simultaneously to execute context_detector, checkin_service, learning_service, and energy_projection enhancements, while directly modifying another 8 files. All agents completed successfully without conflicts
Key Insight: Wave-based parallel execution can significantly accelerate large-scale refactoring when dependencies are correctly isolated. The key is identifying independent work units that can truly run concurrently
5. AI Repeatedly Attempts to Enter Planning Mode, But Users Prefer Direct Execution Solution: Users rejected the ExitPlanMode tool call, providing planning content directly to AI execution. AI read results from previous research agents in the planning mode and then wrote directly into the documentation, without needing to re-plan
Key Insight: Users prefer rapid iteration over long-term planning; AI should adaptively reduce the frequency of planning mode triggers or learn this preference after multiple user denials
6. CalendarPro Has 6 Completely Untested Source Files, 12 Modified New Methods, And BackgroundCoordinator Is Implemented But Never Started Solution: Created a system’s testing infrastructure: conftest.py for sharing test fixtures, creating 20 new test files (pytest, AsyncMock, patch decorator) based on existing patterns, fixing on_ready() function to actually call coordinator.start_all(), adding 9 missing Discord intent handlers
Key Insight: Large-scale features usually come with incomplete integration. Background services require explicit startup calls, not just class definitions. The system’s testing requires shared test fixtures and consistency in all test files
7. Patch Paths in Test Fixtures Are Incorrect (Patched Call Points Instead of Definition Points), Leading to Mock Failure After Lazy Loading, With Multiple Tests Reporting AttributeError Errors Solution: All singleton factory functions for patching (get_settings/get_calendar_service) must be located in the source module (src.config/src.services.calendar_service), not at the call point (e.g., srcservices.gap_analyzer), as lazy loading delays name binding
Key Insight: Gold rule for unittest.mock.patch - patch at the location where it’s being looked up, not at the definition location; Lazy loading means “looking up” locations are always in the source module, not creating new bindings at import locations
8. How To Discover Hidden Errors and Incomplete Implementations After Major Refactoring of Large-Scale Codebases?Dialogue Summary MIHD✅ RM-IDEAL Cross-Sample Benchmarking Execution: 151673↔151508 17:11:41.875 | claude_code
The user requested a cross-sample RM-ideal-benchmark test for slides 151673 and 151508, using PCA+UNI2+STAIG_fusion embeddings. After confirming some IDs, the AI began background calculations (approximately 3-4 hours), evaluating seven tags bidirectionally. The results showed that Layer_1/5 performed best (Spearman ρ 0.42-0.66, SL@50 reached 0.94-1.00), while Layer_3/6 had a negative correlation (ρ -0.21 to -0.36). P@K remained consistent across some layers. The results are saved in the outputs/rm_ideal_benchmark/ directory.✅ Multiple rounds of iterative optimization described by five sentences of the project
16:46:22.685 | claude_code
The user requested a description with five sentences to leave a deep impression, emphasizing zero-sample processing, differences from STAIG, and the concept of patch query cross-patient knowledge transfer. The AI provided a preliminary draft and adjusted the narrative structure under human guidance: The first sentence starts from a clinical perspective (patch query), highlighting the gap; the second sentence differentiates between STAIG (zero-sample training vs. end-to-end training); the third sentence describes the method; the fourth sentence introduces the findings; the fifth sentence explains the value. After two rounds of iteration, the number of sentences was compressed from over 70 words to two short paragraphs, with a clear narrative thread: clinical perspective → why zero-sample processing is needed → method → findings → value**
🔍 GPU-Accelerated RM-IDEAL Optimal Transfer Planning
17:21:50.123 | claude_code
The user asked whether it was possible to improve the optimal transfer performance of RM-ideal scores for GPU execution. The AI read the rm_ideal.py file and learned that the current implementation used CPU EMD calculations from the POT library (_wasserstein_distance), which was a performance bottleneck (approximately 4000 WWL+Wasserstein per location). The Plan agent was initiated to design an alternative GPU Sinkhorn approximation algorithm, exploring the use of GeomLoss or geomloss libraries for GPU parallel batch optimal transfer calculations, with the goal of reducing the single-sample evaluation time from hours to minutes**
✅ RM-IDEAL Visualization Script: Layer_3 for 151673→151508
17:26:17.865 | claude_code
The user requested a visualization of RM-ideal scores for a specific query on Layer_3 in the direction of 151673→151508. The AI confirmed that the data was ready (embedded data for 151673 and 151508, and the RM-IDEAL cache cross_151673_151508_Layer_3.npy file were available), and wrote the viz_rm_ideal_151673_151508.py script to generate a three-panel comparison: annotations from the reference part, a heatmap of RM-IDEAL scores (topological structure benchmark), and a heatmap of embedding cosine similarity. This script was adapted from the_viz_rm_ideal.py template**
Tools ✅ Generation of a Monthly Summary for February 2026
