title: Bug Journal 2026-02-17

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-16 Daily Overview Completed Work: Three main parallel workflows were executed: (1) Identified MIHD spatial embedding crash issues and corrected error recovery documentation; (2) Implemented a complete VLA integration infrastructure and verified its performance, generating over 150 scenarios to capture natural errors; (3) Designed dialog summaries for the Gadget summary tool. Completion Method: Used systematic engineering methods to optimize the equipment: DCC conducted root cause analysis on the 64-dimensional embedding bottleneck; Tianhe servers completed VLA policy integration (version 4.4), error classification system (version 4.3), API fixes, GPU acceleration deployment verification, and natural error generation architecture (version 4.5); Windows desktop designed JSON structure for dialog summaries and prompt modifications. Impact: Shifted error recovery benchmarks from synthetic injections to real policy failure capture (271 scenarios, 8 error types, achieved M5/M9/M10 milestone goals); identified the key MIHD dimension bottleneck causing 91% of cluster crashes; laid the foundation for improving the readability of daily reports by providing narrative summaries for each conversation. DCC Completed Work: Identified STAIG fusion model crashes in the DLPFC region 151672, attributing the root cause to a combination of a 64-dimensional embedding bottleneck and spatial majority voting. Completion Method: Checked the embeddings (standard deviation of 0.12), verified through KMeans re-clustering; spatial refinement analysis showed that majority voting with a radius of 15 eliminated minority clusters; dimension comparison across methods (concatenation: 1586, attention model: 256, STAIG: 64). Impact: Revealed that 94-96% of information was lost during dimension compression; explained why the mclust with 5 clusters could only produce 2 valid clusters; proposed a method for dimension ablation studies using 64/128/256/512 dimensions to systematically solve crash issues. TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Designed dialog summaries for the Gadget/summary tool, planned JSON structure, prompt modifications, and Markdown rendering logic. Completion Method: Completed the work through code review and planning agent design methods: defined the summary structure for each conversation (project/destination timestamp/topic/summary/results), adopted a single-file modification strategy, and made targeted edits to SUMARY_PROMPT, Anthropic tool patterns, generate_markdown, and max_tokens. Impact: Completed a backward-compatible architecture design, providing narrative summaries for each AI conversation, improving the readability and completeness of daily reports. Tianhe Completed Work: Implemented VLA integration infrastructure (version 4.4), policy error classification system (version 4.3), fixed key API issues, and comprehensively tested and verified with over 150 natural error scenarios; designed a natural error generation system architecture (version 4.5). Completion Method: Adopted a multi-stage development approach: corrected more than 15 document errors through registry-based verification; implemented over 30 files for VLA/category modules (about 3000 lines of code); fixed openpi API calls, observation data preprocessing, and camera key mapping issues; executed GPU acceleration deployment (8xA800 clusters); explored VLA checkpoints and designed a 7-step implementation plan for version 4.5. Impact: Established a VLA operation process with the Pi0 model (although 100% of PickPlace tasks failed, revealing its generalization limitations); generated 271 total scenarios (increased from 121 to 271), covering 8 error types; achieved M5/M9/M10 milestone goals; identified joint_limit_approach (47%) and grasp_wrong_pose (30%) as major natural failure modes; completed the architecture design for generating natural errors using pre-trained encoders + rule-based offline analysis for version 4.5. Completed the comprehensive VLA integration process, used for error recovery benchmarks (versions 4.3-v4.5), diagnosed and analyzed MIHD clustering crashes, and designed dialog summaries for Gadget’s daily reports
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ VLA policy deployment verification and natural error capture — Fixed a critical image transfer漏洞 in get_current_obs(); used 10 injection modes and 50 natural capture deployment methods on GPUs, generating 153 new scenarios (3 injection modes + 150 natural captures); updated M5/M9/M10 milestone status. The total number of scenarios increased from 121 to 271, and the number of error types increased from 4 to 8. ✅ Diagnosing STAIG fusion clustering crashes in DLPFC region 151672 — Investigated why uni+staig_fusion produced only 2 clusters instead of 5. Found that an embedding dimension bottleneck (64 dimensions, standard deviation of 0.12) combined with spatial refinement could eliminate minority clusters through majority voting. ✅ VLA model integration infrastructure (version 4.4) — Implemented a complete VLA integration: vla_server.py (TCP server), PolicyServerAdapter (client), camera observation support, PolicyErrorDetector, capture_natural_errors() method. Fixed API calls using create_trained_policy(), corrected camera key mapping issues, and resolved observation data preprocessing format problems. All 79 unit tests passed. ✅ Policy error detection and classification system (version 4.3) — Implemented 3 classifiers (operation/planning/progression), error classification methods (25 types), PolicyErrorMonitor coordinator, VLM analyzer (Gemini + Claude Code CLI), error analysis module, collector integration. All 73 unit tests passed. ✅ Design of VLA natural error generation system architecture (version 4.5) — Explored the infrastructure of version 4.4, confirmed VLA checkpoints (Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix), and designed a 7-step implementation plan including BC-RNN checkpoint download, using pre-trained encoders (DINOv2/SigLIP) + rule-based analysis offline visual error detectors. 🔄 Design dimension ablation experiments for STAIG — Planned to support configurable hidden dimensions (64/128/256/512 dimensions) for staig_fusion. Users rejected this plan without explanation. ✅ Fixing document errors in error recovery benchmarks — Corrected factual errors in CLAUDE.md, project panorama summary.md, and error_taxonomy.py: reduced the number of detectors from 6 to 5, classifiers from 19 to 20, error types from 25 to 24, categories from 10 to 8, test numbers from 73 to 79, scenarios from 30 to 118. Added missing modules and development commands. Implementation and Fixes 🔄 Downloading VLA checkpoints and BC-RNN models — Confirmed the download of Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix models (250GB HuggingFace cache located in zhaoganlong directory). Some BC-RNN checkpoints were missing (lift succeeded, but the URL from Stanford had a can/square/transport 404 error). The download status of the MimicGen dataset was unknown. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. STAIG fusion produced only 2 clusters instead of 5, ARI of 0.1149 indicates severe crashesSolution: Identified the root cause: a 64-dimensional embedding bottleneck caused 1536-dimensional UNI features to compress by 96% (standard deviation of 0.12), and then spatial refinement algorithm’s radius-15 majority voting mechanism eliminated minority clusters. Proposed dimension ablation studies to test alternative solutions with 128/256/512 dimensions, so as to compare with other fusion methods. Key Insight: Cascading failures exacerbated the problem: embeddings generated by GCN training collapsed almost completely, and subsequent spatial smoothing completely eliminated remaining minor clusters. The 64-dimensional bottleneck was crucial—other fusion methods using 256-512 dimensions did not suffer from such problems.
2. VLA server initially lacked camera images for observation data, leading to “observation/image” KeyError Solution: Fixed the get_current_obs() function in rollout_generator.py by adding the include_images parameter; detected the PolicyServerAdapter type in generate_from_single_rollout() and capture_natural_errors() functions, and passed the include_images=True parameter when camera_names included [‘agentview’,‘robot0_eye_in_hand’].
Key Insight: VLA policies require visual input, but state_extractor.extract() does not include images by default; it was necessary to explicitly identify policy types and set the include_images flag. This was a general issue affecting all policies that relied on cameras (VLA, VisuoMotor BC).
3. VLA server used incorrect openpi API—manual loading of norm_stats caused model initialization failure Solution: Replaced manual loading with openpi’s create_trained_policy(train_config=get_config(config_name), checkpoint_dir=…) method, which can automatically handle norm_stats, transformation, and model architecture. Added the –config_name CLI parameter and IMAGE_KEY_MAP to achieve correct camera key mapping.
Key Insight: The openpi library provides an advanced create_trained_policy() function that encapsulates all model loading complexities. Manual use of low-level APIs is error-prone and problematic with library upgrades.
4. Pi0 (LIBERO checkpoint) achieved a 100% failure rate during natural_capture deployment process Solution: Identified the failure pattern distribution: joint_limit_approach was 47%, grasp_wrong_pose was 30%, misalignment_pre_grasp was 17%, overshoot was 5%, premature_release was 1%; recorded these situations as natural error data; determined that it was necessary to produce solutions specific to PickPlace checkpoints or domain adaptability.
Key Insight: Pre-trained VLA has limited generalization ability; performance declined significantly across tasks (LIBERO→robosuite PickPlace). However, these failure patterns are valuable natural error data and meet the benchmarks’ objectives for natural error capture.
5. VLA integration seemed complete (code and tests passed), but failed to run properly in actual deployment Solution: In-depth investigation revealed obstacles: incorrect openpi API usage, lack of norm_stats.json processing, incomplete checkpoint paths, failure to support –policy vla_server flag. A detailed fix plan was developed and all corrections were systematically implemented.
Key Insight: “Tests passed” does not equal “system is running properly”—before claiming completion, integration testing with actual dependencies (model checkpoints, external APIs) was required. Synthetic unit tests could verify code correctness but could not guarantee end-to-end functionality.
6. _preprocessobs() failed because the state information dictionary returned by StateExtractor differed from the raw robosuite observation data Solution: Reconstructed the preprocessobs() function to detect input formats and standardize them into common structures {“state”: […], “images”: {…}. Added a powerful key lookup function and implemented correct floating-point to uint8 image conversion.
Key Insight: The format of observation dictionaries depends on the source: StateExtractor.extract() returns keys centered around EEF, while env.step() returns raw robosuite observation data with robot0 prefix. A unified preprocessing method was needed.
7. Documentation contained more than 15 factual errors that contradicted the actual code repository status, involving multiple files Solution: Conducted a systematic registry-based verification (DETECTOR_REGISTRY, CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY, ErrorType enumeration), corrected all errors through targeted edits to 3 files, and ran test suites to confirm no failures (79/79 passed).
Key Insight: Even the code’s documentation strings had errors. The real source was the registered code, not the documentation itself. Verification workflow: used grep old values after editing to ensure thorough cleanup.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Methods Strategic Level VLA Integration Operation Preparation Assessment Role Method Human User asked “Can we deploy pi0 now?"— implicitly checking end-to-end preparation beyond code completion AI Initially, AI thought that once the code was written and tested, VLA integration was “completed”— assuming the system could run properly without verifying actual model loading and reasoning processes Difference Analysis: Humans correctly intuited that “completion of implementation” did not equal “system is running properly”. AI was overly optimistic, thinking that passing unit tests meant deployment preparation was complete, ignoring the need for integration verification with actual dependencies.
Identifying the root cause of clustering crashes Role Method Human After seeing the visualization results, humans questioned whether 64-dimensional embeddings were too small and then instructed AI to investigate specific parts AI AI conducted a systematic investigation: loaded embedding files, calculated statistics (standard deviation of 0.12), re-run KMeans to verify crashes, tracked refinement logic, compared dimensions across all fusion methods Difference Analysis: Humans gained intuition about dimension size from domain knowledge; AI followed a mechanical investigation process. Human assumptions were correct and effectively guided the investigation.
Error location strategy Role Method Human Humans did not point out errors directly but allowed AI to conduct end-to-end testing, discovering problems through runtime errors AI AI discovered errors by actually executing failures (VLA server error: “observation/image”), traced back to policy_adapter.py _preprocessobs(), and then to rollout_generator.py _get_current_obs() to identify the root cause Difference Analysis: Humans adopted a “do-and-learn” strategy, allowing AI to discover errors; AI conducted systematic debugging through error stack traces and code analysis. This approach led to deeper understanding rather than just surface-level fixes.
VLA model selection and implementation priority Role Method Human Humans clearly stated in a Q&A format: skipping Flare models (not found), downloading all 4 BC-RNN checkpoints (can/square/transport), using pre-trained encoders + rule-based offline analysis, choosing offline methods over real-time detection AI AI posed 4 open-ended questions to users for decision-making: source code of Flare, range of BC-RNN checkpoints, method of visual detectors (three options), real-time vs. offline detection, providing detailed trade-off analyses for each option Difference Analysis: Humans made practical technical choices based on project constraints (model availability, implementation complexity, time budget); AI provided a comprehensive analysis framework but required human domain knowledge and prioritization decisions for final decisions.
Explanation of VLM backend types Role Method Human Humans explicitly preferred using Claude Code CLI (interactive + scripted local tool) over Claude API (cloud HTTP API), emphasizing the difference between these two usage patterns AI Initially, AI confused Claude with Claude API; needed to clarify this distinction Difference Analysis: Humans distinguished between Claude Code CLI (interactive + scripted local tools) and Claude API (cloud HTTP API); AI initially mixed up the two concepts and needed to correct this.
Design of dialog summary granularity Role Method Human Humans initially asked for “a part to summarize my conversation with AI in the final report,” without specifying a specific structure AI Through planning agents, AI systematically analyzed three granularity options (project aggregation, timeline aggregation, per-conversation summary), and ultimately recommended using per-conversation summaries to retain complete context, flexible rendering, and backward compatibility Difference Analysis: Humans provided high-level requirements; AI translated them into specific architectural decisions, considering readability, maintainability, and backward-compatibility engineering constraints.### Discussion Summary
🔄 STAIG Fusion Clustering Crash Diagnosis and Dimension Analysis 22:26:09.691 | claude_code The user noticed that the visualization results for section 151672 had only 2 clusters instead of 5. It was found that the bottleneck caused by 64-dimensional STAIG embeddings (with a standard deviation of 0.12, compressing 1536-dimensional features by 96%) led to almost complete failure. Spatial refinement operations eliminated the remaining minor clusters through majority voting. Other fusion methods (attention mechanism: 256-512 dimensions) did not experience such problems, indicating that 64-dimensional embeddings are fundamentally insufficient.
Key requirements for VLA end-to-end integration: Ensure correct policy observation input formats, especially camera image transmission. The include_images=True parameter should be explicitly used in state_extractor.extract(), and camera_names should be specified for all policies that rely on cameras. When integrating third-party machine learning libraries like openpi and robomimic, it is essential to check the actual API signatures using inspect.signature() and read the sample code from the source repository. Documentation often lacks completeness or is outdated. The openpi library’s create_trained_policy() provides advanced encapsulation; manual use of lower-level APIs can be error-prone. Natural error capture is more efficient than injection-based methods: 50 iterations generated 150 scenarios (about 3 scenarios per iteration), while injection-based methods produced only 3 scenarios in 10 iterations (about 0.3 scenarios per iteration). Natural error patterns are more diverse and realistic. The plugin-based architecture pattern in the benchmark system: Detectors, classifiers, and injectors all use a basic ABC+ registry pattern to achieve scalability. The error classification system implements registration of 19 classifiers through CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY, allowing for easy addition of new error types without modifying the core logic. Documentation verification requires checking the actual code registry and enumeration values, rather than just reading document strings or previous documentation. The DETECTOR_REGISTRY, CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY, and ErrorType enumerations are reliable references. Systematic editing can prevent residual errors. Communication architecture across conda environments: VLA servers use TCP+pickle protocol to separate model inference (openpi/openpi05 environment) from the benchmark framework (mimicgen_env). Length-prefixed frame format prevents message fragmentation, and action chunking reduces the number of inference calls. Environment isolation is crucial for VLA/robot projects due to dependency conflicts. Observation data preprocessing must handle multiple input formats gracefully: StateExtractor (internal framework), raw env.step() (robosuite), and model-specific preprocessing (VLA server). A robust key lookup mechanism with fall-back strategies can prevent silent failures. The dictionary format of observation data varies depending on the source, so a unified preprocessing layer is required. Spatial majority voting refinement operations (nearest neighbor with radius 15) may amplify rather than solve collapsed clustering errors. Refinement should be based on metrics of embedding quality (e.g., disabling refinement if contour scores are below a certain threshold). The dual VLM backend design (Gemini API + Claude Code CLI) provides flexibility: The API is suitable for batch automation, while the CLI is ideal for interactive depth analysis and debugging. They complement each other to meet different usage scenarios. Adjustments were made to the architecture for generating natural errors in VLA integration: (1) policy server mode across conda environments, (2) camera observation support in environment包装器, (3) observation data preprocessing (quaternions → axis-angle conversion, BGR→RGB, action chunking), and (4) dual iteration mode (synthetic injection + natural capture). This differs significantly from the demonstration-play synthetic perturbation method. A systematic code library exploration strategy (infrastructure check → model file location → configuration parameter confirmation → user decision confirmation) effectively supported complex multi-step project planning, avoiding omission of key dependencies. Backward compatibility is an important consideration in data structure design: The new conversation_summaries field ensures that old reports can continue to be used. This pattern applies to changing data formats. Only 5 out of the 24 error types in the actual VLA iteration triggered errors (joint_limit_approach, grasp_wrong_pose, misalignment_pre_grasp, overshoot, premature_release), indicating that certain error types require specific scenarios to manifest. Practical Experience Using GPUs for batch processing to achieve visualization results (5 GPUs, 20 videos, zero failures) demonstrated the value of parallel infrastructure. The subprocess.Popen + GPU allocation pattern is suitable for tasks with parallelism. The subtleties in handling null values in Python: 0 or default would be evaluated as default, as 0 is a null value, which could lead to errors with counters indexed by 0. The PrematureReleaseClassifier always returns 0 when starting capture at step 0. The solution is to explicitly use is None to check for optional values indexed by 0. Dialogue Summary MIHD 🔄 STAIG Fusion Clustering Crash Diagnosis and Dimension Analysis 22:26:09.691 | claude_code The user noticed that the visualization results for section 151672 had only 2 clusters instead of 5. It was found that the bottleneck caused by 64-dimensional STAIG embeddings (with a standard deviation of 0.12, compressing 1536-dimensional features by 96%) led to almost complete failure. Spatial refinement operations eliminated the remaining minor clusters. Cross-method comparisons showed that 256-512-dimensional embeddings from other methods did not experience such failures. The user decided to conduct dimension ablation experiments but did not provide a clear implementation plan.
Error Recovery Benchmarking ✅ VLA End-to-End Testing + Critical Bug Fixes + Large-scale Natural Error Capture 16:23:03.741 | claude_code The user requested VLA end-to-end testing (10 injection iterations). The Pi0 server was started, and environmental issues were resolved (-no-banner, port occupation). The first run encountered a “observation/image” KeyError error; tracing back to the get_current_obs() method in rollout_generator.py revealed a lack of image transmission functionality, which was fixed by adding the include_images parameter. Three injection scenarios were successfully generated. Then, 50 natural capture iterations were conducted, generating 150 scenarios. The results showed that Pi0 had a failure rate of 100% in the PickPlace task, with the main errors being joint_limit_approach (47%) and grasp_wrong_pose (30%). The total number of scenarios increased from 121 to 271, achieving M5/M9/M10 milestone goals. Project documentation and milestone status were updated.✅ Automatic error injection enabled normal operation for VLA policy release 06:51:17.532 | claude_code The user provided a detailed plan to address VLA integration issues (wrong openpi API, missing argparse, conda mismatch). All 4 tasks were completed: rewriting the VLA server to use create_trained_policy(), adding VLA support in entry scripts and selecting modes, fixing observation data preprocessing to handle multiple formats, and updating config/Makefile/docs. All 79 tests passed. Batch visualization operations were performed (5 GPUs generated 20 videos, no failures). The user requested end-to-end VLA testing but stopped midway.
✅ Policy Error Detection and Classification System (v4.3) Fully Implemented 00:37:58.421 | claude_code A comprehensive error classification system was implemented: error classification体系（3个家族→10个类别→25个类型），操作/规划/进展方面共有19种分类器，PolicyErrorMonitor协调器，VLM分析器，错误分析模块。通过最小的修改整合到collector.py中（增加了3行步骤循环）。创建了32个单元测试，涵盖了所有分类器，修复了3次测试失败问题（严重性逻辑、丢弃阈值、保持时间计算）。最终结果：73/73个测试通过（新增32个+现有41个），v4.3系统已准备好投入生产。
🔄 VLA Checkpoint Confirmation and v4.5 Natural Error Generation System Design 04:59:17.779 | claude_code The user asked if the VLA checkpoints were downloaded. The system’s file system search confirmed that Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix models were in the zhaoganlong directory (total 250GB HuggingFace cache), but BC-RNN, can/square, and transport checkpoints were missing. The existing infrastructure of v4.4 was studied (vla_server.py, PolicyServerAdapter, PolicyErrorDetector, capture_natural_errors). Through the user’s questions and answers, technical choices were made: skipping Flare, downloading 4 BC-RNN checkpoints, using pre-trained encoders + rule-based offline visual detection. A 7-step implementation plan was designed, but the user rejected the ExitPlanMode request.
✅ Fact Verification and Correction of Documents in 3 Files 04:35:19.032 | claude_code The user performed the/init operation to improve CLAUDE.md. It was found that there were 15 factual errors: detectors (6→5), classifiers (19→20), error types (25→24), categories (10→8), tests (73→79), scenarios (30→118). A Chinese plan covering the documentation and error_taxonomy.py document strings was generated. All corrections were made through systematic editing, and verification was done using grep and a test suite (79/79 passed). Missing modules and commands were added.
🔄 VLA Model Integration Architecture Exploration and Checkpoint Download 04:45:08.678 | claude_code The user clarified its objectives: injecting errors into the VLA models (Pi0, Pi0.5, Phoenix, Flare) during the release process to generate natural errors, while adding visual+state detectors. The server file system was explored, and Pi0 (about 12GB) and Phoenix checkpoints were found. Integration requirements were determined: policy servers for cross-environment execution, camera observation support, observation data preprocessing (quaternions→axis-angle conversion, BGR→RGB, action chunking), and dual iteration mode (synthetic injection+natural capture). This differs significantly from the demonstration-play synthetic perturbation method.
✅ Implementation of Error Detection with 3 Files 02:19:08.022 | claude_code The user requested the implementation of a comprehensive policy error detection plan (6 phases). It was found that 95% of the content from previous sessions had been implemented, and 4 specific gaps were identified (GraspWrongPoseClassifier, subpackage export, ErrorMetricsComputer, tests), which were systematically filled. All 79 tests passed. Subsequently, the user asked about the download status of checkpoints; at that time, neither Pi0, MimicGen, nor Robomimic checkpoints were available.
🔄 VLA Model Integration + State-Based Error Detector (v4.4) Implementation 05:30:32.536 | claude_code The complete VLA integration infrastructure was implemented: vla_server.py (for Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix TCP servers), PolicyServerAdapter (client), camera observation support, PolicyErrorDetector, capture_natural_errors() method. The configuration file and documentation were updated. All 79 unit tests passed. However, the user’s question “Can we release pi0 now?” led to an in-depth exploration, revealing that the implementation was not ideal—it needed fixing API issues, correcting checkpoint paths, and updating entry scripts before actual use.
✅ Implementation of VLM Error Detector Using Gemini and Claude Code CLI Backends 07:14:56.791 | claude_code The user requested the implementation of the VLM error detector. The Gemini VLM implementation was found in the zhaoganlong directory (api_client.py), and the existing vlm_analyzer.py was explored. The user clearly expressed its desire to use Gemini and Claude Code CLI (rather than the Claude API). 5 tasks were completed: adding the Gemini backend to vlm_analyzer.py, updating benchmark_v4.yaml configuration, creating extract_error_frames.py and classify_error_vlm.py scripts, writing §12 VLM tutorial, and organizing it into a project panorama summary.md. A complete Chinese tutorial.md was created, with 9 chapters covering environment setup, usage, examples, and FAQs.
Gadgets/Summary ✅ Daily Report Dialogue Summary Function Architecture Design 22:51:46.447 | claude_code The user requested adding dialogue summaries to the reports generated by daily_summary.py. The session was recovered from truncated context. By reading the existing code library, a implementation plan was designed using a planning proxy. The solution was to add a conversation_summaries field to the JSON (at the session granularity, including project/source/time/stamp/summary/results), modify THE SURMMITPROMPT, update the Anthropic tool pattern, enhance the generate_markdown function, and adjust the max_tokens parameter. The plan was written into graceful-beaming-leaf.md, and it successfully exited the planning mode.

summary: The comprehensive VLA integration process has been completed for the error recovery benchmark tests (versions 4.3 to 4.5). Successful diagnosis and analysis of the crashes in the MIHD clustering structure have been carried out. title: Bug Journal 2026-02-16

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W07 (2026-02-09 ~ 2026-02-15) This week, we focused on three areas: robot simulation, bioinformatics, and toolchain development. In the error_recovery_benchmark project, deep bottlenecks were encountered during force injection debugging. A 30N force applied to the OSC controller had no noticeable effect, indicating a fundamental issue with the controller’s countermeasure mechanism. For MIHD, the Chinese documentation for the enhancement plan was completed. ccusage implemented support for GLM multi-model billing and passed type/format checks. Robobrain_pi confirmed that the training process is ready. This week, AI showed obvious pattern-fitting defects in repeated errors (SSH lacking the cd command) and environmental assumptions (proxy, pnpm). Human intervention provided more efficient debugging ideas at critical decision points (extreme force testing, local GPU detection, reuse of existing pricing files).
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-09 ~ 2026-02-15 Active Days 4 / 7 Total Conversations 10 Projects Involved 6 Completed Tasks 10 Ongoing Tasks 2 Total Tokens 54,947,656 Total Cost $23.28 Claude Code Tokens 40,980,880 Claude Code Costs $19.02 Codex Tokens 13,966,776 Codex Costs $4.26 Average Daily Cost $5.82 Project Progress error_recovery_benchmark (2 active days) — 🔄 active Completed:
Verified the current state of approximately 6200 lines of code in the framework, identified high-priority bottlenecks (collision geometry name mapping, target object dynamic recognition) Created AGENTS.md contributor guide (372 words, covering structure/construction/testing/submission guidelines) Implemented –force_override command-line parameter and enhanced force injection mechanism (force range extended to [15,45]N, clip set to 60N, duration automatically cleared after completion) Generated a 30N force injection test video (451KB, 193 frames), establishing a visual force debugging toolchain Bottlenecks:
⚠️ No visible displacement in the video after applying 30N force: The OSC controller (kp=150) can cancel out the impulse within duration_steps=1 (about 20ms). Whether the force injection mechanism works at all remains unverified ⚠️ Complete E2E verification requires GPU node (an53), currently mitigated through hierarchical testing (CPU unit/smoke tests) MIHD (2 active days) — ✅ completed Completed:
Translated ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md into a structured Chinese version (6 major goals: normalization, UNI2+scGPT, Q-Former/LLaVA, niche query, batch correction, full configuration) Written the translated content in ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md, preserving key details like file names, CLI parameters, and configuration fields ccusage (GLM billing support) (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Implemented loadLocalPricing() in _pricing-fetcher.ts to load LiteLLM format pricing from CCUSAGE_PRICING_FILE environment variable or default path ~/.ccusage/ccusage.json, and merged it into the offline cache Fixed three issues: misuse of Result.try() API, filtering of LiteLLM metadata keys, and global variables in ESLint process All format/typechecks and targeted tests passed Bottlenecks:
⚠️ Global testing showed failures unrelated to this change for apps/amp. This needs confirmation before merging the PR robobrain_pi (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Confirmed datasets/demo_v2.hdf5 exists with 50 trajectories and 7-dimensional actions Verified that the complete SAC framework is in place (sac_agent.py, trainer.py, train_sac.py) Confirmed availability of 4 A100-80GB GPUs. Recommended use of GPU 1-3 for training. Provided training command instructions Reconstructed the full project timeline from 2026-01-15 to 2026-02-09 from .ccusage/summaries/ gadget/research documentation (1 active day) — ⏸️ paused Completed:
Initiated updates to research/CLAUDE.md. Users completed preliminary reading of the research directory structure Bottlenecks:
⚠️ Conversations were interrupted after users issued analysis requests. AI has not actually performed or written any analysis yet Key Tasks 🚫 Root cause of ineffective force injection with 30N (2026-02-14) — No visible displacement in the video after applying 30N to the Sawyer arm. Users proposed verifying the force injection mechanism using infinite force. Conversations were interrupted before implementation 🔄 ccusage GLM model billing support (2026-02-12) — Added logic for loading and merging local LiteLLM format pricing files in _pricing-fetcher.ts. Targeted tests passed, but global testing showed unrelated failures that need confirmation ✅ Prepare robobrain_pi SAC Pick-and-Place training environment (2026-02-10) — Confirmed availability of datasets, SAC framework, and 4 A100-80GB GPUs. Provided training command instructions ✅ Implement –force_override parameter and enhanced force injection (2026-02-14) — Added command-line parameters, expanded force range to [15,45]N, implemented automatic clearing after duration, fixed bug in ImpulseInjector nested configuration path ✅ Check progress of error_recovery_benchmark plan (2026-02-10) — Confirmed high-priority bottlenecks: collision geometry name mapping (collision.py/env_wrapper.py) and target object dynamic recognition. Complete E2E verification requires GPU node 🔄 Update gadget research/CLAUDE.md documentation (2026-02-10) — Users requested in-depth reading of the research directory before updating the design document. Conversations were interrupted. AI has not actually performed analysis yet ✅ Generate and verify 30N force injection visualization video (2026-02-14) — Ran 2_visualize_scene.py –force_override 30 to generate impulse_demo_0_step72.mp4 (451KB, 193 frames). No visible disturbance was observed visually ✅ Restore robobrain_pi project history from ccusage summary (2026-02-10) — Read .ccusage/summaries/ for 10 Markdown files to reconstruct the full timeline from 2026-01-15 to 2026-02-09 Issues and Solutions 1. Complete E2E verification of error_recovery_benchmark is hindered by GPU node dependencies (EGL/robosuite runtime constraints) [error_recovery_benchmark] (2026-02-10) Solution: Define a clear testing strategy: perform CPU unit tests and smoke tests first. Delay complete E2E verification until the GPU node (an53) is available to avoid blocking development
2. @praha/byethrow’s Result.try() API was misused: AI used it directly as an result, while it actually returns a reusable parser function [ccusage] (2026-02-12) Solution: Store the return value of Result.try({…}) as a function, and explicitly call this function to obtain Result<T, E>
3. The LiteLLM pricing JSON contained metadata keys like sample_spec, which were treated as empty pricing entries after parsing with valibot schema, leading to test assertion failures [ccusage] (2026-02-12) Solution: After schema parsing, additionally check that input_cost_per_token or output_cost_per_token is not null. Filter out metadata entries without pricing data
4. Multiple attempts to SSH to the GPU node were rejected by pam_slurm_adopt (active SLURM job required), resulting in significant wasted time [error_recovery_benchmark] (2026-02-14) Solution: Users suggested checking whether the current node has a GPU using nvidia-smi -L. It was found that there were already 5 A800 GPUs on the current node, so no SSH transfer was required
Learnings Architecture Using external summary tools (ccusage) to export historical sessions is an effective engineering approach for solving AI context limitations: externalizing session summaries into files allows AI to quickly restore project context in new sessions, avoiding repeated background description Testing strategies for large robot RL projects should be clearly hierarchical: CPU unit tests, CPU smoke tests, GPU E2E testing. Avoid blocking the entire test process due to GPU unavailability Domain Knowledge The OSC controller (kp=150, Λ≈5-10kg) has strong resistance to external forces. An impulse with duration_steps=1 (about 20ms) is canceled out within the controller’s response cycle, effectively equivalent to no force application. To make disturbances visible, continuous force application must be maintained for several control cycles or use forces far beyond what the controller can handle MIHD enhancement plan is implemented in a strict dependency order: configuration basics (Idea 6 Phase 1) → normalization → UNI2+scGPT experiments → Q-Former/LLaVA → niche query → batch correction → full configuration integration. This order is designed to avoid cumulative refactoring costs Tools @praha/byethrow’s Result.try({try, catch}) returns a factory function (which must be called explicitly), rather than directly executing and returning a result. This differs from common Rust/fp-ts implementations of the Result monad. Special attention is needed In HPC clusters, use nvidia-smi -L to check whether the current node has a GPU before executing GPU tasks. If you need to SSH to remote nodes, you must explicitly change directory to the project folder and activate the conda environment in the remote command (SSH does not inherit the current shell working directory) ccusage’s offline pricing is pre-filtered using the isClaudeModel function in _macro.ts during construction. Extending multi-model support requires simultaneous modifications to both macro and _pricing-fetcher.ts ESLint rules for this project require all Node.js built-in objects to be explicitly imported via the node: protocol (e.g., import process from ’node:process’). Global objects cannot be used directly Debugging When debugging force injection or other physical mechanisms, it is important to first verify whether the mechanism itself works using extreme parameters (such as infinite force, 1000 consecutive steps), and then gradually adjust the parameters to a reasonable range. This should not be done starting from reasonable parameters AI Usage Notes Effective Solutions:
✓ Reconstructing cross-session project history by reading Markdown files in the ccusage summaries/ directory is an effective engineering approach for solving AI context window limitations ✓ Static code analysis (reading Makefile, test code, existing documentation) can effectively generate contributor guidelines when there is no git history ✓ Binary extreme testing ideas (first verifying the mechanism with infinite force, then adjusting parameters to a reasonable range) are more efficient than systematic code analysis when debugging physical simulations Limitations:
✗ Repeating the same error (SSH lacking the cd command) 15+ times within a single session. There is no ability to correct behavior based on repeated failures ✗ There are incorrect assumptions about library API behavior (Result.try() factory pattern). These can only be corrected after testing fails ✗ Failed to identify existing data assets (local ccusage.json pricing files). The default approach is to modify code logic rather than reuse existing files ✗ Mechanically following historical command templates (proxy settings) without verifying their applicability. Human intervention is needed to indicate that the task does not require network access Next Week’s Plans Next week will focus on three areas: (1) Debugging the root cause of force injection in error_recovery_benchmark — first verify whether the xfrc_applied mechanism works at all using extreme forces (infinite or 1000 consecutive steps), then investigate body mounting points and force direction issues. Ultimately, solve the collision geometry name mapping, which is a key E2E bottleneck; (2) ccusage GLM billing support — confirm that unrelated tests for apps/amp do not affect merging progress and move forward with the PR; (3) Update gadget/research CLAUDE.md — complete the interrupted documentation analysis and writing tasks. Robobrain.pi SAC training can be started in parallel when the GPU node is available, and we will observe the convergence curve.

summary: This week, we focused on three areas: robot simulation, bioinformatics, and toolchain development. In the error_recovery_benchmark project, deep blocking issues were encountered during force injection debugging (30N applied to the OSC controller had no noticeable effect), revealing fundamental problems with the controller’s compensation mechanisms. The MIHD project successfully completed the Chinese documentation for its enhancement plan. ccusage achieved support for GLM multi-model billing through type/form validation checks. Robobrain_pi confirmed that the training process is now ready for use. This week, AI revealed obvious pattern-matching defects in handling repeated errors (SSH lacking the cd command) and environmental assumptions (proxy, pnpm). Human intervention provided more effective debugging strategies during critical decision-making moments, such as extreme force testing, local GPU detection, and reusing existing pricing documents.
title: Weekly Summary 2026-W07

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-15 Human Thinking and Artificial Intelligence Thinking Strategic Level Depth of Visual Video Repair Role Thinking Style Human The user directly observed the video and concluded that “the robot arm didn’t move at all,” thereby refuting the conclusions based on logs from AI. The user also analyzed the root cause: visual demonstrations used ‘demonstration actions,’ while real-world scenarios used ’neutral actions.’ The behavior of the OSC controller was completely different, providing comparative evidence (tip_angle=2.98 rad was ineffective in the video). Artificial Intelligence AI announced a successful repair based on numerical changes in body position (15mm/117mm). The user pointed out that it is necessary to investigate the entire physical simulation cycle to reveal the controller’s compensation mechanism. Difference Analysis: Human direct observation is more reliable than AI analysis of indirect indicators. AI mistakenly believed it could rely on indirect indicators instead of direct evidence, indicating that human perception has limitations when unable to view videos.
Simplification and Value Proposition of Strategy Injection Architecture Role Thinking Style Human The user asked, “Can I inject errors into human demonstrations? Why can’t we use VLA for this?” This revealed the essence of the architecture and clarified the business value: generating more diverse errors that更接近真实使用场景。 Artificial Intelligence Initially, AI designed a complex multi-module architecture. Further explanation is needed to simplify the description. The only difference lies in the source of actions (HDF5 vs policy.predict()). This process can be fully reused. AI tends to start with technical feasibility analysis rather than discussing business value. Difference Analysis: Humans obtain a concise architecture description through simple questions and dominate in defining business value. AI focuses on implementation complexity rather than core principles.
Framework for Strategy Error Detection Architecture Design Role Thinking Style Human A complete framework was provided to analyze architectural problems (existing error definitions, searching for other methods’ network results, classifier types, data patterns, detector designs), and it was required to be written in a nested format for large/medium/small targets. Artificial Intelligence Parallel searches were conducted (code base ×2 + network ×1) to analyze literature and code status. A two-tier architecture (rule classifier + VLM) and 25 error classification systems were proposed. Initially written in English, but the formatting was incorrect after rewriting. Difference Analysis: Humans provide a framework and key constraints (language/formalization/hierarchy), while AI is responsible for integrating research and specific solutions. AI should confirm format requirements before generating results.
rclone Synchronization Architecture Identification and Device Summary Attribution Issues Role Thinking Style Human Architectural problems were identified, such as the risk of flat directory coverage and the lack of download functionality. During runtime, it was noted that “the devices responsible for summarization were not recorded,” indicating a missing data model. Artificial Intelligence Specific technical solutions were designed based on identified problems. However, during implementation, it limited itself to a single device perspective, storing device summaries as flat dictionaries. Difference Analysis: Humans identify architectural defects from the perspective of multi-device usage scenarios and business needs. AI has strong implementation capabilities but is limited by its current perspective, lacking scalability.
Workflow Preferences: Write Chinese Plan Documents First, Then Consider Execution Role Thinking Style Human The mode was repeatedly rejected, requiring plans to be written in a nested format for large/medium/small targets before execution. Plans were used as persistent tools between sessions to maintain state. Artificial Intelligence The default workflow is exploration → planning → ExitPlanMode → execution. Multiple user interventions are required to adjust the process, resulting in slow adaptation speed. Difference Analysis: There is a continuous gap between AI’s default workflow (ExitPlanMode-driven) and user preferences (plan-driven). Users have developed a more robust workflow.
Submodule Separation and Code Reference Integrity Role Thinking Style Human After completing the submodule conversion, the requirement to “separate from the original GitHub repository” was added. Considering the downstream impact of deleting the repository, it was required to fix all code references within the repository. Artificial Intelligence Submodule separation at the file system level was completed, and confirmation was awaited. It does not actively check old repository references in the code.** Difference Analysis: Humans identify hidden dependencies by considering downstream impacts. AI only completes file system-level separation and lacks awareness of migration task integrity checks.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Main Limitations ### Today’s Summary Key Points The OSC controller continuously applies joint torque during 10 physical substeps per env.step(), which is much greater than the typical external force injection (15-20N). This actually compensates for the force effect. Visual repair must use neutral actions during the force application period, allowing the controller to “maintain the current state” rather than tracking trajectories. Only in this way can the force effect be observed in the video. This is the root cause of differences in behavior during visualization and verification stages. MuJoCo’s xfrc_applied is a temporary buffer that is automatically reset after each physical step. To apply continuous forces, it is necessary to call injector.apply() again at each step. Visual effects cannot be confirmed solely through numerical changes. The controller may immediately correct errors within the same physical substep. Error scenario generation based on demonstration errors is highly efficient (100 scenarios/10 demonstrations), thanks to humans’ prior knowledge of “task-related trajectories.” Strategy deployment, especially random strategies, are less efficient, but their value lies in generating diverse errors that更接近 real deployment scenarios. These two methods complement each other and can balance quantity and quality. VLA and spatial genomics are developing towards the same architectural innovations (diffusion transformers/modal markers/comparing alignments). What is worth transferring to MIHD are flow matching fusion (continuous spatial generation embeddings) and register tokens+AdaLN (unified model across parts). Atomic writing (writing temporary files → os.replace renaming) is a standard method to prevent file corruption. The core three-step process of multi-device cloud synchronization is “uploading to the corresponding sub-directory + unified download entry point + automatically pulling during merging.” These three steps are essential. The diversity of benchmark error types depends on the “proposal diversity” of the detector, not the type of injector. If the detector only proposes pulse specifications, the verifier will never produce other types of errors. The problem lies at the detector level. The PolicyAdapter pattern uses the same process to support any strategy, such as random/robot simulation/VLA. Error detection has a two-tier architecture: a rule classifier (main layer, based on state thresholds, fast and definitive) and a VLM layer (auxiliary layer, generating natural language descriptions and cross-validation after video rendering). The former covers the observable state space, while the latter handles advanced semantics and fuzzy boundaries. Idempotency design: Embedting processed device lists in the exported JSON file (_merged_devices), allowing –summarize to support repeated executions without wasting API calls. Placing the _source_device field at the end of the JSON is the best balance between readability and compatibility (the tail is for quick viewing, merging does not read it, Python 3.7+ dictionaries maintain insertion order). Submodule migration integrity check: File system separation (deleting.git files) is just the first step. It is also necessary to use Grep to remove all hard-coded URL references in the entire library—the lifecycle of code references is much longer than git tracking. Practical Summary Correct lazy loading mode: Use local variables to temporarily store objects, only assigning values after the entire initialization chain (init + start_episode/load) is successful. This prevents half-initialization errors, and in case of failure, it will use _fallback to restore to a safe default value. The most reliable way to retain plans: Write Chinese plan documents into.md files (containing nested large/medium/small targets), and paste the plan text into a new session for implementation. This approach is more persistent than relying on tool call chains, allowing users to view and modify plans at any time. After remapping CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES to update the number of GPUs, MuJoCo EGL should use the mapped virtual numbers (always 0), rather than the physical GPU numbers. The output format of external tools requires defensive type checking; do not assume a fixed structure, especially between different platforms or versions. Session Summary MIHD (Spatial Transcriptomics Multimodal Benchmarking) 🔄 Q-Former Monitoring and Breakpoint Restart, Completing Quick Experiments, scGPT Evaluation, VLA Technology Migration Research 00:01:07.460 | claude_code The DCC session lasted a full day: after detecting a blockage in the Q-Former, it stopped working and focused on completing the general_fast task (47/49 successes, took 13 minutes), while running the scGPT feature extraction task (11 parts, 2 minutes, using 30.6/32.8GB of GPU resources) and the scGPT evaluation task (65/77 successes, with 12 parts causing memory overflow until the Q-Former finished its work). After restarting the Q-Former to run in the background, when the process crashed again, it automatically skipped the completed parts, eventually completing 8/11 parts of the work by the end of the session, with approximately 240 experiments conducted. Another session obtained VLA content from the user’s blog (FAST/Unified World Models/OpenVLA), combined with spatial genomics papers, and generated technology migration suggestions at level 1-3 (Flow Matching Fusion/Register Tokens+AdaLN), which were written into the plan documents. The calls of the Task tool continued intermittently.**
Error Recovery Benchmarking (Tianjin Node) 🔄 Visual Repair, Generating 60 Videos, Implementing PolicyAdapter Framework, Expanding to 118 Scenarios, Classifying Strategy Errors and Planning for VLA Injection 00:00:03.445 | claude_code Five main stages of the day: (1) The user analyzed the root cause of visual problems (demonstration actions offset by the OSC controller). AI implemented the three substeps of the third stage (neutral actions + reapplication + stabilization), and tested three groups simultaneously, with displacements exceeding 90 millimeters. (2) A new_burnish_visualize.py script was created to generate 60 videos for all 30 scenarios, fixing the漏洞 in selected_scenes boundaries. (3) The panoramic summary.md of the project was updated to version v4.1.0 → v4.2.0. (4) The PolicyAdapter framework was implemented (S1-S8 completed, 41/41 tests passed), and the lazy loading vulnerability of BCPolicy was fixed. (5) The smoke test for version v4.1.0 was completed, and it was expanded to 118 scenarios, integrating the BC strategy (trajectory NPZ storage), and implementing various types of detectors (ProximityDetector+pose_perturb, CollisionDetector+friction/pose_perturb). Finally, a Chinese plan document with 543 lines was generated for classifying strategy errors (large targets A-E), as well as a Chinese plan document for the VLA injection architecture (action source replacement, process reuse).**
Utility Tools (Summary of Tool Improvements) ✅ Atomic Writing to Prevent File Corruption, rclone Multi-Device Synchronization Workflow, Idempotency Skip + Device Recognition, ccusage Fixes, Test Submodule Separation 02:02:44.000 | claude_code Improvements on MacBook/Windows systems: (1) The device summary was changed to a per-device dictionary, achieving idempotency skip of _merged_devices, and the _source_device field was added at the end of the JSON file. (2) Atomic writing (temporary files + os.replace) was added, replacing five write calls. Anthropic tool_use mode and OpenAI response_format enforced structured output. (3) A complete rclone multi-device synchronization workflow was implemented (log/report sub-directories, _rclone_download_logs, merging–sync, default export only for reports). The AttributeError issue that occurred when ccusage returned a list was fixed, and the CLAUDE.md/README/tutorial files were updated. (4) A device recognition field was added to the output file, and test/submodule files were converted into regular files. Grep removed old URL references in report.py and benchmark_report.html (TzJ2006/test → TzJ2006/gadget/tree/main/test).**
Utility Tools (Daily Report Generation and Publishing) ✅ Structured daily reports were generated for February 12/13/14, and published on Hugo GitHub Pages 05:00:32.000 | claude_code Multiple sessions on Mac and Windows: as analysts of daily reports, I reviewed the conversation records between Mac and Windows (MIHD benchmarking/CalendarPro refactoring error recovery benchmarking), and generated structured JSON daily reports for February 12/13/14. Then, a Hugo preface was added to each report (a brief summary as a summary, aligned with the current time zone of -05:00), and they were written into the content/content/bugJournal files on the website. update.sh was run to generate 139 pages of content and push it to GitHub Pages. The first session was interrupted in the ExitPlanMode stage, while the second session completed the task by simply pasting the plan text. It was confirmed that the utility tool repository was up-to-date.**

The AI completed all stages in order: added 8 new configuration data classes in config.yaml/config_manager.py. run_benchmark.py included three-point normalization and additional configuration processes. New modules like QFormerFusion.py/BatchCorrection.py/NicheEncoder.py/niche_utils.py were also created. benchmark_config.yaml and models/init.py were updated as well. A summary script was created. Three missing spatial coordinate files were extracted from Dataset.zip. Issues related to frequency domain filtering checks and mclust → KMeans regression were fixed. GPU monitoring background thread sampling issues were resolved. After starting 209 general environment experiments, 58 records showed significant inconsistencies in 15 npz files. This issue was identified as a relative path problem in child process CWD and was fixed. After restarting, approximately 31 tasks were completed without failures. ErrorRecoveryBenchmark ✅ Fixed issues with mismatched demonstration playback environment configurations and recreated 30 valid error scenarios 23:49:10.228 | claude_code By analyzing HDF5 metadata, the user identified the root cause: the demonstration recording used PickPlace_D0 + OSC_POSE controller configuration files, but the script created a simple PickPlace without controller configuration. This caused different trajectories for the same action, and the robotic arm never approached the object (closest distance was 0.22 meters). The issue was resolved by dynamically loading the entire environment configuration from HDF5 env_args. The proximity threshold (0.05 meter limit) was restored. 30 valid scenarios were successfully recreated on an an49 GPU node (triggered steps 52-121, EEF < 5 centimeters). Two videos were generated for verification. The AI forgot to use the correct path three or four times when using SSH commands, but eventually resolved the issue by using absolute paths.
✅ Complete code library reorganization: removed dead code, transplanted 3 injectors, fixed rollout_generator, and completed GPU node smoke tests 18:30:39.228 | claude_code The user-provided 4-stage cleanup plan was executed: about 190 lines of dead code were removed from fingerprint.py, and duplicate detection methods in proximity.py were fixed. The signature inconsistency issue for generate_impulse_specs was also resolved. 12 redundant documents were deleted. FrictionInjector/PosePerturbInjector/GripperBiasInjector was transplanted from mimigen. rollout_generator.py was updated to use registry dynamic loading. injector.clear() was added to all exit paths. Issues with incorrect parameter names between detectors and injectors were resolved. Complete linkability smoke tests were conducted on the an49 node. Additionally, two new validators were implemented: LargeOffset/Stuck (16 unit tests, all passed). A Chinese summary document was generated (395 lines).

Static snapshot recovery logic was removed. Rendering from stage 1 to stage 3 was implemented, and 132 frames of video were generated for verification. The user asked: “The bread didn’t move; was the speed of the robotic arm 0.445 meters per second?” After reviewing the code, the AI identified a fundamental issue in the cvel index: angles/linear directions were reversed. This affected all modules that relied on object speed. Stability metrics like energy margin were also studied. Before making a final decision, the user interrupted the plan to fix and regenerate error-ridden scenario generation plans.
Gadgets ✅ Reorganized the daily report tool into a two-stage multi-device architecture, integrated ccusage, and created a GitHub repository 22:17:00.538 | claude_code daily_summary.py was reorganized into an export/merge two-stage architecture. It supports multi-device collaboration and integrates ccusage to automatically collect token usage and costs (including model details). The GitHub repository for gadgets was initialized (TzJ2006/gadget.git). test/ as a submodule was added. A.gitignore was created to prevent API tokens from being leaked. All source code was pushed to GitHub. Individual READMEs were created for each directory of the 7 tools, and a summary document for all directories was generated (399 lines). All files were pushed to GitHub.
CalendarPro ✅ Systemized code library reorganization: removed outdated files, extracted shared modules, and merged tests 18:23:24.871 | claude_code The user provided a detailed reorganization plan with precise line numbers. The AI executed 8 sub-tasks simultaneously: removed outdated directories like ai_planner/ and old test files. 5 Claude test files were merged into test_claude_provider.py. time_utils.py was created to unify time parsing across three locations. provider_selector.py unified the choice of AI providers in eight places. FIXED/FLEXIBLE_KEYWORDS were set as module-level constants. During execution, the AI used the wrong Python environment twice, but both were corrected by humans. Finally, all 26 core tests passed. Ruff lint reported no errors. **✅ P0 stability enhancement + P1 intelligent improvement: All 9 sub-tasks completed 19:04:15.684 | claude_code The user provided a detailed implementation plan and code examples. At the P0 level, provider.py added three-layer JSON fault tolerance. Three AI providers added asyncio.wait_for and httpx.Timeout to implement double timeout control. done_callback was added in asyncio.create_task. All file read/write operations at the service layer were wrapped in try/except. 56 intent classification test cases were created. At the P1 level, backfill_energy_change() was added to automatically collect learning data. EnergyProjector added personalize_rhythm(). IntelligentScheduler integrated with learning_service. prompts.py added structured dialogue context formatting. 107 out of 110 tests passed. Ruff reported no errors.

Production verification confirmed that breakfast at 08:00 and lunch at 12:00 were successfully scheduled. content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-12 Key Points of the Day Activities: The MIHD spatial transcriptomics multimodal fusion framework was planned and six improvement directions were initiated. Preliminary work included document organization and creating standardized tools. Methodology: The code library and QueST implementation methods were explored through parallel sub-tasks. A 7-stage implementation plan was developed, and the execution priorities of the code were determined (write documents first, then execute). Results: A clear technical roadmap and documentation were established, providing a structured foundation for the standardization of subsequent systems, Q-Former fusion, and batch correction processes. The MIHD project planned and initiated six improvement directions: standardization, Q-Former, QueST integration, and configuration refactoring. Work included revising the CLAUDE.md document, documenting the improvement plan, and creating standardized tools.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Complete documentation of MIHD improvement directions — A detailed implementation plan was developed using a hierarchy from big goals to medium goals and then to small and specific goals. The plan includes standardization, UNI2+scGPT experiments, Q-Former + LLaVA MLP fusion, QueST-specific queries, batch correction, and hyperparameter refactoring. These documents are stored in docs/ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md and.claude/plans/. ❌ 7-stage implementation — Due to user interference, stages 2 to 7 were not completed (requests required the plan document to be written before execution, and the use of ExitPlanMode was rejected). Only stage 1 has been completed so far. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Created standardized tools in utils/normalization.py — Functions such as l2_normalize, standard_scale, normalize_embeddings were implemented, along with their torch versions. Three-point normalization was also supported (after encoding, before fusion, after fusion). 🔄 Extended config.yaml and config_manager.py (Stage 1) — The encoder/fusion hyperparameters section was added to config.yaml, and NormalizationConfig was added as a dataclass in config_manager.py. User interference occurred during the editing of config.yaml. ✅ Revised CLAUDE.md document — The original document was reduced from 579 lines to 188 lines. Duplicated content was removed, and components such as GCN encoder and staig_fusion_e2e were added. ASCII architecture diagrams and test instructions were also included. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. Inconsistency in MIHD embedding standardization: UNI2/scGPT uses L2 normalization, while HIPT/MLP/GCN do not. No standardization was performed after fusion, affecting the quality of downstream clustering. Solution: A three-point normalization architecture was designed (after encoding, before fusion, after fusion). This was achieved by creating utils/normalization.py and integrating CLI parameters from run_benchmark.py.
Key Insight: L2 normalization is idempotent for normalized vectors. Therefore, additional L2 normalization for UNI2/scGPT is safe and harmless, ensuring that all encoder outputs are consistent in space.
General Problems 2. AI starts executing tasks directly after users explicitly request a plan document, causing process interruption. Solution: After the user’s explicit request, AI writes the complete plan into docs/ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md, and then begins the phased implementation. Key Insight: For large multi-stage tasks, it is better to save the plan in a file for review before granting execution rights.
3. AI tries to start execution using ExitPlanMode, but the user rejects it, causing stage 1 to be halted. Solution: When the user rejects it, the execution stops and waits for further instructions. Key Insight: Using ExitPlanMode requires explicit user authorization. It should not be assumed that accepting a plan means granting execution rights.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Writing plans in documents before implementation Role Thinking Style Human The user actively requests to write plans in documents before implementation, establishing a review-authorization workflow. AI AI tends to create TaskCreate tasks and execute them directly, ignoring the documentation process. Difference Analysis: Humans are more concerned with reviewability and version control; AI prefers immediate action, ignoring the value of keeping intermediate results.
Hyperparameter Configuration Role Thinking Style Human The user requests a complete configuration, meaning all hyperparameters are controlled by config. AI AI预设了几个可能的参数范围（仅融合层或仅关键参数），没有建议最大覆盖范围。 Difference Analysis: Humans have higher requirements for system reproducibility and adjustability, preferring to use complete configurations.
Choice between Q-Former and LaVA MLP Role Thinking Style Human The user explicitly requested both implementations, indicating that QueST functionality should be implemented in MIHD, not just analyzed. AI AI provides two options for the user to choose from, suggesting a trade-off rather than superposition. Difference Analysis: Humans have a stronger “complete implementation” mindset, not constrained by resource assumptions; AI tends to provide simplified options.
Limitations of AI General Limitations AI often acts immediately in large multi-stage tasks, without suggesting that plans should be saved in documents for review before execution. User prompts are needed to adjust the workflow. When calling ExitPlanMode, AI did not anticipate that users might want to manually control the execution speed. This led to user interruptions when rejecting it, with a lack of awareness between “plan approval” and “execution authorization”. When providing options (Q-Former vs LaVA MLP), AI defaulted to mutually exclusive options, without suggesting a solution for “two implementation methods”. This requires user correction. Today’s Summary Key Summary - Inconsistency in MIHD fusion process: UNI2/scGPT underwent L2 normalization, while HIPT/GCN/MLP did not. ConcatFusion only performed pre-connection normalization, without post-fusion normalization. The three-point normalization architecture (after encoder, before fusion, after fusion) is a key architectural decision that helps improve downstream clustering results. QueST achieved batch correction through BatchDiscriminator (MLP discriminator) + GIN layer adversarial training. This method can be used as a plug-and-play module for MIHD post-fusion. Its unique query mechanism utilizes k-hop subgraph pooling and cosine similarity for cross-sample retrieval. Both mechanisms are worth replicating. Q-Former (BLIP-2) uses learnable queries and cross-attention to extract visual feature information. In practice, LaVA MLP used a simpler two-layer MLP projector instead of Q-Former. Their roles differ: Q-Former is suitable for scenarios requiring sparse queries, while LaVA MLP is better suited for efficient alignment tasks. Practical Summary For large multi-stage implementations, the correct workflow is: explore code library → write plans in documents → user review → user authorization → phased implementation. Skipping any step may lead to user interference and decreased efficiency. Meeting Summary 🔄 Revised CLAUDE.md + Planning and Documenting MIHD Improvement Plan 17:12:36.312 | claude_code The user triggered the revision of CLAUD.md using the.init command. Claude reduced it from 579 lines to 188 lines, removing duplicate content and adding missing component documentation. The user proposed six improvements: normalization processing, UNI2+scGPT experiments, Q-Former fusion, QueST unique query mechanism, batch correction, and hyperparameter refactoring. Claude explored code libraries and QueST implementation schemes through parallel sub-tasks and confirmed key decisions (both fusion methods were implemented, and the complete process configuration was also completed). Finally, a complete 7-stage implementation plan was written in.claude/plans/, but the user rejected using ExitPlanMode for automatic execution.
🔄 MIHD Improvement Plan Launched: Writing Plan Documents, Creating Normalization Modules, and Expanding Configurations 17:34:50.362 | claude_code The user requested implementation according to the plan. AI wrote the plan into docs/ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md (under the user’s explicit request), then reviewed core files such as run_benchmark.py, Fusion.py, config.yaml, and config_manager.py.
